ME 270: Design for assembly (dfa): Design for Assembly (DFA) minimizes assembly time and cost through systematic design simplification: reducing part count, eliminating fasteners, ensuring easy part handling and insertion, mistake-proofing (poka-yoke), and designing for automated assembly. DFA methodologies provide quantitative metrics for comparing designs based on assembly difficulty, using databases of handling and insertion tim
ME 270: Geometric dimensioning and toler: In manufacturability context, GD&T enables functional specifications maximizing manufacturing tolerance while ensuring assembly and performance requirements. Understanding GD&T allows designers to specify what matters functionally while allowing manufacturing flexibility in non-critical aspects. Proper datum selection aligns with manufacturing and inspection fixtures. Using position tolerances wit
ME 270: Design for economic manufacture: Design for economic manufacture optimizes designs for specific manufacturing processes to minimize cost while meeting requirements. For material removal (machining), this means minimizing cuts, using standard tools, avoiding difficult geometries, and considering setup time. For casting, it involves uniform wall thickness, generous fillets, draft angles, and parting line placement. For forming (sta
ME 270: Product design specification: Product Design Specification (PDS) is a comprehensive document defining all requirements a product must satisfy, including functional performance, regulatory compliance, manufacturing constraints, cost targets, reliability, aesthetics, environmental conditions, and market positioning. The PDS guides design decisions, provides measurable success criteria, and serves as a contract between stakeholde
ME 270: Introduction to machine tools (i: Machine tools remove material to create precise shapes through operations like turning (lathe), milling (end mill/face mill), drilling, grinding, and boring. Understanding machine tool capabilities, limitations, and economics informs design decisions. Lathes create cylindrical parts through rotation. Mills create features on flat or contoured surfaces. Each machine has accuracy limits, size constr
ME 270: Rapid prototyping: Rapid prototyping (additive manufacturing/3D printing) builds parts layer-by-layer from CAD models without tooling, enabling fast iteration, complex geometries, and customization. Technologies include FDM (fused deposition modeling), SLA (stereolithography), SLS (selective laser sintering), and metal printing. Applications range from concept models to functional prototypes to end-use parts. Design
ME 270: Selection of materials (life-cyc: Material selection considering life-cycle economics evaluates total cost of ownership, not just material purchase price. Analysis includes manufacturing costs (machinability, yield, scrap), operating costs (weight affecting fuel, energy efficiency), maintenance (corrosion resistance, wear), and end-of-life (recyclability, disposal). Methods like Ashby charts plot material properties enabling syste
ME 270: Quality function deployment (qfd: Quality Function Deployment (QFD) is a structured method for translating customer requirements into engineering specifications and manufacturing parameters. The process uses matrices (House of Quality) correlating 'what' customers want with 'how' engineering will deliver it, prioritizing features based on importance and competitive benchmarking. QFD cascades customer voice through multiple stages:
ME 270: Inspection/metrology (cmm/gd&t): Inspection and metrology verify manufactured parts meet design specifications using measurement instruments and techniques. Coordinate Measuring Machines (CMMs) are programmable devices with precision probes measuring 3D coordinates of part features, comparing actual dimensions to CAD models. CMMs excel at verifying GD&T specifications (position, perpendicularity, flatness, etc.) providing objecti
ME 170: Geometric dimensioning and toler: Geometric Dimensioning and Tolerancing (GD&T) uses symbols and standards (ASME Y14.5, ISO 1101) to specify geometric characteristics and tolerances beyond simple dimensional limits. GD&T controls form (straightness, flatness), orientation (parallelism, perpendicularity), location (position, concentricity), profile, and runout. Tolerances are specified in feature control frames relative to datum re
ME 170: Introduction to cad: Computer-Aided Design (CAD) uses software to create, modify, analyze, and optimize designs digitally, replacing traditional drafting with precise digital models. CAD systems enable designers to visualize concepts in 2D or 3D, perform simulations, generate manufacturing instructions, and collaborate efficiently. The technology revolutionized engineering by improving accuracy, enabling rapid iterati
ME 170: Coordinate dimensioning and tole: Coordinate dimensioning specifies locations using X, Y, Z distances from reference datums, providing absolute position information. Tolerancing defines acceptable variation from nominal dimensions, expressed as bilateral (±), unilateral (+/-0), or limit dimensions. Standards (ASME Y14.5, ISO 1101) govern notation, symbol usage, and interpretation. Understanding statistical tolerance accumulation,
ME 170: Basic part modeling: Basic part modeling involves creating simple individual components using fundamental CAD operations: sketching 2D profiles, adding dimensions and constraints, then using operations like extrude, revolve, or sweep to create 3D geometry. The process emphasizes design intent through parametric relationships, feature-based modeling, and proper construction sequence. Beginning with simple geometric sha
ME 170: Variables: Variables in parametric CAD are named parameters storing values (dimensions, counts, options) that can be referenced throughout a model, enabling centralized control of design parameters. Variables can be dimensions (length = 50mm), mathematical expressions (width = 2*length), or conditional (if height > 100, use_reinforcement = yes). Using variables rather than hard-coded numbers makes design int
ME 170: Forms and tables: Forms and tables organize parametric data, enabling efficient configuration of part families and design variants. Design tables use spreadsheet-like interfaces where rows represent configurations and columns represent parameters, allowing rapid generation of multiple part sizes from one parametric model. Forms provide user-friendly interfaces for entering design parameters, often with validation,
ME 170: Creating parametric designs: Parametric design creates models controlled by parameters (dimensions, equations, relationships) that automatically update geometry when parameter values change. This approach captures design intent through dimensional constraints, geometric relationships, and mathematical formulas. Parametric models enable rapid design iteration, family of parts creation (different sizes from one model), and opti
ME 170: Feature selection: Feature selection involves choosing appropriate modeling operations (extrude, revolve, sweep, loft, holes, fillets, chamfers, etc.) to create desired geometry efficiently while capturing design intent. Good feature selection considers manufacturability, model flexibility, computational efficiency, and ease of modification. Features should represent meaningful design elements (a bolt hole, not just
ME 170: Equations: Equations in CAD define mathematical relationships between parameters, creating intelligent geometry that maintains design intent during modifications. Equations use variables, arithmetic operators, and functions (sin, cos, if-then, etc.) to express dependencies like 'hole_spacing = part_length / 4' or 'thickness = max(2mm, load/1000)'. This enables complex parametric behavior: adapting geometry t
ME 170: Detailing and blueprint creation: Detailing involves creating technical drawings (blueprints) from 3D CAD models, providing manufacturing-ready documentation with orthographic views, dimensions, tolerances, notes, and symbols. The process includes selecting appropriate views, adding dimensions following standards (ASME Y14.5, ISO), specifying tolerances, noting materials and finishes, and creating title blocks. Modern CAD automate
TAM 251: Design based on yield strength,: Design based on strength ensures stresses remain below material limits with safety factors. Yield strength σy is stress where permanent deformation begins; ultimate strength σu is maximum stress before failure. Allowable stress: σallow = σy/SF (yield) or σu/SF (ultimate), where SF is safety factor (typically 1.5-3). Required area: A ≥ P/σallow. Design considers worst-case loading, material variabi
ME 451: System assembly: System assembly integrates components into complete products, involving mechanical fastening, welding, adhesive bonding, and other joining methods. Assembly system design considers product architecture, joining methods, sequence planning, tooling, automation level, and quality verification. Design for assembly (DFA) principles simplify products reducing part count, standardizing fasteners, and facilitating orientation and insertion. Assembly systems range from manual workstations to fully automated robotic cells. Balancing assembly lines, minimizing cycle time, and ensuring quality are key challenges. Effective assembly engineering reduces cost and improves product quality..
ME 451: Assembly system design: Assembly system design creates production systems integrating components into products efficiently and reliably. Decisions include manual versus automated assembly, workstation layout, material handling, tooling, quality checks, and pacing (line balancing). Design considerations encompass production volume, product variety, assembly complexity, quality requirements, and investment constraints. System types include single workstations, manual assembly lines, flexible assembly systems, and dedicated automation. Simulation evaluates alternatives for throughput, utilization, and quality. Effective assembly system design balances productivity, flexibility, quality, and cost aligned with business strategy..
ME 451: Computer-aided design (cad): Computer-aided design (CAD) uses software tools to create, modify, analyze, and optimize product designs digitally. CAD systems provide geometric modeling capabilities, parametric design, assembly modeling, and engineering analysis integration. Benefits include rapid design iteration, accurate dimensional control, design reuse, and seamless integration with manufacturing systems. Modern CAD emphasizes parametric and feature-based modeling where designs are defined by parameters and geometric features rather than explicit dimensions. CAD has revolutionized product development across industries from aerospace to consumer products..
ME 455: Classification and coding scheme: Classification and coding schemes in micro-manufacturing organize micro-components by geometry, material, process requirements, and assembly characteristics enabling group technology and process standardization. Codes capture feature sizes, aspect ratios, tolerances, materials, and manufacturing methods. Benefits include design retrieval, process planning templates, and identifying opportunities for design standardization. At micro-scale, additional attributes like surface forces, handling requirements, and inspection methods become important classification criteria. Understanding classification supports efficient micro-manufacturing planning and knowledge management..
ME 451: Geometric tolerancing: Geometric dimensioning and tolerancing (GD&T) is a symbolic language for specifying allowable variations in part geometry beyond simple dimensional tolerances. GD&T controls form (flatness, roundness), orientation (perpendicularity, parallelism), location (position, concentricity), and runout using standardized symbols and datums. This system provides precise, unambiguous communication between design and manufacturing, enables functional tolerancing based on part requirements, and facilitates statistical tolerance analysis. Understanding GD&T is essential for interpreting drawings, planning inspection, and ensuring parts fit and function properly in assemblies..
ME 455: Manufacturing automation: Manufacturing automation in micro-production integrates automated material handling, process control, inspection, and assembly to achieve efficient, consistent micro-component production. Automation addresses challenges of manual micro-part handling including damage, contamination, and low throughput. Systems include robotic pick-and-place, vision-guided alignment, automated inspection, and process monitoring. Design considerations encompass part presentation, fixturing, error recovery, and human-machine interface. Understanding automation principles enables scaling micro-manufacturing from laboratory prototypes to production volumes while maintaining quality and reducing cost..
ME 451: Just-in-time philosophy: Just-in-time (JIT) philosophy produces and delivers parts exactly when needed, eliminating inventory waste and exposing production problems. Core elements include pull systems triggered by customer demand, small lot production, quick changeover, and supplier partnerships for frequent delivery. JIT requires stable, capable processes since inventory doesn't buffer problems. Benefits include reduced inventory costs, faster problem detection, increased flexibility, and better space utilization. Implementing JIT transforms operations from push (build-to-forecast) to pull (build-to-order), dramatically improving responsiveness and efficiency..
ME 451: Case study-micro-bearing assembl: The micro-bearing assembly case study examines challenges of assembling miniature precision components with tight tolerances and delicate handling requirements. Issues include part feeding and orientation at small scales, precision placement, force control preventing damage, inspection with microscopy or vision, and automation justification for volume and consistency. The case illustrates design for assembly principles, fixture design, vision-guided robotics, and quality assurance strategies specific to micro-assembly. Analyzing this real-world application integrates assembly engineering concepts and reveals practical challenges translating theory to practice..
ME 451: Computer-aided process planning: Computer-aided process planning (CAPP) automates the conversion of design specifications into detailed manufacturing instructions. CAPP systems determine operation sequences, select machines and tools, calculate speeds and feeds, and estimate times and costs. Approaches include variant CAPP (retrieving and modifying existing plans for similar parts) and generative CAPP (creating plans from scratch using decision rules). Integration with CAD provides design data while connection to scheduling systems enables production planning. CAPP improves planning consistency, reduces lead time, and captures manufacturing knowledge..
ME 451: Creo parametric solid modeling a: Creo Parametric (formerly Pro/ENGINEER) is a comprehensive CAD/CAM software emphasizing parametric solid modeling and manufacturing integration. ProManufacture modules enable direct NC programming from CAD models, including toolpath generation for milling, turning, and multi-axis machining. The parametric foundation allows design changes to automatically propagate to manufacturing programs. Creo integrates part design, assembly, simulation, and manufacturing in a unified environment. Learning Creo provides practical experience with industry-standard tools and parametric design workflows, bridging design and manufacturing..
ME 451: Machining simulation and process: Machining simulation creates virtual models predicting cutting processes, tool paths, material removal, and results before physical production. Simulations verify NC programs for collisions, gouges, and excess material, predict surface finish, calculate cycle times, and optimize parameters. Integration with process planning enables evaluating alternative sequences and parameters virtually. Simulation reduces trial-and-error, prevents machine crashes, and enables complex multi-axis programming. Modern systems incorporate geometric verification, physics-based cutting simulation, and optimization capabilities, significantly improving first-part quality and reducing development time..
ME 451: Optimal process plans using a co: Optimal process planning uses cost models to select manufacturing sequences minimizing total cost while meeting quality requirements. Cost models include machine hourly rates, tooling costs, setup times, material costs, and quality costs. Optimization considers alternative operation sequences, machine selections, tool choices, and cutting parameters. Techniques include mathematical programming, heuristic search, and simulation. Trade-offs balance machining time, tool costs, machine utilization, and quality. Understanding cost-based optimization enables economically efficient manufacturing decisions aligning with business objectives..
ME 455: Microfactory: architecture and s: Microfactory is a compact, integrated manufacturing system for producing micro-products combining micro-machine tools, automated handling, inspection, and assembly in minimal footprint. Architecture emphasizes modularity, reconfigurability, and distributed control. Benefits include reduced environmental impact, localized production, and efficient use of space and resources. System integration challenges include communication protocols, workflow coordination, and balancing automation with flexibility. Understanding microfactory concepts represents the future of sustainable, distributed micro-manufacturing aligned with Industry 4.0 principles..
ME 451: Parametric design: Parametric design defines models using parameters and relationships rather than fixed dimensions, enabling rapid design exploration and automated updates. Changing a parameter (like diameter or length) automatically updates dependent features throughout the model. Design intent is captured through constraints (parallel, perpendicular, tangent) and equations linking dimensions. Parametric modeling supports design families where variations share common structure but differ in specific dimensions. This approach dramatically reduces redesign time and maintains design consistency. Most modern CAD systems employ parametric modeling as the primary paradigm..
ME 451: Inspection and measurement: Inspection and measurement verify that manufactured parts meet design specifications through dimensional, geometric, and surface finish checks. Methods range from manual tools (calipers, micrometers) to automated systems (CMMs, vision systems, laser scanners). Sampling strategies balance cost with risk of accepting defective parts. Statistical process control (SPC) monitors production trends enabling corrective action before defects occur. Modern quality systems integrate measurement with production systems for real-time feedback. Understanding inspection principles ensures quality assurance and continuous improvement..
ME 455: Issues with micro-assembly: Micro-assembly faces unique challenges stemming from scaling effects and precision requirements. Adhesion forces (van der Waals, electrostatic, capillary) dominate over gravity making parts stick to tools. Positional uncertainty from thermal drift and vibration becomes significant relative to feature sizes. Vision system depth-of-field limits and optical resolution constrain observation. Part variability requires adaptive strategies. Yield and throughput challenges affect economic viability. Understanding these issues guides developing robust micro-assembly solutions through appropriate tool design, environmental control, and process strategies..
ME 451: Machining: Machining removes material from workpieces using cutting tools to create desired shapes and dimensions. Primary processes include milling (rotating cutter, moving workpiece), turning (rotating workpiece, stationary tool), drilling, boring, and grinding. Key parameters include cutting speed, feed rate, depth of cut, and tool geometry, affecting surface finish, dimensional accuracy, tool life, and productivity. Material properties, cutting forces, chip formation, and heat generation influence process selection and parameters. Understanding machining fundamentals is essential for manufacturing engineering, enabling informed decisions about process planning and optimization..
ME 330: Commercial processes: Commercial processes are industrial-scale manufacturing methods combining multiple steps optimized for efficiency, quality, and cost. Examples include integrated steel production (blast furnace, BOF, continuous casting, rolling), aluminum production (Bayer process, Hall-Héroult electrolysis, casting, forming), and semiconductor processing (crystal growth, wafer slicing, lithography). Understanding
ME 330: Composites processing: Composites processing creates shaped parts from reinforcement and matrix, including hand layup (labor-intensive, low volume), spray-up (faster, moderate volume), filament winding (cylindrical parts), pultrusion (constant cross-section), resin transfer molding (RTM), and autoclave curing. Processing goals include achieving fiber content and orientation, eliminating voids, developing matrix properti
ME 402: Conceptual design of thermal sys: Conceptual design generates and evaluates alternative system configurations before detailed design. This creative phase identifies possible thermodynamic cycles, component arrangements, heat integration opportunities, and control strategies to meet design objectives. Techniques include brainstorming, morphological analysis, functional decomposition, and benchmarking existing systems. Feasibility screening eliminates impractical concepts using first-order analysis and constraint checking. Promising concepts advance to preliminary modeling and comparison. Conceptual design establishes the system architecture—arguably the most critical phase determining ultimate performance and cost. Success requires creativity, broad knowledge of available technologies, understanding of physical constraints, and ability to synthesize information into coherent system concepts..
ME 458: Product architecture: The scheme by which functional elements of a product are arranged into physical components and how these components interact and interface with each other. Architecture defines the modular decomposition of a product, establishing which functions are implemented by which components, how components are connected, and the nature of those connections. In additive manufacturing, architecture decisions affect manufacturability, assembly requirements, customization potential, and maintenance strategies. The choice between modular versus integral architectures impacts manufacturing complexity, performance optimization, and product variety management..
ME 458: Product specifications: The precise, measurable, and verifiable criteria that define what a product must achieve to satisfy customer needs and technical requirements. Specifications translate qualitative customer needs into quantitative metrics including performance targets, physical dimensions, material properties, manufacturing constraints, and regulatory compliance standards. In additive manufacturing, specifications must account for layer resolution, material properties, surface finish, geometric accuracy, and post-processing requirements. These serve as objective benchmarks against which design concepts and prototypes are evaluated throughout development..
ME 371: Machine components: Machine components are standardized mechanical elements performing specific functions: power transmission (gears, belts, chains, shafts), support and guidance (bearings, slides), fastening (bolts, pins, welds), motion control (springs, clutches, brakes), and sealing (gaskets, O-rings). Understanding component selection involves matching characteristics to application requirements, considering stan
ME 458: Development processes and organi: Additive manufacturing transforms product development processes and organizational structures by enabling rapid prototyping, design iteration, customization, and distributed manufacturing. Development workflows integrate AM for functional prototyping, tooling, and end-use parts production. Design for additive manufacturing (DfAM) exploits capabilities like topology optimization, lattice structures, and part consolidation impossible with traditional methods. Organizational impacts include reduced supply chains, on-demand production, and new business models around digital inventory. Understanding AM's strategic implications enables leveraging it for competitive advantage beyond simply replacing traditional manufacturing..
ME 371: Cae evaluate the strength of unk: Computer-Aided Engineering (CAE) evaluates component strength when geometry is known but materials, loads, or design details are uncertain. Techniques include reverse engineering (measuring geometry, identifying materials through testing), load estimation (usage scenarios, similar components, customer requirements), parametric studies (varying uncertain parameters), and validation through testing
ME 458: Opportunity identification and p: The systematic process of recognizing market gaps, unmet user needs, and technological possibilities that can be addressed through new product development in additive manufacturing contexts. This involves analyzing market trends, competitive landscapes, technological capabilities, and customer pain points to determine viable product opportunities. The planning phase establishes strategic goals, resource allocation, timelines, and success metrics for product development initiatives, ensuring alignment between market opportunities and organizational capabilities in the additive manufacturing domain..
ME 458: Additive manufacturing processes: Additive manufacturing (AM) builds parts layer-by-layer from digital models using diverse processes and materials. Processes include fused deposition modeling (polymers), stereolithography (photopolymers), selective laser sintering (metals, polymers), binder jetting, and directed energy deposition. Each process offers distinct capabilities regarding resolution, materials, build volume, and properties. Materials span polymers, metals, ceramics, and composites with expanding options. Understanding process-material relationships enables selecting appropriate AM approaches for specific applications balancing part requirements, production volume, and economics..
ME 458: Concept generation, selection, a: The iterative process of creating multiple design alternatives through creative ideation techniques, systematically evaluating these concepts against product specifications and constraints, and validating selected concepts through experimentation and prototyping. Generation employs methods like brainstorming, morphological analysis, and biomimicry to produce diverse solutions. Selection uses decision matrices, weighted scoring, and expert judgment to identify the most promising concepts. Testing validates assumptions through physical prototypes, simulations, and user feedback, particularly leveraging the rapid prototyping capabilities of additive manufacturing to accelerate learning cycles..
ME 458: Industrial design: The professional practice of designing products that balance aesthetic appeal, ergonomic functionality, user experience, and manufacturing feasibility. Industrial design addresses form, color, texture, visual semantics, and emotional response while ensuring products are comfortable, intuitive, and delightful to use. In additive manufacturing contexts, industrial designers leverage geometric freedom to create organic forms, customized ergonomics, and complex surface textures previously difficult or impossible with traditional manufacturing. The discipline bridges art and engineering, considering cultural context, brand identity, and sustainable materials alongside technical performance..
ME 452: Performance analysis of nc syste: NC performance analysis quantifies machine capabilities through standardized tests and metrics. Key measures include positioning accuracy (deviation from target), repeatability (consistency), circular interpolation accuracy (contouring error), and dynamic performance (acceleration, jerk limits). Test methods include laser interferometry for linear accuracy, ballbar testing for circular motion, and step response characterization. Analysis identifies limiting factors (mechanical, thermal, control) and guides improvement efforts. Performance data supports process capability studies, tolerance assignment, and machine selection. Regular performance monitoring detects degradation enabling preventive maintenance..
ME 270: Concept selection: Concept selection systematically evaluates and chooses among alternative design concepts using structured methods like Pugh matrices, weighted decision matrices, or analytical hierarchy process (AHP). The process defines evaluation criteria (performance, cost, manufacturability, risk, etc.), weights their importance, scores alternatives, and calculates overall ratings. Effective concept selection
ME 270: Dfm overview/strategy: Design for Manufacturability (DFM) is a systematic approach integrating manufacturing considerations early in design, reducing production costs, improving quality, and shortening time-to-market. DFM strategies include simplifying designs, reducing part counts, using standard components, selecting appropriate materials and processes, designing for existing capabilities, and considering assembly. Th
ME 270: Design of experiments: Design of Experiments (DOE) is a statistical methodology for systematically varying process or design parameters to understand their effects on outcomes, optimize performance, and quantify relationships. DOE techniques include factorial designs (testing all combinations), fractional factorial (testing subset), response surface methodology (optimizing), and Taguchi methods (robust design). DOE enab
ME 451: Quality engineering: Quality engineering applies engineering methods to prevent defects rather than detect them after occurrence. Approaches include robust design making products insensitive to variation sources, design of experiments optimizing parameters, failure mode and effects analysis (FMEA) preventing problems, and statistical process control monitoring production. Quality function deployment (QFD) translates customer requirements into engineering specifications. Six Sigma methodology uses DMAIC (Define-Measure-Analyze-Improve-Control) for breakthrough improvement. Quality engineering integrates prevention throughout product and process development, achieving high quality at low cost..
ME 451: Lean manufacturing systems: Lean manufacturing is a systematic approach to eliminating waste and maximizing value from the customer perspective. Core principles include identifying value, mapping value streams, creating flow, implementing pull systems, and pursuing perfection through continuous improvement (kaizen). Waste categories include overproduction, waiting, transportation, excess inventory, unnecessary motion, defects, and unused creativity. Tools include 5S workplace organization, visual management, standardized work, and mistake-proofing (poka-yoke). Lean transforms organizations toward efficient, responsive production systems delivering quality products with minimal resources..
ME 451: Improving product flow and elimi: Improving product flow and eliminating waste involves systematically analyzing and redesigning processes to remove non-value-adding activities. Value stream mapping visualizes material and information flow, identifying waste. Strategies include cellular manufacturing grouping equipment by product family, balancing workloads, implementing single-piece flow, reducing setup times through SMED (Single Minute Exchange of Dies), and synchronizing operations. Visual management makes problems visible, enabling rapid response. Continuous improvement engages workers in identifying and solving problems. These strategies transform batch-and-queue production into smooth, efficient flow..
ME 451: Classification and coding scheme: Classification and coding schemes organize parts into families sharing similar characteristics enabling group technology and cellular manufacturing. Codes capture attributes like geometry, material, size, and manufacturing processes using hierarchical or chain-type structures. The Opitz code, for example, uses digits representing form features, dimensions, and materials. Benefits include design retrieval (finding existing similar parts), process planning standardization, and manufacturing cell formation. Database systems use codes for efficient search and retrieval. Implementing classification systems requires balancing code complexity with practical usability..
ME 451: Design of experiments for proces: Design of experiments (DOE) systematically varies process parameters to determine their effects on outcomes, enabling optimization. Experimental designs like factorial, fractional factorial, and response surface methods efficiently explore parameter spaces. Statistical analysis identifies significant factors, interactions, and optimal settings. DOE provides more information than one-factor-at-a-time experiments while requiring fewer runs. Applications include optimizing machining parameters, injection molding conditions, and assembly processes. Understanding DOE enables data-driven decision-making, reduces trial-and-error, and achieves robust process settings..
ME 370: Critical design reviews: Critical design reviews are formal evaluations where teams present designs to stakeholders, experts, or instructors for feedback and approval before proceeding. Reviews assess technical feasibility, requirement satisfaction, risk identification, resource adequacy, and schedule realism. Participants ask challenging questions, identify flaws, suggest improvements, and verify readiness for next phase
ME 370: Engineering design competition: Engineering design competitions challenge students to design, build, and test solutions meeting specific performance criteria within constraints (time, budget, materials, rules). Competitions develop practical skills: project management, teamwork, fabrication, testing, iteration, and presentation. Common formats include robot competitions, bridge building, efficiency challenges, and product design
ME 370: Design thinking: Design thinking is a human-centered, iterative problem-solving methodology emphasizing empathy, ideation, prototyping, and testing. The process involves understanding user needs through observation and interviews (empathize), defining the core problem (define), generating diverse solutions (ideate), building tangible representations (prototype), and testing with users (test). Design thinking encou
ME 370: Human centered design project: Human-centered design projects apply design thinking principles to real-world problems, focusing intensely on end-user needs, contexts, and experiences. Projects involve extensive user research (interviews, observations, immersion), defining meaningful problem statements, ideating numerous solutions, creating prototypes, and testing with actual users. The process iterates based on user feedback, e
ME 458: Identifying customer needs: A structured methodology for discovering, analyzing, and documenting what customers truly require from a product through interviews, observations, surveys, and ethnographic research. In additive manufacturing contexts, this involves understanding both explicit needs (what customers say they want) and latent needs (underlying problems they may not articulate). The process translates qualitative customer data into actionable design requirements, prioritizing needs based on importance and frequency to guide product development decisions and ensure market fit..
ME 402: Optimization and economic analys: Optimization systematically finds design parameters maximizing performance (efficiency, capacity) or minimizing cost while satisfying constraints. Techniques include calculus-based methods (Lagrange multipliers), search methods (golden section, gradient descent), genetic algorithms, and linear/nonlinear programming. Economic analysis evaluates life-cycle costs including capital investment, operating expenses, maintenance, and energy costs considering time value of money. Metrics include net present value (NPV), payback period, and internal rate of return (IRR). Trade-offs between capital and operating costs guide design decisions. Sensitivity analysis addresses uncertainty. Optimization integrated with economic analysis produces designs that are not just technically feasible but economically viable, ensuring successful implementation..
ME 453: Modeling of process quality: Process quality modeling establishes mathematical relationships between process inputs (parameters, conditions) and quality outputs (dimensions, defects, properties). Models range from simple linear regression to complex physics-based or machine learning models. Techniques include design of experiments identifying significant factors, response surface methodology optimizing parameters, and multivariate analysis handling correlated variables. Quality models enable prediction, optimization, and control of manufacturing processes. Understanding modeling approaches and their assumptions is essential for developing effective quality monitoring and improvement systems..
ME 453: Inferences about quality: Quality inference involves drawing conclusions about process performance and product quality from sample data through statistical hypothesis testing. Common inferences include comparing process means against specifications, testing for differences between processes or machines, and determining if process changes affect quality. Techniques include t-tests, ANOVA, chi-square tests, and confidence intervals. Understanding sampling distributions, Type I and Type II errors, and power analysis ensures valid conclusions. Proper inference accounts for measurement uncertainty and variation, enabling data-driven decisions about process adjustments, acceptance/rejection, and capability..
ME 453: Quality monitoring in today's ma: Modern quality monitoring integrates real-time sensors, automated data collection, cloud analytics, and machine learning to provide comprehensive process oversight. Systems collect data from machines, vision systems, and in-line gauges, analyzing it for trends, anomalies, and predictions. Digital dashboards provide visibility across production floors and facilities. Predictive analytics forecast quality issues before occurrence enabling preemptive action. Integration with MES (Manufacturing Execution Systems) closes the loop between quality data and process control. Understanding modern quality monitoring approaches prepares engineers for Industry 4.0 manufacturing environments..
ME 458: Design feedback: The systematic process of gathering, interpreting, and incorporating critiques, observations, and suggestions about design concepts from stakeholders including users, team members, domain experts, and instructors. Effective feedback practices include structured critique sessions, usability testing protocols, peer reviews, and expert evaluations using established criteria. The process requires presenting designs clearly, asking specific questions, listening actively without defensiveness, distinguishing between observations and interpretations, and prioritizing feedback based on project goals. Additive manufacturing's rapid prototyping enables more frequent, tangible feedback cycles with physical artifacts rather than abstract descriptions..
ME 453: Control charts for attributes: Attribute control charts monitor discrete quality characteristics like defect counts, nonconformities, or pass/fail classifications. Common charts include p-charts (proportion defective), np-charts (number defective), c-charts (defect count per unit), and u-charts (defects per unit with varying sample sizes). Based on binomial or Poisson distributions rather than normal, attribute charts require larger sample sizes than variables charts. They're simpler to implement when measurements are expensive or impractical. Understanding attribute charts enables quality monitoring for characteristics difficult to measure continuously..
ME 453: Measurement system analysis: Measurement System Analysis (MSA) evaluates the quality of measurement processes through studies quantifying repeatability (same operator, same part), reproducibility (different operators, same part), accuracy (bias from true value), and stability (consistency over time). Gage R&R studies partition total observed variation into part variation and measurement system variation. MSA ensures measurement systems are adequate for their intended purpose before using data for process control or decision making. Poor measurement systems mask true process variation or create false alarms. Understanding MSA is critical for reliable quality data..
ME 453: Feature generation: Feature generation creates meaningful variables from raw sensor data and process signals for quality analysis and prediction. Techniques include statistical features (mean, variance, skewness), frequency domain features (FFT components, spectral characteristics), time-series features (trends, autocorrelations), and domain-specific transformations. Feature generation converts high-dimensional raw data into compact representations capturing relevant information. Effective features improve machine learning model performance, reduce computational requirements, and provide interpretable insights. Understanding feature engineering is essential for developing data-driven quality monitoring systems..
ME 453: Methods and philosophies of spc: Statistical Process Control (SPC) uses statistical methods to monitor and control processes by distinguishing common cause variation (inherent randomness) from special cause variation (assignable problems). SPC philosophy emphasizes prevention over detection, continuous improvement, and data-driven decision making. Methods include control charts visualizing variation over time, process capability studies comparing variation to specifications, and root cause analysis identifying improvement opportunities. SPC empowers operators to detect and correct problems quickly rather than relying solely on final inspection. Understanding SPC transforms quality from inspection-based to process-control-based..
ME 453: Bayesian decision: Bayesian decision theory provides a probabilistic framework for quality classification incorporating prior knowledge and updating beliefs based on evidence. The approach calculates posterior probabilities of different quality states given observed features, selecting actions minimizing expected cost. Bayes classifiers are optimal when probability distributions are known and particularly valuable when incorporating domain expertise as priors. In manufacturing, Bayesian methods handle uncertainty, combine multiple information sources, and support risk-based decision making. Understanding Bayesian approaches enables principled quality decisions under uncertainty..
ME 458: User centered design: A design philosophy and process that places end-users at the center of all design decisions, ensuring products meet actual user needs, abilities, and contexts rather than designer assumptions. User-centered design involves users throughout development through participatory design sessions, usability testing, iterative feedback loops, and validation studies. The approach considers diverse user populations including those with disabilities, varying skill levels, and different cultural contexts. In additive manufacturing, user-centered design can enable mass customization, personalized ergonomics, and assistive devices tailored to individual anthropometric and functional requirements..
ME 453: Classification tree: Classification trees partition feature space through recursive binary splits creating a tree structure where leaves represent quality classifications. Each internal node tests a feature threshold, routing samples left or right toward increasingly pure subsets. Tree construction uses impurity measures (Gini, entropy) to select optimal splits. Trees are interpretable, handle mixed variable types, automatically select features, and capture non-linear relationships. However, they tend to overfit without pruning or ensemble methods. Understanding decision trees provides transparent, rule-based quality classification suitable for manufacturing applications requiring interpretability..
ME 453: Feature selection: Feature selection identifies the most relevant variables from available features to improve model performance, reduce overfitting, and decrease computational cost. Methods include filter techniques (correlation, mutual information), wrapper methods (forward/backward selection using model performance), and embedded approaches (LASSO, decision trees with built-in selection). Feature selection addresses the curse of dimensionality where too many features relative to samples degrades generalization. Understanding when and how to select features is crucial for building robust, interpretable quality prediction models from high-dimensional manufacturing data..
ME 453: Neural networks: Neural networks are multi-layer computational models learning complex, non-linear mappings from input features to quality classifications through iterative weight adjustment. Architectures include input layer, hidden layers with activation functions, and output layer. Training via backpropagation and gradient descent minimizes prediction error on labeled data. Neural networks excel at capturing intricate patterns in high-dimensional manufacturing data including sensor streams, images, and time series. Deep learning with many layers handles very complex relationships. Understanding neural networks enables developing powerful quality prediction models from diverse data sources..
ME 458: Design thinking: A human-centered, iterative problem-solving methodology that emphasizes empathy with users, creative ideation, rapid prototyping, and learning through experimentation. Design thinking typically follows phases of empathizing (understanding users), defining (framing the problem), ideating (generating solutions), prototyping (building representations), and testing (gathering feedback). This approach values failing fast and cheaply, challenging assumptions, and reframing problems from multiple perspectives. In additive manufacturing contexts, design thinking leverages rapid prototyping capabilities to accelerate iteration cycles and test radical design alternatives with users early and often..
ME 431: Analysis of service failures: Service failure analysis investigates why components failed during operation, determining root causes and recommending corrective actions. Analysis involves examining failed parts (fractography), reviewing service history (loads, environment, maintenance), analyzing design calculations, performing material testing, and recreating failure mechanisms. Common findings include design deficiencies (inadequate strength, stress concentrations), material defects (inclusions, improper heat treatment), manufacturing flaws (machining damage, welding defects), or service abuse (overloading, corrosion). Understanding failure analysis methods enables improving designs, specifications, and maintenance practices preventing recurrence. Systematic investigation distinguishes symptoms from root causes..
ME 458: Brainstorming: A structured group creativity technique for generating large quantities of ideas in a judgment-free environment, emphasizing idea quantity over quality during the generative phase. Effective brainstorming follows principles including deferring criticism, encouraging wild ideas, building on others' contributions, and staying focused on the topic. Techniques include mind mapping, SCAMPER (Substitute, Combine, Adapt, Modify, Put to other uses, Eliminate, Reverse), brainwriting, and provocative prompts. In additive manufacturing contexts, brainstorming explores geometric possibilities freed from traditional manufacturing constraints, enabling radical redesigns and novel functional integrations..
MATH 221: Optimization problems: Optimization problems find maximum or minimum values of quantities in applied contexts, such as maximizing profit, minimizing cost, or optimizing dimensions. The method involves: identifying the quantity to optimize (objective function), expressing it in terms of one variable using constraints, finding critical points by differentiating and setting f'(x) = 0, and verifying the solution is a maximu
MATH 241: Optimization examples: Optimization examples apply theoretical methods to practical problems: maximizing volumes or areas given constraints, minimizing material costs in manufacturing, finding shortest distances to curves or surfaces, optimizing resource allocation, and determining best-fit parameters in models. These problems involve translating word problems into mathematical functions, finding critical points, applyi
MATH 241: Intro to optimization: Optimization in multivariable calculus finds maximum or minimum values of functions f(x,y) or f(x,y,z). Critical points occur where ∇f = 0 (all partial derivatives equal zero) or where partial derivatives don't exist. These points are candidates for local extrema or saddle points. The process involves finding critical points algebraically, then classifying them using the second derivative test. Ap
CHEM 104: Functional groups: Functional groups are specific groups of atoms within molecules that determine chemical reactivity and properties. Common functional groups include alcohols (-OH), aldehydes (-CHO), ketones (C=O), carboxylic acids (-COOH), amines (-NH₂), and others. Molecules with the same functional group undergo similar reactions regardless of the rest of their structure. Recognition of functional groups allows
ME 330: Properties of materials (mechani: Material properties characterize how materials respond to external stimuli, categorized as mechanical (strength, stiffness, toughness, hardness), thermal (conductivity, expansion, specific heat), optical (transparency, reflectivity, refractive index), and electrical (conductivity, resistivity, dielectric constant). These properties derive from atomic bonding, crystal structure, and microstructure.
ME 330: Phase diagrams: Phase diagrams are graphical representations showing which phases are stable at different compositions and temperatures, essential tools for predicting material behavior during processing and service. Binary phase diagrams plot temperature versus composition for two-component systems, indicating phase boundaries, transformation temperatures, and relative amounts of phases present (lever rule). Com
ME 431: Probabilistic design: Probabilistic design accounts for uncertainties in loads, material properties, geometry, and models using statistical methods rather than deterministic worst-case assumptions. Approaches include safety factors covering uncertainties, reliability analysis (probability of failure), and probabilistic optimization. Methods use load and strength probability distributions computing probability of failure (load distribution overlapping strength distribution). Reliability targets vary by consequence: 10^-3 for non-critical, 10^-6 for safety-critical. Benefits include optimized designs, quantified risk, and informed decision-making. Understanding probabilistic design enables balancing safety, weight, cost, and performance accounting for real-world variability..
ME 462: Genetic algorithms: Population-based optimization inspired by biological evolution, maintaining population of candidate solutions, evaluating fitness, selecting parents probabilistically, creating offspring through crossover and mutation, and iterating toward optimal solutions. Unlike gradient methods, handles discrete variables, non-differentiable objectives, and multiple objectives. Parameters include population size, selection method, crossover/mutation rates. No gradient information required. Computationally expensive but robust to local minima. Applications include controller parameter tuning with non-smooth performance metrics, multi-objective optimization balancing competing goals, and combinatorial problems like path planning or scheduling where traditional methods fail..
TAM 470: Numerical experiments: Numerical experiments use computational models to study physical phenomena, parameter effects, and design variations. Analogous to physical experiments but using simulations. Advantages: cost-effective, safe (no physical failure), parametric studies easy, access to all field variables. Design of numerical experiments follows scientific method: hypothesis → simulation plan → execution → data analys
ME 170: Parent/child relationships: Parent/child relationships in parametric CAD describe feature dependencies where child features reference parent geometry, dimensions, or parameters. For example, a hole (child) depends on the face (parent) it's placed on. Changes to parents propagate to children, enabling design exploration but also causing failures if dependencies break. Understanding these relationships is crucial for creating
ME 370: Low-fidelity prototyping: Low-fidelity prototyping creates quick, inexpensive, rough representations of ideas using simple materials (paper, cardboard, foam, LEGO) enabling rapid iteration and user feedback. Low-fidelity prototypes communicate concepts without investing in polish, allowing testing multiple ideas quickly and failing cheaply. They help stakeholders visualize concepts, identify flaws early, and refine underst
ME 458: Low resolution prototyping: The creation of quick, inexpensive, simplified representations of design concepts using readily available materials and minimal time investment to test ideas and gather feedback early in development. Low-resolution prototypes deliberately omit details and polish to focus attention on specific aspects like form, interaction flow, or concept viability. Methods include paper sketches, cardboard models, foam core assemblies, and rapid 3D prints with minimal finishing. This approach enables rapid iteration, reduces emotional attachment to ideas, encourages exploration of alternatives, and validates assumptions before committing resources to high-fidelity development..
ME 170: Defining the coordinate systems: Defining coordinate systems establishes the mathematical framework for locating points, specifying directions, and orienting parts in CAD models. The global coordinate system (World Coordinate System) provides absolute reference, while local coordinate systems (part or feature-based) simplify modeling specific geometries. Coordinate system definition includes origin location, axis orientation (fol
ME 451: Workholding principles: Workholding secures workpieces during machining to resist cutting forces while maintaining dimensional accuracy and repeatability. Principles include the 3-2-1 locating scheme (constraining six degrees of freedom), adequate clamping force without deformation, and accessibility for machining operations. Devices include vises, chucks, fixtures, and modular tooling systems. Design considerations encompass part geometry, material, required accuracy, batch size, and automation compatibility. Proper workholding is critical for quality, productivity, and safety. Poor fixturing causes part movement, distortion, or damage..
ME 451: Configurations: Robot configurations refer to mechanical architectures defining how joints are arranged to achieve workspace coverage. Common configurations include Cartesian (three linear axes), cylindrical (rotation plus linear), SCARA (selective compliance assembly), articulated (multiple revolute joints), and parallel (multiple chains). Each configuration offers different workspace shapes, reach, precision, stiffness, and cost characteristics. Selection depends on application requirements like reach envelope, payload, speed, and accuracy. Understanding configurations enables matching robot capabilities to manufacturing tasks and optimizing workcell layout..
ME 330: Wire drawing: Wire drawing produces wire, rod, and bar products by pulling material through progressively smaller conical dies, reducing cross-section while increasing length. The process is cold working (room temperature), work hardening the material while elongating grains in the drawing direction. Multiple drawing passes with intermediate annealing may be necessary for large reductions. Drawing force conside
ME 445: Workcell generation: Workcell generation involves designing and configuring the complete robotic workspace including robot placement, fixture locations, safety barriers, and peripheral equipment. This process optimizes robot reach, minimizes cycle time, ensures safety compliance, and facilitates material flow. Considerations include workspace analysis to verify all required positions are reachable, singularity avoidance, collision checking with environment obstacles, and integration with conveyors, sensors, and other automation. Effective workcell layout balances performance, safety, maintainability, and cost. Simulation tools assist in virtual prototyping and optimization before physical installation..
ME 452: General introduction to numerica: Numerically controlled machines automate manufacturing operations using programmed numerical instructions controlling tool position, feed rate, spindle speed, and auxiliary functions. NC systems include the machine tool (structure, slides, spindle), control unit (computer interpreting programs), and drive systems (motors and amplifiers). Classifications include point-to-point (drilling), straight-cut (simple milling), and contouring (complex curves) systems. NC revolutionized manufacturing by enabling complex geometry production, rapid changeover, and consistent quality. Understanding NC fundamentals is essential for modern manufacturing engineering spanning programming, operation, and process planning..
ME 270: Sand casting: Sand casting creates metal parts by pouring molten metal into sand molds formed around patterns. The process accommodates large parts, complex shapes, and various alloys at relatively low tooling cost. Design considerations include shrinkage allowance, draft angles, uniform wall thickness, generous fillets, parting line placement, coring for internal cavities, and avoiding sharp corners causing st
ME 270: Injection molding: Injection molding manufactures plastic parts by injecting molten polymer into a mold cavity under high pressure, then cooling and ejecting. The process enables complex geometries, high production rates, excellent repeatability, and minimal secondary operations. Design considerations include wall thickness uniformity (preventing sink marks), draft angles (enabling ejection), parting line location,
ME 270: Designing with plastics (snap-fi: Designing with plastics exploits unique material properties: flexibility for snap-fits, chemical bonding for welding, flow characteristics for complex shapes, and moldability for integral features. Snap-fits use material elasticity for tool-free assembly. Integral hinges use thin sections in polypropylene creating durable flexure points. Living hinges, texture, and overmolding enable designs impos
ME 270: Cnc machining: Computer Numerical Control (CNC) machining uses computer programs to control machine tool motion, enabling precise, repeatable, and complex part manufacturing. CNC systems include mills, lathes, routers, and multi-axis machines. Programming can be manual (G-code) or CAM-generated from CAD models. CNC advantages include high precision, repeatability, lights-out operation, and handling complex geome
ME 170: Adding ribs and bosses: Ribs are thin-walled stiffening features connecting surfaces, increasing strength and rigidity while minimizing weight. Bosses are raised cylindrical features providing mounting points for fasteners or other components. Both are essential for injection molded parts where thick sections cause defects. Ribs typically run perpendicular to stress directions with thickness 50-60% of adjacent walls and
ME 455: Micro-forming processes: Micro-forming plastically deforms metals to create micro-components and features through processes like micro-stamping, micro-forging, and micro-extrusion. Challenges include material grain size effects (when feature size approaches grain size), friction dominance, and handling difficulties. Process design must account for size effects on material behavior, including flow stress increase and formability decrease at micro-scales. Applications include micro-connectors, sensors, and medical devices. Tooling requires extreme precision and wear resistance. Understanding micro-forming enables mass production of metallic micro-parts economically..
ME 455: Scaling laws: Scaling laws describe how physical phenomena change with size, critical for understanding micro-manufacturing. As dimensions decrease, surface effects dominate over volume effects - surface area scales as length squared while volume scales as length cubed. This affects adhesion, surface tension, friction, heat transfer, and stress distribution. For example, surface tension and capillary forces become significant at micro-scales, while inertia diminishes. Understanding scaling laws guides process selection, parameter determination, and design decisions in micro-manufacturing. Intuitions from macro-scale manufacturing often fail at micro-scales due to different dominant physics..
ME 455: Advanced micromanufacturing proc: Advanced micromanufacturing processes encompass specialized techniques enabling fabrication at micro and nano scales beyond conventional machining capabilities. Methods include LIGA (lithography, electroplating, molding), micro-stereolithography, focused ion beam milling, laser-based processes, and hybrid approaches combining multiple techniques. Each process offers unique advantages for specific materials, features, and tolerances. Process selection depends on material, feature size, aspect ratio, surface finish, and production volume. Understanding the diverse process landscape enables selecting optimal manufacturing approaches for micro-device applications..
ME 455: Micro-machine tool (mmt) systems: Micro-machine tool systems are precision machines designed specifically for manufacturing miniature components with dimensional features in the micrometer range. MMTs feature ultra-high-speed spindles (100,000+ RPM), precision linear stages with nanometer resolution, specialized micro-tools, and vibration isolation. Design emphasizes thermal stability, geometric accuracy, and dynamic stiffness despite compact size. Applications include micro-mold fabrication, medical device manufacturing, and electronics production. Understanding MMT design principles enables specifying, operating, and optimizing equipment for micro-manufacturing applications..
ME 330: Forming processes: Forming processes shape metals through plastic deformation without material removal, including rolling (reducing thickness between rollers), forging (compressing with hammers/presses), extrusion (forcing through dies), drawing (pulling through dies), and sheet metal forming (stamping, deep drawing). These processes exploit metal ductility, offering high production rates, good mechanical properties
ME 330: Forging: Forging shapes metal through compressive forces applied by hammers, presses, or dies, creating parts with superior mechanical properties due to refined grain structure and aligned grain flow. Open-die forging shapes between flat or simple dies; closed-die forging uses shaped cavity dies creating complex geometries. Hot forging (above recrystallization temperature) enables large deformation with lo
ME 330: Annealing: Annealing is a heat treatment involving heating metal to specific temperature, holding, then slowly cooling to soften material, relieve stresses, improve ductility, refine grain structure, or remove work hardening effects. Full annealing (heating above transformation temperature) completely recrystallizes the structure. Process annealing (lower temperature) partially softens cold-worked material e
ME 330: Solidification processing (casti: Solidification processing creates metal parts by pouring molten metal into molds. Solidification time depends on volume/surface area ratio (Chvorinov's rule), affecting cooling rate and microstructure. As metal contracts during cooling, stresses develop from differential solidification, constraint from mold/cores, and thermal contraction. Hot tears occur when tensile stresses exceed low strength o
ME 330: Cold work and annealing: Cold work is plastic deformation below recrystallization temperature, increasing strength and hardness through work hardening while reducing ductility. Applications include wire drawing, rolling, and sheet forming. Excessive cold work causes brittleness, requiring intermediate annealing to restore ductility for further forming. Annealing heats to allow recovery (stress relief) or recrystallization
ME 330: Polymer processing (molding, ext: Polymer processing shapes polymers through thermomechanical operations exploiting their viscoelastic flow behavior at elevated temperatures. Injection molding forces molten polymer into closed molds for complex 3D parts. Extrusion pushes polymer through shaped dies creating continuous profiles (pipes, films, sheet). Both processes involve heating polymer above Tg (amorphous) or melting point (semi
ME 330: Solidification: cast micro- and: Solidification creates microstructure as liquid metal freezes, with cooling rate, composition, and geometry determining grain size, dendrite structure, and defect formation. Microstructure includes grain size (fine grains from rapid cooling or grain refiners), dendrite arm spacing (indicating cooling rate), and segregation (composition variations). Macrostructure shows columnar grains (directional
ME 170: Shelled/molded parts: Shelled parts are created by hollowing solid models, leaving thin walls of specified thickness - essential for injection molded plastics, castings, and lightweight structures. The shell operation removes one or more faces and offsets remaining faces inward/outward by the wall thickness. Molded parts require additional considerations: draft angles (taper for mold release), uniform wall thickness (a
ME 170: Protrusions: Protrusions are features that add material to a part, creating raised sections extending from base surfaces. Common protrusion types include extruded bosses (raised cylindrical sections for mounting), ribs (thin walls for reinforcement), and custom shaped projections. Creating protrusions involves sketching the profile on a reference plane, specifying depth or termination condition, and defining d
ME 170: Revolving: Revolving (revolution) creates 3D geometry by rotating a 2D profile about an axis, generating rotationally symmetric parts. The operation requires a sketch profile and axis of revolution, with options for full 360° rotation or partial angles. Revolution efficiently creates cylindrical parts, spheres, cones, bottles, and any axisymmetric geometry. This operation is fundamental for parts like shafts
ME 170: Complex parts and surfaces: Complex parts feature intricate geometries including curved surfaces, blended transitions, variable cross-sections, and organic shapes beyond simple prismatic forms. Creating complex surfaces requires advanced techniques: lofting between multiple profiles, sweeping along paths, boundary surfaces from edge curves, and surface editing tools. These parts challenge manufacturability, often requiring m
TAM 211: Moments of inertia: Moment of inertia (second moment of area) I measures how area is distributed relative to an axis, quantifying resistance to bending. For area A about x-axis: Iₓ = ∫y² dA; about y-axis: Iᵧ = ∫x² dA. Units: length⁴ (mm⁴, in⁴). Unlike first moment (linear), second moment gives greater weight to area farther from axis (y² term). Critical for beam bending stress (σ = My/I) and deflection calculations.
TAM 251: Yield criteria: Yield criteria predict when material begins plastic deformation under multiaxial stress. Maximum shear stress theory (Tresca): yielding occurs when τmax = (σ₁-σ₃)/2 = σy/2. Von Mises theory: yielding when √[(σ₁-σ₂)² + (σ₂-σ₃)² + (σ₃-σ₁)²]/√2 = σy, equivalent to distortion energy reaching critical value. Von Mises generally more accurate for ductile metals; Tresca is conservative. For 2D: (σx-σy)²
ME 455: Microassembly: Microassembly combines micro-components into functional devices through precise positioning, alignment, and joining at micro-scale. Challenges include part feeding and orientation, sub-micrometer positioning accuracy, force control preventing damage, adhesion effects, and real-time vision feedback. Strategies include surface treatment to manage adhesion, compliant grippers, and vision-guided robotic assembly. Applications span medical devices, electronics, MEMS, and micro-optics. Understanding microassembly principles enables creating complex micro-devices from individually manufactured components..
ME 455: Additive (3d printing): Additive manufacturing for micro-applications builds components layer-by-layer at micro-scale resolution using techniques like micro-stereolithography, two-photon polymerization, and aerosol jet printing. These processes create complex 3D micro-structures impossible with traditional manufacturing, including internal channels, overhangs, and lattices. Resolution reaches sub-micrometer levels with two-photon polymerization. Applications span micro-fluidics, micro-optics, tissue scaffolds, and micro-electro-mechanical systems (MEMS). Understanding micro-additive manufacturing enables design freedom for micro-devices while managing material limitations and post-processing requirements..
ME 455: Micro-joining processes: Micro-joining connects micro-components through welding, bonding, or mechanical fastening at microscopic scales. Techniques include laser micro-welding, ultrasonic bonding, adhesive joining, and micro-soldering. Challenges include thermal management (avoiding heat-affected zones), alignment precision, joint strength verification, and material compatibility. Process selection depends on materials, joint requirements, production volume, and accessibility. Applications include electronics assembly, medical devices, and MEMS packaging. Understanding micro-joining enables creating functional micro-assemblies from individually manufactured components..
ME 330: Tensile stress-strain relations: Tensile testing stretches specimens measuring stress-strain relationships, revealing elastic modulus (stiffness), yield strength (onset of plasticity), ultimate tensile strength (maximum stress), and ductility (elongation to fracture). The stress-strain curve shows linear elastic region (Hooke's law), yield point, work hardening region, necking, and fracture. Engineering stress (load/original area
ME 330: Polymer structure: Polymer structure encompasses molecular architecture (linear, branched, crosslinked, networked chains) and arrangement (amorphous versus semicrystalline). Polymers are long-chain molecules (macromolecules) formed by repeating monomers through polymerization. Chain length (molecular weight) affects properties: longer chains increase strength, viscosity. Linear thermoplastics soften when heated; cro
ME 330: Work hardening and plasticity: Work hardening (strain hardening) is the strengthening of metals through plastic deformation as dislocation density increases and dislocations obstruct each other's motion, requiring higher stress for continued deformation. Cold working processes (rolling, forging, drawing) at room temperature exploit work hardening to strengthen materials. The stress-strain curve's upward slope during plastic def
TAM 324: Elastic behavior: The reversible, instantaneous deformation response of materials to applied stress where materials return to their original shape upon load removal, governed by atomic bond stretching without permanent displacement. Elastic behavior is characterized by Young's modulus (stiffness), Poisson's ratio (lateral contraction), and shear modulus, following Hooke's law where stress is proportional to strain. Understanding elastic limits, elastic energy storage, and anisotropic elastic properties enables structural design ensuring components remain within the elastic regime during service. Different materials exhibit vastly different elastic moduli from soft polymers to stiff ceramics and diamonds..
ME 330: Polymer and composite materials: Polymers and composites offer unique property combinations unattainable in traditional materials. Polymers provide low density, corrosion resistance, easy processing, but limited temperature range and lower stiffness. Composites combine reinforcement (fibers, particles) and matrix (polymer, metal, ceramic) tailoring properties: high strength-to-weight, directional properties, design flexibility. A
ME 330: Tg, crystallization, morphology: Glass transition temperature (Tg) is where amorphous polymers transition from rigid, glassy to soft, rubbery as temperature increases, marking onset of chain segment mobility. Below Tg, polymers are brittle; above, they're flexible. Crystallization in polymers is partial, never 100%, creating semicrystalline morphology with crystalline lamellae separated by amorphous regions. Spherulites are commo
TAM 324: Creep of metals and polymers: Time-dependent deformation under sustained load at elevated temperatures where metals exhibit diffusion-controlled mechanisms (vacancy flow, dislocation climb, grain boundary sliding) while polymers show viscous flow as molecular chains slide past each other. Metal creep follows power-law relationships with stress and temperature, with activation energies indicating dominant mechanisms. Polymer creep occurs at much lower temperatures due to weaker intermolecular forces, with viscoelastic behavior exhibiting both instantaneous elastic response and time-dependent viscous flow. Applications include turbine blades operating at high temperatures, polymer structural components under sustained loads, and prediction of dimensional stability over service life..
ME 371: Bolts and bolted joints: Bolts create demountable joints through threaded fasteners loaded in tension or shear. Design considerations include bolt strength (grades SAE or ISO), thread engagement, preload (controlled via torque or turn-of-nut), joint stiffness ratio, and factors of safety against yielding or fatigue. Joint analysis considers bolt tension, clamping force maintaining joint integrity, external loads (tension,
TAM 324: Microstructure of wood, bitumen: The hierarchical cellular structure of wood comprising cellulose, hemicellulose, and lignin forming oriented tubular cells that determine anisotropic mechanical properties, moisture sensitivity, and dimensional stability. Bitumens are complex mixtures of hydrocarbons forming viscoelastic binders in asphalt, with temperature-dependent behavior transitioning from brittle to fluid states. Sealants are polymer-based materials providing elastic joints accommodating differential movement, with microstructure affecting modulus, adhesion, elongation, and environmental durability. Understanding these natural and engineered materials' microstructures explains their distinct behaviors including wood's orthotropic properties, bitumen's rheological response, and sealant relaxation..
TAM 324: Notch sensitivity: The degree to which material strength and ductility are reduced by stress concentrations created by geometric discontinuities like holes, fillets, keyways, and surface scratches. Notch-sensitive materials (high-strength steels, brittle materials) show significant strength reduction due to notches, while notch-insensitive materials (low-strength ductile metals, polymers) can redistribute stress through local plastic deformation. Sensitivity depends on notch acuity, material toughness, and loading conditions. Impact tests with notched specimens (Charpy, Izod) quantify notch sensitivity. Design strategies include avoiding sharp corners, using generous fillets, surface finishing to remove stress raisers, and selecting materials with low notch sensitivity for components with unavoidable stress concentrations..
TAM 324: Introduction to materials behav: The foundational study of how materials respond to applied forces, temperature changes, environmental conditions, and time under load, establishing the scientific basis for material selection and structural design. Materials behavior encompasses elastic and plastic deformation, fracture, fatigue, creep, and environmental degradation. The field connects atomic-scale bonding and microstructure to macroscopic mechanical properties including strength, stiffness, toughness, hardness, and ductility. Understanding materials behavior enables prediction of performance, prevention of failures, and optimization of material usage across civil infrastructure, aerospace, automotive, and manufacturing applications..
ME 371: Design against yielding: Design against yielding prevents permanent deformation by ensuring maximum stress remains below yield strength with appropriate safety factor. Analysis uses yield criteria for uniaxial loading (σ
ME 447: Soft robotic modeling with cosse: Cosserat rod theory provides a mathematical framework for modeling slender, flexible structures by describing centerline deformation and cross-sectional rotation. In soft robotics, this approach captures bending, twisting, stretching, and shearing of continuum structures like tentacles, flexible manipulators, and snake-like robots. The theory reduces 3D continuum mechanics to 1D curves with attached material frames, significantly decreasing computational complexity while maintaining physical accuracy. Cosserat rods are particularly suited for modeling highly deformable soft systems undergoing large displacements and rotations..
ME 447: Example of potential experimenta: Potential experimental applications demonstrate how computational soft robotics models translate to physical prototypes and real-world implementations. Examples include soft grippers adapting to object geometry, continuum manipulators for minimally invasive surgery, soft wearable exosuits for rehabilitation, and bio-inspired locomoting robots. Validation involves comparing simulation predictions with experimental measurements of deformation, force, and motion. Successful applications require addressing fabrication techniques, material characterization, sensing integration, and control implementation. This topic bridges computational design with physical realization, highlighting both opportunities and challenges..
ME 447: Complex creatures modeling: Complex creatures modeling extends soft system simulation to multi-component organisms with diverse material properties, joints, and actuation mechanisms. This involves coupling multiple Cosserat rods or soft bodies, implementing contact between components, and coordinating distributed actuation. Examples include modeling octopus arms with suction cups, elephant trunks with muscles, or soft robotic grippers with multiple fingers. The challenge lies in efficiently simulating numerous interacting soft elements while maintaining numerical stability and physical realism.
