Applications span biomechanics research, animation, and advanced soft robot design..
TAM 445: Elastic solids: Elastic solids store deformation energy and return to original configuration when loads removed. Constitutive relation: stress depends on strain (not strain rate). Linear elasticity: σ = C:ε (Hooke's law), where C is fourth-order elasticity tensor. Isotropic linear elasticity: σᵢⱼ = λδᵢⱼεₖₖ + 2μεᵢⱼ using Lamé parameters λ, μ. Equivalently, use E (Young's modulus), ν (Poisson's ratio), G (shear mod
ME 447: Soft robotic modeling: implement: Implementing soft robotic models translates mathematical formulations into working simulation code. This involves discretizing Cosserat rod equations, implementing time integration schemes (explicit or implicit methods), handling boundary conditions and external forces, and incorporating contact mechanics. Students build simulations that predict soft robot deformation, dynamics, and locomotion. Implementation requires balancing numerical accuracy with computational efficiency, ensuring stability, and validating against analytical solutions or experimental data. Practical coding experience reveals numerical challenges and solution strategies specific to soft systems..
ME 440: Cables: Cables are flexible elements that can support tension but not compression or bending, used for force transmission and motion guidance. Cable dynamics involves catenary equations for hanging cables under self-weight, pulley systems for mechanical advantage, and cable-driven mechanisms in robotics and cranes. Cables introduce unique constraints: they only pull, change direction at pulleys creating geometric constraints, and may have significant mass affecting dynamics. Analysis considers static equilibrium, tension distribution, and how cable stretching affects precision in cable-driven systems..
ME 431: Plastic collapse: Plastic collapse occurs when structure or component develops sufficient plasticity (yielding) that it can no longer support applied loads, forming collapse mechanism with unbounded deformation. Unlike elastic buckling (reversible instability), plastic collapse involves permanent deformation and occurs in ductile materials. Analysis uses limit analysis determining collapse load from yield conditions and equilibrium, independent of stress-strain details. Plastic collapse load exceeds elastic stress-based predictions, justifying plastic design methods using load factors. Understanding plastic collapse enables more accurate failure predictions for ductile structures, appropriate safety factors, and designing for ductile rather than brittle failure..
ME 431: Elastic stiffness and stability: Elastic stiffness characterizes structural resistance to deformation under load (force/deflection ratio), while stability addresses whether equilibrium is maintained under load or if structures buckle/snap-through. Stiffness analysis ensures deflections remain acceptable for function and appearance. Stability analysis prevents buckling (columns, thin shells) or snap-through (shallow arches, panels) where structures suddenly collapse at critical loads below material strength limits. Design ensures adequate stiffness through proper cross-sections and configurations, and adequate stability margins through critical load calculations. Understanding stiffness and stability prevents failures from excessive deformation or instability rather than material strength..
ME 451: Cmm and metrology: Coordinate Measuring Machines (CMMs) are programmable measurement systems using touch probes or optical sensors to determine 3D coordinates of part features. CMMs provide high accuracy (micron-level), measure complex geometries, and evaluate GD&T characteristics through software analysis. Applications include first article inspection, in-process verification, and reverse engineering. Programming involves defining measurement strategies, probe paths, and tolerance evaluation routines. Modern CMMs integrate with CAD for model-based inspection and production systems for closed-loop quality control. Understanding CMM capabilities and limitations enables effective quality planning..
ME 451: Robotics: Robotics in manufacturing applies programmable manipulators to automate tasks including assembly, welding, painting, material handling, and inspection. Industrial robots offer repeatability, speed, precision, and operation in hazardous environments. Selection criteria include payload capacity, reach, accuracy, speed, and environmental rating. Integration involves end-effector design, programming, safety systems, and coordination with other automation. While initial investment is significant, robots enable lights-out manufacturing, consistent quality, and flexibility through reprogramming. Understanding robotics capabilities and economics is essential for modern manufacturing engineering..
ME 451: Kinematics and applications: Robot kinematics in manufacturing context focuses on relating joint motions to end-effector positions for specific applications. Forward kinematics determines tool location from joint angles, while inverse kinematics solves for joint angles achieving desired positions. Applications include welding (maintaining torch angle), assembly (precise insertion), painting (uniform coverage), and palletizing (efficient stacking). Understanding kinematics enables programming complex paths, avoiding singularities, optimizing cycle times, and selecting appropriate robots. Application-specific considerations include accuracy requirements, speed, payload, and environmental constraints..
ME 451: Numerical control (nc) systems: Numerical control (NC) systems operate machine tools through programmed numerical commands specifying tool positions, feed rates, and auxiliary functions. NC enables automated, repeatable production of complex parts from digital designs. Systems read programs in G-code or proprietary languages, interpreting commands to control motors, spindles, and tool changers. Modern CNC (Computer Numerical Control) integrates microprocessors for enhanced capabilities like interpolation, compensation, and adaptive control. NC systems revolutionized manufacturing by enabling lights-out operation, consistent quality, and rapid changeover between different parts..
ME 452: Trends and new developments in n: Current NC trends include intelligent machining with adaptive control responding to force and vibration, advanced CAM integration with digital twins and simulation, IoT connectivity enabling remote monitoring and predictive maintenance, AI-based process optimization, and multi-tasking machines combining milling, turning, and additive capabilities. Developments in high-speed machining, precision control algorithms, and open-architecture controllers enable customization and integration. Cloud-based manufacturing connects machines globally for distributed production. Understanding emerging technologies prepares engineers for future manufacturing systems emphasizing flexibility, intelligence, and connectivity..
TAM 211: Statics and structural design: Statics principles form the foundation for structural design, ensuring buildings, bridges, and machines safely support applied loads. Design process integrates load determination (dead, live, environmental), structural analysis (forces, moments, reactions), stress analysis, and member sizing based on strength requirements with safety factors. Considerations include material properties, failure mod
PHYS 213: Heat capacity: Heat capacity (C) is the amount of heat required to raise a system's temperature by one degree: C = Q/ΔT. Specific heat capacity (c) is per unit mass: c = C/m. The heat capacity depends on the process: Cₚ (constant pressure) differs from Cᵥ (constant volume) because expansion work is involved. For ideal gases, Cₚ - Cᵥ = nR. Heat capacity reflects how much energy a substance can store and varies wi
ME 200: Availability: Availability (also called exergy or available energy) represents the maximum useful work obtainable from a system as it comes to equilibrium with a reference environment through reversible processes. Unlike energy, which is conserved, availability can be destroyed through irreversibilities. Availability combines first and second law considerations, accounting for both energy quantity and quality.
ME 200: Component efficiency: Component efficiency measures how well individual devices (turbines, compressors, pumps, nozzles) perform compared to ideal, reversible operation. Isentropic efficiency compares actual work or enthalpy change to the isentropic (constant entropy) ideal case. For turbines and nozzles, efficiency is actual output divided by ideal output; for compressors and pumps, it's ideal input divided by actual i
ME 200: Cycle efficiency: Cycle efficiency quantifies the performance of thermodynamic cycles (power or refrigeration) by comparing desired output to required input. For power cycles, thermal efficiency is net work output divided by heat input. For refrigeration and heat pump cycles, the coefficient of performance (COP) is cooling or heating effect divided by work input. Carnot efficiency represents the theoretical maximum
ME 200: Irreversibility: Irreversibility quantifies the destruction of available work potential (exergy) due to real process imperfections such as friction, unrestrained expansion, mixing, heat transfer across finite temperature differences, and electrical resistance. Mathematically, irreversibility equals the difference between reversible and actual work, or equivalently, the ambient temperature multiplied by total entro
ME 330: Effect of processing on structur: Processing history profoundly affects material structure and resulting properties. In metals: cooling rate determines grain size and phase distribution; deformation creates texture and residual stresses; heat treatment modifies phases and microstructure. In polymers: processing affects molecular orientation, crystallinity, and morphology. In composites: processing determines fiber distribution and
TAM 324: Durability: The ability of materials to resist degradation and maintain performance over time when exposed to environmental conditions including moisture, temperature cycles, chemical attack, UV radiation, freeze-thaw, and biological agents. Durability involves corrosion of metals through electrochemical reactions, degradation of polymers through oxidation and chain scission, deterioration of concrete through sulfate attack and alkali-silica reaction, and decay of wood through fungal and insect attack. Understanding degradation mechanisms, protective strategies (coatings, alloying, sealers), and accelerated testing methods enables service life prediction and design for longevity in infrastructure, vehicles, and consumer products..
ME 330: Aging: Aging (precipitation hardening) is heat treatment following solution treatment and quenching, where supersaturated solid solution decomposes forming fine precipitate particles that strengthen material by obstructing dislocation motion. Natural aging occurs at room temperature; artificial aging uses elevated temperature accelerating precipitation. Optimal aging produces maximum strength when precip
ME 402: Component modeling and selection: Component modeling develops mathematical representations of individual devices (heat exchangers, pumps, turbines, compressors, etc.) relating performance to operating conditions and design parameters. Models range from simple (constant efficiency) to detailed (performance maps, off-design behavior). Selection involves matching component characteristics to system requirements considering capacity, efficiency, cost, reliability, and availability. Manufacturers' catalogs provide performance data. Understanding component operating principles, performance curves, scaling laws, and integration with adjacent components enables appropriate modeling fidelity and optimal selection. Component models form building blocks for complete system simulation, and selection decisions critically affect overall system performance and economics..
ME 402: Thermal system modeling: System modeling assembles component models into complete system simulations solving coupled energy, mass, and momentum balances. Models predict system performance for given operating conditions and design parameters. Approaches include equation-based (solving simultaneous equations), modular (sequential calculation), and specialized software (EES, Aspen, TRNSYS). Challenges include handling property variations, iteration convergence, and computational efficiency. Model validation against experimental data ensures accuracy. Sensitivity analysis identifies critical parameters. System models enable performance prediction, parametric studies, control strategy evaluation, and optimization serving as virtual prototypes before physical construction. Effective modeling balances complexity (accuracy) against simplicity (understanding, computation time)..
ME 371: Material failure modes: Material failure modes describe how components fail under service conditions, including yielding (permanent deformation), fracture (separation), fatigue (cyclic loading damage), creep (time-dependent deformation at temperature), wear (surface material loss), corrosion (chemical/electrochemical degradation), and buckling (instability). Each mode has distinct mechanisms, predictive theories, and pre
ME 400: Power cycles: Power cycles convert thermal energy from fuel combustion or heat sources into mechanical work through thermodynamic cycles. Major cycles include Rankine (steam power plants using vapor phase change), Brayton (gas turbines using gas compression/expansion), Otto and Diesel (internal combustion engines), and combined cycles (cascading Brayton and Rankine). Analysis involves applying first and second laws to each component (turbines, compressors, pumps, heat exchangers) and the overall cycle. Performance metrics include thermal efficiency, specific work, back work ratio, and exergy efficiency. Modern power generation emphasizes efficiency improvement, waste heat recovery, emissions reduction, and integration of renewable energy sources..
ME 432: Materials availability: Materials availability assesses whether sufficient raw materials exist for large-scale PV deployment meeting future energy needs. Silicon is Earth's second-most abundant element, ensuring long-term availability. However, thin-film technologies using scarcer elements face constraints: Tellurium (CdTe), Indium (CIGS), and Silver (contacts) have limited reserves relative to multi-TW deployment scenarios. Analysis considers crustal abundance, current production, extraction economics, recycling potential, and alternative materials development. Thin-films offer lower material usage but some use scarce elements, while silicon uses abundant materials but requires more material. Understanding availability constraints guides sustainable technology development and circular economy strategies..
ME 170: 2d cad: 2D CAD creates flat, two-dimensional drawings using lines, circles, arcs, and other geometric primitives with precise dimensions. This approach mimics traditional drafting but with digital precision, layer management, easy modification, and automatic dimensioning. 2D CAD is used for floor plans, circuit diagrams, schematic layouts, and creating manufacturing drawings from 3D models. Commands inclu
ME 455: Machine tool calibration: Machine tool calibration determines and compensates for geometric errors ensuring accurate micro-manufacturing. At micro-scale, errors imperceptible in conventional machining become significant relative to feature sizes. Calibration involves measuring straightness, squareness, positioning accuracy, and repeatability using laser interferometry, ball plates, and precision artifacts. Error compensation through software correction improves accuracy without mechanical adjustment. Regular calibration maintains performance as components wear and thermal conditions change. Understanding calibration principles and procedures is essential for achieving required micro-scale tolerances..
ME 451: Kinematics of nc machines: Kinematics of NC machines analyzes the geometric relationships between machine axis motions and resulting tool-workpiece relative motion. Topics include coordinate systems, axis configurations (Cartesian, cylindrical, SCARA), multi-axis interpolation, and transformation matrices relating tool coordinates to workpiece coordinates. Understanding kinematics enables programming complex paths, avoiding singularities, and optimizing machine utilization. For multi-axis machines (4-axis, 5-axis), kinematics become more complex, requiring sophisticated transformations to achieve desired tool orientations while avoiding collisions and reaching limits..
ME 451: Nc path programming: NC path programming creates the numerical commands guiding machine tool motion to manufacture parts. Programming approaches include manual G-code writing, conversational programming at the machine, and CAM software generating programs from CAD models. Key elements include coordinate specification, feed rates, spindle speeds, tool selection, and auxiliary commands. Programmers must consider tool geometry, cutting forces, material properties, and machine capabilities. Efficient programs balance cycle time, tool life, surface finish, and dimensional accuracy. Modern CAM systems automate much programming but understanding fundamentals remains essential..
ME 455: Micro-hard turning: Micro-hard turning machines hardened materials (>45 HRC) using single-crystal diamond or CBN tools at micro-scale, producing precision surfaces and features. The process combines advantages of turning (continuous cutting, good surface finish) with hard material capability, eliminating grinding in some applications. Challenges include tool wear, chip formation at small scales, and achieving nano-scale surface finishes. Applications include micro-shafts, optical components, and precision bearing surfaces. Understanding micro-hard turning enables efficient production of hard, precise micro-components with excellent surface quality..
ME 452: Errors and error sources in nc m: NC machine errors arise from geometric errors (straightness, squareness, positioning), thermal effects (expansion causing dimensional changes), dynamic errors (servo lag, vibration), and kinematic errors (interpolation approximation). Error sources include mechanical imperfections, thermal gradients, cutting forces, wear, and control system limitations. Characterization involves measuring positioning accuracy, repeatability, and contouring performance using laser interferometry, ball bars, and precision artifacts. Understanding error sources enables compensation strategies, preventive maintenance, and realistic tolerance planning. Total machine accuracy results from the combination of multiple error sources..
ME 270: Water jet cutting: Water jet cutting uses high-pressure water (often with abrasive) to cut materials through erosion rather than melting or burning. The process cuts virtually any material (metals, composites, glass, stone, plastics) without heat-affected zones, mechanical stresses, or hazardous gases. Advantages include no thermal distortion, cutting thick sections, tight nesting minimizing scrap, and safe operatio
ME 170: Extruding: Extruding creates 3D geometry by projecting a 2D sketch profile perpendicular to the sketch plane for a specified distance or to a termination surface. The operation can add material (boss), remove material (cut), or create thin-walled features. Extrusion options include blind (fixed depth), through all, up to surface, and symmetric. This is the most common CAD operation, creating prismatic featur
ME 455: Electro-chemical machining: Electro-chemical machining (ECM) dissolves conductive material through controlled electrochemical reactions, enabling micro-feature fabrication without tool wear or mechanical stress. Current passing through electrolyte between tool (cathode) and workpiece (anode) removes material via anodic dissolution. Micro-ECM achieves precise feature control through masked electrodes, pulsed current, and confined electrolyte gaps. The process handles difficult-to-machine materials, produces burr-free surfaces, and creates complex geometries. Applications include micro-holes, fine slots, and surface texturing. Understanding electrochemical principles enables exploiting this non-traditional micro-manufacturing process..
ME 370: Cam design: Cam design creates mechanical devices converting rotary motion to oscillating or reciprocating motion through shaped profiles. Design involves selecting cam type (plate, cylindrical), follower type (flat, roller, knife-edge), and creating cam profile producing desired follower motion (displacement, velocity, acceleration). Common motion programs include constant velocity, parabolic, cycloidal, and
ME 451: Computer control of manufacturin: Computer control of manufacturing systems uses digital computers to direct machine operations, coordinate production sequences, and monitor processes. This encompasses numerical control (NC) machines, programmable logic controllers (PLCs), industrial robots, and distributed control systems. Computer control enables precision, repeatability, flexibility, and integration with CAD/CAM systems. Control architectures range from standalone CNC machines to hierarchical systems managing entire factories. Real-time requirements, reliability, and safety are critical considerations. Understanding computer control is fundamental to modern automated manufacturing..
ME 370: Machine dynamics: Machine dynamics analyzes forces and torques in moving machinery, relating motion (kinematics) to causing forces using Newton's laws and Euler equations. This includes determining input torques/forces required for desired motion, computing bearing reactions, identifying maximum forces for component sizing, and analyzing vibrations. Machine dynamics considers both rigid body effects (mass, inertia)
ME 452: Hardware in the nc loop: Hardware in the NC loop encompasses all physical components enabling automated machining: motors (servo/stepper) driving axes, encoders providing position feedback, amplifiers controlling motor current, spindle drive systems, tool changers, coolant systems, and safety interlocks. The control loop reads position, compares with commanded position, and adjusts motor drive to minimize error. Understanding hardware components, their functions, capabilities, and failure modes enables troubleshooting, maintenance, specification of machine upgrades, and optimization of performance. Hardware capabilities ultimately limit achievable accuracy, speed, and reliability..
ME 445: Off-line programming: Off-line programming creates robot programs using simulation software without requiring access to the physical robot. Engineers design and test tasks in a virtual environment with CAD models of the robot, workcell, and parts. This approach enables program development while the robot continues production, reduces downtime, allows complex optimization, and provides collision checking before deployment. Off-line programming supports sophisticated path planning, cycle time analysis, and reach studies. Challenges include maintaining accurate calibration between the virtual and real environments, and handling uncertainties that simulation may not capture completely..
ME 445: Robots in automated manufacturin: Industrial robots perform repetitive, precise, dangerous, or ergonomically challenging manufacturing tasks including welding, painting, assembly, material handling, machining, and inspection. Benefits include consistency, 24/7 operation, high speed, precision, and flexibility through reprogramming. Implementation challenges include high initial cost, programming complexity, inflexibility to product variations, and safety requirements. Modern trends include collaborative robots (cobots) working alongside humans, vision-guided manipulation, force-controlled assembly, and integration with IoT/Industry 4.0. Understanding manufacturing applications reveals robot value proposition: precision and repeatability for quality, speed for productivity, and danger elimination for safety..
ME 452: Tuning and configuration of nc s: NC system tuning adjusts controller parameters (gains, filters, feedforward) to optimize performance for specific machine characteristics and applications. Configuration involves setting axis parameters, backlash compensation, coordinate systems, tool offsets, and operational limits. Tuning procedures include manual adjustment observing step responses, automatic tuning algorithms, and optimization for specific performance metrics. Poor tuning causes overshoot, oscillation, or sluggish response affecting part quality and cycle time. Proper tuning balances positioning accuracy, speed, stability, and robustness. Understanding tuning principles enables extracting maximum performance from NC machines..
ME 445: Matlab and mathematica code for: MATLAB and Mathematica enable computing robot kinematics through symbolic manipulation (deriving equations) and numerical evaluation (calculating specific values). Symbolic computation derives kinematic equations, Jacobians, and dynamics equations from robot parameters. Numerical computation evaluates positions, velocities, forces for specific configurations. Implementation includes defining transformation matrices, composing them for forward kinematics, computing Jacobians through differentiation, and visualizing robot configurations. Software skills enable rapid prototyping, design iteration, simulation, and verification before hardware implementation. Understanding computational tools is essential for modern robotics engineering workflow..
ME 445: The jacobian: The Jacobian J is a matrix relating joint velocities to end-effector velocity: v = J·q̇. Each Jacobian column corresponds to one joint, describing end-effector velocity contribution from that joint moving at unit velocity. Computation methods include geometric (velocity propagation) and analytical (differentiating forward kinematics). The Jacobian enables velocity control, maps joint torques to end-effector forces (τ = J^T·F), and reveals singularities (rank deficiency). Jacobian dependence on configuration q requires recomputation as robot moves. Understanding the Jacobian is fundamental to robot control, force analysis, and motion planning..
ME 452: Programming of nc machines: NC programming creates instruction sequences directing machine operations to produce parts. Programming methods include manual G-code writing (specifying coordinates, feeds, speeds), conversational programming (interactive prompts at machine), and CAM-generated programs (automatic from CAD models). Key concepts include work coordinate systems, tool offsets, canned cycles for common operations, and program structure. Lab-based learning provides hands-on experience writing programs, setting up machines, and verifying operations. Practical programming develops understanding of machining fundamentals, geometric thinking, and troubleshooting skills essential for manufacturing engineering..
ME 452: Interpolators (linear and circul: Interpolators generate intermediate position commands between programmed waypoints to create smooth tool paths. Linear interpolation creates straight-line motion by computing equally-spaced points along the line connecting start and end coordinates. Circular interpolation generates arc motion by calculating points satisfying the circle equation with specified center, radius, and endpoints. Interpolators operate at high frequency (typically 1-10 kHz) within the NC controller, outputting position commands to axis drives. Proper interpolation ensures accurate contour following, smooth motion, and consistent feedrate along programmed paths..
ME 455: Ultrasonic and laser machining: Ultrasonic machining uses high-frequency tool vibration combined with abrasive slurry to remove material from hard, brittle workpieces, while laser machining employs focused light energy for material removal. Ultrasonic machining excels at ceramics, glass, and composites, creating micro-features without thermal damage. Laser micromachining variants include ablation, drilling, and cutting with precision control and no tool contact. Each process offers distinct advantages: ultrasonic for brittle materials and laser for speed and flexibility. Understanding both processes expands micro-manufacturing capabilities for diverse material-feature combinations..
ME 330: Rolling: Rolling passes metal between rotating cylindrical rolls, reducing thickness and elongating material. Hot rolling (above recrystallization temperature) shapes ingots into slabs, plates, and structural shapes with concurrent grain refinement. Cold rolling (below recrystallization) produces precise dimensions, smooth surfaces, and work-hardened products like sheet, foil, and strip. Rolling variables
ME 330: Ceramics processing (particulate: Ceramic processing starts with powders, forming green bodies, then densifying through sintering. Particulate processing includes mixing, milling, and granulation. Slip casting pours ceramic slurry into porous molds, water absorption forms shape. Dry pressing compacts powder in dies. Sintering heats to below melting point, diffusion bonds particles reducing porosity. Glass processing melts raw mate
ME 330: Phase diagrams and casting: Phase diagrams guide casting by predicting solidification behavior: liquidus/solidus temperatures, freezing range, phases present during cooling, and segregation tendencies. Narrow freezing range (eutectic or near-pure metals) enables easy casting with less shrinkage porosity. Wide freezing range causes mushy zone, complicating feeding and increasing segregation. Eutectic compositions have lowest
ME 330: Recrystallization: Recrystallization is the formation of new strain-free grains in cold-worked material during heating, replacing deformed structure with new equiaxed grains. The process begins at the recrystallization temperature (typically 0.3-0.5 times melting point in Kelvin), where new grains nucleate at high-energy sites (grain boundaries, deformation bands) and grow consuming the deformed matrix. Recrystalliz
ME 330: Hardenability of steels: Hardenability indicates a steel's ability to form martensite in the interior when quenched, differing from hardness (resistance to indentation). High hardenability steels form martensite deeply with slower cooling (oil/air quench), while low hardenability requires rapid cooling (water quench) and only surface hardens. Hardenability depends on alloy content and grain size, measured by Jominy end-qu
ME 330: Heat treatment processes: Heat treatment alters material properties through controlled heating and cooling cycles without changing composition. Common processes include annealing (softening), normalizing (refining structure), hardening (quenching for strength), tempering (reducing brittleness), and aging (precipitation hardening). Each process has specific temperature, hold time, and cooling rate requirements. Heat treatme
ME 330: Heat treat solution: Solution treatment (solutionizing) is the first step in precipitation hardening, involving heating alloy above solvus temperature to dissolve alloying elements into a single-phase solid solution, followed by rapid quenching to retain supersaturated solid solution at room temperature. This metastable state contains excess solute that will precipitate during subsequent aging. Solution treatment temp
ME 330: Heat treatment of steel: Steel heat treatment exploits iron-carbon phase diagram creating diverse microstructures with different properties. Processes include annealing (soft, ductile ferrite-pearlite), normalizing (refined grains), hardening (quenching to martensite - very hard/brittle), and tempering (reheating martensite for toughness). Carburizing and nitriding add carbon/nitrogen to surfaces for hard cases with tough
ME 330: Grain growth: Grain growth is the increase in average grain size through boundary migration after recrystallization, driven by reduction in total grain boundary energy. Larger grains grow at the expense of smaller ones as boundaries migrate toward their centers of curvature. Grain growth occurs during prolonged annealing at elevated temperatures where atomic mobility is sufficient. Controlling grain size is cru
ME 371: Use beams, solid and shell eleme: Different finite element types suit different geometries: beam elements for slender structures (shafts, frames), solid elements for 3D bulk components, and shell elements for thin-walled structures (pressure vessels, sheet metal). Element selection balances accuracy with computational cost. Beams are most efficient but require simplified geometry; solids handle complex geometry but are computation
ME 170: Setting up datum planes: Datum planes are reference planes establishing coordinate systems for part modeling, serving as construction surfaces for sketches and features. Setting up datums involves defining three mutually perpendicular planes (typically Front, Top, Right) analogous to XY, YZ, and XZ planes. Additional datum planes can be created offset from, angled to, or through specific geometry. Proper datum selection i
ME 451: Geometric modeling: Geometric modeling creates mathematical representations of physical objects for computer processing, forming the foundation of CAD/CAM systems. Representations include wireframe models (vertices and edges), surface models (boundary surfaces), and solid models (volumetric representations). Techniques employ parametric curves (Bezier, B-splines, NURBS), surfaces, and Boolean operations. Geometric models enable visualization, analysis, manufacturing planning, and numerical control programming. Accuracy, completeness, and computational efficiency are key considerations. Understanding geometric modeling is essential for digital product development and automated manufacturing..
ME 487: Additive processes: Fabrication techniques that deposit or grow material layers onto substrates to build up device structures, including physical vapor deposition (PVD), chemical vapor deposition (CVD), sputtering, evaporation, electroplating, and atomic layer deposition (ALD). These processes enable creation of thin films with controlled composition, thickness (nanometers to microns), conformality, stress, and microstructure. Process parameters including temperature, pressure, deposition rate, and precursor chemistry determine film properties such as adhesion, step coverage, grain structure, and impurity levels. Additive processes are fundamental for creating conductive, insulating, and structural layers in MEMS/NEMS devices..
CHEM 102: Heat: Heat (q) is energy transferred between objects or systems due to temperature difference, always flowing spontaneously from higher to lower temperature. Heat capacity (C) is the energy required to raise temperature by 1°C, while specific heat (c) is heat capacity per gram. The relationship is q = mcΔT, where m is mass, c is specific heat, and ΔT is temperature change. Different substances have diff
CHEM 104: Electroplating: Electroplating is an electrolytic process that deposits a thin layer of metal onto a conducting surface using electrical current. The object to be plated serves as the cathode where metal ions in solution are reduced and deposited. A metal anode (often the plating metal) undergoes oxidation to replenish metal ions in solution. Electroplating improves corrosion resistance, appearance, and wear resi
TAM 251: Transverse shear: Transverse shear stress in beams results from shear force, varying across section depth (unlike bending stress). For rectangular beam: τ = VQ/Ib, where V is shear force, Q = ∫y dA is first moment of area above point, I is moment of inertia, b is width. Maximum at neutral axis: τmax = 3V/2A for rectangle. Zero at extreme fibers. Shear flow q = VQ/I represents shear force per unit length along beam.
ME 310: Linear momentum: The linear momentum equation for a control volume applies Newton's second law to fluid flow: the sum of external forces on the control volume equals the rate of momentum change within the volume plus the net momentum flux leaving. Expressed as ΣF = ∂/∂t(∫ρVdV) + ∫ρV(V⋅n)dA, this vector equation is applied component by component. For steady flow, it simplifies to force equals net momentum outflow r
ME 310: Orifice meters: An orifice meter measures flow rate using a thin plate with a circular hole inserted in a pipe. The abrupt area contraction creates a high-velocity jet at the vena contracta (minimum flow area downstream of the orifice) with corresponding pressure drop. Flow rate is calculated from the pressure difference across the orifice using Q = C_d A₂√(2ΔP/ρ), where C_d is the discharge coefficient accountin
ME 455: Micro-electro-discharge machinin: Micro-electro-discharge machining (micro-EDM) removes material through electrical sparks eroding conductive workpieces with precise control at micro-scale. The process machines hard materials regardless of hardness, creates complex shapes with high aspect ratios, and avoids mechanical cutting forces. Variants include micro-wire EDM for 2D profiles and micro-die sinking EDM for 3D cavities. Challenges include electrode wear, surface integrity, and debris removal. Applications include micro-holes, fine slots, and micro-mold cavities. Understanding micro-EDM enables manufacturing features difficult or impossible with mechanical micro-machining..
TAM 335: Turbines (impulse, reaction): Turbines extract energy from flowing fluids, converting fluid kinetic or pressure energy to shaft work driving generators or machinery. Impulse turbines (Pelton wheel) use high-velocity jets striking buckets at atmospheric pressure; energy conversion via momentum change. Reaction turbines (Francis, Kaplan) operate submerged with pressure drop across rotor; energy conversion via combined momentum and pressure changes. Performance depends on head, flow rate, speed, and efficiency. Specific speed N_s characterizes turbine type. Hydraulic turbines in dams generate hydroelectric power. Gas and steam turbines power aircraft and power plants. Understanding turbine types enables selection for specific applications matching head and flow conditions..
TAM 335: Cavitation: Cavitation occurs when local pressure in a liquid drops below vapor pressure, causing vapor bubbles to form, grow, and subsequently collapse violently when entering higher-pressure regions. Collapse produces shock waves, noise, vibration, and can erode nearby surfaces (cavitation damage). It commonly occurs in pumps, propellers, valves, and hydraulic turbines at high speeds or low inlet pressures. The cavitation number σ = (P - P_v)/(½ρV²) characterizes susceptibility; low σ indicates cavitation risk. Prevention involves increasing inlet pressure, reducing velocity, or changing geometry. Understanding cavitation is essential for pump design (NPSH requirements) and avoiding equipment damage..
TAM 335: Air flow through a nozzle: Nozzle experiments demonstrate converging nozzle accelerating subsonic flow (pressure decreasing, velocity increasing per Bernoulli), possible choking at throat (M=1 limits mass flow), and shock formation if back pressure creates supersonic conditions. Measurements include pressure distribution along nozzle, mass flow rate, and velocity profiles at exit. Isentropic relations predict ideal performance; comparison with measurements reveals losses. Converging-diverging nozzles for supersonic flow show complex behavior with shocks depending on back pressure. Understanding nozzle flows is fundamental for propulsion, steam turbines, and flow measurement. Experiments illustrate compressibility effects and pressure-velocity relationships..
ME 330: Welding and joining processes: Welding joins materials through coalescence using heat, pressure, or both, creating metallurgical bonds. Fusion welding (arc, gas, laser, electron beam) melts base metals with/without filler. Solid-state welding (friction, ultrasonic, diffusion) joins without melting. Brazing/soldering use lower-melting filler metals. Each process has advantages: arc welding is versatile and economical, laser weld
ME 330: Welding and allied processes: Welding processes join materials, each with advantages: arc welding (versatile, economical), MIG/TIG (quality, control), laser/electron beam (precision, deep penetration), resistance welding (high-speed spot welding), friction welding (solid-state, dissimilar metals). Allied processes include brazing (lower temperature, filler wets surfaces) and soldering (electronics). Weld quality depends on pro
ME 330: Quenching and heat transfer: Quenching rapidly cools materials from elevated temperatures using media (water, oil, polymer solutions, air) to achieve desired microstructures or retain high-temperature phases. Cooling rate varies with location (surface versus core) creating property gradients and residual stresses. Heat transfer occurs through film boiling (slow cooling), nucleate boiling (rapid), and convection stages, with q
ME 330: Diffusion and growth: Diffusion is atomic migration from high to low concentration regions, enabling solid-state phase transformations, homogenization, and surface treatments (carburizing, nitriding). Fick's laws govern diffusion: flux proportional to concentration gradient, with diffusivity following Arrhenius relationship (exponentially increasing with temperature). Growth processes in phase transformations occur thr
ME 401: Miscellaneous cooling devices: Beyond conventional vapor compression and absorption systems, various specialized cooling technologies serve niche applications. These include vortex tubes (compressed air separates into hot and cold streams), thermoelectric coolers (Peltier effect solid-state devices), thermoacoustic refrigerators (sound waves drive heat pumping), pulse tube coolers (oscillating gas flow for cryogenic cooling), Stirling cryocoolers, and evaporative cooling. Each has unique advantages: no moving parts, compact size, precise control, or specific temperature ranges. Understanding working principles, performance characteristics, and application domains enables engineers to select appropriate technology when conventional systems are unsuitable due to size, reliability, control, or special requirements..
ME 330: Nucleation: Nucleation initiates phase transformations by forming small clusters of new phase within parent phase. Homogeneous nucleation occurs spontaneously in uniform material requiring significant undercooling to overcome surface energy penalty of creating interfaces. Heterogeneous nucleation occurs at preferential sites (grain boundaries, inclusions, container walls) requiring less undercooling since exi
ME 330: Dislocations and vacancies: Dislocations are line defects where crystal structure is disrupted, enabling plastic deformation by allowing atomic planes to slip past each other incrementally rather than breaking all bonds simultaneously. Edge and screw dislocations are primary types. Vacancies are point defects where atomic sites are empty. These defects profoundly affect properties: dislocations enable ductility (metals defor
ME 330: It and cct diagrams: Isothermal Transformation (IT or TTT - Time-Temperature-Transformation) diagrams show transformation progress at constant temperatures, displaying C-curves indicating start and finish of phase transformations. Continuous Cooling Transformation (CCT) diagrams show transformations during continuous cooling at various rates, accounting for transformation suppression during cooling. These diagrams are
ME 320: Conduction: Conduction is heat transfer through matter by molecular interactions and energy carrier diffusion (electrons in metals, phonons in insulators) without bulk motion. Governed by Fourier's law q'' = -k∇T, thermal conductivity k is a material property varying with temperature and structure. Conduction analysis solves the heat diffusion equation with appropriate boundary/initial conditions for steady o
ME 371: Stress concentrations and notch: Stress concentrations are localized stress elevations at geometric discontinuities (holes, fillets, notches, threads), where peak stress exceeds nominal stress by stress concentration factor (Kt). Notch sensitivity (q) indicates material's susceptibility to stress concentrations, varying from 0 (insensitive, like cast iron) to 1 (fully sensitive, like high-strength steel). Fatigue strength reducti
ME 487: Dry and wet etching: Two fundamental material removal approaches distinguished by etchant phase and mechanism. Wet etching uses liquid chemical solutions (acids, bases, or solvents) that react with exposed materials, typically producing isotropic profiles with undercutting, though crystallographic etching of silicon can be highly anisotropic. Dry etching employs plasmas containing reactive ions and radicals in vacuum chambers, enabling anisotropic vertical profiles through directional ion bombardment and chemical reactions. Trade-offs include wet etching's simplicity and high selectivity versus dry etching's superior dimensional control and vertical sidewalls. Etch rate, selectivity to masks and underlying layers, surface roughness, and profile control vary significantly between approaches..
ME 420: Condensation and boiling: Condensation and boiling involve phase change at surfaces with extremely high heat transfer coefficients due to latent heat effects. Film condensation forms liquid layer on cold surfaces with h ~ 5000-20000 W/m²K; dropwise condensation (h ~ 50000-250000 W/m²K) achieves even higher rates but is difficult to maintain. Boiling includes nucleate boiling (bubbles form at nucleation sites), critical heat flux (CHF or burnout), and film boiling. The boiling curve shows heat flux vs. temperature difference. Applications include power plant condensers, refrigeration evaporators, heat pipes, and cooling of high-power electronics. Understanding two-phase heat transfer enables design of compact, efficient thermal systems exploiting phase change..
ME 487: Metal deposition: Specialized thin film deposition processes for creating metallic layers serving as electrodes, interconnects, reflectors, seed layers for electroplating, and structural materials in MEMS/NEMS devices. Common metals include aluminum, gold, copper, titanium, chromium, and platinum, deposited via sputtering, e-beam evaporation, thermal evaporation, or electroplating. Critical considerations include adhesion to underlying layers often requiring adhesion promoters like titanium or chromium, step coverage over topography, grain structure affecting conductivity and stress, electromigration resistance, and compatibility with subsequent processing temperatures. Metal deposition parameters control film stress, resistivity, and microstructure..
CHEM 103: Laboratory equipment and the b: Laboratory equipment encompasses the tools and instruments used for chemical experimentation, with the analytical balance being fundamental for precise mass measurements. Balances measure mass to several decimal places (typically 0.001g or better) and require proper technique including taring, calibration, and avoiding contamination. Understanding equipment names, functions, and proper usage is es
CHEM 103: Lechatelier's principle: Le Chatelier's principle states that when a system at equilibrium experiences a disturbance (stress), it responds by shifting to counteract that stress and re-establish equilibrium. In laboratory demonstrations, students observe how changes in concentration, temperature, or pressure affect equilibrium position. For example, adding reactants shifts equilibrium toward products, increasing temperatur
CHEM 102: Equilibrium constants: The equilibrium constant (K) is a numerical value expressing the ratio of product to reactant concentrations at equilibrium, each raised to their stoichiometric coefficients. For aA + bB ⇌ cC + dD, K = [C]^c[D]^d / [A]^a[B]^b. Large K (>1) indicates products favored; small K (
CHEM 102: Limiting reagent concepts & pe: Limiting reagent concepts involve identifying which reactant determines the maximum theoretical yield of product. Percentage yield compares actual yield (obtained experimentally) to theoretical yield (calculated from stoichiometry): % yield = (actual/theoretical) × 100. Yields less than 100% result from side reactions, incomplete reactions, product loss during separation, or measurement errors. Th
CHEM 102: Limiting reagent: The limiting reagent is the reactant that is completely consumed first in a chemical reaction, thus determining the maximum amount of product that can form. Other reactants present in excess remain after the reaction completes. Identifying the limiting reagent requires comparing the mole ratio of reactants available to the mole ratio required by the balanced equation. The limiting reagent concept
CHEM 102: Chemical equilibrium: Chemical equilibrium is a dynamic state where forward and reverse reaction rates are equal, resulting in no net change in concentrations of reactants and products over time. Equilibrium is dynamic because reactions continue in both directions but at equal rates. The position of equilibrium (relative amounts of reactants and products) depends on reaction conditions but eventually reaches a characte
CHEM 103: Standard curves: A standard curve (calibration curve) is a graph plotting known concentrations of a substance against corresponding measured values (such as absorbance in spectroscopy). By measuring standards of known concentration and plotting the relationship, unknown sample concentrations can be determined by interpolation. The curve establishes a quantitative relationship between concentration and measurement,
CHEM 103: Serial dilutions: Serial dilution is a systematic technique for creating progressively more dilute solutions by repeatedly diluting a stock solution by a constant factor. Each dilution step uses a portion of the previous solution as the starting material for the next dilution, often following patterns like 1:10 or 1:2 ratios. This method is essential for preparing standard solutions for calibration curves, generati
MATH 221: Related rates: Related rates problems involve finding how fast one quantity changes given information about how fast related quantities change. The technique uses the chain rule to differentiate equations relating multiple time-varying quantities with respect to time. Key steps include: draw and label diagrams, write equations relating variables, differentiate both sides with respect to time using implicit diffe
CS 101: Numerical optimization: Numerical optimization finds parameter values minimizing or maximizing objective functions using algorithms like gradient descent, Newton's method, or genetic algorithms. Applications include parameter fitting, machine learning training, and engineering design. Constraints can limit feasible solutions. Understanding optimization enables finding best-fit parameters for models, optimizing experiment
MATH 241: Visualizing functions of sever: Functions of several variables map multiple inputs to one output: f(x,y) or f(x,y,z). Visualizing f(x,y) uses 3D surfaces (graphs z = f(x,y)) or level curves (contour maps showing f(x,y) = k for various constants k). For f(x,y,z), level surfaces show f(x,y,z) = k. Visualization techniques include 3D plots, contour plots, cross-sections, and interactive tools. Understanding multivariable function b
MATH 257: Introduction to linear systems: Linear systems are collections of linear equations involving multiple variables that must be satisfied simultaneously. Each equation represents a geometric constraint (lines in 2D, planes in 3D, hyperplanes in higher dimensions), and solutions are points where all constraints intersect. These systems appear throughout engineering when modeling relationships with proportional dependencies, such as electrical circuits, chemical reactions, structural forces, and economic models..
CS 101: Curve fitting & regression: Curve fitting determines function parameters best matching data by minimizing error between model predictions and observations. Linear regression fits lines; polynomial and nonlinear regression fit more complex functions. Least squares is common criterion. Understanding regression enables extracting relationships from data, validating theoretical models, predicting outcomes, and quantifying uncert
CHEM 104: Rate law determination: Rate law determination involves experimental methods to find reaction orders and rate constants. The method of initial rates compares how changing initial concentrations affects initial rates - if doubling [A] doubles rate, reaction is first-order in A; if rate quadruples, second-order; if unchanged, zero-order. Graphical methods plot concentration vs time data: zero-order gives linear [A] vs t, f
CHEM 104: Rates: Reaction rate measures how quickly reactant concentrations decrease or product concentrations increase over time, typically expressed as change in concentration per unit time (M/s). Rates depend on reactant concentrations, temperature, presence of catalysts, and surface area for heterogeneous reactions. Instantaneous rate at any moment differs from average rate over a time interval. Initial rate (
CHEM 104: Rate laws: A rate law expresses the mathematical relationship between reaction rate and reactant concentrations: rate = k[A]^m[B]^n, where k is the rate constant, m and n are reaction orders (usually integers determined experimentally). The overall reaction order is m+n. Rate laws must be determined experimentally and cannot be predicted from balanced equations. First-order reactions have rate proportional t
CHEM 104: Equilibrium: Chemical equilibrium is a dynamic state where forward and reverse reaction rates are equal, resulting in constant concentrations of reactants and products. At equilibrium, ΔG = 0 and Q = K. The equilibrium constant K relates to standard free energy by ΔG° = -RT ln K. Le Chatelier's principle predicts how equilibrium shifts in response to disturbances. Equilibrium calculations involve ICE tables, e
CHEM 104: Mechanisms: A reaction mechanism is the step-by-step molecular pathway showing how reactants convert to products, consisting of elementary steps that may involve intermediates. The rate-determining step (slowest step) controls overall reaction rate. Mechanisms must be consistent with the stoichiometric equation (elementary steps sum to overall reaction) and the experimentally determined rate law. Intermediate
ME 310: Design project: A design project integrates fluid mechanics principles to solve an open-ended, realistic engineering problem. Students apply continuity, momentum, and energy equations; select appropriate components (pumps, valves, meters); size piping and equipment; consider costs, constraints, and optimization; and present professional documentation. Projects might include designing water distribution systems, v
ME 200: Equations of state: Equations of state are mathematical relationships that describe the interdependence of thermodynamic properties such as pressure, temperature, volume, and internal energy for a substance. In thermodynamics courses, students learn various equations including the ideal gas law (PV=nRT), van der Waals equation, and other real gas models. These equations serve as the foundation for analyzing thermodyn
ME 200: Properties: Measurable characteristics describing thermodynamic state including primitive properties (pressure, temperature, volume, mass, composition) and derived properties (density, specific volume, internal energy, enthalpy, entropy). Properties are either intensive (temperature, pressure, independent of system size) or extensive (volume, energy, proportional to mass). State postulate requires minimum num
TAM 335: Calibration of flowmeters: Flowmeter calibration establishes relationship between meter reading and actual flow rate using reference standards: gravimetric (weighing collected fluid), volumetric (measuring volume in calibrated tank), or master meter comparison. Calibration accounts for installation effects, fluid properties, and flow range. Calibration curves plot indicated flow vs. actual flow; uncertainty analysis quantifies accuracy. Periodic recalibration maintains accuracy. Students learn calibration procedures, data analysis, and uncertainty propagation. Understanding calibration is essential for ensuring measurement accuracy in process control, custody transfer, and research applications. Proper calibration distinguishes between actual flow changes and instrument drift..
TAM 335: Similitude: dimensional analysi: Dimensional analysis uses fundamental dimensions (mass, length, time, temperature) to derive dimensionless groups governing physical phenomena, enabling similarity between model and prototype. The Buckingham Pi Theorem states that a problem with n variables and m fundamental dimensions can be reduced to (n-m) dimensionless groups. Proper scaling ensures these groups match between model and prototype, guaranteeing dynamic similarity. Dimensional analysis identifies important parameters, guides experimental design, correlates data, and enables model testing at different scales. Applications include wind tunnel testing, hydraulic model studies, and correlating heat transfer or drag coefficients, making complex problems tractable..
ME 451: Metrology: Metrology is the science of measurement, providing the theoretical foundation and practical techniques for quantifying physical dimensions and properties. Topics include measurement uncertainty, calibration, traceability to standards, statistical analysis, and instrument selection. Metrology ensures measurements are accurate, repeatable, and comparable across locations and times. For manufacturing, metrology enables process control, quality assurance, and product verification. Understanding measurement system capability (MSA/GR&R studies), error sources, and proper technique is essential for reliable quality data supporting decision-making..
ME 402: Application of python for therma: Python programming enables thermal system simulation, optimization, and analysis through numerical computation and data visualization. Libraries like NumPy (numerical arrays), SciPy (scientific computing), Matplotlib (plotting), and CoolProp (thermophysical properties) provide powerful tools. Students develop programs to solve energy balances, iterate designs, optimize parameters, and visualize results. Python's readability and extensive libraries make it ideal for engineering calculations compared to lower-level languages. Skills include function definition, loops, conditional statements, numerical methods implementation, and data handling. Proficiency with Python enables rapid prototyping, parameter studies, and automation of repetitive calculations, significantly accelerating the design process and enabling exploration of complex design spaces..
ME 453: Control charts for variables: Control charts for variables monitor continuous measurements like dimensions, weight, or temperature using charts plotting sample statistics over time. Common charts include X-bar and R charts (sample mean and range), X-bar and S charts (mean and standard deviation), and individual-X and moving range charts. Control limits at ±3 standard deviations from the centerline indicate normal variation. Points outside limits or non-random patterns signal special causes requiring investigation. Variables charts provide detailed information about process centering and spread, enabling early problem detection and process improvement..
ME 460: Closed loop system identificatio: Identifying plant models while the system operates under feedback control, necessary when open-loop testing is impractical due to instability, safety concerns, or operational requirements. Challenges include correlation between input and disturbances through feedback, identifiability issues requiring persistent excitation, and bias from disturbances. Methods include indirect identification (identifying closed-loop then extracting plant), direct identification with external excitation, and instrumental variable techniques handling correlation. Applications include model refinement for improved control, adaptive control updating models during operation, and system monitoring detecting degradation while maintaining production in continuous processes..
ME 475: Shape, behavior, and function: The analysis framework examining relationships between biological form (shape/structure), actions (behavior/processes), and purpose (function/performance) to identify transferable design principles. Shape includes morphology at multiple scales from molecular to organismal, behavior encompasses dynamic responses and processes, and function represents the selective advantage or performance outcome. Understanding these relationships reveals how structure enables function (lotus leaf texture creates water repellency), how behavior optimizes performance (flocking behavior improves predator avoidance), and how constraints shape evolution. Abstraction from this triad helps identify which aspects transfer to engineering contexts where constraints and objectives may differ..
ME 460: Internal model control: Model-based control architecture using plant model in parallel with actual plant, comparing outputs to detect disturbances and model mismatch, with controller designed based on model inverse filtered for robustness. Structure provides perfect tracking and disturbance rejection for perfect model, with performance degrading gracefully with model error. IMC filter determines tradeoff between performance (aggressive, sensitive to mismatch) and robustness (conservative, insensitive). Method simplifies design, provides intuitive tuning, guarantees internal stability, and translates directly to conventional feedback form. Applications include chemical process control where reasonably accurate models enable model-based design approaches..
ME 455: Micrometrology: Micrometrology measures dimensions, surface characteristics, and geometric accuracy of micro-components using specialized instruments achieving nanometer-level resolution. Techniques include optical microscopy, scanning probe microscopy (AFM, STM), scanning electron microscopy, white light interferometry, and coordinate measuring machines with micro-probes. Challenges include part handling, measurement uncertainty quantification, and non-destructive testing. Calibration and traceability ensure measurement reliability. Understanding micrometrology enables quality verification and process control in micro-manufacturing..
TAM 210: Review of vector algebra: Vector algebra reviews mathematical operations for vectors in 2D and 3D. Vectors have magnitude and direction, represented as bold or with arrows: **F** or F⃗. Operations include: addition (parallelogram/triangle rule), subtraction, scalar multiplication, dot product (**a**·**b** = |**a**||**b**|cos θ, yields scalar), cross product (**a**×**b** = |**a**||**b**|sin θ **n̂**, yields vector perpendic
PHYS 211: Vectors: Vectors are mathematical objects with both magnitude and direction, essential for representing physical quantities like displacement, velocity, force, and acceleration. Vector operations include addition, subtraction, scalar multiplication, dot product, and cross product. Understanding component decomposition, unit vectors, and vector algebra is crucial for analyzing motion and forces in two and t

TAM 211: Forces: Forces are fundamental vector quantities in statics representing interactions between bodies. Measured in Newtons (SI) or pounds (Imperial), forces are characterized by magnitude, direction, line of action, and point of application. Types include contact forces (normal, friction, tension, compression) and field forces (gravitational, electromagnetic). In statics, forces on bodies in equilibrium su
TAM 210: Forces: Forces are vector quantities representing push or pull interactions, measured in Newtons (N) or pounds (lb). Characterized by magnitude, direction, and point of application (line of action). Types: contact forces (normal, friction, tension) and body forces (gravity, magnetic). Newton's first law (statics): object in equilibrium has ΣF = 0 (zero net force). Forces can be resolved into components, c
MATH 241: Cross product: The cross product u × v of vectors u and v produces a vector perpendicular to both, with magnitude ||u × v|| = ||u|| ||v|| sin(θ) where θ is the angle between them. Direction follows the right-hand rule. Computed as u × v =
TAM 211: Resultants: A resultant is the single force, or force-couple combination, that replaces a system of forces while producing the same external effect. The force resultant R = ΣF is the vector sum of all forces in the system. The moment resultant about point O is MO = Σ(ri × Fi). For general force systems, reduction to a point yields a force and couple. For special cases (concurrent, parallel, or coplanar forces
TAM 211: Centroids and center of mass: Centroid is the geometric center of an area, volume, or line; center of mass is the mass-weighted average position where total weight effectively acts. For homogeneous bodies, centroid and center of mass coincide. Calculated by weighted averaging: x̄ = ∫x dA / ∫dA for areas, or x̄ = ΣxᵢAᵢ / ΣAᵢ for composite shapes. Centroids determine resultant location for distributed loads, stability analysis,
TAM 211: Moments: Moments quantify the rotational effect of forces about points or axes. The moment vector M = r × F represents the tendency of force F to rotate about a point, where r is the position vector from the moment center to the force application point. Moment magnitude M = Fd, where d is the perpendicular distance (moment arm) from the axis to the force line of action. The right-hand rule determines momen
TAM 210: Resultants: The resultant is the single force and/or couple that replaces a system of forces, producing the same external effect. Force resultant **R** = ΣF (vector sum of all forces). Moment resultant **M**ᴼ = Σ(**rᵢ** × **Fᵢ**) about point O. General system reduces to force-couple system at chosen point, or single resultant force if moment about one point is zero (concurrent or parallel forces). Finding res

PHYS 211: Newton's laws: Newton's three laws form the foundation of classical mechanics. The first law (inertia) states objects maintain constant velocity unless acted upon by net force. The second law quantifies force as mass times acceleration (F=ma), relating force to motion. The third law states that forces occur in equal and opposite action-reaction pairs. These laws enable prediction of motion from forces and are fu
TAM 212: Newton's laws: Newton's three laws form the foundation of classical mechanics. First law (inertia): object remains at rest or constant velocity unless acted upon by net force. Second law: ΣF = ma relates net force to acceleration. Third law: for every action force, there exists equal and opposite reaction force. These laws apply to particles and centers of mass of systems. First law defines inertial reference fr
PHYS 211: Statics 1 & statics 2: Statics analyzes systems in mechanical equilibrium where net force and net torque are both zero, resulting in no translational or rotational acceleration. Problems involve determining forces, tensions, and reaction forces in structures like bridges, ladders, and cranes. Success requires drawing complete free body diagrams, choosing convenient rotation axes for torque calculations, and systematical
PHYS 211: Conservative forces: Conservative forces are forces where the work done depends only on initial and final positions, not the path taken. Examples include gravity and spring forces. For conservative forces, work done around any closed path is zero, and a potential energy function can be defined. The mechanical energy (kinetic plus potential) remains constant in systems with only conservative forces, enabling powerful e
TAM 211: Virtual work: Virtual work principle states that for system in equilibrium, total virtual work done by all forces through compatible virtual displacements is zero: δW = ΣFᵢ·δrᵢ = 0. Virtual displacement is imaginary, infinitesimal displacement consistent with constraints. Method determines equilibrium configuration or unknown forces without considering internal forces or reactions at ideal constraints. Particul
PHYS 211: 1d and 2d kinematics: Kinematics describes motion without considering the forces causing it, focusing on position, velocity, and acceleration as functions of time. In one dimension, motion occurs along a line with scalar quantities. Two-dimensional kinematics extends this to planar motion using vector components, commonly applied to projectile motion. Understanding displacement, average and instantaneous velocities, an
PHYS 211: Work & potential energy: Potential energy is stored energy due to an object's position in a conservative force field, such as gravitational or elastic potential energy. Work done by conservative forces equals the negative change in potential energy. The total mechanical energy (kinetic plus potential) is conserved when only conservative forces act. This principle simplifies many mechanics problems by avoiding detailed for
TAM 210: Equilibrium: Static equilibrium occurs when object remains at rest: no translation (ΣF = 0) and no rotation (ΣM = 0). Six scalar equations in 3D: three for force components, three for moment components. In 2D: three equations (ΣFₓ=0, ΣFᵧ=0, ΣM=0). Equilibrium requires balancing all forces and moments. Used to determine unknown forces, reactions, and internal forces in structures. Two-force members have forces
TAM 211: Practical examples: Practical examples apply statics principles to real-world engineering problems including structures (bridges, buildings, cranes), machines (levers, pulleys, gears), and everyday objects. These applications demonstrate how theoretical concepts of forces, moments, and equilibrium solve actual design challenges. Examples range from simple scenarios (ladder against wall, hanging sign) to complex syste
TAM 210: Equilibrium analysis: Equilibrium analysis applies ΣF = 0 and ΣM = 0 to determine unknown forces and reactions in static systems. Process: (1) draw free-body diagram showing all forces, (2) choose coordinate system, (3) write equilibrium equations, (4) solve simultaneously for unknowns. Systematic approach handles multiple connected bodies. Statically determinate systems: number of unknowns equals number of equilibrium
TAM 211: Equilibrium analysis: Equilibrium analysis is the systematic process of determining unknown forces and reactions in static systems by applying equilibrium equations. The method involves: (1) isolating the body and drawing a complete free-body diagram, (2) selecting appropriate coordinate system, (3) writing equilibrium equations (ΣF=0, ΣM=0), and (4) solving for unknowns. For 2D problems: three independent equations; f
MATH 241: Dot product: The dot product (scalar product) of vectors u =
TAM 211: Equipollent systems: Equipollent force systems are different force arrangements that produce identical external effects on a rigid body. Two systems are equipollent if they have equal resultant forces (ΣF₁ = ΣF₂) and equal resultant moments about any point (ΣM₁ = ΣM₂). While producing the same overall effect, equipollent systems may have different internal stress distributions. This concept allows replacement of compl

MATH 257: Mass-spring system: Mass-spring systems model physical systems where masses are connected by springs, creating a network of coupled oscillators. The system's behavior is described by linear differential equations that can be expressed in matrix form: Mẍ + Kx = f, where M is the mass matrix, K is the stiffness matrix, and f is the external force vector. Solving this system requires eigenvalue analysis to find natural frequencies and mode shapes. This application demonstrates how linear algebra models real-world mechanical systems in engineering..
MATH 257: Coordinates: Coordinates represent vectors as n-tuples of scalars relative to a chosen basis. Given a basis B = {b₁, b₂, ..., bₙ} for a vector space V, any vector v can be uniquely written as v = c₁b₁ + c₂b₂ + ... + cₙbₙ, and [v]ᵦ = [c₁, c₂, ..., cₙ]ᵀ is the coordinate vector. Different bases yield different coordinate representations of the same vector. Understanding coordinates is essential for change of basis, simplifying computations, and connecting abstract vector spaces to computational representations..
TAM 211: Equilibrium: Equilibrium is the state where a rigid body remains at rest (static equilibrium) or moves with constant velocity (dynamic equilibrium). Static equilibrium requires two conditions: force equilibrium (ΣF = 0, preventing translation) and moment equilibrium (ΣM = 0, preventing rotation). In 3D, this yields six scalar equations; in 2D, three equations. Equilibrium principles enable determination of unk
TAM 210: Moments: Moment (torque) is the rotational effect of a force about a point or axis, calculated as **M** = **r** × **F**, where **r** is position vector from pivot to force application point. Magnitude: M = rF sin θ = Fd, where d is perpendicular distance (moment arm). Units: N·m or lb·ft. Moments cause rotation; positive/negative indicates direction (right-hand rule). Varignon's theorem: moment of resultan
MATH 241: Surface integrals of vector fi: Surface integrals of vector fields, also called flux integrals, measure the flow of a vector field through a surface in three-dimensional space. The integral computes the dot product of the vector field with the surface's normal vector over the entire surface. This quantifies how much of the field passes through the surface and is crucial for applications in fluid dynamics, electromagnetism, and h
ME 370: Virtual work approach: The virtual work approach analyzes machine dynamics using energy principles rather than force balance. Virtual work is the work done by forces through virtual (imaginary, infinitesimal) displacements consistent with constraints. For systems in equilibrium (including dynamic equilibrium using d'Alembert's principle), virtual work equals zero. This method is powerful for mechanisms with many compone
TAM 445: Vectors: Vectors are first-order tensors with magnitude and direction, represented by components in a basis: v = vᵢeᵢ. Vector operations include addition, scalar multiplication, dot product (a·b = aᵢbᵢ yields scalar), and cross product (a×b yields vector perpendicular to both). Magnitude: ‖v‖ = √(vᵢvᵢ). Unit vector: v̂ = v/‖v‖. Vectors represent physical quantities like displacement, velocity, force. Vecto
TAM 445: Vector products: Vector product (cross product) of vectors a and b yields vector perpendicular to both: c = a×b with magnitude ‖c‖ = ‖a‖‖b‖sinθ and direction given by right-hand rule. In components: (a×b)ᵢ = εᵢⱼₖaⱼbₖ using permutation symbol εᵢⱼₖ. Properties: antisymmetric (a×b = -b×a), distributive, zero when parallel. Applications: torque M = r×F, angular momentum L = r×mv, curl (∇×v). Vector triple product iden
TAM 445: Matrices: Matrices are rectangular arrays of numbers representing linear transformations and second-order tensors. Operations include addition, scalar multiplication, matrix multiplication (not commutative), transpose, and inverse. Matrix multiplication represents composition of transformations. Identity matrix I is multiplicative identity. Orthogonal matrices Q satisfy QᵀQ = I, representing rotations. Symm
TAM 412: Generalized velocities and virt: Generalized velocities are time derivatives of generalized coordinates, describing system motion in configuration space. Virtual displacements are infinitesimal, instantaneous changes in coordinates consistent with constraints but not necessarily physically realizable (frozen time, constraints maintained). Virtual displacements form the basis for variational principles: for a system in equilibrium
TAM 412: Matrix representations of equat: Expressing equations of motion in matrix form: Mq̈ + Cq̇ + Kq = F, where M is the mass matrix, C is damping, K is stiffness, q is the coordinate vector, and F is the force vector. This compact notation facilitates numerical solution, eigenvalue analysis, and system identification. Mass matrices may be diagonal (uncoupled inertias) or full (coupled through kinetic energy). Stiffness matrices arise
TAM 445: Second-order tensors: Second-order tensors are linear transformations mapping vectors to vectors: y = A·x, with components Aᵢⱼ forming matrix. Transform under coordinate change: A'ᵢⱼ = QᵢₖQⱼₗAₖₗ. Symmetric tensors Aᵢⱼ = Aⱼᵢ have orthogonal eigenvectors; antisymmetric have Aᵢⱼ = -Aⱼᵢ. Decomposition: A = sym(A) + skew(A). Operations include addition, multiplication, transpose, inverse, trace, determinant. Physical exampl
TAM 412: D'alembert's principle of virtu: D'Alembert's principle extends the virtual work principle to dynamics by treating inertial terms (ma) as forces. For a system, the principle states: Σ(F_i - m_i a_i)·δr_i = 0 for all virtual displacements δr_i consistent with constraints. This formulation eliminates constraint forces (since they do no virtual work) and provides the foundation for deriving Lagrange's equations. The principle transf
ME 440: D'alembert's principle: D'Alembert's principle reformulates Newton's second law (F = ma) by moving the inertial term to the force side: F - ma = 0, treating -ma as an 'inertial force.' This allows dynamic problems to be solved using virtual work methods from statics. For systems with constraints, d'Alembert's principle states that the virtual work done by applied forces minus inertial forces, through virtual displacements consistent with constraints, equals zero. This principle provides a powerful alternative to direct force-balance methods, particularly for complex constrained systems..
TAM 445: Tensor algebra and analysis: Tensor algebra provides mathematical framework for continuum mechanics. Tensors are geometric objects independent of coordinate choice - scalars (rank 0), vectors (rank 1), and higher-order tensors (rank ≥2). Tensor operations include addition, multiplication, contraction, and transformation under coordinate changes. Tensor analysis extends to derivatives and integrals of tensor fields. Indicial n
TAM 412: Generalized forces: Generalized forces are quantities conjugate to generalized coordinates in expressing virtual work: δW = ΣQᵢδqᵢ. They have units of energy per unit coordinate change - forces for length coordinates, torques for angular coordinates. Generalized forces incorporate all applied forces, calculated by taking the virtual work and extracting coefficients of virtual displacements. This concept enables syste
TAM 445: Scalar products: Scalar product (inner product, dot product) of vectors a and b yields scalar: a·b = aᵢbᵢ = ‖a‖‖b‖cosθ, where θ is angle between vectors. Properties: commutative (a·b = b·a), distributive, and bilinear. Orthogonality: a·b = 0 when perpendicular. For second-order tensors: A:B = AᵢⱼBᵢⱼ (double contraction). Scalar product measures projection, computes work (F·dr), defines norms, and tests orthogonali
TAM 412: Potential energy and relation t: Potential energy V is stored energy depending on configuration: gravitational (mgh), elastic (½kx²), etc. The relation to generalized forces is Q_i = -∂V/∂q_i: conservative forces derive from potential energy's negative gradient with respect to generalized coordinates. This relationship allows replacing explicit force analysis with energy function specification in Lagrangian mechanics. For conserv
