TAM 412: Lagrange multipliers: Lagrange multipliers are additional variables λ_j introduced to enforce constraint equations in optimization or dynamics problems. In Lagrangian mechanics with constraints g_j(q) = 0, multipliers represent constraint forces (forces of reaction) and enter augmented Lagrange equations. The method converts constrained problems into unconstrained ones with additional variables, enabling systematic sol
ME 440: Linear and angular velocities: Linear velocity describes the rate of change of position (translation) of a point, while angular velocity describes the rate of rotation about an axis. For rigid bodies, these are interconnected: a point's linear velocity depends on the body's angular velocity and the point's position relative to the rotation axis (v = ω × r). Understanding this relationship is crucial for analyzing mechanisms, wheels, gears, and any rotating machinery. Angular velocity is a vector quantity with magnitude (rotation rate) and direction (rotation axis), following right-hand rule conventions..
TAM 412: Configuration constraints and r: Configuration constraints are equations relating generalized coordinates, often arising when using more coordinates than necessary for convenience. Redundant (dependent) coordinates exceed the minimum needed to specify configuration, with constraint equations relating them. Using redundant coordinates can simplify kinematics at the cost of introducing constraint forces or Lagrange multipliers in d
TAM 445: Principal directions of symmetr: Principal directions are eigenvectors of symmetric tensor: A·n = λn, where n is principal direction (eigenvector), λ is principal value (eigenvalue). For symmetric tensor, eigenvalues are real and eigenvectors mutually orthogonal. Principal axes provide coordinate system where tensor is diagonal - off-diagonal components zero. In principal coordinates: Aᵢⱼ = λ₁n₁ᵢn₁ⱼ + λ₂n₂ᵢn₂ⱼ + λ₃n₃ᵢn₃ⱼ (spectra
ME 462: Equations of motions: Fundamental differential equations governing dynamic system behavior derived from Newton's laws (for mechanical systems), Kirchhoff's laws (electrical), or energy methods (Lagrangian/Hamiltonian). For aircraft, six-degree-of-freedom equations describe translational (uvw velocities) and rotational (pqr rates) motion in body frame. State-space form ẋ = f(x,u) enables control design. Linear approximations around equilibria provide transfer functions. Understanding equation structure reveals coupling, nonlinearities, and control authority. Applications span all dynamic systems requiring mathematical models for analysis, simulation, and control design from vehicles to robots to industrial processes..
TAM 412: Work and kinetic energy: Work is energy transferred by forces acting through displacements: W = ∫F·ds. Kinetic energy T represents energy of motion: T = ½mv² for particles, more complex for rigid bodies including rotational kinetic energy. The work-energy theorem states that net work equals change in kinetic energy. In Lagrangian mechanics, kinetic energy's functional dependence on velocities and positions is central to f
TAM 210: Free-body diagrams: Free-body diagram (FBD) is a sketch isolating a body from surroundings, showing all external forces and moments acting on it. Essential for equilibrium analysis. Includes: applied forces, reaction forces (supports), weight, dimensions. Constraints removed and replaced with reaction forces. Types of supports: pin (two reactions), roller (one reaction perpendicular), fixed (three reactions: two forc
TAM 210: Equipollent systems: Two force systems are equipollent if they have the same resultant force and same resultant moment about any point. Equipollent systems produce identical external effects but may differ internally. Test: ΣF₁ = ΣF₂ and ΣM₁ = ΣM₂ (about same point). Useful for simplifying complex force distributions to equivalent simpler systems. Example: distributed load replaced by single resultant force. Equipolle
TAM 211: Review of vector algebra: Vector algebra forms the mathematical foundation for statics analysis. Vectors represent physical quantities with both magnitude and direction, such as forces, displacements, and velocities. Essential operations include vector addition (tip-to-tail or parallelogram method), scalar multiplication, dot product (a·b = |a||b|cosθ for projection and work), and cross product (a×b perpendicular to both,
PHYS 211: Forces & fbd: Forces are pushes or pulls that cause acceleration, measured in Newtons. Free body diagrams (FBD) are visual tools isolating an object and showing all forces acting on it as vectors. Common forces include gravity (weight), normal force, tension, friction, and applied forces. Drawing accurate FBDs is essential for applying Newton's second law to determine net force and resulting acceleration in mec
TAM 211: Couples: A couple is a special force system consisting of two parallel forces equal in magnitude, opposite in direction, and separated by a perpendicular distance. Couples produce pure rotational effect without resultant force. The couple moment M = F×d is independent of the moment center - a unique property making couples free vectors. Couples cannot be balanced by single forces, only by equal and opposit
TAM 212: Dynamics of particles: Particle dynamics relates forces to motion through Newton's second law: ΣF = ma, where F is net force, m is mass, a is acceleration. Analysis determines motion from known forces (kinetics) or forces from known motion (inverse dynamics). Free-body diagrams identify all forces; kinematic equations relate motion quantities. Common forces include gravity, friction, normal forces, tension, and applied
PHYS 211: Impulse & reference frames: Impulse is the change in momentum, equal to the integral of force over time. It provides an alternative to Newton's second law useful when forces vary with time or act over short intervals. Reference frames are coordinate systems from which motion is observed; inertial frames move at constant velocity while non-inertial frames accelerate. Understanding how physical laws appear in different frames
TAM 210: Multi-force members: Multi-force members have forces acting at three or more points or in non-collinear directions, unlike two-force members (forces only at ends along axis). Experience bending and shear in addition to axial forces. Examples: beams, frames, machine parts. Analysis requires considering forces and moments at multiple points. Internal forces vary along member length. Free-body diagrams show distributed l
TAM 211: Free-body diagrams: A free-body diagram (FBD) is a schematic representation showing an isolated body with all external forces and moments acting on it. The FBD is created by: (1) selecting the body to analyze, (2) isolating it from all supports and connections, (3) replacing constraints with reaction forces/moments, (4) showing all applied forces including weight, and (5) indicating relevant dimensions and angles. Su
MATH 241: Computing work in a vector fie: Work done by force field F moving an object along curve C from point A to B is W = ∫_C F·dr = ∫[a,b] F(r(t))·r'(t) dt, where r(t) parameterizes C. This measures energy transferred by the force along the path. When F points with motion direction (positive dot product), work is positive; opposite direction gives negative work; perpendicular force does no work. For conservative fields, work depends o
PHYS 211: Work & kinetic energy: Work is energy transferred by a force acting over a distance, calculated as the dot product of force and displacement vectors. Kinetic energy is the energy of motion, equal to (1/2)mv². The work-energy theorem states that net work done on an object equals its change in kinetic energy, providing a powerful alternative to Newton's laws for analyzing motion. This connects force, displacement, and spe
TAM 212: Impulse-momentum: Impulse-momentum principle relates force impulse to momentum change. Linear impulse: ∫F dt = Δ(mv) states that impulse equals momentum change. Angular impulse: ∫M dt = Δ(Iω) for rotation. Particularly useful for impact problems, collisions, and impulsive forces. Momentum conservation: for isolated system with no external impulse, momentum is conserved. Coefficient of restitution e relates relative
PHYS 211: Conservation of momentum: Momentum is the product of mass and velocity (p = mv), representing an object's quantity of motion. The law of conservation of momentum states that total momentum of an isolated system remains constant when no external forces act. This principle is fundamental to analyzing collisions, explosions, and interactions between objects. Unlike energy, momentum is always conserved in isolated systems rega
TAM 212: Methods of work-energy: Work-energy methods relate work done by forces to kinetic energy change: W₁₋₂ = ΔT, where W₁₋₂ = ∫F·dr is work, T = ½mv² (particle) or T = ½mv²cm + ½Iω² (rigid body) is kinetic energy. For conservative forces, mechanical energy conserves: T₁ + V₁ = T₂ + V₂, where V is potential energy. Work-energy avoids calculating acceleration and is efficient for problems involving velocities at different posit
MATH 221: Work: Work in physics is force times distance: W = Fd when force is constant. For variable force F(x), work is computed as W = ∫[a,b] F(x)dx, integrating force over distance. Applications include stretching springs (Hooke's Law: F = kx), lifting objects against gravity, pumping fluids from tanks, and pulling chains. Work done pumping fluids requires accounting for varying distances different layers must
TAM 251: Basic concepts of stress and st: Stress is internal force intensity (force per unit area) within deformed body, measured in Pa or psi. Normal stress σ acts perpendicular to surface; shear stress τ acts parallel. Strain is deformation intensity (change in length per unit length or angular distortion), dimensionless. Normal strain ε = ΔL/L; shear strain γ is angular change. Stress and strain are tensors with multiple components. Th
TAM 335: Definitions, properties, system: Fluid mechanics fundamentals begin with defining fluids (substances deforming continuously under shear stress) and key properties. Density ρ is mass per volume; specific weight γ = ρg; specific gravity is density relative to water. Viscosity μ quantifies resistance to shear deformation, distinguishing Newtonian (constant μ) from non-Newtonian fluids. Surface tension σ arises from intermolecular forces at interfaces. Vapor pressure is saturation pressure at given temperature. Bulk modulus K measures compressibility. Consistent units (SI or English) are essential for calculations. Understanding properties enables prediction of fluid behavior: will it flow easily (low viscosity), compress significantly (low bulk modulus), or form droplets (high surface tension)?.
ME 440: Newtonian rigid-body dynamics: Application of Newton's laws to rigid bodies, which unlike particles have finite size and can rotate. The analysis requires two fundamental equations: ΣF = ma for translation of the center of mass, and ΣM = Iα for rotation about the center of mass (or fixed point), where I is the moment of inertia tensor. These coupled equations predict how forces and moments cause rigid body motion. Understanding requires careful free body diagrams, proper reference frame selection, and recognizing how force application points affect rotational motion through moments..
TAM 412: Dynamically equivalent bodies: Dynamically equivalent bodies have identical mass, center of mass location, and inertia tensor, producing identical responses to applied forces and moments despite different physical appearances. This equivalence allows replacing complex mass distributions with simpler equivalent systems (like point masses and massless rods) for analysis. The concept is powerful for model reduction: a connecting r
TAM 445: Cauchy's stress principle: Cauchy's stress principle states that internal forces in continuum are transmitted through contact across surfaces via stress vector t(n) depending on position x, time t, and surface normal n. Stress vector: t(n) = σ·n, where σ is Cauchy stress tensor (second-order). On opposite face: t(-n) = -t(n) (action-reaction). Stress tensor is independent of n, fully characterizing internal force state. Com
TAM 445: Balance laws: Balance laws express conservation or balance of physical quantities. General form: rate of change = production + flux. Mass conservation: Dρ/Dt + ρ∇·v = 0 (continuity equation). Linear momentum: ρDv/Dt = ∇·σ + ρb (Cauchy equation), where σ is stress, b is body force. Angular momentum: σ = σᵀ (stress symmetry). Energy: ρDe/Dt = σ:D + ∇·q + ρr, where e is internal energy, q is heat flux, r is heat s
TAM 412: Forces of constraint: Constraint forces maintain system constraints (like a ball staying on a surface or a door rotating about hinges), doing no work through virtual displacements consistent with constraints. In Lagrangian mechanics, generalized coordinates automatically satisfy constraints, making constraint forces unnecessary for equations of motion. However, constraint forces can be calculated post-solution using La
TAM 412: Fundamentals of rigid body mech: Rigid body mechanics analyzes objects with fixed internal geometry (distances between points remain constant). The fundamental principle separates motion into translation of the center of mass (governed by ΣF = ma_cm) and rotation about the center of mass (governed by ΣM = Iω̇). The moment of inertia tensor I relates angular acceleration to applied moments, depending on mass distribution and rotat
ME 411: Strain: Strain quantifies cumulative deformation of fluid elements over time, though for fluids (unlike solids) instantaneous strain rate is more fundamental than accumulated strain. For Newtonian fluids, shear stress relates linearly to strain rate: τ = μ(dv/dy) for simple shear. Normal strain rates describe stretching/compression along coordinate directions. Shear strain rates describe angular deformation. The strain rate tensor is symmetric, with diagonal elements representing normal strain rates and off-diagonal elements representing shear strain rates. Understanding strain connects fluid deformation to viscous stresses, enabling formulation of constitutive relations for momentum transport. Material behavior (Newtonian vs. non-Newtonian) is characterized by stress-strain rate relationships..
TAM 211: Product of inertia: Product of inertia Iₓᵧ = ∫xy dA measures asymmetry of area distribution relative to xy axes. Unlike moments of inertia (always positive), product of inertia can be positive, negative, or zero. Zero when either axis is symmetry axis. Used in determining principal axes (axes where Iₓᵧ = 0) and analyzing unsymmetric bending. Transformation equations relate Iₓᵧ in different coordinate systems. For sym
PHYS 211: Parallel axis theorem & torque: The parallel axis theorem relates a body's moment of inertia about any axis to its moment of inertia about a parallel axis through the center of mass. Torque is rotational force, the cross product of position vector and force, measuring the tendency to cause rotation. Torque's magnitude depends on force magnitude, distance from rotation axis, and angle between them. These concepts are fundamental
TAM 211: Polar moment of inertia: Polar moment of inertia J measures area distribution relative to a point (typically centroid), quantifying torsional rigidity. Defined as J = ∫r² dA where r is distance from point to area element. For circular sections, J = Iₓ + Iᵧ (perpendicular axis theorem). Used in torsion analysis: shear stress τ = Tρ/J and angle of twist φ = TL/GJ, where T is torque, G is shear modulus. Circular shafts are e
TAM 210: Couples: A couple consists of two parallel forces of equal magnitude but opposite direction, separated by distance d. Couple produces pure rotation without translation. Couple moment **M** = **r** × **F** has magnitude M = Fd, where d is perpendicular distance between forces. Unique property: couple's moment is same about any point (independent of moment center). Couples cannot be balanced by single force,
TAM 211: Parallel-axis theorem: Parallel-axis theorem relates moment of inertia about any axis to moment about parallel centroidal axis: I = Īc + Ad², where Īc is moment about centroidal axis, A is area, d is distance between axes. Always increases I when moving away from centroid - minimum I occurs at centroidal axis. Also applies to polar moments: J = J̄c + Ad². Essential for composite shape analysis and determining I about no
TAM 210: Bending-moment diagrams: Bending moment M(x) is internal moment about beam's cross-section at position x, causing beam to curve. Sign convention: positive moment causes compression on top, tension on bottom (sagging). Relationship: dM/dx = V(x), connecting moment and shear. M maximum where dM/dx = V = 0. Moment diagrams plot M(x) vs. x. Distributed load creates parabolic moment curves. Maximum moment location critical for
TAM 211: Bending-moment diagrams: Bending moment M(x) is the internal moment about beam's neutral axis at any cross-section, causing beam curvature. Positive moment creates compression on top, tension on bottom (sagging). Calculated by summing moments on either side of section. Key relationship: dM/dx = V(x) connects moment slope to shear. Maximum moment occurs where shear equals zero. Moment diagrams plot M(x) along beam length,
TAM 211: Radius of gyration: Radius of gyration k is the distance from axis where area could be concentrated as a thin line to produce same moment of inertia: I = Ak². Defined as k = √(I/A). Provides intuitive measure of area distribution - larger k means area more spread from axis. For structural members, k relates to slenderness ratio L/k used in buckling analysis. For circular shaft, k = d/4 for bending; k = d/2√2 for tors
TAM 210: Centroids and center of mass: Centroid is geometric center of area, volume, or line; center of mass is mass-weighted average position. For homogeneous body, centroid coincides with center of mass (center of gravity). Coordinates: x̄ = ∫x dA / ∫dA (and similarly ȳ, z̄). For composite shapes: x̄ = ΣxᵢAᵢ / ΣAᵢ (weighted average of parts). Center of gravity is where weight W = mg effectively acts. Centroids used for locating resul
PHYS 211: Rotational dynamics: Rotational dynamics applies Newton's second law to rotation: net torque equals moment of inertia times angular acceleration (τ = Iα). This relates how applied torques cause angular acceleration, analogous to how forces cause linear acceleration. Moment of inertia (I) is rotational mass, depending on mass distribution relative to rotation axis. Understanding rotational dynamics enables analysis of
TAM 251: Bending moments: Bending moment M(x) represents internal moment about beam's neutral axis at any cross-section, causing bending curvature. Calculated from equilibrium: M = sum of moments about section from forces on one side. Sign convention: positive moment causes compression on top, tension on bottom (sagging). Relationships: dM/dx = V(x) and d²M/dx² = -w(x). Moment diagrams visualize M(x) distribution. Maximum
TAM 251: Multi-axial stress and strain s: Multi-axial stress states involve stress components in multiple directions: normal stresses (σx, σy, σz) and shear stresses (τxy, τyz, τzx). Stress is a tensor with nine components (six independent by symmetry). Principal stresses are normal stresses on planes with zero shear; found from eigenvalues of stress tensor. Maximum shear stress: τmax = (σ₁-σ₃)/2. Mohr's circle graphically represents 2D s
TAM 251: Transformation of stress and st: Transformation of stress and strain involves determining stress and strain components in different coordinate systems. Stress and strain are tensors that transform according to specific rules when rotating axes. Transformation equations for 2D stress: σx' = (σx+σy)/2 + (σx-σy)/2·cos(2θ) + τxy·sin(2θ). Similar equations exist for strain. Understanding transformations enables finding stresses on any
TAM 251: Principal stresses and strains: Principal stresses are maximum and minimum normal stresses occurring on planes where shear stress equals zero. For 2D: σ₁,₂ = (σx+σy)/2 ± √[(σx-σy)²/4 + τxy²]. Principal planes are mutually perpendicular. Maximum shear stress: τmax = (σ₁-σ₂)/2 occurs on plane at 45° to principal planes. Similarly, principal strains are maximum and minimum normal strains. Principal stress/strain directions define n
TAM 251: Shear forces: Shear force V(x) in solid mechanics context represents internal force perpendicular to beam axis resisting transverse loads. Calculated from equilibrium: V = sum of transverse forces on one side of section. Relationships: dV/dx = -w(x) and dM/dx = V, linking load, shear, and moment. Sign convention: positive shear causes clockwise rotation on left face. Shear diagrams visualize V(x) distribution.
TAM 251: Symmetric elastic beams: Symmetric elastic beams have at least one axis of symmetry through centroid, simplifying analysis. Common sections include rectangular, circular, I-beams, channels. For symmetric sections, neutral axis coincides with centroidal axis, principal axes align with symmetry axes, and product of inertia Ixy = 0. Bending about symmetry axis produces pure bending without twisting. Elastic implies linear st
TAM 251: Geometry of deformation: Geometry of deformation describes how beam cross-sections deform under bending. Plane sections remain plane (Bernoulli-Euler assumption): cross-sections perpendicular to neutral axis before bending remain plane after, but rotate. Curvature κ = 1/ρ relates to bending strain: ε = -y/ρ, where ρ is radius of curvature, y is distance from neutral axis. Combining with Hooke's law (σ = Eε) and stress res
TAM 412: Moments and ellipsoids of inert: Moments of inertia quantify mass distribution relative to rotation axes: I = ∫r²dm, determining rotational resistance. The inertia tensor is a symmetric 3×3 matrix containing moments (diagonal elements) and products of inertia (off-diagonal elements), relating angular momentum to angular velocity: L = Iω. The inertia ellipsoid provides geometric visualization: principal axes are ellipsoid axes whe
TAM 445: Some properties of the stress t: Key stress tensor properties: (1) Symmetry: σᵢⱼ = σⱼᵢ from angular momentum balance. (2) Transformation law: σ' = QσQᵀ under rotation Q. (3) Principal stresses: eigenvalues σ₁, σ₂, σ₃ with orthogonal eigenvectors (principal directions). (4) Invariants: I₁ = tr(σ), I₂ = ½[(tr σ)² - tr(σ²)], I₃ = det(σ) are coordinate-independent. (5) Decomposition: σ = σmI + s into mean (hydrostatic) and deviatoric
TAM 445: The stress tensor: Cauchy stress tensor σ is symmetric second-order tensor characterizing internal force state in continuum. Components σᵢⱼ represent force per area: j-direction indicates surface normal, i-direction indicates force component. Diagonal terms (σ₁₁, σ₂₂, σ₃₃) are normal stresses; off-diagonal are shear stresses. Stress vector on surface with normal n: t = σ·n. Principal stresses σ₁, σ₂, σ₃ (eigenvalues
TAM 445: Higher-order tensors: Higher-order tensors extend second-order concept to more indices. Third-order tensor Tᵢⱼₖ maps two vectors to scalar or vector to second-order tensor. Fourth-order tensor Cᵢⱼₖₗ maps second-order tensor to second-order tensor. Elasticity tensor C relating stress to strain is fourth-order: σᵢⱼ = Cᵢⱼₖₗεₖₗ. Transformation rules extend naturally: T'ᵢⱼₖ = QᵢₘQⱼₙQₖₚTₘₙₚ. Symmetries reduce independent com
TAM 211: Multi-force members: Multi-force members experience forces at three or more points or directions, resulting in combined axial force, shear force, and bending moment. Unlike two-force members (axial loads only), multi-force members are common in frames, machines, and beams where loads apply at multiple locations. Internal forces vary along member length, requiring consideration of shear and bending in addition to axial
TAM 210: Shear-force: Shear force V(x) is internal force perpendicular to beam axis at position x, representing transverse loading effect. Sign convention: positive shear causes clockwise rotation of left section. V varies along beam length according to applied loads. Relationship: dV/dx = -w(x), where w is distributed load intensity. Shear maximum often at supports or concentrated load points. Shear diagrams plot V(x)
TAM 211: Shear-force: Shear force V(x) is the internal force perpendicular to a beam's longitudinal axis at any cross-section, representing resistance to transverse loading. Calculated by summing vertical forces on either side of section. Sign convention: positive shear causes clockwise rotation of element. Shear varies along beam according to applied loads, with relationship dV/dx = -w(x) where w is distributed load i
TAM 251: Statically determinate and inde: In torsion, statically determinate shafts have torque distribution determinable from equilibrium (ΣT = 0) alone. Single-segment shafts or those with one fixed end are typically determinate. Indeterminate torsion problems have redundant constraints, requiring compatibility equations: sum of twist angles in closed loop equals zero. Combined with torque-twist relationship φ = TL/GJ, these enable solu
TAM 251: Stress distribution: Stress distribution describes how stress varies throughout a member. In bending: normal stress varies linearly with depth (σ = -My/I), maximum at extreme fibers. In torsion: shear stress varies linearly with radius (τ = Tρ/J for circular sections), maximum at outer surface. For combined loading, superposition applies (if material remains elastic). Stress concentrations occur at geometric discontin
TAM 251: Slopes: Slope θ = dy/dx is the angular rotation of beam's tangent line at any point along its length. Related to curvature by EI d²y/dx² = M, so EI dθ/dx = M, meaning slope change depends on bending moment. Boundary conditions include: fixed support (θ = 0), pin support (moment = 0 but θ ≠ 0), free end (moment = 0). Continuity conditions at internal points require slope continuous unless hinge present. Sl
TAM 251: Statically determinate and inde: Statically determinate systems have reactions and internal forces solvable using equilibrium equations alone. Number of unknowns equals number of independent equilibrium equations. Statically indeterminate (redundant) systems have more unknowns than equilibrium equations, requiring compatibility conditions (deformation constraints) and material properties for solution. Degree of indeterminacy: num
ME 370: Linkage dynamics: Linkage dynamics specifically analyzes forces in linkage mechanisms, determining bearing reactions, required input torques, and internal forces considering link masses and inertias. Analysis uses Newton-Euler approach applying force and moment balance to each link, or energy methods like Lagrangian mechanics. Inertia forces from link accelerations create dynamic forces exceeding static loads, requ
ME 370: Balancing of mechanisms: Balancing eliminates or reduces undesired inertia forces and moments in mechanisms through strategic mass redistribution or counterweight addition. Static balancing addresses forces (center of mass fixed), while dynamic balancing addresses both forces and moments. Unbalanced mechanisms generate vibrations, noise, bearing wear, and structural fatigue. Balancing techniques include adding counterweig
ME 370: Mechanisms and gears: Mechanisms and gears transform motion and transmit power through interconnected rigid bodies. Mechanisms include linkages (four-bar, slider-crank), cams, and gear trains. Gears provide precise speed ratios, high efficiency, and compact power transmission. Gear types include spur (parallel shafts), helical (smoother, higher capacity), bevel (intersecting shafts), and worm (large ratios, non-backdri
ME 370: Kinematic fundamentals: Kinematic fundamentals cover basic concepts for analyzing mechanism motion: degrees of freedom (independent motions), mobility (Grübler's equation), kinematic pairs (joints like revolute, prismatic, cam), links (rigid bodies), and kinematic chains (interconnected links). Understanding these building blocks enables classifying mechanisms, determining mobility, and selecting appropriate joints for d
TAM 324: Bending and torsion tests: Mechanical tests evaluating material response to bending moments and twisting torques. Bending tests (three-point or four-point) subject beams to transverse loads creating combined tensile and compressive stresses, determining flexural strength and modulus particularly useful for brittle materials that fail before yielding in tension. Torsion tests twist cylindrical specimens measuring shear stress-strain behavior, shear modulus, shear strength, and ductility through angle of twist to failure. These tests simulate service conditions for shafts, beams, and structural components, with failure typically initiating on the tensile side in bending or maximum shear planes in torsion..
ME 371: Gear forces: Gear forces arise from power transmission through meshing teeth, including tangential force (transmitting torque), radial force (separating gears), and for helical/bevel gears, axial thrust forces. Forces depend on transmitted torque, pitch radius, pressure angle, and helix angle. Force analysis determines bearing reactions, shaft bending moments, and required mounting rigidity. High forces can ca
ME 371: Friction drives (clutches, brake: Friction drives transmit torque or control motion through friction between contacting surfaces, including clutches (engaging/disengaging power transmission), brakes (dissipating kinetic energy), and traction drives (continuously variable transmission). Design considerations include required torque capacity (friction coefficient × normal force × radius), heat dissipation from sliding, wear life, ac
TAM 212: Dynamics of 2d motion of rigid: Rigid body dynamics in 2D applies Newton's second law for translation (ΣF = macm) and rotation (ΣMG = IGα), where ΣF is net force, macm is center of mass acceleration, ΣMG is net moment about center of mass, IG is mass moment of inertia, α is angular acceleration. These equations are independent and solved simultaneously. Mass moment of inertia I = ∫r²dm depends on mass distribution. Common proble
TAM 212: Kinematics of 2d motion of rigi: Rigid body kinematics in 2D describes translation and rotation without deformation. General plane motion combines translation of reference point (often center of mass) and rotation about axis perpendicular to plane. Position of any point P: rₚ = rₒ + rₚ/ₒ. Velocity: vₚ = vₒ + ω × rₚ/ₒ, where ω is angular velocity. Acceleration: aₚ = aₒ + α × rₚ/ₒ - ω²rₚ/ₒ, including tangential (α × r) and centripe
ME 340: Lagrange's equations: Lagrange's equations provide a systematic, energy-based approach to deriving equations of motion for complex mechanical systems. By defining the Lagrangian L = T - V (kinetic minus potential energy) and generalized coordinates qᵢ, the equations of motion follow from d/dt(∂L/∂q̇ᵢ) - ∂L/∂qᵢ = Qᵢ, where Qᵢ are non-conservative generalized forces. This method automatically accounts for constraint forc
PHYS 211: Center of mass: The center of mass is the average position of mass in a system, the point where the system's total mass can be considered concentrated for translational motion analysis. For a system of particles, it's the mass-weighted average of positions. The center of mass moves as if all external forces act on the total mass located there, simplifying analysis of complex systems like collisions, explosions, a
TAM 251: Differential equations: Differential equation method for beam deflection starts with EI d⁴y/dx⁴ = w(x), where w is distributed load, or EI d²y/dx² = M(x). Successive integration yields slope and deflection functions with integration constants determined by boundary conditions. For simple cases with constant EI, direct integration is efficient. Method provides exact solutions for deflection, slope, moment, and shear throu
TAM 412: Kinetic energy: Kinetic energy for rigid bodies includes both translational and rotational contributions: T = ½mv_cm² + ½ω·Iω, where the first term is center-of-mass translation and the second is rotation about the center of mass. The rotational term involves the inertia tensor and angular velocity. This energy decomposition simplifies analysis using the work-energy theorem and is central to Lagrangian mechanics
TAM 412: Euler-lagrange's equation of mo: The Euler-Lagrange equations provide systematic derivation of equations of motion from the Lagrangian L = T - V: d/dt(∂L/∂q̇ᵢ) - ∂L/∂qᵢ = Qᵢ, where qᵢ are generalized coordinates and Qᵢ are non-conservative generalized forces. For constrained systems, Lagrange multipliers λ_j enforce constraint equations: the augmented form includes additional terms. These equations automatically yield correct equ
TAM 445: Introduction to constitutive eq: Constitutive equations relate kinematic quantities (strain, rate of deformation) to kinetic quantities (stress), characterizing specific material behavior. Balance laws alone are insufficient - need material-specific relations. Requirements: (1) Material objectivity (frame-invariance), (2) material symmetry (isotropy, anisotropy), (3) thermodynamic admissibility. Examples: Hooke's law for elastic
TAM 445: Bodies, deformations, displacem: Body B is collection of material points X occupying reference region. Deformation χ maps B to current configuration: x = χ(X,t). Bijective mapping ensures no material interpenetration or voids. Displacement field u(X,t) = χ(X,t) - X measures motion from reference. Deformation gradient F = I + ∇u (using ∇ = ∂/∂X). Smooth deformations require F continuous and det(F) > 0 (no volume collapse). Large d
TAM 470: Systems with a finite number of: Discrete systems have finite degrees of freedom, leading to systems of ODEs. Examples: lumped parameter models, mass-spring chains, structural finite elements, modal analysis of continuous systems. Equations: Mq̈ + Cq̇ + Kq = f, where M (mass), C (damping), K (stiffness) are matrices, q is displacement vector. Modal analysis decomposes motion into natural modes (eigenvectors) oscillating at natura
TAM 445: Rigid deformations: Rigid deformation preserves distances between all material points - no stretching or distortion, only translation and rotation. Mathematically: x = c(t) + Q(t)·X, where c is translation, Q is proper orthogonal tensor (rotation) satisfying QᵀQ = I and det(Q) = 1. Deformation gradient F = Q (pure rotation). Material line elements: dx = Q·dX preserve length ‖dx‖ = ‖dX‖ and angles. Rigid motion repres
TAM 412: Small oscillations about positi: Analyzing motion near stable equilibria by Taylor-expanding potential energy to second order: V ≈ V₀ + ½ΣΣ(∂²V/∂qᵢ∂qⱼ)qᵢqⱼ, where equilibrium is at qᵢ = 0 and first derivatives vanish. The resulting linearized equations of motion become coupled harmonic oscillators, analyzed through eigenvalue problems to find normal modes and natural frequencies. This approach predicts small-amplitude vibrations
TAM 211: Distributed forces: Distributed forces are loads spread continuously over a line, area, or volume rather than concentrated at points. Common examples include weight of beams, hydrostatic pressure, wind loading, and soil pressure. Characterized by intensity w(x) (force per unit length/area/volume). Total force magnitude found by integration: FR = ∫w dA. Resultant location (centroid of load distribution) determined by:
TAM 210: Distributed forces: Distributed forces spread continuously over area or volume, rather than acting at single point. Examples: pressure, weight of beam, fluid forces, wind loading. Represented by intensity w(x) (force per unit length/area). Total force: F = ∫w(x)dx. Location of resultant: x̄ = ∫xw(x)dx / ∫w(x)dx (centroid of loading diagram). Common distributions: uniform (constant w), linear, parabolic. Replace distr
TAM 445: Volume-preserving deformations: Volume-preserving (isochoric) deformations maintain constant volume during motion: dv = dV, requiring Jacobian J = det(F) = 1. Material may distort but volume remains unchanged. Examples: incompressible fluids, rubber-like materials. Constraint det(F) = 1 reduces independent components of F. Pressure acts as Lagrange multiplier enforcing constraint. Decomposition F = J^(1/3)F̄ separates volumetric
ME 411: Deformation: Deformation describes changes in fluid element shape and size during motion. The rate of deformation tensor characterizes stretching and shearing rates of fluid parcels. Volumetric strain rate (divergence of velocity) gives rate of volume change per unit volume—zero for incompressible flow. Deviatoric (shear) deformation causes shape change without volume change. In Newtonian fluids, stress relates linearly to deformation rate through viscosity: τ_ij = μ(∂v_i/∂x_j + ∂v_j/∂x_i). Understanding deformation connects kinematics to dynamics (stresses and forces), enabling formulation of momentum equations for viscous flow. Deformation rate determines viscous dissipation, energy loss, and heat generation in flows..
TAM 251: Direct integration: Direct integration determines beam deflection by integrating load, shear, moment, slope, and deflection relationships sequentially. Starting from w(x): integrate to get V = ∫w dx, then M = ∫V dx, then EIθ = ∫M dx, finally EIy = ∫θ dx. Each integration introduces constant determined by boundary or continuity conditions. Method systematically builds from loading to deflection through all intermediat
ME 340: Boundary-value problems for cant: Beam vibration analysis requires solving the fourth-order PDE governing beam motion subject to boundary conditions at the ends. Cantilevered beams (fixed-free) have zero displacement and slope at the fixed end and zero moment and shear at the free end. Clamped-clamped beams (fixed-fixed) have zero displacement and slope at both ends. These boundary conditions profoundly affect natural frequencies
TAM 210: Statics and structural design: Statics principles applied to structural design ensure buildings, bridges, and machines safely support loads. Process: (1) identify loads (dead, live, wind, seismic), (2) structural analysis (forces, moments, reactions), (3) member sizing based on stress limits, (4) safety factors for uncertainties. Considerations: material properties (strength, stiffness), failure modes (yielding, buckling, fatig
ME 340: Simulations with a finite-elemen: Finite element analysis (FEA) discretizes continuous systems into finite elements (small regions) connected at nodes, converting PDEs into large systems of ODEs solvable computationally. For beam vibration, elements represent beam segments with displacement and rotation DOFs at each node. FEA captures complex geometries, material variations, and boundary conditions impractical for analytical solut
ME 340: Mode shapes and resonance: Mode shapes are the spatial deformation patterns associated with each natural frequency in multi-DOF and continuous systems. At resonance, when excitation frequency matches a natural frequency, the system vibrates in the corresponding mode shape with theoretically infinite amplitude (in undamped systems) or very large amplitude (with damping). Understanding mode shapes is critical for avoiding des
TAM 211: Trusses: Trusses are structural frameworks composed of straight members connected at joints, arranged in triangular patterns to efficiently resist loads through axial forces only. Members experience pure tension or compression, with no bending if loads apply only at joints and connections are idealized as frictionless pins. Common truss types include Pratt, Howe, Warren, and K-trusses, each optimized for d
TAM 251: Beam deflections: Beam deflection is lateral displacement from undeformed position under load. Governed by differential equation: EI d²y/dx² = M(x), relating curvature to bending moment. Solutions provide deflection y(x) and slope θ = dy/dx along beam. Boundary conditions (support constraints) determine integration constants. Methods include direct integration, superposition, moment-area, and energy methods. Deflec
TAM 251: Method of superposition: Superposition method determines beam deflection by combining solutions for simpler load cases. Total deflection equals sum of individual deflections: ytotal = y₁ + y₂ + y₃ + ... Uses tabulated solutions for standard cases (concentrated loads, distributed loads, moments on various beam configurations). Applicable only for linear elastic behavior where deflections are small. Efficient for complex lo
TAM 251: Design based on deflections: Deflection-based design limits beam deformation to acceptable levels for serviceability. Requirements: δmax ≤ δallow, commonly L/360 for floors, L/240 for roofs, where L is span. Calculate maximum deflection using appropriate method (integration, superposition, formulas). Required moment of inertia: I ≥ (load factor × Lⁿ)/(E × δallow), where n depends on loading type. Often governs design for long
TAM 251: Design of beams for structural: Beam design ensures adequate strength and stiffness for applied loads. Requirements: σmax ≤ σallow (bending stress), τmax ≤ τallow (shear stress), δmax ≤ δallow (deflection limit), often L/360 for buildings. Select section providing required section modulus S ≥ Mmax/σallow and moment of inertia I ≥ (load function)/δallow. Consider economy (minimum weight/cost), availability (standard sections), co
TAM 251: Stresses due to bending: Bending creates normal stresses varying linearly across beam depth: σ = -My/I, where M is bending moment, y is distance from neutral axis, I is second moment of area. Maximum stress at extreme fibers: σmax = Mc/I = M/S, where c is distance to extreme fiber, S = I/c is section modulus. Tension on one side, compression on other; zero at neutral axis (centroid for symmetric sections). Compressive str
TAM 251: Uniaxial loading and deformatio: Uniaxial loading involves force along single axis, creating uniform normal stress σ = P/A and strain ε = δ/L in simple members. For elastic materials, Hooke's law: σ = Eε, where E is Young's modulus (material stiffness). Poisson's ratio ν describes lateral contraction: εlateral = -νεaxial. Elongation δ = PL/AE for constant cross-section. Stress-strain curves characterize material behavior: elastic
TAM 251: Built-up beams: Built-up beams consist of multiple components fastened together (bolts, welds, adhesives) to create composite section. Examples include wooden beams with multiple planks, steel I-beams with welded plates, reinforced concrete. Analysis requires checking component stresses and connection capacity. Shear flow q = VQ/I determines fastener force per length. Fastener spacing s ≤ Fallow/q ensures adequat
TAM 251: States of plane stress and plan: Plane stress assumes stress components perpendicular to plane are negligible: σz = τxz = τyz = 0. Common in thin plates and shells where thickness dimension is small. Plane strain assumes strain perpendicular to plane is negligible: εz = γxz = γyz = 0, typical in long structures where length >> cross-section (dams, tunnels). Both simplify 3D problems to 2D analysis. Plane stress allows σx, σy, τxy
TAM 251: Design problems for combined st: Combined loading creates multiaxial stress states requiring sophisticated analysis. Examples: shaft under bending and torsion, pressure vessel with internal pressure and axial load. Design process: (1) determine stress components from each loading, (2) combine using superposition, (3) find principal stresses and maximum shear, (4) apply failure criterion (yield or fracture), (5) calculate safety f
ME 371: Demonstrate the strengths and we: Elastic Finite Element Analysis (FEA) predicts stress and deformation assuming linear elastic material behavior, valid for stresses below yield. Strengths include accuracy for most structural problems, computational efficiency, well-established methods, and extensive commercial software. Weaknesses include inability to capture plasticity, nonlinear geometry (large deformations), contact nonlineari
TAM 456: Theoretical analysis of represe: Theoretical analysis establishes baseline solutions for benchmark stress problems using analytical methods from elasticity theory. Representative problems include beams under various loadings, plates with holes (Kirsch solution), contact problems (Hertz theory), and stress concentrations. Closed-form solutions provide validation benchmarks for experimental measurements and numerical simulations. Understanding theoretical predictions enables identifying discrepancies in experiments, assessing measurement accuracy, and interpreting physical phenomena. Classical solutions reveal stress distribution characteristics, concentration factors, and boundary effects. This foundation bridges mechanics theory with experimental practice..
ME 340: Modeling of dynamic systems: Dynamic system modeling derives mathematical descriptions from physical principles. Process: (1) identify components and interactions, (2) apply conservation laws (mass, momentum, energy), (3) write governing equations, (4) linearize if needed, (5) validate against experiments. Lumped parameter models use ODEs (discrete components). Distributed models use PDEs (continuous). Multi-domain systems (m
ME 340: Dynamic simulation: Dynamic simulation numerically solves system equations to predict behavior over time. Process: (1) formulate model (ODEs/DAEs), (2) specify initial conditions and parameters, (3) select integration method (Runge-Kutta, ode45), (4) run simulation, (5) analyze results. Tools: MATLAB/Simulink, Python, Modelica. Enables: design validation, parameter studies, scenario analysis, control testing. Conside
ME 340: Principles of conservation - mas: Conservation principles form foundation for dynamic system modeling. Mass conservation: dm/dt = ṁᵢₙ - ṁₒᵤₜ (continuity equation). Energy conservation (first law): dE/dt = Q̇ - Ẇ + Σ(ṁh)ᵢₙ - Σ(ṁh)ₒᵤₜ, where Q̇ is heat transfer, Ẇ is power output, h is specific enthalpy. For closed systems: dE/dt = Q̇ - Ẇ. Mechanical energy: kinetic + potential. Momentum conservation: F = dp/dt. These principles gen
TAM 470: Introduction to computational m: Computational mechanics applies numerical methods to solve mechanical problems governed by differential equations. Bridges continuum mechanics theory with computer simulation. Enables analyzing complex geometries, nonlinear materials, and coupled phenomena intractable analytically. Major areas: computational solid mechanics (structural analysis, fracture, contact), computational fluid dynamics (CF
TAM 412: Free oscillations: Unforced motion resulting from initial conditions in conservative or damped systems. For linear systems, free oscillations are superpositions of normal modes with amplitudes and phases determined by initial conditions. In undamped systems, oscillations persist indefinitely at natural frequencies. Damping causes exponential decay, with decay rates determined by eigenvalue real parts. Analyzing free
TAM 470: Ordinary differential equations: Mechanical systems generate ODEs from governing laws. Examples: spring-mass-damper (mẍ + cẋ + kx = f), pendulum (θ̈ + (g/L)sinθ = 0), vibrating structures (Mü + Cu̇ + Ku = F), particle dynamics (mẍ = F). First-order systems: ẋ = f(x,t) arise from reducing higher-order equations or from inherently first-order physics. Initial value problems (IVPs) specify initial conditions. Stiff systems have wide
ME 481: Dynamical analysis and modeling: The application of mechanical principles and mathematical models to understand forces, torques, and energetics governing human movement. Analysis combines measured kinematics with body segment parameters (mass, inertia, dimensions) to calculate joint forces and moments using inverse dynamics, or solves forward dynamics predicting motion from applied forces. Models range from simple linked rigid bodies representing segments to complex musculoskeletal models including individual muscle force predictions. Applications include optimizing athletic technique, designing assistive devices and prosthetics, understanding injury mechanisms, evaluating surgical outcomes, and developing robotic systems mimicking biological movement. Understanding modeling assumptions, validation approaches, and computational methods enables interpretation of results and appropriate application..
TAM 412: Automated formulation of config: Systematic computational methods for deriving constraint equations from mechanical system descriptions, typically used in multibody dynamics software. Given joint types (revolute, prismatic, spherical, etc.) and connectivity, algorithms automatically generate holonomic and nonholonomic constraint equations. This automation enables rapid model development, reduces human error, and handles complex s
ME 481: Lagrangian mechanics: An alternative formulation of classical mechanics using energy methods rather than forces, expressing system dynamics through the Lagrangian function (kinetic minus potential energy) and deriving equations of motion using the principle of least action. For biomechanics, Lagrangian approaches elegantly handle complex multi-body systems with constraints (joints restricting relative motion), automatically incorporating constraint forces without explicit calculation. The method systematically generates equations of motion for systems with many degrees of freedom, facilitating analysis of complex movements like gymnastics, diving, or robotic systems. Understanding Lagrangian mechanics provides powerful mathematical tools for forward dynamics simulations and optimal control problems..
ME 481: Inverse dynamics: The computational method calculating net joint forces and moments from measured kinematics (positions, velocities, accelerations), ground reaction forces, and body segment properties using Newton-Euler equations working from distal to proximal segments. Inverse dynamics enables determination of joint loading during activities without directly measuring internal forces, assuming the body is a system of linked rigid segments. Applications include analyzing gait to identify abnormal loading patterns, evaluating surgical interventions by comparing pre- and post-operative joint moments, optimizing sports technique to maximize performance while minimizing injury risk, and validating musculoskeletal models. Accuracy depends on precise kinematics, accurate body segment parameters, and appropriate modeling assumptions..
ME 440: Simulation and animation of mult: Computational techniques for numerically solving multibody dynamics equations and visualizing resulting motion. Simulation integrates differential equations of motion forward in time using numerical methods (Runge-Kutta, etc.), while animation renders body positions and orientations as visual sequences. These tools enable virtual prototyping, verifying mechanism designs before fabrication, studying complex scenarios impractical experimentally, and communicating designs to stakeholders. Modern software packages automate equation formulation, efficient numerical solution, and realistic 3D visualization for systems from small mechanisms to vehicles and robots..
ME 440: Configuration and motion constra: Constraints are mathematical conditions restricting possible positions (configuration constraints) or velocities (motion constraints) of mechanical systems. Holonomic constraints relate coordinates algebraically and can be integrated (like a ball constrained to a surface). Nonholonomic constraints involve velocities that cannot be integrated to position constraints (like no-slip rolling). Constraints reduce the system's effective degrees of freedom and generate constraint forces. Understanding constraints is essential for correctly formulating equations of motion and designing mechanisms with desired motion characteristics..
ME 440: Mechanism design solutions: Systematic approaches to designing mechanical systems that transform input motion or force into desired output motion or force. This involves kinematic synthesis (determining link lengths and joint types to achieve motion requirements), force analysis (ensuring adequate strength and actuation), and optimization to meet multiple objectives. Common design methods include graphical techniques, analytical approaches using kinematic equations, and computational optimization. Successful mechanism design balances functionality, manufacturability, cost, reliability, and performance constraints..
TAM 210: Trusses: Trusses are structures composed of slender members (bars) connected at joints (pins), forming triangular units. Assumptions: members are two-force members (axial forces only), loads applied only at joints, joints are frictionless pins. Members in tension (pulling) or compression (pushing). Trusses efficiently carry loads through internal axial forces. Common types: Pratt, Howe, Warren. Analysis de
TAM 211: Methods of joint and sections: Methods of joints and sections are systematic techniques for analyzing truss internal forces. Method of Joints isolates each joint as a free-body diagram with equilibrium equations ΣFₓ=0 and ΣFᵧ=0, solving for member forces sequentially starting where two or fewer unknowns exist. Method of Sections cuts through members of interest, isolating a portion and applying all three equilibrium equations (
TAM 210: Methods of joint and sections: Two methods analyze truss internal forces. Method of Joints: isolate each joint as FBD, apply equilibrium ΣFₓ=0, ΣFᵧ=0. Start at joint with ≤2 unknowns. Proceeds joint-by-joint. Method of Sections: cut through members of interest, isolate portion as FBD, apply all three equilibrium equations. Efficient for finding specific member forces without solving entire truss. Both yield internal member forc
TAM 251: Buckling of columns: Buckling is sudden lateral deflection instability of slender columns under axial compression when critical load Pcr is reached. Unlike material failure (yielding/fracture), buckling is geometric instability where straight configuration becomes unstable. Critical load depends on stiffness EI, length L, and end conditions: Pcr = π²EI/(KL)² where K is effective length factor (K=1 for pinned-pinned, K
TAM 251: Design of columns: Column design accounts for buckling and material failure. Process: (1) determine loads and effective length KL considering end conditions, (2) calculate slenderness ratio λ = KL/r, (3) use appropriate formula: Euler (long columns), empirical formulas like AISC equations (intermediate), yielding (short columns). Design equations often incorporate safety factors and account for imperfections. Select
TAM 324: Tension test: A fundamental mechanical test where a specimen is subjected to uniaxial tensile loading while measuring force and elongation to determine stress-strain behavior, yield strength, ultimate tensile strength, modulus of elasticity, ductility, and toughness. Standard specimens with reduced gage sections are gripped in a testing machine, loaded at controlled strain rates, and monitored until fracture. The test produces engineering stress-strain curves (using original dimensions) and true stress-strain curves (using instantaneous dimensions), revealing elastic modulus from initial slope, yield point where plastic deformation begins, strain hardening behavior, necking onset, and fracture characteristics including percent elongation and reduction in area..
TAM 324: Fracture mechanisms: The processes by which materials separate into two or more pieces under stress, encompassing ductile fracture through void coalescence, brittle fracture through cleavage or intergranular separation, and mixed-mode failures. Fracture mechanics characterizes crack initiation, propagation, and instability using stress intensity factors, fracture toughness, and energy release rates. Mechanisms depend on material microstructure, temperature, loading rate, and stress state. Understanding crack tip stress concentration, plastic zone development, and critical crack sizes enables design against fracture through material selection, damage-tolerant design, inspection intervals, and safe-life or fail-safe structural concepts..
TAM 324: Impact; fracture-toughness test: Evaluation of material behavior under rapid loading rates and measurement of resistance to crack propagation. Impact tests (Charpy V-notch, Izod) measure energy absorbed during fracture of notched specimens struck by a pendulum, revealing ductile-brittle transition temperature and notch sensitivity. Fracture toughness tests measure critical stress intensity factor (KIc) or J-integral representing resistance to crack extension under quasi-static loading, using pre-cracked specimens with precise geometries. Toughness differs from strength - tough materials resist crack propagation by absorbing energy through plastic deformation. Testing reveals temperature and rate effects, guiding material selection for impact resistance and damage tolerance..
ME 371: Design against fracture: Design against fracture prevents catastrophic separation by ensuring components can tolerate crack-like flaws without sudden propagation. Fracture mechanics uses stress intensity factor (K) comparing applied stress and crack size to material toughness (KIc). Linear elastic fracture mechanics (LEFM) assumes brittle behavior; elastic-plastic fracture mechanics handles ductile tearing. Design approac
TAM 456: Review of strength of materials: Strength of materials (mechanics of materials) fundamentals include: stress (σ = F/A), strain (ε = ΔL/L), Hooke's law (σ = Eε), axial loading, torsion (τ = Tρ/J, φ = TL/GJ), bending (σ = -My/I), shear in beams (τ = VQ/Ib), combined loading, stress transformation, Mohr's circle, principal stresses, yield criteria, and failure theories. This foundation enables understanding experimental methods for measuring and analyzing stresses in actual structures and components. Review solidifies theoretical knowledge before applying experimental techniques like strain gaging, photoelasticity, and digital image correlation..
ME 340: Physical experiments with a cant: This laboratory provides hands-on experience with continuous system dynamics using a physical cantilevered beam (fixed at one end, free at the other). Students excite the beam and measure vibration to identify natural frequencies and visualize mode shapes experimentally. Techniques include impact testing, frequency sweeps, and modal testing with accelerometers or strain gauges. Comparing measured
TAM 251: Two-dimensional elastic stress-: For plane stress: εx = (σx - νσy)/E, εy = (σy - νσx)/E, γxy = τxy/G where E is Young's modulus, ν is Poisson's ratio, G = E/2(1+ν) is shear modulus. For plane strain: similar but with modified elastic constants E' = E/(1-ν²), ν' = ν/(1-ν). These constitutive relations connect stress to strain for isotropic linear elastic materials. Inverse relations give stress from strain. Essential for solving e
ME 371: Multiaxial stress: Multiaxial stress occurs when components experience simultaneous stresses in multiple directions (normal and shear), requiring tensor representation and appropriate failure criteria. Real components typically experience complex 3D stress states from combined loading (tension, torsion, bending, pressure). Principal stresses are the extreme normal stresses on planes with zero shear stress. Failure p
TAM 456: Generalized hooke's law: Generalized Hooke's law relates stress and strain tensors for 3D elastic behavior: εᵢⱼ = Sᵢⱼₖₗσₖₗ or σᵢⱼ = Cᵢⱼₖₗεₖₗ, where S is compliance tensor (inverse of stiffness C). For isotropic materials: εᵢⱼ = [(1+ν)/E]σᵢⱼ - (ν/E)δᵢⱼσₖₖ. In experimental context, enables calculating stresses from measured strains. Plane stress (thin specimens): σz = τxz = τyz = 0 gives εx = (σx - νσy)/E, εy = (σy - νσx)/E, γxy = τxy/G. Requires knowing E, ν, and G. Anisotropic materials need more constants. Foundation for interpreting strain gage data..
TAM 211: Method of composite parts: Method of composite parts determines properties (centroid, area, volume, moment of inertia) of complex shapes by subdividing into simple standard shapes with known properties. Total property equals sum of individual contributions: x̄total = Σx̄ᵢAᵢ / ΣAᵢ for centroids. Negative areas handle cutouts or holes by subtracting their contributions. Standard shapes (rectangles, triangles, circles, semicir
TAM 251: Double integration: Double integration method solves EI d²y/dx² = M(x) by integrating twice to obtain slope θ = dy/dx and deflection y(x). First integration: EI dy/dx = ∫M dx + C₁. Second integration: EI y = ∫(∫M dx) dx + C₁x + C₂. Constants C₁ and C₂ determined from boundary conditions (deflection and slope at supports). Method is straightforward for beams with simple moment functions. Each load region may require s
ME 330: Toughness and impact: Toughness measures energy absorption before fracture, combining strength and ductility. Charpy and Izod impact tests measure energy absorbed breaking notched specimens with pendulum, identifying ductile-to-brittle transition temperature (DBTT) where behavior changes. Face-centered cubic metals remain ductile at low temperatures; body-centered cubic (steels) show DBTT. Impact toughness is critical
ME 371: Design against fatigue: Design against fatigue prevents progressive damage from cyclic loading causing crack nucleation, growth, and eventual fracture at stresses below yield strength. Analysis uses S-N curves (stress versus cycles to failure), modified Goodman or Gerber diagrams (combining mean and alternating stress), and Miner's rule (cumulative damage). Design factors affecting fatigue include surface finish, size, s
ME 431: Fracture analysis: Fracture analysis applies fracture mechanics determining whether cracks propagate catastrophically or remain stable, enabling safe operation with crack-like defects. Analysis compares stress intensity factor (K) from applied loads and crack geometry to material fracture toughness (KIc), ensuring K
TAM 210: Coulomb friction: Coulomb (dry) friction is resistance force between contacting surfaces, opposing relative motion or impending motion. Static friction fs ≤ μsN prevents motion until threshold; kinetic friction fk = μkN opposes sliding motion, where μs (static) and μk (kinetic) are friction coefficients, N is normal force. Static friction adjusts up to maximum: fs,max = μsN. Friction force direction opposes motion
PHYS 211: Friction: Friction is a contact force opposing relative motion or attempted motion between surfaces. Static friction prevents motion up to a maximum value proportional to the normal force, while kinetic friction opposes sliding with magnitude also proportional to normal force but typically smaller. Friction coefficients (μ_s and μ_k) characterize surface interactions. Understanding friction is crucial for a
TAM 211: Hydrostatic pressure: Hydrostatic pressure is force per unit area exerted by fluid at rest, increasing linearly with depth: p = ρgh, where ρ is fluid density, g is gravity, h is depth. Pressure acts perpendicular to surfaces (normal direction), with magnitude depending only on depth, not surface orientation. Total force on submerged surface: F = ∫p dA, acting at center of pressure (below centroid for vertical surfaces)
PHYS 211: Fluid statics: Fluid statics studies fluids at rest, focusing on pressure variation with depth, buoyancy, and forces on submerged surfaces. Pascal's principle states pressure applied to a confined fluid transmits equally throughout. Archimedes' principle states buoyant force equals the weight of displaced fluid. Understanding hydrostatic pressure (P = ρgh), pressure measurement, and floating/sinking conditions i
ME 340: A quarter-car model: The quarter-car model simplifies vehicle suspension analysis by representing one wheel, suspension, and one-quarter of the vehicle mass as a two-degree-of-freedom system. The unsprung mass (wheel and suspension components) connects to ground through a tire spring, while the sprung mass (vehicle body portion) connects to the unsprung mass through the suspension spring and damper. This model capture
ME 340: Numerical experiments with a mec: This computational laboratory uses simulation to explore mechanical suspension system behavior, typically modeling a vehicle's spring-mass-damper system. Students conduct numerical experiments varying parameters like spring stiffness, damping coefficient, and mass to observe effects on ride comfort, handling, and vibration isolation. By comparing responses to different road inputs (bumps, potholes
ME 310: Boundary layers: A boundary layer is the thin region adjacent to a solid surface where viscous effects are significant and velocity transitions from zero at the wall (no-slip condition) to the free-stream value. Boundary layers can be laminar (smooth, orderly flow) or turbulent (chaotic fluctuations), with transition depending on Reynolds number. Boundary layer thickness, displacement thickness, and momentum thick
TAM 335: Pressure: Pressure is normal force per unit area exerted by fluid on surfaces or within the fluid. Absolute pressure is measured relative to perfect vacuum; gage pressure is relative to atmospheric pressure; vacuum pressure indicates pressure below atmospheric. Pascal's law states that pressure at a point acts equally in all directions. Pressure transmits force through fluids enabling hydraulic systems to multiply force. Units include Pa (N/m²), psi (lbf/in²), bar, atm, and mm Hg. Understanding pressure is fundamental to fluid mechanics: it drives flow, creates forces on structures, and governs fluid behavior. Proper pressure measurement and interpretation are essential for engineering analysis and design..
TAM 335: Drag: Drag is the force component parallel to flow direction opposing body motion through fluid, comprising friction drag (surface shear) and pressure drag (pressure difference between front and back). Friction drag dominates on streamlined bodies with attached flow; pressure drag dominates on bluff bodies with separation and large wakes. Drag coefficient C_D = D/(½ρU²A) enables comparison across geometries and conditions. C_D varies with Reynolds number, body shape, and surface roughness. Drag reduction strategies include streamlining, boundary layer control, and surface treatments. Applications span aerospace (aircraft drag minimization), automotive (fuel efficiency), sports (cycling, swimming), and civil engineering (wind loads on structures)..
TAM 335: Fluid statics: Fluid statics analyzes fluids at rest or moving uniformly (no shear stress). The fundamental equation dP/dz = -ρg relates pressure change to elevation in static fluids. For constant density (incompressible), pressure increases linearly with depth: P = P₀ + ρgh. Applications include calculating hydrostatic forces on dams, gates, and submerged surfaces; determining buoyancy forces (Archimedes' principle); and stability of floating/submerged bodies. Manometers measure pressure using hydrostatic principles. Understanding fluid statics is essential for hydraulic system design, naval architecture, dam engineering, and any application involving fluid pressure distribution. It provides foundation before tackling complexities of fluid motion..
TAM 335: Total drag transition: Total drag on a body transitions from being dominated by skin friction (laminar) to including significant pressure drag (turbulent with possible separation). For streamlined bodies at low Re, drag is primarily skin friction from laminar boundary layer. As Re increases, boundary layer transition increases skin friction but delays separation, potentially reducing total drag (drag crisis on spheres/cylinders). For bluff bodies, form drag dominates. The drag coefficient C_D = D/(½ρU²A) varies with Re showing characteristic transitions. Understanding drag transition enables design optimization: trip wires on golf balls reduce drag by triggering early transition; dimples serve similar purpose. Predicting total drag requires accounting for both friction and pressure components..
TAM 335: Boundary layers: Boundary layer is the thin region near solid surfaces where viscous effects are significant and velocity transitions from zero at wall (no-slip condition) to free-stream value. Thickness δ is defined where velocity reaches 99% of free-stream. Boundary layers can be laminar (orderly, low friction) or turbulent (chaotic, high friction), with transition at critical Reynolds number. Displacement thickness δ* quantifies effective body thickness increase due to velocity deficit. Momentum thickness θ relates to drag. Boundary layer concept simplifies analysis by localizing viscous effects, enabling separation of inviscid outer flow and viscous inner layer. Understanding boundary layers is fundamental to aerodynamics, heat transfer, and drag prediction..
TAM 335: Forces on plane and submerged s: Hydrostatic forces on submerged surfaces arise from pressure distribution acting normal to surfaces. The resultant force F_R = ρgĀh̄A equals pressure at centroid times area. The center of pressure (where resultant acts) differs from centroid, located deeper by I_c/Āy where I_c is second moment of area. For vertical surfaces, force increases as depth²; for inclined surfaces, projection affects force magnitude. Applications include dam design, pressure vessel analysis, gates, and bulkheads in ships. Understanding how to calculate magnitude, direction, and location of hydrostatic forces enables structural design of water-retaining structures ensuring adequate strength against fluid pressure..
TAM 335: Fluid dynamics: Fluid dynamics analyzes forces and resulting motion in flowing fluids, applying Newton's second law to fluid elements. Key equations include continuity (mass conservation), momentum equation (force balance), and energy equation (first law of thermodynamics). The Navier-Stokes equations govern viscous flow; Euler equations govern inviscid flow. Dimensionless numbers (Reynolds, Froude, Mach) characterize flow regimes and enable similarity scaling. Fluid dynamics explains pressure variation in flowing fluids, forces on objects in flows, and energy losses. Applications span all engineering: aerodynamics, hydraulics, turbomachinery, and biological flows. Understanding fluid dynamics enables prediction and control of fluid behavior for engineering design..
ME 370: Impact dynamics: Impact dynamics analyzes collisions between mechanism components or with external objects, involving rapid force exchanges over short time periods. Analysis uses impulse-momentum principles, coefficient of restitution (energy loss during impact), and sometimes energy methods. Impacts can be elastic (energy conserved), plastic (maximum energy loss), or partially elastic. Understanding impact dynami
TAM 412: Frictional contact: Analysis of systems with friction forces at contact surfaces, including Coulomb friction (F_friction ≤ μN, opposing motion), stiction (preventing motion below threshold), and rolling resistance. Friction introduces nonlinearity and potential discontinuities (stiction transitions, stick-slip motion), complicating analysis. Contact dynamics requires determining contact states (sliding, sticking, sep
TAM 324: Yielding under stress; ductile: The transition from elastic to plastic deformation when applied stress exceeds the yield strength, causing permanent shape change, followed by continued deformation leading to ductile failure through necking and eventual rupture with significant plastic strain. Yielding mechanisms involve dislocation motion, grain boundary sliding, and void nucleation. Ductile failure progresses through uniform plastic deformation, necking localization, void nucleation at inclusions, void growth and coalescence forming cracks, and final shear fracture creating cup-and-cone fracture surfaces. Engineering applications exploit ductility for energy absorption in crash structures and forming operations while designing to prevent unexpected yielding in service..
ME 371: Fluid film lubrication: Fluid film lubrication separates surfaces with pressurized fluid film preventing direct contact, providing extremely low friction and wear. Hydrodynamic lubrication generates pressure through converging wedge geometry in relative motion (journal bearings, thrust bearings). Hydrostatic lubrication supplies pressurized fluid externally, supporting loads even at zero speed. Analysis uses Reynolds equ
ME 440: Rolling motion: Rolling motion combines translation and rotation such that contact points have zero instantaneous velocity relative to the surface (no-slip condition). This constraint couples linear and angular velocities: for a wheel of radius r, v = ωr. Rolling motion appears in wheels, gears, bearings, and cylinders on inclined planes. Understanding rolling requires analyzing the instantaneous center of rotation, friction requirements to prevent slipping, and energy distribution between translational and rotational kinetic energy. The no-slip constraint reduces degrees of freedom, simplifying analysis of wheeled vehicles and mechanisms..
TAM 445: Newtonian fluids: Newtonian fluids have stress linearly proportional to rate of deformation: σ = -pI + λ(tr D)I + 2μD, where λ and μ are viscosity coefficients (Lamé parameters for fluids). Incompressible Newtonian: σ = -pI + 2μD with ∇·v = 0. Navier-Stokes equations: ρDv/Dt = -∇p + μ∇²v + ρb (incompressible). Dynamic viscosity μ resists shearing; bulk viscosity relates to compression. Most common fluids (water, ai
ME 461: Response and control of electro-: Analyzing and controlling systems combining electrical and mechanical components like DC motors, solenoids, and piezo actuators where electrical inputs produce mechanical outputs through electromagnetic or piezoelectric coupling. Modeling includes electrical dynamics (L-R circuits), electromechanical coupling (torque proportional to current, back-EMF proportional to velocity), and mechanical dynamics (inertia-friction-spring). Response analysis reveals multiple time scales and coupled behavior. Control design addresses both electrical and mechanical limitations including current limits, saturation, resonances, and friction. Applications include precision motion control in robotics, disk drives, machine tools, and automotive actuators requiring coordinated electrical-mechanical control..
TAM 445: Euler's laws: Euler's laws extend Newton's laws to continua. First law (linear momentum balance): d/dt ∫v ρv dv = ∫s t dS + ∫v ρb dv, where t is surface traction, b is body force. Localized using transport and divergence theorems: ρDv/Dt = ∇·σ + ρb (Cauchy's equation of motion). Second law (angular momentum balance): d/dt ∫v x×ρv dv = ∫s x×t dS + ∫v x×ρb dv. Localizes to stress symmetry: σ = σᵀ (in absence of c
TAM 445: Rate of deformation and vortici: Velocity gradient L = ∇v decomposes into symmetric rate of deformation D = sym(L) = (L+Lᵀ)/2 and antisymmetric vorticity (spin) W = skew(L) = (L-Lᵀ)/2. Rate of deformation D measures stretching and shearing rates. Vorticity W measures local rotation rate, related to angular velocity ω by W = -ω×. In component form: Dᵢⱼ = (∂vᵢ/∂xⱼ + ∂vⱼ/∂xᵢ)/2, Wᵢⱼ = (∂vᵢ/∂xⱼ - ∂vⱼ/∂xᵢ)/2. Vorticity vector: ωᵢ = εᵢ
TAM 445: Kinematics: Kinematics describes motion and deformation of continua without considering forces. Maps material points X (reference configuration) to current positions x = χ(X,t) (current configuration). Displacement u = x - X. Deformation gradient F = ∂x/∂X = ∂χ/∂X relates material to spatial line elements: dx = F·dX. Jacobian J = det(F) gives volume ratio dv/dV. Velocity v = ∂χ/∂t (material derivative), accel
TAM 251: Euler theory: Euler's theory derives critical buckling load for ideal elastic columns. Assumes: perfectly straight column, axial load through centroid, elastic material, small deflections. Differential equation: EI d²y/dx² + Py = 0. Solution gives sine wave deflection with critical load Pcr = π²EI/L² for pinned-pinned column. Critical stress: σcr = π²E/(L/r)², where r = √(I/A) is radius of gyration. Theory vali
TAM 251: Torsion of circular shafts and: Torsion involves twisting of members under applied torque. For circular shafts, shear stress varies linearly from zero at center to maximum at surface: τmax = Tρ/J, where T is torque, ρ is radius, J is polar moment of inertia. Angle of twist: φ = TL/GJ, where G is shear modulus, L is length. For solid circular shaft: J = πd⁴/32; hollow: J = π(do⁴-di⁴)/32. Thin-walled tubes: τ = T/(2At), where A is
TAM 251: Design of shafts for power tran: Power transmission shafts design involves sizing for torque, stress, twist angle, and critical speed. Power-torque-speed relationship: P = Tω = 2πnT, where P is power (W), T is torque (N·m), ω is angular velocity (rad/s), n is rotational speed (rev/s). Design requirements: τmax ≤ τallow (strength) and φ ≤ φallow (stiffness). Diameter from strength: d ≥ ∛(16T/πτallow) for solid shaft. Critical spee
ME 310: Angular momentum: The angular momentum equation for a control volume relates torques to rotational motion of fluid: the sum of torques equals the rate of angular momentum change plus net angular momentum flux. Written as Στ = ∂/∂t(∫r×ρVdV) + ∫r×ρV(V⋅n)dA, where r is position vector from the axis of rotation. This principle is fundamental for analyzing rotating fluid machinery like turbines, pumps, compressors, and
ME 370: Rotating machinery: Rotating machinery encompasses devices with primary rotating components: turbines, pumps, motors, generators, compressors, and machine tools. Analysis includes understanding rotordynamics (critical speeds, whirl, vibration), balancing (static and dynamic), bearing loads, power transmission, and efficiency. Rotating machinery requires attention to centrifugal effects, gyroscopic moments, torsional
ME 371: Design and construct a compact m: Designing compact power transmission systems requires integrating multiple components (motor, gears, shafts, bearings, couplings) within tight space constraints while meeting power, speed, efficiency, and reliability requirements. Design challenges include heat dissipation in confined spaces, maintaining alignment with close tolerances, selecting compact components (planetary gears, high-pole moto
ME 371: Gears and gear trains: Gears transmit power and motion between shafts through meshing teeth, providing precise speed ratios, high efficiency, and compact packaging. Gear trains are systems of multiple gears achieving desired speed ratios or multiple outputs. Common configurations include simple (one stage), compound (multiple stages on common shafts), planetary (central, ring, and planet gears), and differential (two in
MATH 231: Area of a surface of revolutio: Surface area of revolution calculates the area of a surface formed by rotating a curve around an axis. For rotation about the x-axis: S = ∫[a,b] 2πy√(1 + [dy/dx]²)dx, where 2πy represents the circumference at each point and √(1 + [dy/dx]²)dx is the arc length element. Similar formulas exist for rotation about the y-axis or parametric curves. This integral sums the lateral surface areas of infinite
TAM 251: Mohr's circle representations: Mohr's circle is a graphical method for visualizing stress transformation in two dimensions. Circle is plotted with normal stress σ as horizontal axis and shear stress τ as vertical axis. Each point on circle represents stress state on a particular plane orientation. Center at (σavg, 0) with radius R = √[(σx-σy)²/4 + τxy²]. Principal stresses are circle's rightmost and leftmost points (maximum and
TAM 445: Polar decompositions: Polar decomposition uniquely factors deformation gradient F into rotation R and stretch U or V: F = RU (right stretch) or F = VR (left stretch), where R is orthogonal (RᵀR = I), U and V are symmetric positive-definite.
