U = √(FᵀF) is right stretch tensor, V = √(FFᵀ) is left stretch tensor, related by V = RURᵀ. Decomposition separates rigid rotation from pure deformation. Principal stretches λᵢ (eig
MATH 231: Polar coordinates: Polar coordinates (r, θ) specify points using distance r from origin and angle θ from positive x-axis, related to Cartesian coordinates by x = r·cos(θ) and y = r·sin(θ). Curves in polar form r = f(θ) include circles, spirals, rose curves, and cardioids. Polar coordinates naturally describe curves with radial symmetry and rotational features. Converting between Cartesian and polar forms uses the re
MATH 241: Polar changes of coordinates: Polar coordinate substitution in double integrals uses x = r cos(θ), y = r sin(θ), transforming ∫∫_R f(x,y) dA to ∫∫ f(r cos θ, r sin θ) r dr dθ. The extra factor r is the Jacobian accounting for area distortion. Polar coordinates simplify integrals over circular regions, sectors, and when integrand involves x² + y². Limits: r typically ranges from 0 or an inner radius to outer radius, θ ranges ov
MATH 241: Cylindrical and spherical coor: Cylindrical coordinates (r, θ, z) extend polar coordinates to 3D: x = r cos(θ), y = r sin(θ), z = z, useful for regions with circular symmetry about the z-axis. Spherical coordinates (ρ, θ, φ) use distance from origin ρ, azimuthal angle θ, and polar angle φ: x = ρ sin(φ) cos(θ), y = ρ sin(φ) sin(θ), z = ρ cos(φ), ideal for spherical regions. Both systems simplify integrals over certain geometries,
MATH 241: Integrating in cylindrical and: Integration in cylindrical coordinates: ∫∫∫_E f(x,y,z) dV = ∫∫∫ f(r cos θ, r sin θ, z) r dr dθ dz with limits describing E in cylindrical coordinates. In spherical: ∫∫∫_E f(x,y,z) dV = ∫∫∫ f(ρ sin φ cos θ, ρ sin φ sin θ, ρ cos φ) ρ² sin φ dρ dφ dθ. The Jacobian factors (r or ρ² sin φ) are crucial. These coordinate systems dramatically simplify integrals over cylinders, cones, spheres, and other sy
MATH 241: Integrating along a curve: Line integrals compute accumulated quantities along curves. The scalar line integral ∫_C f ds integrates scalar function f along curve C with respect to arc length, measuring total of f distributed along the curve. For vector field F, the line integral ∫_C F·dr measures work done by F moving along C, computed as ∫[a,b] F(r(t))·r'(t) dt. Applications include computing mass of curved wires, work aga
MATH 231: Areas and lengths in polar coo: Area in polar coordinates for region bounded by r = f(θ) from θ = α to θ = β is A = (1/2)∫[α,β] [f(θ)]²dθ, derived by summing areas of circular sectors. Arc length of polar curve r = f(θ) is L = ∫[α,β] √[r² + (dr/dθ)²]dθ, using the parametric arc length formula with x = r·cos(θ), y = r·sin(θ). These formulas account for the polar coordinate geometry where area elements are sectors and distance ele
MATH 441: Polar coordinates: Converting Cartesian system dx/dt = f(x,y), dy/dt = g(x,y) to polar coordinates (r,θ) where x = r cos θ, y = r sin θ yields dr/dt = (xf + yg)/r and dθ/dt = (xg - yf)/r². Polar form simplifies radially symmetric systems and reveals rotational structure. If dr/dt depends only on r (uncoupled), radial and angular motions separate. Useful for identifying limit cycles (closed orbits where dr/dt = 0 at r = r₀), spiral structure, and central force problems. Polar coordinates naturally describe systems with circular symmetry..
MATH 231: Arc length: Arc length calculates the distance along a curve from one point to another using the formula L = ∫[a,b] √(1 + [f'(x)]²)dx for y = f(x), or L = ∫[a,b] √([dx/dt]² + [dy/dt]²)dt for parametric curves. The formula derives from approximating the curve with many tiny straight line segments and taking the limit as segment size approaches zero. Arc length integrals often require numerical methods or compu
MATH 241: Linear change of coordinates: Linear change of coordinates uses linear transformation to convert integration from (x,y) to (u,v): x = au + bv, y = cu + dv. The change of variables formula ∫∫_R f(x,y) dx dy = ∫∫_S f(x(u,v), y(u,v)) |J| du dv uses the Jacobian J = ∂(x,y)/∂(u,v) = |det([∂x/∂u, ∂x/∂v; ∂y/∂u, ∂y/∂v])|. This generalizes polar coordinates and simplifies integrals when the transformed region S has simpler boundaries t
MATH 241: Triple integrals: Triple integrals ∫∫∫_E f(x,y,z) dV integrate over three-dimensional regions E, computed as iterated integrals in six possible orders (choosing order strategically based on region boundaries). When f = 1, the integral gives volume of E; when f = δ(x,y,z) is density, it gives mass. Applications include finding volumes, masses, centers of mass, moments of inertia in 3D solids, and computing average v
MATH 241: Planes in 3-space: A plane in 3D space is a flat two-dimensional surface extending infinitely, uniquely determined by a point and a normal vector (perpendicular to the plane). The plane equation is a(x-x₀) + b(y-y₀) + c(z-z₀) = 0 or ax + by + cz = d, where
MATH 241: Green's theorem: Green's theorem relates a line integral around a simple closed curve in the plane to a double integral over the region it encloses. It states that the circulation of a vector field around a closed curve equals the double integral of the curl (or a related expression) over the enclosed region. This fundamental theorem connects boundary integrals to interior properties and is essential for vector ca
MATH 241: Arclength: Arc length of a space curve r(t) from t = a to t = b is L = ∫[a,b] ||r'(t)|| dt = ∫[a,b] √[(x'(t))² + (y'(t))² + (z'(t))²] dt. This generalizes 2D arc length to three dimensions, measuring distance traveled along the curve. Arc length parameterization uses s = ∫[a,t] ||r'(u)|| du to reparameterize by distance traveled, giving unit-speed curves where ||r'(s)|| = 1. Arc length is fundamental for com
MATH 241: Integrating over triangles: Integrating over triangular regions involves setting up double integrals with limits describing the triangle. For triangle with vertices, express one variable's limits as constants (outer integral) and the other variable's limits as linear functions of the outer variable (inner integral). Alternatively, parameterize the triangle using barycentric coordinates. This extends rectangular integration r
MATH 241: Computing integrals over recta: Double integrals over rectangular regions R = [a,b] × [c,d] are computed using iterated integrals: ∫∫_R f(x,y) dA = ∫[a,b] ∫[c,d] f(x,y) dy dx or equivalently with reversed order. Fubini's Theorem guarantees that for continuous functions, the order of integration doesn't matter. The double integral represents volume under the surface z = f(x,y) above rectangle R when f ≥ 0, or signed volume when f
MATH 241: Application of double integral: Double integral applications include: computing areas (∫∫_R 1 dA), volumes between surfaces (∫∫_R [f(x,y) - g(x,y)] dA), masses of laminas (∫∫_R δ(x,y) dA with density δ), centers of mass (using moments M_x = ∫∫ y δ dA), moments of inertia, surface areas (with additional ds factor), probabilities (joint probability density functions), and average values of functions over regions. These application
MATH 241: Nonlinear change of coordinate: Nonlinear coordinate transformations use general functions x = x(u,v), y = y(u,v) to change integration variables, with Jacobian J = |∂(x,y)/∂(u,v)| accounting for area distortion that now varies with position. Examples include polar coordinates (nonlinear due to trigonometric functions) and other curvilinear systems. The formula ∫∫_R f(x,y) dA = ∫∫_S f(x(u,v), y(u,v)) |J(u,v)| du dv generalizes t
MATH 231: Trigonometric integrals: Trigonometric integrals involve products and powers of trigonometric functions like ∫sin^m(x)cos^n(x)dx. Strategies depend on the powers: if either power is odd, use substitution with the other function; if both are even, use power-reducing identities (sin²x = (1-cos2x)/2). For products like ∫sin(mx)cos(nx)dx, use product-to-sum formulas. Integrals of tan, sec, and their powers require specific te
MATH 221: Areas between curves: The area between two curves f(x) and g(x) from x=a to x=b, where f(x) ≥ g(x), is computed as ∫[a,b] [f(x) - g(x)]dx. This extends single-function area calculations by subtracting the lower curve from the upper curve. For curves intersecting multiple times, split the integral at intersection points and determine which function is greater on each subinterval. When curves are better described as func
MATH 221: Volumes: Volumes of solids of revolution are computed by rotating regions about axes and integrating cross-sectional areas. The disk method uses V = ∫[a,b] π[r(x)]²dx when rotating around the x-axis, where r(x) is the radius function. The washer method handles regions between two curves: V = ∫[a,b] π[R(x)² - r(x)²]dx, where R is outer radius and r is inner radius (creating a washer-shaped cross-section). S
MATH 221: Areas and distance: Areas under curves and distances traveled are computed by summing infinitely many infinitesimally small rectangles or increments, motivating the definite integral. The area under f(x) from a to b is approximated using Riemann sums: Σf(x_i)Δx, which becomes exact as Δx→0, yielding ∫[a,b] f(x)dx. Similarly, distance traveled equals ∫velocity dt, and net change equals ∫(rate of change)dt. These geome
CHEM 102: Work & enthalpy: Work in thermodynamics is energy transfer through mechanical processes, particularly pressure-volume work in gases (w = -PΔV). Enthalpy (H) is a state function representing heat content at constant pressure, defined as H = U + PV where U is internal energy. Enthalpy change (ΔH) equals heat transferred at constant pressure, making it convenient for measuring heat changes in typical laboratory condi
ME 200: Work: In thermodynamics, work is energy transfer that occurs when a system exerts a force through a distance on its surroundings, or vice versa. Work is a path function, meaning its value depends on the process taken between initial and final states. Common forms include boundary work (P-dV work), shaft work, electrical work, and flow work. Understanding work is essential for analyzing engines, compress
TAM 445: Virtual power: Virtual power principle (principle of virtual velocities) states: internal power equals external power for any kinematically admissible virtual velocity field. ∫v σ:D* dv = ∫s t·v* dS + ∫v ρb·v* dv, where * denotes virtual quantities, D* = sym(∇v*). Equivalent to Cauchy equation if holds for all v*. Provides weak form of momentum balance, foundation for variational methods and finite element analy
TAM 412: Conservative systems: Conservative systems have forces derivable from a potential energy function V, where F = -∇V. Such forces are path-independent: work depends only on endpoints, not trajectory. In conservative systems, total mechanical energy E = T + V is conserved. This conservation law provides first integrals of motion, reducing problem complexity and enabling energy methods for solution. Common conservative for
PHYS 211: Fluid dynamics: Fluid dynamics studies fluids in motion, using conservation laws for mass, momentum, and energy. The continuity equation expresses mass conservation, relating fluid speed to cross-sectional area. Bernoulli's equation relates pressure, velocity, and height along streamlines for ideal fluids. Understanding laminar versus turbulent flow, viscosity effects, and applications like airplane lift and bloo
ME 340: Fluid flow: Fluid flow modeling for dynamic systems: continuity (mass conservation), momentum (Navier-Stokes or Euler), energy equations. Simplified models: incompressible (ρ = constant, ∇·v = 0), inviscid (Euler), laminar pipe flow (Hagen-Poiseuille). Lumped parameter: resistance (pressure drop), capacitance (volume storage), inertance (fluid inertia). Fluid systems interact with mechanical (hydraulic actuat
ME 310: Hydrostatics: Hydrostatics analyzes fluids at rest or moving as a rigid body without shear stresses. The fundamental equation of hydrostatics, dP/dz = -ρg, relates pressure changes to fluid depth, showing that pressure increases linearly with depth in constant-density fluids. This leads to calculating hydrostatic forces on submerged surfaces (flat or curved), determining centers of pressure, analyzing buoyancy
TAM 335: Pressure variation: Pressure variation describes how pressure changes with position in static or moving fluids. In static fluids, vertical pressure variation follows dP/dz = -ρg; horizontal pressure is constant at same elevation in connected fluid. In accelerating fluids or rotating containers, pseudo-forces modify pressure distributions. Atmospheric pressure decreases exponentially with altitude due to decreasing density. In compressible gases, pressure and density variation are coupled through equation of state. Understanding pressure variation enables calculation of forces on submerged objects, design of hydraulic systems, and prediction of atmospheric phenomena. It explains why dam thickness increases with depth and how barometers measure atmospheric pressure..
ME 310: Differential continuity and mome: The differential forms of continuity and momentum equations describe fluid motion at a point rather than over a control volume. The continuity equation ∂ρ/∂t + ∇⋅(ρV) = 0 expresses mass conservation infinitesimally. The momentum equation (Navier-Stokes equations) ρ(∂V/∂t + V⋅∇V) = -∇P + μ∇²V + ρg relates acceleration to pressure gradient, viscous forces, and body forces. These coupled partial diff
ME 310: Bernoulli's equation: Bernoulli's equation states that for steady, incompressible, frictionless flow along a streamline, the sum of pressure energy, kinetic energy, and potential energy per unit volume remains constant: P + ½ρV² + ρgz = constant. This can be interpreted as conservation of mechanical energy per unit volume or written per unit mass. Bernoulli's equation explains many phenomena like lift generation, ventu
TAM 335: Hydrostatics and manometry: Hydrostatics experiments demonstrate pressure variation with depth, buoyancy, and pressure measurement using manometers. Typical experiments: measuring pressure distribution on submerged surfaces, determining center of pressure, verifying Archimedes' principle through buoyancy measurements, and calibrating pressure gauges using manometers. Students learn proper manometer setup, fluid selection, meniscus reading, and error analysis. Hydrostatic pressure cells measure forces on plane or curved surfaces. Understanding experimental techniques reinforces theoretical concepts, develops measurement skills, and introduces uncertainty quantification. These fundamental experiments establish proper laboratory practices for advanced fluid mechanics experiments..
TAM 335: Buoyancy: Buoyancy is the upward force on objects immersed in fluids, arising from pressure increasing with depth. Archimedes' principle states buoyancy force equals weight of displaced fluid: F_B = ρ_fluid g V_displaced. Objects float when buoyancy balances weight (ρ_object
ME 310: Fluid properties: Fluid properties characterize material behavior and response to forces. Key properties include density (mass per volume), viscosity (resistance to shear deformation), surface tension (intermolecular forces at interfaces), vapor pressure (saturation pressure at given temperature), bulk modulus (resistance to compression), and thermal properties like specific heat and thermal conductivity. Understan
ME 310: Energy: The energy equation for a control volume applies the first law of thermodynamics to flowing fluids: the rate of energy change within the control volume plus net energy flux equals heat transfer rate minus work rate. Energy includes internal, kinetic, potential, and flow work (pressure-volume work). For steady flow through devices like turbines, pumps, and heat exchangers, this becomes the steady-f
TAM 335: Hydraulic jump: Hydraulic jump is abrupt transition from supercritical to subcritical flow, characterized by rapid depth increase, strong turbulence, energy dissipation, and surface roller. Momentum equation relates upstream and downstream depths (sequent depths) since energy is not conserved. The Froude number upstream Fr_1 determines jump type: undular (Fr_1
TAM 335: Application of basic principles: This integrative topic applies continuity, momentum, and energy principles to both ideal (inviscid, incompressible) and real (viscous) fluid flows. Ideal fluid analysis using Bernoulli provides baseline performance; real fluid analysis adds viscous losses using head loss or friction factor. Applications include pipe flow (Darcy-Weisbach equation), open channel flow, flow through valves and fittings, pumps and turbines, and flow measurement devices. Minor losses from fittings add to major friction losses. System analysis combines multiple components using energy equation. Understanding when ideal assumptions suffice versus when viscous effects dominate guides engineering analysis. This practical application synthesizes fluid mechanics principles for design calculations..
TAM 335: Euler and bernoulli equations: Euler equation applies Newton's second law to inviscid flow: ρDV/Dt = -∇P + ρg. For steady flow along streamline, it integrates to Bernoulli equation: P + ½ρV² + ρgz = constant. Bernoulli equation states that sum of pressure energy, kinetic energy, and potential energy per unit volume remains constant along a streamline in steady, incompressible, inviscid flow. It explains pressure-velocity relationships: pressure decreases where velocity increases. Applications include pitot tubes, venturi meters, flow over airfoils, and siphons. Limitations include neglecting viscosity, requiring steady flow, and applying only along streamlines. Bernoulli equation is the most recognized fluid mechanics principle..
TAM 335: Gradually varying flow: Gradually varied flow (GVF) has depth changing slowly with position due to changes in bottom slope, roughness, or cross-section, allowing hydrostatic pressure assumption. Analysis uses energy equation incrementally: dy/dx = (S_0 - S_f)/(1 - Fr²) where S_0 is bottom slope, S_f is friction slope. Flow profiles are classified by depth relative to normal (y_n) and critical (y_c) depths, yielding 12 possible profile types (M1, M2, S1, S2, etc.). Backwater curves show depth increasing upstream from control; drawdown curves show depth decreasing. Applications include reservoir backwater effects, flow upstream of dams, and channel transitions. Understanding GVF enables prediction of water surface profiles for flood forecasting and hydraulic structure design..
TAM 335: Navier-stokes equations: Navier-Stokes equations are fundamental PDEs governing viscous fluid motion, expressing Newton's second law for fluid elements. The momentum equation ρ(∂V/∂t + V·∇V) = -∇P + μ∇²V + ρg balances inertia, pressure gradient, viscous forces, and body forces. Coupled with continuity equation, they form a complete system describing fluid velocity and pressure fields. These nonlinear equations have analytical solutions only for simple flows; most practical problems require numerical solution. Understanding Navier-Stokes is central to fluid mechanics, forming the basis for computational fluid dynamics, turbulence modeling, and all advanced flow analysis..
TAM 335: Local shear relations: Local wall shear stress τ_w relates to velocity gradient at wall: τ_w = μ(∂u/∂y)|_wall. The shear stress coefficient (skin friction coefficient) c_f = τ_w/(½ρU²) normalizes shear. For laminar boundary layers, Blasius solution gives c_f = 0.664/√(Re_x); for turbulent, empirical correlations yield c_f ≈ 0.058/Re_x^(1/5). Local shear determines drag on differential surface element; integration gives total skin friction drag. Shear stress distribution affects heat transfer (Reynolds analogy) and mass transfer. Understanding local shear relations enables calculation of drag, design of surface treatments for drag reduction, and prediction of convective heat transfer coefficients..
TAM 335: Control-volume concepts: Control volume approach analyzes fluid flow by selecting a fixed region in space (control volume) bounded by control surface through which fluid flows. Unlike closed system (fixed mass), control volume allows mass to enter and exit. The Reynolds Transport Theorem relates time rate of change of system properties to control volume terms. This approach is natural for analyzing devices like turbines, pumps, nozzles, and pipes where fluid continuously flows through. Conservation laws (mass, momentum, energy) formulated for control volumes yield practical engineering equations. Understanding control volume analysis is fundamental to fluid mechanics, providing systematic framework for applying conservation principles to flowing fluids..
TAM 335: Hydraulic jump in a horizontal: Hydraulic jump experiments in horizontal flumes demonstrate abrupt supercritical-to-subcritical transition. By varying upstream Froude number (using flow rate and gate opening), students observe different jump types from undular to strong, turbulent jumps. Measurements include sequent depths (y1, y2) compared with momentum equation predictions, energy dissipation calculation, and jump length. Surface roller and turbulence intensity vary with Fr1. Understanding jump experiments validates momentum analysis, demonstrates energy loss mechanisms, and illustrates practical applications like stilling basins for energy dissipation. Students learn to predict jump characteristics for hydraulic structure design..
TAM 335: Flow around a body: Flow around immersed bodies exhibits complex patterns including stagnation points (zero velocity), acceleration around sides, boundary layer development, possible separation, and wake formation. Inviscid flow theory predicts surface pressure distribution using potential flow; viscous effects modify this through boundary layers and separation. Symmetrical bodies in aligned flow experience drag but no lift; asymmetric bodies or inclined symmetric bodies generate lift. Flow visualization reveals streamlines, separation points, and wake structure. Reynolds number dramatically affects flow pattern: attached at low Re, separated with vortex shedding at moderate Re, turbulent wake at high Re. Understanding flow patterns enables aerodynamic design minimizing drag and controlling lift..
TAM 335: Acceleration: Acceleration of fluid particles combines local acceleration (∂V/∂t, time rate of change at fixed point) and convective acceleration (V·∇V, spatial rate due to particle moving through non-uniform field). The material derivative DV/Dt = ∂V/∂t + V·∇V gives total acceleration following a particle. Even in steady flow (∂V/∂t = 0), particles accelerate due to convective effects when moving through velocity gradients. Understanding acceleration is essential for applying Newton's second law (F = ma) to fluids, leading to momentum equations and Navier-Stokes equations. Acceleration appears in Euler's equation and explains forces needed to deflect flows around bends or through nozzles..
TAM 335: Specific energy critical depth: Specific energy E = y + V²/2g is energy per unit weight relative to channel bottom, combining flow depth and velocity head. For given discharge, E has minimum value at critical depth y_c where Fr = 1 (critical flow). Below y_c (small depth, high velocity) flow is supercritical (Fr > 1); above y_c (large depth, low velocity) flow is subcritical (Fr
TAM 335: Boundary layer growth: Boundary layer growth describes thickness increase with distance from leading edge. For laminar flow on flat plate: δ(x) ≈ 5x/√(Re_x), showing square root growth. After transition, turbulent boundary layer grows faster: δ(x) ≈ 0.37x/Re_x^(1/5), approximately linear for high Re. Growth results from continuous entrainment of fluid into viscous layer. Favorable pressure gradient (accelerating flow) suppresses growth; adverse gradient enhances growth and can cause separation. Displacement thickness δ* grows similarly, affecting effective body shape. Understanding growth enables prediction of drag, heat transfer variation, and separation location on bodies, crucial for aerodynamic design..
TAM 335: Water table: Water table apparatus uses a Hele-Shaw cell (closely-spaced parallel plates) creating quasi-two-dimensional flow analogous to groundwater flow through porous media. Darcy's law q = -K∇h applies where K is permeability, h is hydraulic head. Experiments model flow around wells, beneath dams, and through soil layers. Dye injection visualizes streamlines and equipotential lines forming flow nets. Boundary conditions represent impermeable layers, constant head surfaces, and wells (sources/sinks). Understanding water table experiments introduces groundwater flow fundamentals, demonstrates potential flow analogy, and develops skills in interpreting flow patterns essential for environmental engineering, water resource management, and foundation design..
TAM 445: Inviscid fluids: Inviscid fluids have no viscosity (internal friction) - constitutive equation: σ = -pI, where p is thermodynamic pressure (function of density and temperature). Tangential stresses (shear) absent, only normal stress (pressure). Euler equations of fluid mechanics: ρDv/Dt = -∇p + ρb. Incompressible inviscid: ∇·v = 0 and ρ = constant. Bernoulli's equation for steady flow: v²/2 + p/ρ + gz = constant.
TAM 470: Examples from solid and fluid m: Solid mechanics ODEs: free vibration (ẍ + ω²x = 0), damped oscillation (ẍ + 2ζωẋ + ω²x = 0), forced vibration with resonance, multidegree-of-freedom systems (matrix equations), rigid body dynamics. Fluid mechanics examples: vortex dynamics, compressible flow equations in 1D, unsteady heat transfer, diffusion problems. These examples illustrate physical phenomena, mathematical structures, and numer
TAM 445: Cauchy's stress equations of mo: Cauchy's equations of motion are differential equations governing continuum motion: ρDv/Dt = ∇·σ + ρb, or component form: ρDvᵢ/Dt = ∂σᵢⱼ/∂xⱼ + ρbᵢ. Derived from linear momentum balance using stress principle and localization. Relate stress gradients and body forces to acceleration. Three scalar equations (i=1,2,3) for three velocity components. Supplemented by mass conservation and constitutive eq
ME 411: Thermal and momentum boundary la: Boundary layers are thin regions near solid surfaces where viscous and thermal effects are concentrated. Velocity boundary layer thickness δ defines the region where velocity transitions from zero (no-slip) to free-stream value. Thermal boundary layer thickness δ_t defines where temperature transitions from wall to free-stream value. Boundary layer equations simplify Navier-Stokes by neglecting small terms (boundary layer approximation). The Blasius solution describes laminar boundary layer on flat plate: δ ∝ √(νx/U∞), showing growth with distance and viscosity. Thermal boundary layers similarly develop with thickness depending on Prandtl number Pr = ν/α. Understanding boundary layers enables drag, heat transfer, and mass transfer predictions crucial for aerodynamics and heat exchanger design..
ME 411: Fluid kinematics: Fluid kinematics describes motion and deformation of fluid elements without considering forces. Key concepts include Lagrangian (following particles) vs. Eulerian (fixed location) descriptions, material derivative (rate of change following a particle), velocity and acceleration fields, streamlines, pathlines, streaklines, and vorticity. The material derivative D/Dt = ∂/∂t + V·∇ combines local time rate and convective changes. Velocity gradient tensor decomposition separates translation, rotation, and deformation. Understanding kinematics provides the mathematical framework for describing complex flow patterns, turbulence, and deformation essential for analyzing viscous effects where fluid parcels stretch, rotate, and shear. This forms the foundation for momentum and energy equations in viscous flows..
TAM 470: The nature of computational sol: Computational solid mechanics solves elasticity, plasticity, fracture, and structural dynamics problems. Lagrangian description (following material points) typical. Challenges include material nonlinearity, geometric nonlinearity, contact, and failure. Computational fluid mechanics (CFD) solves Navier-Stokes equations for incompressible/compressible flow. Eulerian description (observing fixed loca
ME 411: Conservation principles - mass,: Conservation principles provide the governing equations for viscous flow and heat transfer. Mass conservation (continuity equation) ∂ρ/∂t + ∇·(ρV) = 0 ensures mass is neither created nor destroyed. Momentum conservation (Navier-Stokes equations) ρ(∂V/∂t + V·∇V) = -∇P + μ∇²V + ρg balances inertia, pressure, viscous, and body forces. Energy conservation includes convection, conduction, viscous dissipation, and work terms. These coupled, nonlinear partial differential equations govern all viscous fluid motion. Boundary conditions (no-slip, thermal conditions) complete the mathematical problem. Understanding these conservation laws enables analysis of laminar/turbulent flows, heat transfer, and forms the foundation for analytical solutions and numerical simulation of realistic flow problems..
PHYS 211: Rotational kinematics: Rotational kinematics describes rotational motion using angular position, angular velocity, and angular acceleration, analogous to linear kinematics. Angular displacement (θ), angular velocity (ω), and angular acceleration (α) relate through calculus just as their linear counterparts. Constant angular acceleration equations parallel linear kinematic equations. Understanding the connection between
TAM 212: Kinematics of a particle: Kinematics describes particle motion geometry without considering forces causing motion. Position vector r(t) locates particle; velocity v = dr/dt is rate of position change; acceleration a = dv/dt = d²r/dt² is rate of velocity change. Coordinate systems include Cartesian (x,y,z), normal-tangential (along path), and polar/cylindrical. Equations relate position, velocity, acceleration, and time. Fo
PHYS 211: Simple & physical pendula: A simple pendulum consists of a point mass on a massless string, exhibiting simple harmonic motion for small angles with period depending on length and gravity. A physical pendulum is any extended rigid body pivoting about a point, with period depending on moment of inertia and distance from pivot to center of mass. Both demonstrate oscillatory motion and provide practical examples of rotational d
PHYS 211: Relative & circular motion: Relative motion examines how velocity and position depend on the observer's reference frame, showing that motion is observer-dependent. Circular motion involves objects moving along circular paths, requiring centripetal acceleration directed toward the center even at constant speed. Understanding relative velocity addition, reference frames, angular velocity, period, frequency, and centripetal acc
ME 446: Review of kinematics: A review of kinematics revisits fundamental concepts of robot motion geometry before advancing to dynamics. This includes forward kinematics using transformation matrices, inverse kinematics solutions, velocity kinematics through Jacobians, and differential motion analysis. Understanding these kinematic relationships is prerequisite for dynamics study, as dynamic equations describe how forces and torques relate to motion. The review ensures students have solid grounding in position, velocity, and acceleration relationships in joint and task spaces, coordinate transformations, and singularity concepts before introducing inertial effects, Coriolis forces, and dynamic modeling..
ME 370: Planar kinematics: Planar kinematics studies motion of mechanisms constrained to move in a single plane, analyzing position, velocity, and acceleration of links and joints without considering forces. This includes determining displacement paths, angular velocities, linear velocities, and accelerations of mechanism components. Planar mechanisms are common in machinery (linkages, cams, gears) where motion occurs in pa
ME 370: Position, velocity, and accelera: Position, velocity, and acceleration analysis determines kinematic quantities at all points in a mechanism for any given input. Position analysis solves for link angles and point locations using loop-closure equations or graphical methods. Velocity analysis uses differentiation, instant centers, or velocity polygons. Acceleration analysis requires second derivatives or acceleration polygons, accou
ME 371: Kinematics of gear trains: Kinematics of gear trains analyzes motion relationships in gear systems, determining output speeds from input speeds and gear ratios. For simple trains, velocity ratio equals product of driving gear teeth divided by driven gear teeth. Compound trains have multiple stages, with overall ratio being the product of individual stage ratios. Planetary (epicyclic) gear trains are more complex, requiring
TAM 412: Foundations of lagrangian mecha: Lagrangian mechanics provides an alternative, energy-based formulation of classical mechanics using generalized coordinates and the Lagrangian function L = T - V (kinetic minus potential energy). Instead of analyzing forces and accelerations in Cartesian coordinates, Lagrangian mechanics derives equations of motion through variational principles (principle of least action). This approach automatic
TAM 412: Small oscillations about steady: Analyzing vibrations about non-equilibrium steady motions (like spinning shafts or orbiting satellites) by linearizing about the steady trajectory. The approach parallels small oscillations about equilibrium but accounts for gyroscopic effects and time-varying linearization. Perturbation coordinates describe deviations from steady motion: q = q_steady + δq. Linearized equations often have constant
TAM 412: Angular velocities: Angular velocity ω is a vector describing instantaneous rotation rate and axis: magnitude gives rotation speed (rad/s), direction indicates rotation axis via right-hand rule. For rigid bodies, angular velocity describes how the body's orientation changes: v = ω × r relates any point's velocity to its position r from the rotation axis. Angular velocities add vectorially when rotations combine, thou
TAM 412: Generalized coordinates and deg: Generalized coordinates are independent variables completely specifying a system's configuration, chosen to exploit problem structure and automatically satisfy constraints. The minimum number of generalized coordinates equals the system's degrees of freedom. Unlike Cartesian coordinates fixed to spatial directions, generalized coordinates can be angles, arc lengths, or other parameters natural to
TAM 412: Inverse dynamics: Inverse dynamics determines forces and torques required to produce specified motion, opposite to forward dynamics (finding motion from forces). Given trajectory q(t), inverse dynamics computes F = Mq̈ + C(q,q̇)q̇ + K(q) required to generate it. This is crucial for robot control (computing joint torques for desired motion), animation (making motion look physically realistic), and biomechanics (esti
ME 445: Kinematics: Kinematics studies robot motion geometry without considering forces, focusing on the relationship between joint positions and end-effector pose. Forward kinematics computes end-effector position and orientation given joint angles using sequential coordinate transformations, typically via Denavit-Hartenberg parameters. This mathematical framework enables determining where the robot hand is located for any joint configuration. Kinematics forms the foundation for motion planning, control, and simulation. Understanding kinematic chains, transformation matrices, and rotation representations is essential for analyzing and programming robotic manipulators..
MATH 257: Abstract vector spaces: An abstract vector space is a set V with operations of addition and scalar multiplication satisfying eight axioms: closure under addition and scalar multiplication, commutativity and associativity of addition, existence of additive identity and inverses, and distributive and associative properties for scalars. Examples extend beyond Euclidean vectors to include function spaces, polynomial spaces, and matrix spaces. This abstraction reveals that linear algebra principles apply to diverse mathematical objects, unifying apparently different areas of mathematics..
MATH 257: Linear transformations: A linear transformation T: V → W between vector spaces is a function that preserves vector addition and scalar multiplication: T(cv + dw) = cT(v) + dT(w). Every linear transformation from Rⁿ to Rᵐ can be represented as matrix multiplication T(x) = Ax. Important properties include: T(0) = 0, transformations map lines to lines (or points), and they map subspaces to subspaces. Linear transformations include rotations, reflections, projections, scaling, and shearing, forming the foundation of computer graphics and geometric understanding..
MATH 257: Matrix-vector: Matrix-vector multiplication combines a matrix A with a vector x to produce a new vector b, written as Ax = b. Each entry of the result is the dot product of a row from A with the vector x. Alternatively, it can be viewed as a linear combination of the columns of A weighted by the entries of x. This operation is fundamental for representing linear transformations, systems of equations (where Ax = b represents the system), and numerous computational applications in engineering, physics, and data science..
MATH 257: Basis and dimension: A basis for a vector space V is a linearly independent set that spans V - every vector in V can be uniquely expressed as a linear combination of basis vectors. The dimension of V is the number of vectors in any basis, which is the same for all bases. For Rⁿ, the standard basis has n vectors (e₁, e₂, ..., eₙ). Bases provide coordinate systems for vector spaces, and dimension quantifies the space's size or degrees of freedom. These concepts are central to understanding vector spaces structurally..
MATH 241: Coordinate systems: Coordinate systems provide frameworks for specifying positions in space using ordered tuples of numbers. In Calculus III, key systems include Cartesian (x, y, z), cylindrical (r, θ, z), and spherical (ρ, θ, φ) coordinates. Each system has advantages for different geometries: Cartesian for rectangular structures, cylindrical for objects with circular symmetry about an axis, and spherical for radial
MATH 257: Inner products: An inner product (or dot product) is a generalization of the Euclidean dot product that satisfies four properties: linearity in the first argument, conjugate symmetry, positive definiteness, and ⟨v,v⟩ ≥ 0 with equality only when v = 0. For real vectors in Rⁿ, the standard inner product is ⟨x,y⟩ = x·y = Σxᵢyᵢ. Inner products define geometric concepts like length (‖v‖ = √⟨v,v⟩), angle, and orthogonality (vectors are orthogonal when ⟨x,y⟩ = 0). They are fundamental to optimization, projections, and Fourier analysis..
MATH 257: Coordinate matrix: The coordinate matrix (or change-of-basis matrix) converts coordinates from one basis to another. If B and C are two bases for a vector space, the matrix P that transforms [v]ᵦ to [v]c is constructed with columns being the C-coordinates of B's basis vectors. For linear transformation T, its matrix representation depends on chosen bases - different bases yield different (but similar) matrices representing the same transformation. Coordinate matrices enable working in convenient bases while maintaining mathematical consistency..
MATH 241: Curl and divergence: Curl and divergence are two fundamental differential operators applied to vector fields. Divergence measures the rate at which 'stuff' expands or contracts from a point, producing a scalar field. Curl measures the rotation or circulation of a field around a point, producing a vector field. Together, these operators characterize the local behavior of vector fields and are central to physics applica
MATH 241: Integral theorems overview: This topic provides a comprehensive view of the major integral theorems in vector calculus: Green's theorem, Stokes's theorem, and the divergence theorem. These theorems form a unified framework connecting line integrals, surface integrals, and volume integrals through boundary relationships. They represent the multivariable generalizations of the fundamental theorem of calculus and are cornerston
MATH 257: Linear combinations: A linear combination is an expression formed by multiplying vectors by scalar coefficients and adding the results: c₁v₁ + c₂v₂ + ... + cₙvₙ. This fundamental concept describes how vectors can be combined to create new vectors. Linear combinations are central to understanding span (all possible linear combinations of a set of vectors), linear independence, and vector spaces. Many problems in linear algebra reduce to determining whether a vector can be expressed as a linear combination of other vectors..
MATH 241: Linear transformations: Linear transformations T: R^n → R^m are functions that preserve vector addition and scalar multiplication: T(u + v) = T(u) + T(v) and T(cu) = cT(u). Represented by matrices, T(x) = Ax maps vector x to Ax. Key properties include transforming lines to lines (or points), parallels to parallels, and zero to zero. Understanding includes finding transformation matrices, computing images of vectors and r
MATH 241: Determinant: The determinant of a 3×3 matrix is a scalar computed using a specific formula involving products and differences of matrix entries. For matrix with rows [a₁,a₂,a₃], [b₁,b₂,b₃], [c₁,c₂,c₃], det = a₁(b₂c₃-b₃c₂) - a₂(b₁c₃-b₃c₁) + a₃(b₁c₂-b₂c₁). Geometrically, the determinant gives the signed volume of the parallelepiped formed by the three row vectors. Determinants appear in computing cross products,
MATH 285: Matrices and determinants: Matrices and determinants are fundamental algebraic structures for solving systems of differential equations. Matrices represent linear transformations and systems of equations compactly, while determinants provide information about matrix invertibility, solution existence, and geometric scaling. Understanding matrix operations, properties, and determinant calculation is essential for analyzing sy
MATH 257: Subspaces of r^n: A subspace of Rⁿ is a subset that is closed under vector addition and scalar multiplication, meaning any linear combination of vectors in the subspace remains in the subspace. Every subspace must contain the zero vector. Examples include lines through the origin (1D subspaces), planes through the origin (2D subspaces), and the entire space Rⁿ itself. Subspaces are the fundamental geometric objects in linear algebra, providing the setting for understanding solutions to linear systems and transformations..
MATH 241: Interpreting divergence and 2d: This topic focuses on understanding the physical and geometric meaning of divergence and curl in two dimensions. Divergence at a point indicates the net outward flux per unit area from that point, representing sources or sinks. In 2D, curl is a scalar measuring counterclockwise rotation intensity. These interpretations help visualize and understand vector field behavior in applications like fluid
MATH 241: Jacobian of multivariable func: The Jacobian is a matrix of all first-order partial derivatives of a vector-valued function. It represents the best linear approximation of a multivariable function near a given point and describes how the function transforms infinitesimal volumes in space. The determinant of the Jacobian matrix indicates how areas or volumes scale under the transformation and is crucial for changing variables in
TAM 412: Coordinate systems and transfor: Coordinate systems provide frameworks for describing positions and orientations using numbers. Common systems include Cartesian (x,y,z), cylindrical (r,θ,z), spherical (r,θ,φ), and body-fixed coordinates. Transformation equations convert quantities between coordinate systems, essential when problems have natural symmetries in non-Cartesian systems. Transformations involve rotation matrices, Jacobi
TAM 445: Change of coordinate frame: Change of coordinate frame (basis transformation) relates tensor components in different coordinate systems. Rotation matrix Q transforms components: v'ᵢ = Qᵢⱼvⱼ for vectors, T'ᵢⱼ = QᵢₖQⱼₗTₖₗ for second-order tensors. Orthogonal transformations (rotations) preserve lengths and angles: QᵀQ = I. Tensors themselves are independent of coordinates; only components change. Active interpretation: rotatin
ME 440: Algebras of three-dimensional tr: The mathematical framework describing how translations (linear displacements) and rotations (angular displacements) in 3D space can be composed, inverted, and represented using group theory and linear algebra. This includes rotation matrices, quaternions, homogeneous transformations, and understanding that rotations don't commute (order matters). These algebras provide the foundation for describing rigid body motion, where objects maintain their shape while translating and rotating. Understanding these structures is essential for robotics, computer graphics, aerospace engineering, and any field requiring precise spatial transformations..
TAM 445: Indicial notation: Indicial notation (index notation) represents tensor components using subscripts and Einstein summation convention. Repeated index implies summation: aᵢbᵢ = Σᵢaᵢbᵢ. Free indices appear once and represent components; dummy indices appear twice and are summed. Kronecker delta δᵢⱼ (=1 if i=j, =0 otherwise) and permutation symbol εᵢⱼₖ are key tensors. Vector dot product: a·b = aᵢbᵢ; cross product: (a×
TAM 445: Positive-definite tensors: Positive-definite tensor A satisfies v·A·v > 0 for all non-zero vectors v, or equivalently, all eigenvalues positive: λᵢ > 0. Positive-semidefinite allows equality: v·A·v ≥ 0, λᵢ ≥ 0. Examples: metric tensor, right and left stretch tensors (U, V in polar decomposition). Positive-definiteness ensures physical realizability (e.g., positive strain energy). For symmetric positive-definite A, unique po
TAM 212: Moving reference frames: Moving reference frames analyze motion relative to accelerating or rotating coordinate systems. Absolute acceleration: aₐbs = arel + 2ω × vrel + ω × (ω × r) + a₀, where arel is relative acceleration, 2ω × vrel is Coriolis acceleration, ω × (ω × r) is centripetal acceleration, a₀ is frame acceleration. These apparent accelerations arise from non-inertial frames. Applications include Earth-surface p
ME 340: Simulation and physical experime: The double pendulum (two pendulums connected in series) exemplifies rich nonlinear dynamics despite simple construction. For small angles, linearization yields four natural modes, but large-angle motion exhibits chaos - extreme sensitivity to initial conditions producing unpredictable long-term behavior. Students simulate the system numerically and compare to physical experiments, observing how sm
PHYS 211: Angular momentum vector: Angular momentum as a vector quantity has both magnitude and direction, with direction determined by the right-hand rule perpendicular to the plane of rotation. Vector analysis of angular momentum enables understanding of gyroscopic effects, precession, and three-dimensional rotation. The vector nature explains why spinning objects resist changes in orientation and is crucial for analyzing tops, g
MATH 241: Understanding curl: A deeper exploration of curl focusing on its physical interpretation as measuring local rotation intensity and axis in a vector field. Curl at a point indicates how much and around which axis the field tends to rotate. Zero curl indicates irrotational flow (conservative field), while non-zero curl indicates circulation. This understanding is crucial for applications in fluid mechanics, electromagn
ME 340: A nonlinear pendulum: Unlike the linearized simple pendulum (sin θ ≈ θ), the nonlinear pendulum retains the full sine term in its equation of motion: θ̈ + (g/L)sin(θ) = 0. This seemingly minor change introduces rich behavior including amplitude-dependent frequency, non-sinusoidal oscillations for large angles, and chaotic motion under certain driving conditions. Studying nonlinear pendulums teaches the limitations of l
ME 446: Kinematic transformations: Kinematic transformations describe coordinate frame relationships and convert quantities (positions, velocities, forces) between different reference frames. Homogeneous transformation matrices combine rotation and translation, enabling position transformation from tool frame to base frame or world coordinates. Velocity and force transformations require Jacobian matrices and adjoint transformations. Understanding these transformations is fundamental for expressing task specifications in convenient frames, sensor data fusion, and relating control commands in different coordinate systems. Proper transformation handling ensures consistent interpretation of geometric and dynamic quantities throughout the control system..
TAM 335: Velocity: Velocity in fluids is a vector field V(x,y,z,t) describing fluid speed and direction at every point and time. Components (u,v,w) represent velocities in (x,y,z) directions. Velocity describes how fast and in what direction fluid moves. In Eulerian description, velocity is function of position and time; in Lagrangian, it's velocity of identified particle. Velocity fields visualize through streamlines, vector plots, and contour maps. Measuring techniques include pitot tubes, hot wire anemometry, Laser Doppler Velocimetry (LDV), and Particle Image Velocimetry (PIV). Understanding velocity fields is fundamental to all fluid flow analysis, from simple pipe flow to complex turbulent flows around aircraft..
ME 370: Motion input: Motion input involves specifying and analyzing how mechanisms are driven - typically through motors, actuators, or manual inputs providing angular or linear displacement, velocity, or acceleration. Understanding input characteristics enables matching prime movers to mechanisms, determining required power, and designing control strategies. Input types include constant velocity (motors with flywheel
TAM 412: Moving frames: Moving (non-inertial) reference frames accelerate or rotate relative to inertial frames. Dynamics in moving frames introduces fictitious forces: centrifugal, Coriolis, Euler, and translational inertial forces. These apparent forces arise from the frame's motion, not physical interactions, but must be included for correct force balance. Moving frames are essential for analyzing Earth-based systems
ME 440: Reference frames: Reference frames are coordinate systems used to describe positions, velocities, and accelerations of points in mechanical systems. Choosing appropriate reference frames simplifies analysis by exploiting symmetries or natural coordinates for specific problems. Inertial reference frames (non-accelerating) allow direct application of Newton's laws, while non-inertial frames (accelerating or rotating) require fictitious forces (centrifugal, Coriolis). Understanding reference frame transformations - expressing quantities measured in one frame relative to another - is fundamental for analyzing complex multibody systems, spacecraft, robotics, and vehicle dynamics..
ME 445: Angular velocity and acceleratio: Angular velocity ω is a 3×1 vector describing instantaneous rotation rate and axis (right-hand rule). Angular velocity composition for chained rotations uses vector addition: ω_total = ω_1 + ω_2. Angular acceleration α = dω/dt describes change in rotational motion. For robot links, angular velocities propagate through kinematic chain: each link's angular velocity equals previous link's plus joint contribution. Understanding angular velocity enables computing Jacobian rows corresponding to orientation, analyzing rotational dynamics, and implementing orientation control. Angular velocity in body versus spatial coordinates requires careful frame tracking..
TAM 412: Euler angles: Euler angles (typically φ, θ, ψ) are three successive rotations about specified axes that transform from inertial to body-fixed coordinates, parametrizing arbitrary 3D orientations. Multiple conventions exist (e.g., 3-1-3, 3-2-1 sequences), each with advantages for specific applications. Euler angles provide intuitive orientation description but suffer from singularities (gimbal lock) where two ax
ME 440: Observer hierarchies: Observer hierarchies organize multiple reference frames in tree structures representing how frames attach to moving bodies. Each frame in the hierarchy has a parent frame, forming chains from inertial space to specific body-fixed frames. Velocities and accelerations computed using chain rules accumulate contributions from each frame in the hierarchy. This systematic framework is essential for multibody dynamics, allowing automatic generation of equations of motion for systems like robots, vehicles, and spacecraft with many interconnected moving parts. Software packages implement observer hierarchies for efficient kinematic and dynamic analysis..
ME 445: Rigid motions and homogeneous tr: Rigid motions preserve distances and angles between points, including translations (displacement without rotation) and rotations (orientation change about fixed point). Homogeneous transformations represent rigid motions using 4×4 matrices combining rotation (3×3 matrix R) and translation (3×1 vector p) in unified form. This representation enables composing multiple transformations through matrix multiplication, relating coordinate frames in kinematic chains. Homogeneous coordinates add a fourth coordinate (typically 1) to 3D vectors, enabling translation representation as matrix multiplication rather than vector addition. Understanding homogeneous transformations provides the mathematical foundation for robot kinematics..
ME 445: Homogeneous transformations: Homogeneous transformations are 4×4 matrices representing combined rotation and translation using homogeneous coordinates. Form: T = [R p; 0 1] where R is 3×3 rotation, p is 3×1 translation, and bottom row [0 0 0 1]. Transformations compose through matrix multiplication: T3 = T1·T2 transforms from frame 2→1 then 1→3. Inverse transformations: T⁻¹ = [R^T -R^T·p; 0 1]. Homogeneous representations enable treating translation and rotation uniformly, chaining transformations in kinematic chains, and implementing in computer code efficiently. Understanding homogeneous transformations is fundamental to forward kinematics, planning, and coordinate frame manipulation..
ME 170: Visualization: Visualization in CAD encompasses techniques for viewing and representing 3D models to communicate design intent, including wireframe, hidden line, shaded, rendered, and sectioned views. Effective visualization enables design evaluation, error detection, and communication with stakeholders. Techniques include view orientation (isometric, orthographic), display modes (solid, transparent, x-ray), lig
Physical interpretation extracts meaning from computational results, connecting numbers to engineering insight. Includes: stress/strain fields visualization, identifying load paths, understanding failure mechanisms, recognizing boundary effects, detecting numerical artifacts vs. physical phenomena. Sanity checks: equilibrium satisfaction, symmetry consistency, limit case comparison, dimensional an
TAM 211: Coulomb friction: Coulomb (dry) friction is the tangential resistance force between contacting surfaces opposing motion or impending motion. Static friction fs adjusts from zero up to maximum fs,max = μsN to prevent motion, where μs is static friction coefficient and N is normal force. Once motion occurs, kinetic friction fk = μkN opposes sliding (typically μk
ME 310: Viscous flows: Viscous flows involve significant friction effects where viscous forces are comparable to or dominate inertial forces. Viscosity causes velocity gradients and shear stresses within the fluid, dissipating mechanical energy into heat. The Reynolds number (Re = ρVL/μ) characterizes the relative importance of inertial to viscous forces. Low Reynolds number flows are dominated by viscosity (Stokes flow
TAM 445: Motions: Motion describes time-dependent deformation of continuum: x = χ(X,t) maps reference configuration to current at each time t. Material description follows particles: f(X,t). Spatial description observes locations: f(x,t). Velocity: material v(X,t) = ∂χ/∂t, spatial v(x,t). Acceleration a = ∂v/∂t + (v·∇)v using material derivative (convective acceleration included). Pathlines follow particles; stream
ME 411: Laminar and turbulent flow over: External flow over flat plates develops velocity and thermal boundary layers growing from the leading edge. Laminar flow (Re_x
ME 431: Surface degradation: Surface degradation includes wear (adhesive, abrasive, erosive, fretting), corrosion (uniform, pitting, crevice, stress corrosion cracking), and surface fatigue (pitting, spalling). These modes progressively remove or damage material, degrading function and potentially causing catastrophic failure. Prevention includes material selection (hardness for wear resistance, corrosion resistance), surface treatments (hardening, coatings, platings), lubrication (reducing wear), design (avoiding crevices, reducing contact stress), and environmental control (inhibitors, dry gases). Understanding surface degradation modes guides selection of materials, coatings, lubricants, and designs ensuring adequate surface durability..
PHYS 213: Quasistatic processes: A quasistatic process proceeds infinitely slowly, allowing the system to remain in thermal and mechanical equilibrium throughout. Each intermediate state is an equilibrium state, making the process reversible in principle. Quasistatic processes can be represented as smooth curves on thermodynamic diagrams (P-V, T-S). Examples include slow compression with movable piston or gradual heating with inf
PHYS 213: Internal energy: Internal energy (U) is the total microscopic energy of a system, including kinetic energy of molecular motion and potential energy from intermolecular forces. It is a state function depending only on the system's current state, not how it arrived there. The first law of thermodynamics relates internal energy change to heat and work: ΔU = Q - W. For ideal gases, internal energy depends only on temp
MATH 231: Applications to physics and en: Calculus II techniques apply to numerous physics and engineering problems including calculating work (W = ∫F dx), fluid pressure and force on submerged surfaces, center of mass, moments of inertia, and electric/magnetic field calculations. These applications typically involve setting up integrals that model physical quantities as accumulations of infinitesimal contributions. Students learn to tran
PHYS 213: Temperature: Temperature is a measure of the average kinetic energy of particles in a system and determines the direction of spontaneous heat flow (from hot to cold). It is measured in Kelvin (absolute scale), Celsius, or Fahrenheit. Temperature defines thermal equilibrium - two systems at the same temperature exchange no net heat (zeroth law of thermodynamics). At the microscopic level, temperature relates to
MATH 221: Indefinite integrals and the n: Indefinite integrals ∫f(x)dx represent the family of all antiderivatives F(x) + C of f(x). The Net Change Theorem states that ∫[a,b] F'(x)dx = F(b) - F(a), meaning the integral of a rate of change equals the total net change. Applications include finding displacement from velocity, total amount from rate of flow, and change in quantity from rate of change. This theorem formalizes how integration a
ME 200: Reversible work: Reversible work represents the maximum useful work obtainable from a system undergoing a process between two states, or the minimum work required to drive a process. It assumes the process occurs infinitely slowly through equilibrium states with no dissipative effects like friction or unrestrained expansion. Reversible work serves as the ideal benchmark for evaluating real process performance. The
ME 200: Second law of thermodynamics: The second law of thermodynamics establishes the direction of natural processes and defines the concept of entropy. It can be stated in multiple equivalent forms: entropy of an isolated system increases in any spontaneous process; heat cannot spontaneously flow from cold to hot; no heat engine can have 100% efficiency; and it is impossible to construct a device operating in a cycle that produces n
ME 200: Heat transfer: Heat transfer in thermodynamics refers to energy transfer driven by temperature differences between a system and its surroundings. Unlike work, heat is also a path function and is defined only during a process, not as a property of a state. Heat flows naturally from higher to lower temperature regions and can occur through conduction, convection, or radiation. In thermodynamic analysis, heat trans
ME 200: Entropy: Entropy is a thermodynamic property that quantifies the degree of disorder or randomness in a system, and serves as a measure of energy unavailability for doing work. In classical thermodynamics, entropy changes are calculated from heat transfer divided by absolute temperature for reversible processes. Entropy always increases in isolated systems undergoing irreversible processes (second law), mak
ME 200: First law of thermodynamics for: The first law of thermodynamics for a closed system (fixed mass) is a statement of energy conservation: the change in total energy of the system equals the net energy transfer as heat and work. Mathematically expressed as ΔE = Q - W, where E is total energy (internal, kinetic, potential), Q is heat transferred to the system, and W is work done by the system. This fundamental principle allows engin
TAM 335: Momentum and energy principles: Momentum principle applies Newton's second law to control volumes: sum of forces equals rate of momentum out minus momentum in plus momentum accumulation. For steady flow: ΣF = ṁ(V_out - V_in). This determines forces on bends, nozzles, turbine blades, and any flow device. Energy principle (first law) states energy in minus energy out equals energy accumulation: includes kinetic, potential, internal, flow work, heat transfer, and shaft work. For steady flow: energy per unit mass equation relates pressure, velocity, elevation, and energy interactions. These principles enable analysis of pumps, turbines, propulsion, and any energy conversion involving fluid flow..
TAM 445: Conservation of mass: Mass conservation states material mass remains constant: dm = 0, or equivalently, mass density ρ evolves as Dρ/Dt + ρ∇·v = 0 (continuity equation). Integral form: d/dt ∫v(t) ρ dv = 0 for material volume. For incompressible materials (ρ = constant), reduces to ∇·v = 0 (divergence-free velocity). Alternative forms: ∂ρ/∂t + ∇·(ρv) = 0 (conservation form). Material derivative of J: DJ/Dt = J∇·v. Mass
TAM 412: Energy-time: When the Lagrangian has no explicit time dependence (∂L/∂t = 0), representing time-translation symmetry, the Hamiltonian H = Σpᵢq̇ᵢ - L (total energy for natural systems) is conserved. This energy-time relationship connects temporal invariance to energy conservation: if physics is the same today as tomorrow, energy is conserved. For conservative systems with velocity-independent constraints, H = T
MATH 241: Conservative vector fields: A vector field F is conservative if F = ∇f for some scalar potential function f. Conservative fields have several equivalent properties: line integrals are path-independent, ∫_C F·dr = 0 for closed curves, and curl F = 0 (in simply connected domains). Finding potential function: integrate P = ∂f/∂x to get f (up to function of y,z), then use Q = ∂f/∂y and R = ∂f/∂z to determine f completely. Conser
MATH 241: Conservative vector field in 3: This topic examines conservative vector fields in three-dimensional spaces with non-trivial topology. While conservative fields have zero curl everywhere, in multiply-connected spaces (with holes), a field can have zero curl yet not be conservative. Understanding the topological requirements for conservative fields—such as simply-connected domains—is essential for properly applying potential theor
ME 340: Continuous systems: Continuous systems have infinite degrees of freedom, requiring partial differential equations (PDEs) rather than ordinary differential equations for their description. Examples include vibrating strings, beams, membranes, and fluid flows where properties vary continuously over spatial dimensions. Unlike lumped-parameter models with discrete masses and springs, continuous systems exhibit spatial va
MATH 285: Constant coefficient linear eq: Constant coefficient linear differential equations have constant coefficients multiplying each derivative term, making them particularly tractable. The characteristic equation method converts the differential equation into an algebraic equation whose roots determine the solution structure. Solutions involve exponentials, sines, and cosines depending on whether roots are real, repeated, or complex.
ME 447: Space and time discretization: Space and time discretization converts continuous differential equations governing soft system dynamics into discrete computational representations. Spatial discretization divides the continuum into finite elements, nodes, or particles, while temporal discretization breaks continuous time into discrete steps. Methods include finite element methods (FEM), finite difference methods, and discrete elastic rods. Choosing appropriate discretization schemes balances accuracy, stability, and computational efficiency. For soft robotics, discretization must handle large deformations, complex contact, and preserve physical properties like energy conservation and momentum..
ME 447: Time discretization: numerical a: Time discretization numerical analysis examines how temporal integration schemes affect simulation accuracy, stability, and efficiency. Students compare explicit methods (like forward Euler, Runge-Kutta) with implicit methods (backward Euler, implicit midpoint), analyzing stability regions, convergence order, and computational cost. For soft systems with stiff dynamics, implicit methods often provide better stability despite higher per-step cost. Analysis includes studying time step sensitivity, energy conservation, and long-term behavior. Understanding these trade-offs enables selecting appropriate integrators for specific soft robotics simulations..
TAM 470: Lagrangian and hamiltonian dyna: Lagrangian mechanics uses energy methods: L = T - V (kinetic minus potential energy). Euler-Lagrange equations: d/dt(∂L/∂q̇ᵢ) - ∂L/∂qᵢ = 0 for generalized coordinates qᵢ. Advantages: automatically incorporates constraints, coordinate-independent formulation, systematic derivation. Hamiltonian mechanics uses H = T + V: q̇ᵢ = ∂H/∂pᵢ, ṗᵢ = -∂H/∂qᵢ where pᵢ are conjugate momenta. Phase space (q,p) for
ME 200: Definitions: Fundamental terminology and concepts forming the foundation of thermodynamics including system (region of interest separated from surroundings by boundary), state (condition described by properties), process (path of state changes), property (macroscopic characteristic like temperature, pressure, volume), extensive properties (proportional to mass), intensive properties (independent of mass), equi
TAM 212: Kinematics of systems of partic: Systems of particles involve multiple interacting particles, requiring description of each particle's motion plus system center of mass. Center of mass position: rcm = Σmᵢrᵢ / Σmᵢ moves as if all mass concentrated there with resultant external force applied. Relative motion between particles described by relative position, velocity, and acceleration vectors. Applications include multi-body systems
PHYS 211: Elastic collisions: Elastic collisions are interactions where both momentum and kinetic energy are conserved. Objects bounce apart with no energy lost to heat, sound, or deformation. These ideal collisions occur approximately in atomic-scale interactions and bouncing balls with minimal energy loss. Analyzing elastic collisions requires simultaneous application of momentum and energy conservation, yielding unique solu
PHYS 213: Kinetic theory of the ideal ga: Kinetic theory explains macroscopic gas properties through microscopic molecular motion. Assumptions include: molecules are point particles in random motion, collisions are perfectly elastic, and intermolecular forces are negligible except during collisions. The theory derives the ideal gas law PV = NkT from mechanics and relates pressure to molecular collisions: P = (1/3)nmv̄². Average kinetic en
MATH 257: Dynamical systems: Dynamical systems describe how states evolve over time according to deterministic rules. Discrete systems use x_(k+1) = Ax_k (difference equations), while continuous systems use dx/dt = Ax (differential equations). Long-term behavior is determined by eigenvalues and eigenvectors: eigenvectors give stable directions, eigenvalues determine growth/decay rates. Phase portraits visualize trajectories in state space. Applications span physics, biology, economics, and engineering, from planetary orbits to population models to control systems..
PHYS 213: Chemical potential and phases: Chemical potential (μ) is the change in free energy per particle added: μ = (∂G/∂N). It represents the 'drive' for particles to move between phases or regions. At equilibrium between phases (solid-liquid-gas), chemical potentials must be equal: μₛₒₗᵢ𝒹 = μₗᵢᵩᵤᵢ𝒹 = μ_gas. Differences in chemical potential drive phase transitions, diffusion, and chemical reactions. Temperature and pressure changes af
CHEM 102: Gases: Gases are a state of matter characterized by particles widely separated, moving randomly at high speeds, with negligible attractive forces between particles. Gas particles occupy all available space in their container, have low density compared to liquids and solids, and are highly compressible. The kinetic molecular theory explains gas behavior: particle collisions with container walls create pre
TAM 470: Particle systems: Particle systems model collections of discrete interacting particles. Governed by Newton's laws: mᵢẍᵢ = ΣFᵢⱼ + Fᵢ,ext. Forces include: gravity, springs, damping, contact, intermolecular (molecular dynamics). N-body problems computationally expensive: O(N²) for direct calculation, reduced to O(N log N) with fast multipole or tree methods. Applications: molecular dynamics, granular materials, astrop
TAM 412: Hamilton's canonical equations: Hamilton's canonical equations are a pair of first-order differential equations describing system dynamics: q̇ᵢ = ∂H/∂pᵢ and ṗᵢ = -∂H/∂qᵢ, where H is the Hamiltonian, q are generalized coordinates, and p are conjugate momenta. These equations are symmetric, revealing phase space structure and symplectic geometry. They double the number of variables compared to Lagrange's second-order equations but
TAM 412: Momentum-translation: When the Lagrangian doesn't depend on a Cartesian coordinate (spatial translation symmetry, ∂L/∂xᵢ = 0), the corresponding momentum component pᵢ is conserved. This momentum-translation relationship states that if physics is invariant under spatial translation (no preferred position), linear momentum is conserved. For example, in the absence of external forces, a system's center-of-mass momentum is
TAM 412: Foundations of hamiltonian mech: Hamiltonian mechanics reformulates dynamics using generalized coordinates q and conjugate momenta p (instead of coordinates and velocities), with the Hamiltonian H(q,p,t) = T + V for natural systems representing total energy. Hamilton's equations are first-order: q̇ = ∂H/∂p, ṗ = -∂H/∂q, doubling variables but gaining symmetry and geometric structure. This formulation reveals phase space structure,
PHYS 211: Angular momentum: Angular momentum is rotational momentum, defined as the cross product of position vector and linear momentum (L = r × p) for a point mass, or as moment of inertia times angular velocity (L = Iω) for rigid bodies. When net external torque is zero, angular momentum is conserved. This conservation law is fundamental to understanding planetary orbits, figure skating spins, gyroscopes, and quantum mech
PHYS 213: Entropy: Entropy (S) is a measure of a system's disorder or the number of microscopic states consistent with its macroscopic properties. The second law of thermodynamics states that entropy of an isolated system never decreases. For reversible processes, dS = dQ/T. Entropy quantifies energy dispersal and determines process spontaneity - systems naturally evolve toward higher entropy. In statistical mechani
CHEM 102: Gas pressures: Gas pressure is the force per unit area exerted by gas particles colliding with container walls, measured in various units including atmospheres (atm), pascals (Pa), torr, and mmHg. At sea level, atmospheric pressure is approximately 1 atm = 101,325 Pa = 760 mmHg = 760 torr. Pressure depends on the number of collisions and force per collision, which increase with higher particle concentration, fas
CHEM 102: Dalton's atomic theory: Dalton's atomic theory proposes that all matter is composed of indivisible atoms, which are the fundamental building blocks of elements. Each element consists of identical atoms with characteristic mass, while atoms of different elements differ in mass and properties. Chemical reactions involve the rearrangement of atoms, not their creation or destruction, and compounds form when atoms combine in
CHEM 102: Energy of phase changes: Phase changes involve energy transfer without temperature change as substances convert between solid, liquid, and gas phases. Heat of fusion (ΔH_fus) is energy required for melting, while heat of vaporization (ΔH_vap) is energy for vaporization. These are endothermic processes requiring energy to overcome intermolecular forces. Reverse processes (freezing, condensation) release equivalent energy.
CHEM 102: Gas laws & the ideal gas laws: Gas laws describe mathematical relationships between pressure (P), volume (V), temperature (T), and moles (n) of gases. Boyle's Law (P₁V₁ = P₂V₂) relates pressure and volume at constant temperature. Charles's Law (V₁/T₁ = V₂/T₂) relates volume and temperature at constant pressure. The Ideal Gas Law (PV = nRT) combines these relationships, where R is the gas constant. These laws assume ideal behavi
CHEM 102: Dalton's law: Dalton's Law of Partial Pressures states that the total pressure of a gas mixture equals the sum of the partial pressures of individual component gases: P_total = P₁ + P₂ + P₃ + ... Each gas behaves independently, and its partial pressure is the pressure it would exert if it alone occupied the entire volume. This principle applies to ideal gas mixtures and is used in calculations involving gas col
CHEM 104: 2nd law of thermodynamics: The Second Law of Thermodynamics states that the entropy of the universe increases in spontaneous processes: ΔSuniverse = ΔSsystem + ΔSsurroundings > 0 for spontaneous changes. This law introduces entropy (S) as a measure of disorder or number of microstates available to a system. While energy is conserved (First Law), the Second Law determines directionality - processes naturally proceed toward m
PHYS 214: Momentum: In quantum mechanics, momentum is represented by the operator p̂ = -iℏ(∂/∂x), where ℏ = h/2π. Momentum eigenfunctions are plane waves e^(ikx) with eigenvalues p = ℏk (de Broglie relation). Expectation value ⟨p⟩ = ∫ψ*(-iℏ∂ψ/∂x)dx gives average momentum. Heisenberg uncertainty principle Δx·Δp ≥ ℏ/2 states position and momentum cannot both be precisely determined. Momentum operator generates spatial
PHYS 214: Waves: Waves are disturbances that propagate through space and time, transferring energy without net transport of matter. Characterized by wavelength λ (spatial period), frequency f (temporal oscillation rate), amplitude A (maximum displacement), and speed v = fλ. Waves can be transverse (displacement perpendicular to propagation) or longitudinal (displacement parallel). Wave phenomena include superposit
PHYS 213: Boltzmann examples: Applications of Boltzmann statistics include: (1) Maxwell-Boltzmann velocity distribution describing gas molecule speeds, (2) barometric formula for atmospheric pressure P(h) ∝ e^(-mgh/kT), (3) population of atomic energy levels explaining spectral line intensities, (4) Arrhenius equation for reaction rates k ∝ e^(-Ea/kT), and (5) thermal excitation across energy gaps in semiconductors. These exam
CHEM 104: Collision theory: Collision theory explains reaction rates by proposing that molecules must collide with sufficient energy (activation energy, Ea) and proper orientation to react. Not all collisions lead to reaction - only those meeting energy and orientation requirements are effective. Reaction rate depends on collision frequency, which increases with concentration and temperature. The Arrhenius equation relates r
CHEM 102: Bohr's hydrogen model: Bohr's model describes the hydrogen atom with electrons orbiting the nucleus in fixed, quantized circular paths called energy levels or shells. Electrons can only exist in these specific orbits, each with a defined energy value. When electrons transition between levels, they absorb or emit photons with energy exactly equal to the difference between levels. This model successfully explained hydroge
ME 404: Monatomic gas properties: Monatomic gases (noble gases: He, Ne, Ar) provide the simplest case for statistical thermodynamics with only translational energy modes. The translational partition function leads to the Sackur-Tetrode equation for entropy and predicts constant Cv = (3/2)R independent of temperature (classical limit). The canonical partition function yields internal energy U = (3/2)NkT. These predictions match experimental data excellently, validating statistical thermodynamics. Monatomic gases serve as benchmark cases demonstrating fundamental concepts without complications of rotation and vibration. Understanding monatomic gas properties from first principles builds confidence in statistical methods before tackling more complex molecules with additional energy modes..
ME 404: Diatomic gas properties: Diatomic molecules (O₂, N₂, H₂, CO) possess translational, rotational, and vibrational energy modes, each contributing to thermodynamic properties. Rotational energy levels (rigid rotor model) contribute significantly at moderate temperatures with characteristic rotational temperature Θ_rot. Vibrational energy (harmonic oscillator model) activates at higher temperatures characterized by Θ_vib. Electronic excitation requires very high temperatures. The total partition function multiplies contributions from each mode. Heat capacity varies with temperature as different modes activate: Cv ≈ (5/2)R at room temperature, increasing toward (7/2)R as vibration activates. This temperature dependence explains real gas behavior and demonstrates quantum effects. Understanding diatomic molecules extends statistical thermodynamics to most common gases..
ME 404: Crystalline solid properties: Crystalline solids exhibit ordered atomic arrangements with vibrational energy as the primary thermal excitation mode. The Einstein model treats atoms as independent harmonic oscillators with characteristic frequency ν_E. The more sophisticated Debye model accounts for phonon dispersion with a characteristic Debye temperature Θ_D. At high temperatures, Cv approaches 3R per mole of atoms (Dulong-Petit law). At low temperatures, Cv ∝ T³ (Debye law), dramatically different from classical predictions. Heat capacity, thermal expansion, and other properties derive from the vibrational partition function.
