Understanding solid properties demonstrates quantum effects at low temperatures, explains specific heat behavior, and provides foundation for material thermodynamics including phase transitions and thermal properties of structural materials..
ME 310: Fluid kinematics: Fluid kinematics describes fluid motion without considering the forces causing the motion. It encompasses concepts like velocity and acceleration fields, streamlines (lines tangent to velocity vectors), pathlines (trajectories of fluid particles), and streaklines (locations of particles passing through a point). The material derivative relates local changes and convective changes in properties fol
ME 445: Euler angles: Euler angles represent orientation using three successive rotations about coordinate axes, with multiple conventions (e.g., ZYZ, ZYX) specifying rotation sequence. Each convention uses three angles (typically α, β, γ) achieving any 3D orientation. Advantages include intuitive representation and minimal parameters (3 vs. 9 for rotation matrices). Disadvantages include gimbal lock (singularity where two axes align losing one DOF), 24 possible conventions causing confusion, and non-unique representations (multiple angle sets for same orientation). Understanding Euler angles enables interfacing with robot controllers often using this representation and recognizing gimbal lock problems requiring alternative representations (quaternions)..
ME 445: Rotations and their composition: Rotations describe orientation changes, represented by 3×3 orthogonal matrices with determinant +1 forming the special orthogonal group SO(3). Rotation composition (sequential rotations) corresponds to matrix multiplication, but order matters (rotations don't commute). A rotation requires specifying axis and angle, or three parameters like Euler angles. Properties include inverse equals transpose (R⁻¹ = R^T), preserving vector lengths, and preserving handedness. Understanding rotation composition enables analyzing robot orientations through kinematic chains, composing coordinate frame orientations, and planning orientation trajectories. The non-commutative property explains why rotation order matters in practice..
ME 445: Forward kinematics: Forward kinematics computes end-effector position and orientation from joint angles/displacements using transformation matrices from robot geometry. Process involves assigning coordinate frames to each link, determining transformation matrices between frames using DH parameters, composing transformations from base to end-effector: T_end = T_base·T_1·T_2·...·T_n. Forward kinematics always has a unique solution obtained through matrix multiplication. Applications include trajectory execution (computing where end-effector moves for joint commands), simulation, visualization, and planning validation. Understanding forward kinematics enables predicting robot behavior from control inputs and validating designs..
ME 446: Robot dynamics: Robot dynamics studies the relationship between forces/torques and robot motion, accounting for inertia, Coriolis, centrifugal, gravitational, and friction effects. The equations of motion describe how joint torques produce accelerations considering the robot's mass distribution and current configuration. Derived using Lagrangian mechanics, Newton-Euler formulation, or Kane's method, dynamic models enable model-based control, accurate simulation, and performance prediction. Understanding dynamics is crucial for high-speed operation, heavy payload handling, and precise trajectory tracking where inertial and coupling effects significantly impact behavior..
ME 440: Four-bar linkages: Four-bar linkages consist of four rigid links connected by revolute (pin) joints forming a closed chain. The simplest planar mechanism with one degree of freedom, four-bars can generate diverse motion patterns: crank-rocker (input rotates fully, output oscillates), double-crank, double-rocker, and more. Grashof's criterion determines whether continuous rotation is possible. Four-bar linkages appear in windshield wipers, folding chairs, landing gear, and countless machines. Analysis involves position, velocity, and acceleration analysis using vector loops or analytical geometry..
TAM 335: Turbomachinery: Turbomachinery encompasses rotating devices exchanging energy between fluid and rotor: pumps and fans add energy (increase pressure/head); turbines extract energy (produce shaft work). Energy transfer occurs through dynamic interaction between fluid and rotating blades. Euler turbomachinery equation relates torque to angular momentum change: T = ṁr(V_θ2 - V_θ1). Performance characterized by head (energy per unit weight), power, efficiency, and flow rate. Dimensionless parameters (flow coefficient, head coefficient, specific speed) enable scaling and comparison. Types include centrifugal (radial flow), axial flow, and mixed flow. Applications span power generation, water/wastewater systems, HVAC, and propulsion. Understanding turbomachinery fundamentals enables selection, design, and operation of pumps and turbines..
TAM 412: Angular momentum-rotation: When the Lagrangian is rotationally symmetric about an axis (∂L/∂θ = 0), the corresponding angular momentum component about that axis is conserved. This angular momentum-rotation relationship states that rotational invariance implies angular momentum conservation. For example, central force problems (like planetary orbits) have spherical symmetry, conserving total angular momentum. Figure skaters
PHYS 211: Simple harmonic motion: Simple harmonic motion (SHM) is oscillatory motion where restoring force is proportional to displacement from equilibrium (F = -kx), resulting in sinusoidal motion. Examples include mass-spring systems and small-angle pendulums. SHM is characterized by period, frequency, amplitude, and phase, with energy continuously converting between kinetic and potential. Understanding SHM is fundamental to wav
CHEM 102: Quantum mechanical model: The quantum mechanical model describes electrons not as particles in fixed orbits, but as wave-like entities existing in three-dimensional regions called orbitals, where their exact position cannot be precisely determined. Instead of definite paths, we describe the probability of finding an electron in a particular region of space. This model uses quantum numbers to characterize orbitals and succe
ME 200: State postulate: The state postulate is a fundamental principle in thermodynamics that establishes how many independent intensive properties are needed to completely define the thermodynamic state of a simple compressible system. It states that for a pure substance in equilibrium, specifying two independent intensive properties (such as temperature and pressure) is sufficient to determine all other thermodynamic p
TAM 412: Generalized momenta: Generalized momenta p_i = ∂L/∂q̇_i are quantities conjugate to generalized coordinates in Lagrangian mechanics, representing 'motion content' associated with coordinate q_i. For Cartesian coordinates, p reduces to linear momentum; for angular coordinates, to angular momentum. When a coordinate is ignorable (absent from L), its conjugate momentum is conserved. Generalized momenta form the basis for
PHYS 214: Position: In quantum mechanics, position is represented by the operator x̂ that multiplies the wave function by x. Position eigenfunctions are delta functions δ(x - x₀) representing particles precisely at x₀. Position expectation value ⟨x⟩ = ∫x|ψ(x)|²dx gives average measured position. Position representation uses wave functions ψ(x); momentum representation uses Fourier transforms ψ̃(p). The position-momen
TAM 412: Elimination of ignorable coordi: The Routhian function R is a hybrid Lagrangian-Hamiltonian formulation eliminating ignorable (cyclic) coordinates by Legendre transformation with respect to their velocities only: R = L - Σpᵢq̇ᵢ (sum over cyclic coordinates), where pᵢ are constant conjugate momenta. The Routhian depends on remaining coordinates and velocities plus cyclic coordinate momenta as parameters. Euler-Lagrange equations i
PHYS 214: Polarization and spin: Polarization describes orientation of electromagnetic wave oscillations (light). Linear, circular, and elliptical polarizations exist. Quantum mechanically, photons have spin ±1 (helicity), corresponding to right/left circular polarization. Electrons have intrinsic spin s = ½ with two states (spin up/down), measurable along any axis. Spin is purely quantum - no classical analog. Stern-Gerlach expe
TAM 412: Derivation of hamilton's princi: Hamilton's principle of least action states that the actual path taken by a system between two configurations minimizes (or makes stationary) the action integral S = ∫L dt, where L is the Lagrangian. The principle derives from d'Alembert's principle through calculus of variations, showing that requiring δS = 0 for all variations yields Euler-Lagrange equations. This variational principle provides
TAM 412: Ignorable coordinates and conse: Ignorable (cyclic) coordinates are generalized coordinates absent from the Lagrangian (∂L/∂qᵢ = 0), though their velocities appear. For such coordinates, the conjugate generalized momentum pᵢ = ∂L/∂q̇ᵢ is conserved. This connection between symmetries (coordinate absence) and conservation laws exemplifies Noether's theorem. Common examples: absence of θ in rotationally symmetric systems conserves a
TAM 412: Euler-lagrange's equations in t: The compact formulation of Euler-Lagrange equations using the Lagrangian L = T - V (kinetic minus potential energy) for conservative systems: d/dt(∂L/∂q̇ᵢ) - ∂L/∂qᵢ = 0. This elegant formulation automatically incorporates conservative forces through the potential energy term, requiring only scalar energy functions rather than vector force analysis. The Lagrangian approach exploits variational prin
PHYS 214: Probability: In quantum mechanics, probability plays a fundamental role - measurements yield probabilistic outcomes rather than deterministic predictions. The wave function ψ(x,t) contains all information about a system, with |ψ(x,t)|² giving probability density of finding a particle at position x. Total probability integrates to unity (normalization). Unlike classical probability from ignorance, quantum proba
PHYS 214: Complex numbers: Complex numbers z = a + bi (where i² = -1) are essential for quantum mechanics because wave functions are complex-valued. Key operations: addition, multiplication, complex conjugate z* = a - bi, and magnitude |z| = √(a² + b²). Euler's formula e^(iθ) = cos θ + i sin θ connects exponentials to oscillations. In quantum mechanics, complex phases encode interference, and physical observables come from
PHYS 214: Energy eigenstates: Energy eigenstates (stationary states) are solutions to the time-independent Schrödinger equation Ĥψₙ = Eₙψₙ, where Ĥ is the Hamiltonian operator. These states have definite energy Eₙ and time evolution ψₙ(t) = ψₙ(0)e^(-iEₙt/ℏ). Probability density |ψₙ|² is time-independent (hence 'stationary'). General states are superpositions of energy eigenstates. Energy quantization emerges from boundary cond
ME 404: Quantum mechanics: Quantum mechanics fundamentals relevant to statistical thermodynamics include energy quantization, wave-particle duality, uncertainty principle, and the Schrödinger equation. Molecules possess discrete energy levels for translational, rotational, vibrational, and electronic motion. Quantum numbers characterize these states. Energy level spacing determines which modes contribute to heat capacity at given temperatures. The correspondence principle shows quantum results approach classical at high quantum numbers. Fermi-Dirac and Bose-Einstein statistics apply to indistinguishable particles; Boltzmann statistics approximate classical limits. Understanding quantum mechanics enables calculation of molecular energy levels, prediction of thermodynamic properties from first principles, and explanation of temperature-dependent behaviors like freezing out of vibrational modes at low temperatures..
PHYS 214: The wave function: The wave function ψ(x,t) is a complex-valued function containing complete information about a quantum system's state. Born's interpretation: |ψ(x,t)|² is the probability density of measuring position x at time t. Wave functions must be normalized (∫|ψ|²dx = 1), single-valued, and continuous. The Schrödinger equation governs wave function evolution. Measuring observables 'collapses' the wave functi
PHYS 214: Wave equations: The wave equation ∂²ψ/∂t² = v²∂²ψ/∂x² describes wave propagation, relating temporal evolution to spatial curvature. Solutions include traveling waves ψ(x,t) = A sin(kx - ωt) and standing waves. The dispersion relation ω = vk connects frequency to wavelength. In quantum mechanics, the Schrödinger equation is the wave equation for matter waves, with complex wave functions. Understanding classical wa
ME 404: Statistical thermodynamics appli: Applications of statistical thermodynamics span diverse engineering problems including property prediction for new substances, high-temperature gas behavior, chemical reaction equilibria from molecular data, thermal radiation, and quantum gases. Statistical methods calculate entropy, specific heats, and equilibrium constants directly from molecular parameters without empirical fitting. Applications to real gases explain departures from ideality. Radiation thermodynamics derives Planck's law and Stefan-Boltzmann law from photon statistics. Fermi-Dirac statistics model electrons in semiconductors. Understanding these applications demonstrates statistical thermodynamics' power, connecting molecular-scale physics to engineering design of combustion systems, propulsion, materials processing, and emerging technologies like quantum computing and nanodevices..
MATH 441: Mechanical oscillators (un/dam: Mechanical oscillators are modeled by mx'' + cx' + kx = F(t), where m is mass, c is damping, k is spring stiffness, and F is external force. Undamped (c=0), unforced (F=0): simple harmonic motion x = A cos(ω₀t + φ), where ω₀ = √(k/m) is natural frequency. Damping creates three regimes: underdamped (oscillations decay), critically damped (fastest return without oscillation), overdamped (slow return). Forced oscillations with F = F₀ cos(ωt) produce resonance when driving frequency ω ≈ ω₀, causing large amplitude. Understanding oscillators is crucial for vibrations, circuits (LC), and wave phenomena..
MATH 285: Higher order linear equations: Higher-order linear differential equations involve derivatives of order two or higher, with the unknown function and its derivatives appearing linearly. These equations model phenomena like mechanical vibrations, electrical circuits, and wave propagation. The general solution combines the complementary (homogeneous) solution with a particular solution. Understanding their structure, including line
MATH 285: Undamped vibrations: Undamped vibrations model oscillatory systems with no energy dissipation, described by second-order differential equations with constant coefficients and no damping term. Solutions are purely sinusoidal, representing perpetual oscillation at the system's natural frequency. Understanding undamped vibrations provides the foundation for analyzing real systems with damping and forced oscillations, suc
ME 340: Natural frequencies and modal os: Natural frequencies are the discrete frequencies at which systems oscillate freely, determined by system properties (mass, stiffness, geometry) rather than external forcing. Modal oscillations refer to vibration in a single mode shape at the corresponding natural frequency. Each mode represents an independent oscillation pattern where all points move synchronously at the mode's natural frequency.
MATH 285: Damped vibrations: Damped vibrations incorporate energy dissipation into oscillatory systems through a damping term proportional to velocity. Depending on damping strength, systems exhibit underdamped (oscillatory decay), critically damped (fastest return without oscillation), or overdamped (slow non-oscillatory return) behavior. These equations model real physical systems like shock absorbers, RLC circuits, and str
PHYS 211: Harmonic waves: Harmonic waves are sinusoidal disturbances propagating through a medium, characterized by wavelength, frequency, amplitude, and wave speed. They satisfy the wave equation and transport energy without net material transport. Understanding wave parameters, the relationship v = fλ, and mathematical representation using sine or cosine functions is fundamental to acoustics, optics, and electromagnetic
ME 340: Natural frequencies and normal m: Natural frequencies are the characteristic frequencies at which MDOF systems oscillate freely without external forcing, while normal modes (mode shapes) are the specific spatial patterns of motion at each natural frequency. During normal mode oscillation, all points move sinusoidally at the same frequency and pass through equilibrium simultaneously. Each mode shape represents an independent oscill
MATH 285: The wave equation: The wave equation is a hyperbolic partial differential equation describing oscillatory phenomena that propagate through space at finite speed. It models vibrating strings, sound waves, electromagnetic waves, and seismic waves. Solutions represent traveling or standing waves and typically involve superposition of sinusoidal modes. The wave equation exhibits characteristic properties like reflection
ME 340: Second-order systems: Second-order systems are governed by second-order differential equations of the form: ẍ + 2ζωₙẋ + ωₙ²x = f(t), involving acceleration, velocity, and position terms. These systems contain two energy storage elements (like mass and spring) and can exhibit oscillatory behavior. Characterized by natural frequency ωₙ and damping ratio ζ, second-order systems show diverse responses: underdamped (oscilla
ME 340: Applications to beat generation: Beat generation occurs when two closely-spaced natural frequencies are excited, creating periodic amplitude modulation where energy transfers between modes. This phenomenon can be exploited in vibration absorbers (tuned mass dampers) which are auxiliary mass-spring systems attached to a primary structure. When properly tuned, the absorber's natural frequency matches the problematic excitation freq
ME 340: Exponentially decaying harmonic: Exponentially decaying harmonic signals combine oscillatory and decay behavior, expressed as x(t) = Ae^(-ζωₙt)cos(ωdt + φ), where A is amplitude, ζωₙ is the decay rate, ωd is the damped natural frequency, and φ is phase. These signals characterize underdamped second-order system responses, appearing in vibrating structures, damped electrical circuits, and countless engineering applications. The ex
ME 340: Complex numbers: Complex numbers, expressed as a + bi where i = √(-1), are essential mathematical tools in dynamics for representing sinusoidal motion and analyzing linear systems. Euler's formula (e^(iωt) = cos(ωt) + i sin(ωt)) allows compact representation of oscillatory behavior, converting differential equations into algebraic equations. Complex numbers naturally encode both magnitude and phase information, si
MATH 285: Fourier series: Fourier series represent periodic functions as infinite sums of sines and cosines with frequencies that are integer multiples of a fundamental frequency. This powerful decomposition allows any reasonable periodic function to be expressed in terms of simple harmonic components. Fourier series are foundational in solving partial differential equations, signal processing, and analyzing any phenomenon
PHYS 211: Superposition: The superposition principle states that when multiple waves overlap, the resulting displacement is the algebraic sum of individual wave displacements. This linear principle enables understanding of interference (constructive and destructive), standing waves, and beats. Superposition is fundamental to wave physics, explaining phenomena from noise-canceling headphones to quantum mechanics, and enabl
MATH 441: Second order linear des: Second-order linear DEs have form ay'' + by' + cy = g(x), where a, b, c are constants or functions. Homogeneous if g(x) = 0; nonhomogeneous otherwise. General solution = homogeneous solution (complementary) + particular solution. For constant coefficients, try y = e^(rx), yielding characteristic equation ar² + br + c = 0. Roots determine solution form: distinct real roots (y = c₁e^(r₁x) + c₂e^(r₂x)), repeated roots (y = (c₁ + c₂x)e^(rx)), complex roots (y = e^(αx)(c₁cos(βx) + c₂sin(βx))). These equations model oscillations, circuits, and mechanical systems..
ME 340: Harmonic excitation and resonanc: When continuous systems experience harmonic forcing at a natural frequency, resonance occurs with theoretically unbounded amplitude growth (bounded by damping in real systems). The spatial distribution of resonant vibration matches the corresponding mode shape. Distributed forcing (pressure, body forces) couples differently to various modes based on spatial correlation between forcing pattern and
MATH 285: Eigenvalue problems: Eigenvalue problems in differential equations seek functions satisfying a differential operator equation where the function equals a scalar multiple (eigenvalue) of itself, subject to boundary conditions. These problems determine special values (eigenvalues) for which non-trivial solutions (eigenfunctions) exist. Eigenvalue problems are fundamental to vibration analysis, quantum mechanics, and par
MATH 441: Spirals: Spiral points occur when 2×2 system has complex conjugate eigenvalues λ = α ± βi with α ≠ 0. Stable spiral (α
ME 340: Nonlinear systems: Nonlinear systems contain terms that violate superposition, including products of variables, higher-order terms, or nonlinear functions. Unlike linear systems with predictable, scalable responses, nonlinear systems exhibit phenomena like amplitude-dependent frequency, multiple equilibria, limit cycles, chaos, and jump phenomena. Many real systems are inherently nonlinear (large-angle pendulums, ge
PHYS 212: Maxwell's displacement current: Maxwell introduced displacement current to complete Ampère's law, recognizing that changing electric fields (even in vacuum) create magnetic fields just as currents do. This modification predicts electromagnetic waves: self-propagating oscillations of coupled electric and magnetic fields traveling at light speed. Maxwell's equations unified electricity, magnetism, and optics, predicting radio wave
ME 340: Harmonic excitation: Harmonic excitation involves applying sinusoidal forcing functions to dynamic systems, mathematically represented as F(t) = F₀cos(ωt) or F₀sin(ωt). This is fundamental in vibration analysis because many real-world forces are periodic (engine vibrations, rotating machinery, electromagnetic forces) and because Fourier analysis allows decomposing arbitrary periodic inputs into harmonic components. Sy
MATH 285: Fourier sine series: Fourier sine series represent odd functions on an interval using an infinite sum of sine functions with integer multiples of a fundamental frequency. Each coefficient is computed by projecting the function onto the corresponding sine basis function. This representation is crucial for solving partial differential equations with specific boundary conditions, particularly when the function value is z
ME 340: Damping ratio and natural freque: The damping ratio (ζ) and natural frequency (ωn) are fundamental parameters characterizing second-order systems. Natural frequency represents the frequency at which an undamped system would oscillate freely, while the damping ratio quantifies the level of energy dissipation relative to critical damping. Together, these parameters determine whether a system exhibits underdamped (oscillatory), criti
ME 340: Physical experiments with a two-: This laboratory extends single-DOF experiments to coupled systems with two masses, springs, and dampers. Students observe phenomena unique to MDOF systems including modal behavior, beat generation, and energy transfer between degrees of freedom. Experiments demonstrate how coupling coordinates leads to interactions, how two natural frequencies emerge from one-DOF components, and how mode shapes ma
PHYS 212: Electromagnetic waves: Electromagnetic waves are coupled oscillations of electric and magnetic fields propagating through space at light speed (c = 3×10⁸ m/s), requiring no medium. E and B fields are perpendicular to each other and to propagation direction, with magnitudes related by E = cB. The electromagnetic spectrum ranges from radio waves to gamma rays, all traveling at c in vacuum. Understanding EM waves is fundam
PHYS 214: Harmonic oscillator: The quantum harmonic oscillator models a particle in parabolic potential V(x) = ½kx² with equally-spaced energy levels Eₙ = ℏω(n + ½), where ω = √(k/m) and n = 0, 1, 2, ... The ground state has non-zero energy E₀ = ½ℏω (zero-point energy). Eigenstates are described by Hermite polynomials. The harmonic oscillator is exactly solvable and models molecular vibrations, phonons in solids, and quantum fi
TAM 412: Eigenvalue and eigenvector deco: Symmetric matrices (like mass and stiffness matrices in dynamics) can be diagonalized by orthogonal transformations: K = ΦΛΦᵀ, where Φ contains orthonormal eigenvectors (mode shapes) and Λ is diagonal with eigenvalues (related to natural frequencies). This spectral decomposition reveals fundamental system properties: eigenvalues determine stability and resonance, eigenvectors define independent os
MATH 442: Flows, vibrations, and diffusi: Three fundamental PDE types model distinct physics: (1) Hyperbolic (wave equation): ∂²u/∂t² = c²∂²u/∂x² describes vibrations with finite propagation speed - waves on strings, sound, electromagnetic waves. (2) Parabolic (diffusion equation): ∂u/∂t = α∂²u/∂x² describes diffusive processes - heat flow, chemical diffusion, smoothing. (3) Elliptic (Laplace equation): ∂²u/∂x² + ∂²u/∂y² = 0 describes steady states - electrostatics, steady temperature. Classification determines mathematical properties, solution methods, and physical interpretation. Understanding these canonical equations provides foundation for general PDE theory..
MATH 442: The wave equation: The wave equation ∂²u/∂t² = c²∂²u/∂x² models propagating disturbances with speed c. D'Alembert's solution for infinite domain: u(x,t) = f(x-ct) + g(x+ct), representing right-moving and left-moving waves. Initial conditions u(x,0) = φ(x) and ∂u/∂t(x,0) = ψ(x) determine f and g. Solutions exhibit finite propagation speed - disturbances travel at speed c, creating sharp wavefronts. Energy conserved for wave motion. Extensions: multi-dimensional waves, damped waves, forced vibrations. Wave equation is fundamental to acoustics, optics, electromagnetism, and seismology..
TAM 412: Free and forced vibrations: Free vibrations occur without external forcing after initial disturbance, characterized by natural frequencies and decay rates determined by system properties. The motion is a superposition of normal modes: q(t) = Σcᵢφᵢe^(λᵢt), where φᵢ are mode shapes and λᵢ are eigenvalues. Forced vibrations result from sustained external excitation, with steady-state response depending on forcing frequency rela
MATH 285: Fourier cosine series: Fourier cosine series represent even functions on an interval using an infinite sum of cosine functions with integer multiples of a fundamental frequency, plus a constant term. Coefficients are determined by projecting the function onto cosine basis functions. This representation is essential for solving partial differential equations with symmetric boundary conditions, such as heat flow in insula
ME 310: Superposition: Superposition in fluid mechanics exploits the linearity of governing equations (for incompressible, inviscid flow) to combine simple flow solutions into complex patterns. Elementary solutions include uniform flow, sources, sinks, doublets (source-sink pairs), and vortices. For example, superposing uniform flow with a doublet creates flow around a cylinder; adding circulation generates lift. This m
MATH 285: Interactive graph: traveling w: Traveling waves are wave patterns that propagate through space carrying energy from one location to another at constant velocity. Interactive visualizations allow students to adjust parameters like wave speed, amplitude, frequency, and initial disturbances to see how waves move, reflect at boundaries, and interact. This helps understand the d'Alembert solution to the wave equation and the relation
MATH 241: Stokes's theorem: Stokes's theorem is a fundamental result relating the surface integral of the curl of a vector field over a surface to the line integral of the field around the surface's boundary curve. It generalizes Green's theorem to three dimensions and states that circulation around a boundary equals the total curl through the enclosed surface. This theorem is essential in physics for understanding electroma
MATH 285: Interactive graph: standing wa: Standing waves are wave patterns that appear stationary, formed by the superposition of two waves traveling in opposite directions with the same frequency and amplitude. In interactive visualizations, students can manipulate parameters like frequency, boundary conditions, and initial conditions to observe how nodes (points of zero amplitude) and antinodes (points of maximum amplitude) form. This t
MATH 285: Undetermined coefficients: The method of undetermined coefficients finds particular solutions to inhomogeneous linear differential equations with constant coefficients when the forcing function has a special form (polynomials, exponentials, sines, cosines). The method guesses a solution form with unknown coefficients, substitutes into the equation, and solves for these coefficients. This technique is efficient and practical
PHYS 212: Polarization: Polarization refers to the orientation of electromagnetic wave oscillations, particularly the electric field vector direction. Light can be linearly polarized (oscillating in one plane), circularly polarized (rotating), or unpolarized (random orientations). Polarization occurs through reflection, scattering, or passing through polarizing filters. Understanding polarization is essential for LCD dis
MATH 285: Variation of parameters: Variation of parameters is a general method for finding particular solutions to inhomogeneous linear differential equations that works even when the forcing function doesn't have a special form. Instead of assuming the parameters in the complementary solution are constants, this method treats them as unknown functions and derives equations to determine them. While computationally intensive, it is
CHEM 102: Intro to electromagnetic radia: Electromagnetic radiation is energy that travels through space as waves, characterized by wavelength and frequency. It encompasses a broad spectrum including radio waves, microwaves, infrared, visible light, ultraviolet, X-rays, and gamma rays. The relationship between wavelength (λ) and frequency (ν) is inversely proportional, connected by the speed of light (c = λν). This form of energy requires
CHEM 102: Photoelectric effect: The photoelectric effect describes the emission of electrons from a material's surface when light of sufficient frequency strikes it. Einstein explained this phenomenon by proposing that light behaves as discrete packets of energy called photons, each carrying energy proportional to its frequency (E = hν). A minimum threshold frequency is required to eject electrons, and increasing light intensity
CHEM 102: Light & energy: Light carries energy that is directly proportional to its frequency and inversely proportional to its wavelength, expressed by E = hν where h is Planck's constant. Different colors of visible light carry different amounts of energy, with violet light being most energetic and red least. When atoms absorb energy, electrons transition to higher energy levels; when they emit light, electrons drop to l
ME 360: Review of harmonic signals: Foundational mathematical treatment of sinusoidal waveforms characterized by amplitude, frequency, and phase, forming the building blocks of signal processing through Fourier decomposition. Harmonic signals include sine and cosine functions expressible as exponential functions through Euler's formula, exhibiting periodic behavior with fundamental frequency and integer harmonics (overtones). Unders
ME 360: Fourier series: Mathematical representation of periodic signals as infinite sums of harmonic sinusoids with frequencies at integer multiples of the fundamental frequency, enabling decomposition of complex periodic waveforms into constituent frequency components. Each harmonic has an amplitude and phase determined by Fourier coefficients calculated through integration over one period. Fourier series reveals that a
MATH 442: Waves with a source: Inhomogeneous wave equation ∂²u/∂t² = c²∂²u/∂x² + f(x,t) includes forcing term f representing external driving force. Solution via Duhamel's principle: superpose responses to each infinitesimal impulse. Retarded Green's function accounts for finite propagation speed - source at (y,s) affects point x at time t only if t > s + |x-y|/c (causality). Applications: forced vibrations, driven oscillations, acoustic waves with sources. Unlike diffusion sources (immediate global effect), wave sources have delayed, localized influence due to finite c..
MATH 442: Comparison of waves and diffus: Wave and diffusion equations exhibit fundamentally different behaviors. Waves: finite propagation speed c, sharp wavefronts, energy conservation, reversible in time, second-order in time (∂²u/∂t²). Diffusion: infinite propagation speed, smooth profiles, energy dissipation, irreversible in time, first-order in time (∂u/∂t). Waves maintain shape while propagating; diffusion spreads and smooths. Waves have domain of dependence (cone); diffusion instantly affects entire domain. Waves exhibit superposition cleanly; diffusion smooths irregularities. Understanding these contrasts is fundamental to PDE classification and physical interpretation..
MATH 442: Causality and energy: Causality for wave equation: solution at (x,t) depends only on initial data within domain of dependence |x - x₀| ≤ ct (cone in spacetime). Information cannot travel faster than wave speed c. Finite speed of propagation creates well-defined cause-effect relationships. Energy for wave equation E = ∫[(∂u/∂t)² + c²(∂u/∂x)²]dx combines kinetic and potential energy. For homogeneous wave equation (no sources), total energy is conserved: dE/dt = 0. Energy methods provide stability estimates and existence proofs. Conservation laws reflect physical principles underlying wave phenomena..
ME 410: One-dimensional wave motion: One-dimensional wave motion describes propagation of small pressure disturbances through gases at the speed of sound c = √(γRT). Acoustic waves are isentropic, infinitesimal perturbations superimposed on base flow. The wave equation ∂²p/∂t² = c²∂²p/∂x² governs linearized disturbances. Mach waves (characteristic lines) define disturbance propagation in supersonic flow. Finite-amplitude waves steepen into shock waves due to nonlinear effects. Understanding wave motion explains how information propagates, why shock waves form, and provides foundation for method of characteristics. Applications include shock tube analysis, supersonic inlet design, and understanding transient phenomena in high-speed flows and propulsion systems..
PHYS 214: Interference: Interference occurs when two or more waves overlap, combining according to superposition principle. Constructive interference (waves in phase) produces larger amplitude; destructive interference (waves out of phase) produces cancellation. For two coherent sources with path difference Δr, constructive interference occurs when Δr = nλ, destructive when Δr = (n+1/2)λ. Young's double-slit experiment d
PHYS 214: Diffraction: Diffraction is wave bending around obstacles and spreading through apertures, most pronounced when obstacle/aperture size is comparable to wavelength. Single-slit diffraction produces a central bright fringe with diminishing side fringes, with minima at a sin θ = nλ. Diffraction gratings with many slits produce sharp interference maxima used in spectroscopy. Diffraction limits optical resolution t
MATH 442: Solid vibrations in a ball: Wave equation in sphere (ball) requires spherical coordinates (r,θ,φ). Separation of variables: u(r,θ,φ,t) = R(r)Θ(θ)Φ(φ)T(t). Angular part yields spherical harmonics Yₗᵐ(θ,φ), eigenfunctions of angular Laplacian. Radial part yields Bessel functions or related functions. Time part: T(t) = cos(ωt) or sin(ωt). Modes characterized by (l,m,n): angular quantum numbers and radial nodes. Applications: seismology (Earth's normal modes), acoustics (spherical cavities), quantum mechanics (atomic orbitals). Reveals 3D wave structure and spherical symmetry..
MATH 442: Applications to waves/diffusio: Fourier transforms solve wave/diffusion equations on infinite line (-∞,∞). Diffusion: ∂u/∂t = α∂²u/∂x² becomes ∂û/∂t = -αk²û (ODE in k), giving û(k,t) = û(k,0)e^(-αk²t). Solution: u(x,t) = F⁻¹[û(k,t)]. High frequencies (large |k|) decay faster - smoothing effect. Waves: ∂²u/∂t² = c²∂²u/∂x² becomes -ω² = -c²k², so ω = ±ck (dispersion relation). Solution: u = f(x-ct) + g(x+ct). Transform reduces PDEs to algebraic equations, explicitly showing frequency behavior..
PHYS 214: Photons: Photons are discrete packets (quanta) of electromagnetic radiation with energy E = hf and momentum p = h/λ, where h is Planck's constant. Einstein's photoelectric effect explanation required photon concept - light comes in particles, not just waves. Photons are massless, travel at speed c, and exhibit both particle and wave properties (wave-particle duality). Photon absorption and emission by atom
TAM 470: Wave equation: Wave equation ∂²u/∂t² = c²∇²u describes elastic waves, acoustics, electromagnetics. Hyperbolic PDE: finite propagation speed c, preserves discontinuities. D'Alembert solution (1D): u(x,t) = f(x-ct) + g(x+ct) - superposition of left and right traveling waves. Numerical methods: central difference in space and time (explicit, second-order, requires CFL ≤ 1), Newmark methods (implicit family), spectr
MATH 442: Legendre functions: Legendre polynomials Pₗ(x) arise from Laplace's equation in spherical coordinates, forming orthogonal basis on [-1,1]. Satisfy Legendre equation: (1-x²)y'' - 2xy' + l(l+1)y = 0. First few: P₀=1, P₁=x, P₂=(3x²-1)/2. Orthogonality: ∫₋₁¹ Pₗ(x)Pₘ(x)dx = 0 for l≠m. Used in expansion of functions on sphere (zonal harmonics). Recurrence relations enable efficient computation. Applications: potential theory, scattering problems, quantum mechanics (angular momentum). Generating function: (1-2xs+s²)⁻¹/² = Σ Pₗ(x)sˡ..
TAM 470: Fourier transformation: Fourier transform decomposes function into frequency components: F(ω) = ∫f(x)e^(-iωx)dx. Inverse: f(x) = ∫F(ω)e^(iωx)dω. Converts differentiation to multiplication: d/dx → iω. Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT) for sampled data, computed efficiently via Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) in O(N log N). Applications: solving PDEs in spectral methods, signal processing, filtering. Convolution becomes multip
MATH 442: What is a partial differential: A partial differential equation (PDE) relates a function of multiple independent variables to its partial derivatives. Examples: wave equation ∂²u/∂t² = c²∂²u/∂x², heat equation ∂u/∂t = α∂²u/∂x², Laplace equation ∂²u/∂x² + ∂²u/∂y² = 0. PDEs model phenomena depending on multiple variables (space and time, multiple spatial dimensions). Classification: order (highest derivative), linearity, and type (elliptic, parabolic, hyperbolic). Solutions are functions of multiple variables. PDEs are fundamental to physics, engineering, and applied mathematics, describing waves, diffusion, electromagnetism, and fluid flow..
MATH 442: Even, odd, periodic, and compl: Function symmetries simplify Fourier analysis. Even function: f(-x) = f(x), Fourier series has only cosines (even harmonics). Odd function: f(-x) = -f(x), series has only sines (odd harmonics). Periodic function: f(x+p) = f(x) extends to full Fourier series. Complex exponential form: f(x) = Σcₙ e^(inπx/L) combines sine/cosine using Euler's formula e^(iθ) = cos θ + i sin θ. Complex form often simplifies calculations. Understanding symmetries reduces computational effort and provides physical insight into mode structure..
ME 420: Special functions: Special functions appear in analytical solutions of heat conduction problems in specific geometries or coordinate systems. Bessel functions (J_n, Y_n) arise in cylindrical coordinates, modified Bessel functions (I_n, K_n) in steady problems, Legendre polynomials in spherical geometries, and error functions in semi-infinite transient problems. These functions have well-studied properties, orthogonality relations, and tabulated values. While numerical methods often supersede analytical solutions, understanding special functions provides physical insight, validates numerical codes, and enables quick parameter studies for standard geometries. They connect mathematical physics to engineering heat transfer, demonstrating how mathematical tools solve practical problems..
ECE 205: Superposition: The superposition theorem states that in linear circuits with multiple independent sources, the response (voltage or current) equals the sum of responses from each source acting alone with others deactivated (voltage sources shorted, current sources opened). This powerful technique simplifies analysis of complex circuits by decomposing into simpler single-source problems. Only applies to linear el
MATH 285: Integrating factors: An integrating factor is a function that, when multiplied by a non-exact first-order linear differential equation, transforms it into an exact equation that can be integrated directly. The method systematically finds this factor, typically an exponential function, making the equation solvable through straightforward integration. This powerful technique extends the range of solvable first-order equ
CHEM 102: The hydrogen atom: The hydrogen atom is the simplest atomic system, consisting of one proton and one electron. It serves as the fundamental model for understanding atomic structure and electron behavior. The electron occupies discrete energy levels around the nucleus, and transitions between these levels involve absorption or emission of specific wavelengths of light. The hydrogen atom's spectral lines provided cruc
MATH 442: Completeness and the gibbs phe: Gibbs phenomenon occurs when Fourier series approximates function with jump discontinuity. Partial sum exhibits ~9% overshoot near discontinuity, persisting as N→∞ (overshoot doesn't vanish, just narrows). Series converges in L² sense (mean-square) but not uniformly at discontinuity. Ringing persists indefinitely. Related to finite bandwidth approximation of sharp transitions. Demonstrates difference between pointwise, uniform, and L² convergence. Important in signal processing (ringing in filtered signals), numerical PDEs, and understanding convergence types. Completeness holds in L² despite Gibbs ringing..
TAM 412: Legendre transformations: Legendre transformations are mathematical operations that convert between different but equivalent formulations of physics problems by changing independent variables. In mechanics, they transform the Lagrangian L(q,q̇,t) depending on coordinates and velocities into the Hamiltonian H(q,p,t) depending on coordinates and momenta. The transformation is defined as H = Σpᵢq̇ᵢ - L, where pᵢ = ∂L/∂q̇ᵢ. Th
ME 310: Compressible flow: Compressible flow occurs when density changes are significant, typically at Mach numbers above 0.3 where gas velocity becomes comparable to sound speed. Unlike incompressible flow, pressure waves propagate at finite speed (speed of sound), creating phenomena like shock waves (abrupt property changes), expansion waves, and choked flow where mass flow rate becomes independent of downstream condition
ME 310: Pitot tubes: A pitot tube measures fluid velocity by converting kinetic energy to pressure. It consists of a tube with an opening facing the flow (measuring stagnation pressure P₀) and static pressure taps (measuring static pressure P). Applying Bernoulli's equation, velocity is calculated from V = √(2(P₀-P)/ρ). Pitot-static tubes combine both measurements in one probe. These devices are widely used in aircraf
ME 310: Potential flow: Potential flow theory analyzes inviscid, irrotational flows where velocity derives from a scalar potential function (V = ∇φ). The velocity potential satisfies Laplace's equation (∇²φ = 0), a linear PDE allowing superposition of elementary solutions like uniform flow, sources, sinks, doublets, and vortices. Combining these building blocks creates complex flow patterns around cylinders, spheres, and
TAM 335: Laminar and turbulent layers: Boundary layers transition from laminar to turbulent based on local Reynolds number. Laminar boundary layers have smooth velocity profiles described by Blasius solution: δ ∝ √(νx/U), friction coefficient c_f ∝ Re_x^(-1/2). Turbulent boundary layers have fuller velocity profiles (flatter in center, steeper at wall), grow faster: δ ∝ x^(4/5), and higher friction: c_f ∝ Re_x^(-1/5). Transition occurs at Re_x ≈ 5×10^5 for smooth flat plates, affected by surface roughness, pressure gradient, and free-stream turbulence. Understanding layer types enables drag prediction: turbulent boundary layers contribute more skin friction but resist separation better than laminar, affecting total drag on bodies..
TAM 335: Drag forces of immersed bodies: Drag experiments measure total drag force on various body shapes (sphere, cylinder, airfoil, flat plate) immersed in flow using force balances or momentum analysis. Drag coefficient C_D = D/(½ρV²A) determined over range of Reynolds numbers reveals characteristic drag crisis for spheres/cylinders where C_D drops dramatically at critical Re due to boundary layer transition delaying separation. Shape effects demonstrate dramatic differences: streamlined bodies have much lower drag than bluff bodies. Students observe transition, measure forces, and compare with literature values. Understanding drag measurement validates theory, demonstrates Reynolds number and shape effects, and develops experimental skills essential for aerodynamic testing..
TAM 335: Laminar and turbulent flows: Laminar flow exhibits smooth, orderly motion with fluid particles moving in parallel layers without mixing between layers. Turbulent flow is chaotic with random, three-dimensional velocity fluctuations and strong mixing. Reynolds number Re = ρVD/μ determines the regime: laminar (Re
ME 320: External and internal flows: External flows involve fluid moving over external surfaces (flat plates, cylinders, spheres) without geometric confinement, developing velocity and thermal boundary layers from leading edges. Internal flows occur within confined geometries (pipes, ducts) where flow is constrained by walls. For internal flows, developing regions transition to fully developed flow where velocity and temperature prof
ME 411: Flow stability and transition: Flow stability analyzes whether small disturbances grow (instability) or decay (stability), determining transition from laminar to turbulent flow. Linear stability theory examines infinitesimal disturbance evolution using Orr-Sommerfeld equation. Critical Reynolds number defines the threshold for linear instability. Subcritical transition occurs via finite-amplitude disturbances below critical Re. Growth mechanisms include shear instability (Kelvin-Helmholtz), centrifugal instability (Taylor-Couette), and thermal instability (Rayleigh-Bénard). Transition scenarios vary by geometry: boundary layers, pipes, jets, and wakes exhibit different paths to turbulence. Understanding stability enables flow control for drag reduction or mixing enhancement and predicts operating regimes for fluid systems..
ME 411: Some limiting exact solutions (s: Exact analytical solutions exist for simplified viscous flow problems providing physical insight and validation benchmarks for numerical methods. Steady solutions include fully developed pipe flow (Poiseuille flow), Couette flow between moving plates, and stagnation point flow. Unsteady solutions include suddenly accelerated plate (Stokes' first problem), oscillating plate (Stokes' second problem), and decaying vortex. These solutions reveal fundamental behaviors: diffusion of momentum from walls, boundary layer growth as √(νt), and vorticity diffusion. Despite simplified geometries and assumptions, exact solutions illuminate physics of viscous diffusion, provide scaling laws, and serve as limiting cases for more complex flows..
ME 410: Review of basic fluid dynamic an: This review establishes foundational concepts for compressible flow analysis including conservation laws (mass, momentum, energy), thermodynamic properties and relations (enthalpy, entropy, speed of sound), isentropic relations, normal shock relations, and dimensionless parameters (Mach number, Reynolds number). The energy equation for compressible flow includes significant kinetic energy terms. The Mach number M = V/c (velocity/sound speed) characterizes compressibility importance. Stagnation properties represent the state achieved through isentropic deceleration to zero velocity. Understanding these fundamentals prepares students for advanced topics in supersonic/hypersonic flow, shock waves, expansion waves, and high-speed aerodynamics crucial for aerospace, propulsion, and high-speed transportation applications..
ME 411: Buoyancy-induced flows: Buoyancy-induced (natural convection) flows arise from density differences caused by temperature variations in gravitational fields. Warm fluid rises, cool fluid descends, creating circulation without external forcing. The Grashof number Gr = gβΔTL³/ν² characterizes buoyancy relative to viscous forces. The Rayleigh number Ra = Gr·Pr determines flow regime: conduction-dominated (low Ra), laminar convection, or turbulent convection. Applications include building ventilation, electronics cooling without fans, atmospheric and oceanic circulations, and heat exchangers. Correlations relate Nusselt number to Ra: Nu = C·Ra^n. Understanding natural convection enables passive cooling system design, building energy efficiency, and prediction of environmental flows driven by thermal gradients..
ME 310: Numerical solutions: Numerical solutions in fluid dynamics involve discretizing governing equations (continuity, momentum, energy) into algebraic equations solved computationally. Methods include finite difference, finite volume, finite element, and spectral methods applied to meshes or grids representing the flow domain. Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD) enables solving complex, realistic problems impossible to solv
ME 310: Control volume equations for con: The continuity equation for a control volume expresses conservation of mass: the rate of mass accumulation within the control volume equals the net mass flow rate entering minus leaving. For steady flow, mass in equals mass out. Written as ∂/∂t(∫ρdV) + ∫ρ(V⋅n)dA = 0, it can be simplified for steady, one-dimensional flow to ṁin = ṁout or ρ₁A₁V₁ = ρ₂A₂V₂. For incompressible flow, this reduces to vol
TAM 335: Flow kinematics: Flow kinematics describes fluid motion patterns without considering forces causing motion. Key concepts include velocity field V(x,y,z,t), pathlines (particle trajectories), streamlines (tangent to velocity at instant), and streaklines (locus of particles passing through a point). Flow classification includes steady vs. unsteady (time-dependent), uniform vs. non-uniform (spatial variation), and one/two/three dimensional. Eulerian description analyzes properties at fixed locations; Lagrangian follows individual particles. Visualization techniques include dye injection, particle tracking, and computational rendering. Understanding kinematics provides language for describing flow patterns before applying dynamics (forces and equations of motion)..
TAM 335: Continuity principles: Continuity equation expresses conservation of mass for fluid flow: mass entering control volume minus mass leaving equals mass accumulation. For steady flow, mass in equals mass out. Differential form ∂ρ/∂t + ∇·(ρV) = 0 applies at every point. For steady incompressible flow, ∇·V = 0 means velocity field is divergence-free. One-dimensional form: ρ₁A₁V₁ = ρ₂A₂V₂; for incompressible flow: A₁V₁ = A₂V₂ = constant (volumetric flow rate). Continuity is the simplest conservation law, enabling calculation of unknown velocities or areas given flow rates. It's essential for analyzing pipes, nozzles, channels, and any flow system..
ME 412: Solving navier-stokes equations: Solving Navier-Stokes equations numerically requires handling coupled nonlinear momentum equations with continuity constraint (incompressible) or coupled with energy/density (compressible). Challenges include pressure-velocity coupling (no explicit pressure equation for incompressible flow), convective nonlinearity requiring linearization, and satisfaction of continuity (divergence-free velocity). Methods include projection methods (fractional step), SIMPLE algorithm (Semi-Implicit Method for Pressure-Linked Equations), and fully coupled approaches. Staggered grids or pressure stabilization prevent checkerboard oscillations. This represents the culmination of CFD fundamentals, enabling simulation of realistic fluid flows from laminar to turbulent regimes in engineering applications..
ME 412: Fractional step and simple: Fractional step (projection method) and SIMPLE algorithm are two major approaches for pressure-velocity coupling in incompressible flows. Fractional step splits momentum equations into predictor (convection-diffusion neglecting pressure) and corrector (pressure Poisson equation projecting velocity to divergence-free space) stages. SIMPLE iteratively solves momentum equations with guessed pressure, derives pressure correction from continuity violation, and updates velocity and pressure. Variants (SIMPLEC, SIMPLER, PISO) improve robustness and convergence. These algorithms transform the challenging incompressible Navier-Stokes problem into sequential solution of simpler sub-problems, enabling practical CFD simulations. Understanding these methods is essential for implementing or using commercial CFD software..
ME 412: Role of numerical solutions: Numerical solutions enable solving complex thermo-fluid problems lacking analytical solutions due to geometry complexity, nonlinear phenomena, or coupled physics. Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD) and Computational Heat Transfer (CHT) discretize governing equations into algebraic systems solved by computers. Numerical methods extend engineering analysis beyond simplified models to realistic designs, enabling virtual prototyping, parametric optimization, and visualization of detailed flow and temperature fields. Applications span all engineering disciplines: aerospace, automotive, energy, HVAC, materials processing, and biomedical devices. Understanding when and how to use numerical methods, their limitations, and validation requirements is essential for modern engineering practice where simulation complements but doesn't replace physical testing and analytical understanding..
ME 412: Finite volume discretization: Finite volume method discretizes conservation laws by integrating over control volumes, ensuring conservation by construction. Unlike finite differences (point-wise), finite volumes balance fluxes across cell faces, inherently conserving mass, momentum, and energy. This approach naturally handles unstructured meshes and complex geometries. Face flux reconstruction (central, upwind, QUICK schemes) determines accuracy and stability. The method is the foundation of most commercial CFD codes (ANSYS Fluent, OpenFOAM). Finite volume formulation directly reflects physical conservation principles: what flows into a cell minus what flows out equals accumulation. Understanding finite volume methods is essential for modern computational fluid dynamics and heat transfer..
ME 451: Sensors and actuators: Sensors and actuators are the physical interfaces enabling computer-controlled manufacturing. Sensors measure position (encoders, resolvers), temperature (thermocouples), force (load cells), proximity, and process variables, providing feedback for closed-loop control. Actuators convert electrical signals into mechanical motion through servo motors, stepper motors, hydraulic cylinders, or pneumatic pistons. Selection criteria include resolution, accuracy, response time, force/torque capacity, and cost. Integration involves signal conditioning, noise reduction, and calibration. Understanding sensor-actuator characteristics is essential for designing and operating automated manufacturing systems..
ME 452: Nc programming, verification and: NC programming workflow includes creating programs (manual G-code, CAM-generated, or conversational), verification through simulation checking for collisions and errors, and execution on the machine with setup, tool loading, and running. Verification tools provide graphical simulation of tool paths, material removal, and collision detection before risking actual machining. Program execution requires workpiece setup, tool offset measurement, and dry run testing. Modern systems integrate CAD/CAM/CNC in digital workflows reducing errors and setup time. Understanding the complete programming-to-execution workflow ensures safe, efficient production..
ME 445: Teach pendant programming: Teach pendant programming involves manually guiding a robot through desired positions using a handheld control device, recording these configurations for playback. The operator jogs the robot using pendant buttons or joystick to each waypoint, saves the position, and builds a program from these recorded points. This intuitive method requires no formal programming knowledge and allows rapid setup of simple repetitive tasks. However, it's limited to point-to-point motions, lacks flexibility for complex logic, and requires the robot to be taken offline during teaching. It remains popular in industrial settings for welding, painting, and material handling..
ME 452: Language, communication and hmi: NC language and communication encompasses G-code programming language syntax, communication protocols between controller and external devices, and human-machine interface design. G-code provides standardized commands for motion (G00, G01), spindle control (M03), and auxiliary functions. Communication protocols (RS232, Ethernet, DNC) enable program transfer and remote monitoring. HMI services include graphical displays, touchscreens, pendant controls, and software interfaces enabling operators to program, monitor, and troubleshoot machines. Effective HMI design balances functionality, usability, and safety, significantly impacting productivity and error rates..
ME 447: Programming with python: Python programming provides the foundational language skills for implementing soft robotics simulations and algorithms. Core concepts include variables and data types, control flow (loops, conditionals), functions, and object-oriented programming. Python's readability and extensive libraries make it ideal for scientific computing. Students learn to structure code, debug errors, and write reusable modules. For soft robotics applications, Python enables prototyping algorithms, processing experimental data, visualizing results, and integrating with numerical libraries. Strong programming fundamentals are essential for implementing sophisticated simulation and optimization frameworks..
TAM 470: Computations as mappings: Computational algorithms are mappings transforming input data to output results. Mathematical view: f: X → Y where X is input space (geometry, boundary conditions, material properties), Y is output space (displacements, stresses, velocities). Discrete approximation maps infinite-dimensional function spaces to finite-dimensional vector spaces. Algorithm properties include: existence (solution exist
ME 370: Programming labs using python to: Programming labs teach using Python for computational mechanism analysis, automating tedious calculations for position, velocity, acceleration, and forces. Students implement algorithms for kinematic analysis (solving loop equations, computing velocities), dynamic analysis (applying Newton-Euler equations), and visualization (animating mechanism motion, plotting results). Programming enables analy
ME 451: Nc controllers: NC controllers are the embedded computer systems that interpret programs, generate motion commands, and manage machine tool operations. Controllers execute interpolation algorithms to create smooth paths from programmed waypoints, implement feedback control to achieve precise positioning, and coordinate auxiliary functions like coolant and tool changes. Modern controllers feature conversational programming, built-in cycles, and adaptive control capabilities. Architecture includes processors, memory, I/O interfaces, and specialized motion control hardware. Controller selection affects machine capabilities, programming ease, and production efficiency..
CS 101: Statements: Statements are complete instructions telling computer to perform actions: assignment (x = 5), conditionals (if-else), loops (for, while), function calls, and input/output. Control flow statements direct program execution order. Understanding statement syntax, control structures, and program flow enables writing programs that make decisions, repeat tasks, and solve computational problems. Statement
ME 340: Simulink realizations: Simulink is MATLAB's graphical programming environment for modeling, simulating, and analyzing dynamic systems using block diagrams. Students construct virtual system models by connecting blocks representing mathematical operations, creating executable simulations without writing code. Simulink automatically solves the underlying differential equations numerically and provides rich visualization t
CS 101: Symbolic algebra & calculus: Symbolic computation manipulates mathematical expressions algebraically without numerical evaluation, using libraries like SymPy. Capabilities include equation solving, differentiation, integration, series expansion, and simplification with exact results. Unlike numerical methods giving approximate answers, symbolic methods provide exact mathematical expressions. Understanding symbolic computing e
CS 101: Functions and scripts: Functions are reusable code blocks performing specific tasks, accepting inputs (parameters) and returning outputs. Scripts are files containing sequences of commands executed together. Functions promote modularity, reusability, and organization. Understanding function definition, calling, scope, and script structure enables writing organized, maintainable code for scientific computing. Functions a
CS 101: Libraries: Libraries are collections of pre-written functions and tools that extend programming language capabilities without writing code from scratch. Common scientific libraries include NumPy (numerical computing), Pandas (data analysis), Matplotlib (plotting), and SciPy (scientific computing). Understanding how to import, use, and explore library functions enables leveraging existing solutions for comple
MATH 221: Newton's method: Newton's Method is an iterative algorithm for approximating roots of equations f(x) = 0 using the recurrence relation: x_(n+1) = x_n - f(x_n)/f'(x_n). Starting from an initial guess x_0, the method generates successively better approximations by following tangent lines to x-intercepts. Newton's Method typically converges rapidly (quadratically) when near a root, but may fail if f'(x_n) ≈ 0, the in
ME 446: Final project, controller in act: The final project applies learned control concepts to implement and demonstrate a working robot controller on actual hardware or high-fidelity simulation. Students design, tune, and validate a control system for a specified task, integrating trajectory planning, dynamic modeling, and feedback control. This hands-on experience reinforces theoretical knowledge through practical implementation challenges like dealing with measurement noise, actuator saturation, model uncertainties, and real-time computation constraints. The project demonstrates controller performance through metrics like tracking accuracy, disturbance rejection, and task completion, bridging the gap between control theory and robotic practice..
MATH 221: Continuity: A function f is continuous at x = a if: (1) f(a) is defined, (2) lim(x→a) f(x) exists, and (3) lim(x→a) f(x) = f(a). Intuitively, a function is continuous if its graph can be drawn without lifting the pencil. Discontinuities include removable (hole), jump (sudden change), and infinite (vertical asymptote). Continuous functions have important properties including the Intermediate Value Theorem. Stu
MATH 221: The chain rule: The chain rule differentiates composite functions: d/dx[f(g(x))] = f'(g(x))·g'(x), often written as dy/dx = (dy/du)(du/dx). This 'outside-inside' rule first differentiates the outer function evaluated at the inner function, then multiplies by the derivative of the inner function. The chain rule is essential for differentiating complex expressions and appears throughout calculus. Students learn to
ME 452: Modeling and analysis of nc driv: NC drive system modeling represents motors, amplifiers, mechanical transmission, and feedback sensors mathematically to predict dynamic behavior. Models include motor torque-speed characteristics, amplifier transfer functions, mechanical inertia and compliance, friction effects, and encoder resolution. Analysis techniques examine frequency response, stability margins, positioning accuracy, and disturbance rejection. System identification determines model parameters from experimental data. Understanding component models enables control design, performance prediction, troubleshooting, and specification of upgrades. Accurate models are essential for advanced control strategies and virtual commissioning..
ME 452: Analysis and design of the nc lo: The NC control loop is a feedback system that continuously compares commanded position with actual position and adjusts motor drive to minimize error. Analysis involves characterizing loop dynamics, stability, bandwidth, and tracking performance using control theory. Design decisions include selecting controller gains, compensation schemes, and feedforward strategies to achieve desired positioning accuracy, speed, and disturbance rejection. Loop design must balance fast response against stability and overshoot. Understanding the NC loop enables optimizing machine performance, diagnosing control issues, and specifying system requirements..
ME 460: Introduction to computer-control: Overview of digital control systems where computers or microcontrollers replace analog controllers, executing control algorithms on sampled measurements and generating discrete-time control signals. Architecture includes sensors converting physical variables to electrical signals, ADC sampling analog signals, digital controller computing control laws, DAC reconstructing continuous commands, and actuators implementing control actions. Benefits include flexibility through software modification, complex algorithm implementation, data logging, and adaptive control. Challenges include sampling effects, quantization, computational delays, and discrete-time stability analysis. Applications span industrial automation, automotive engine control, robotics, and process control requiring programmable, reconfigurable control strategies..
ME 461: Characterization and control of: Experimental identification of permanent magnet DC motor parameters (resistance, inductance, torque constant, back-EMF constant, inertia, friction) through electrical and mechanical tests, then implementing feedback speed or position control. Characterization includes measuring R and L from step voltage response, determining torque constant from stall current-torque relationship, extracting inertia and friction from no-load acceleration. Identified parameters populate model for controller design. Control implementation includes PWM voltage drive, encoder velocity estimation, PID control law, and current limiting protection. Applications include robotic joint control, conveyor drives, and precision positioning requiring model-based control design validated experimentally on actual hardware..
ECE 206: H-bridge control of dc motor (c: Closed-loop control measures actual motor speed (using encoder or tachometer) and adjusts PWM duty cycle to match desired setpoint. Feedback loop compares reference speed to measured speed; error drives controller (typically PID: Proportional-Integral-Derivative). Controller output adjusts H-bridge PWM to minimize error. Advantages: maintains consistent speed despite load changes, compensates for
ME 452: Implementation of reference puls: Implementing interpolators involves coding algorithms that generate position commands at regular time intervals. Reference pulse interpolators output step pulses at rates proportional to desired velocities for stepper or pulse-driven servos. Reference word interpolators output digital position values for direct-command servo systems. Implementation requires handling acceleration/deceleration, feedrate calculation, and real-time execution. Linear interpolators use incremental stepping algorithms (like DDA or Bresenham's), while circular interpolators iterate angle or use parametric equations. Practical implementation addresses computational efficiency, numerical precision, and synchronization across multiple axes..
ME 461: Sensors, transducers, and actuat: Devices interfacing between physical world and electronic control systems through measurement (sensors/transducers converting physical quantities to electrical signals) and actuation (actuators converting electrical signals to physical actions). Common sensors include encoders (position), strain gages (force), thermocouples (temperature), accelerometers (vibration), and pressure sensors. Actuators include motors (rotary/linear motion), solenoids (discrete positioning), piezo actuators (precision motion), and heaters (thermal control). Interfacing requires understanding electrical characteristics, calibration, signal conditioning, and power drive requirements. Applications span all mechatronic systems requiring sensing for feedback and actuation for control authority..
ME 461: Open loop motor control and fric: Controlling motor motion without feedback by applying predetermined voltage commands based on desired motion and motor model, while compensating for friction torques causing steady-state errors and stick-slip behavior. Open-loop control includes voltage-velocity relationships and inertial compensation for accelerations. Friction models include Coulomb (constant opposing torque), viscous (velocity-proportional), and Stribeck (decreasing from stiction at low velocity). Compensation adds feedforward terms canceling friction effects. Limitations include sensitivity to parameter errors and disturbances. Applications include simple positioning tasks, low-cost systems without encoders, and initial motion establishment before closed-loop engagement in systems requiring friction compensation..
ME 360: Speed control of a dc electric m: Closed-loop feedback control system regulating motor rotational velocity by measuring speed with encoder or tachometer, comparing to reference setpoint, and adjusting armature voltage through PWM amplifier based on controller output. Control strategies include PID regulation balancing tracking performance, disturbance rejection, and stability. System dynamics include electrical time constant (L/R)
ME 360: Modeling and identification of a: Experimental and analytical techniques determining motor parameters (resistance, inductance, inertia, friction, torque constant) from measured responses to step voltage inputs. Step response reveals first-order (armature electrical) and second-order (mechanical) dynamics through time constant extraction and curve fitting. Identification process includes applying step input, measuring voltage and c
ME 360: Pid control of a hydraulic cylin: Feedback control system regulating hydraulic actuator position, velocity, or force by measuring cylinder displacement with LVDT or potentiometer, computing PID control law based on error signal, and commanding servo valve opening proportional to controller output. Hydraulic system dynamics include valve flow characteristics, cylinder flow continuity, fluid compressibility effects, and load dynamic
ME 455: Monitoring and control: Monitoring and control in micro-manufacturing employ sensors and feedback systems to ensure process stability and part quality despite disturbances and variations. Monitoring detects tool wear, process anomalies, and quality deviations through force, acoustic, vibration, and vision signals. Control systems adjust process parameters maintaining performance within acceptable ranges. Challenges include sensor resolution, signal-to-noise ratio, and control authority at micro-scales. Advanced approaches use adaptive control and machine learning. Understanding monitoring and control principles enables robust micro-manufacturing achieving consistent results..
TAM 335: Specific speed: Specific speed N_s = NQ^(1/2)/(gH)^(3/4) (or N_s = NP^(1/2)/H^(5/4) for turbines) is a dimensionless parameter characterizing turbomachine type based on operating conditions at best efficiency. Low N_s indicates radial (centrifugal) machines suited for high head, low flow; high N_s indicates axial machines for low head, high flow; intermediate N_s suggests mixed-flow designs. Specific speed guides machine selection: given head and flow requirements, N_s determines appropriate type. It consolidates effects of speed, flow, and head into single parameter independent of machine size. Understanding specific speed enables proper turbomachine selection, preliminary design, and comparison of different machine types..
ME 462: Servo design: Designing control systems for asymptotic tracking of reference commands and rejection of disturbances by augmenting plant dynamics with integrators or reference/disturbance signal generators (internal models) to eliminate steady-state errors. Servo compensator structure incorporates models of command and disturbance signals (steps, ramps, sinusoids), with feedback gains designed for stability and performance. Type number indicates steady-state tracking capability - Type 1 tracks steps, Type 2 tracks ramps. Design methods include integral control, state augmentation, and servo loop transfer function shaping. Applications include precision positioning systems, cruise control, and process regulation requiring zero steady-state tracking error..
ME 460: Frequency domain design of contr: Designing controllers by shaping open-loop frequency response (loop-shaping) to meet closed-loop specifications including bandwidth for tracking speed, low-frequency gain for disturbance rejection, gain/phase margins for robustness, and high-frequency rolloff for noise attenuation. Design involves adding compensator poles and zeros modifying magnitude and phase to achieve desired crossover frequency and margins. Lead compensation increases phase margin improving stability, lag compensation increases low-frequency gain reducing steady-state error. Nichols charts facilitate closed-loop specification. Method provides intuitive visualization and direct handling of frequency-domain requirements. Applications include servo system design balancing performance and robustness..
ME 445: Feedback control: Feedback control continuously measures the robot's state and adjusts control inputs to minimize errors between desired and actual behavior. Sensors provide information about joint positions, velocities, forces, or end-effector pose, which is compared with reference trajectories. The controller computes corrective actions based on errors, compensating for disturbances, model uncertainties, and external forces. Feedback control architectures range from simple PID controllers to advanced model-based and adaptive controllers. Stability, tracking accuracy, disturbance rejection, and robustness are key performance considerations. Feedback is essential for practical robot operation in uncertain, dynamic environments..
ME 462: Pole placement design: State feedback control design method where controller gain matrix K is selected to place closed-loop eigenvalues (poles) at desired locations in the complex plane, achieving specified transient response characteristics including settling time, damping ratio, and overshoot. For system ẋ = Ax + Bu, control law u = -Kx + r places closed-loop poles at eigenvalues of (A-BK). Ackermann's formula or Bass-Gura algorithm compute K for single-input systems, while multi-input systems require additional optimization criteria. Requires full state measurement or observer. Applications include designing controllers meeting specific performance specifications for aerospace, robotics, and process control systems..
ME 460: Root locus control design: Systematic procedure for designing controllers by specifying desired closed-loop pole locations corresponding to performance requirements (damping ratio, natural frequency, settling time), then determining controller parameters placing poles at these locations. Dominant pole approximation simplifies higher-order systems to second-order characteristics. Root locus plots show how varying gain moves poles, guiding gain selection. Adding compensator poles and zeros reshapes locus paths enabling pole placement at desired locations. Method translates time-domain specifications directly to s-plane pole locations, then to controller parameters. Applications include positioning servo design where transient response requirements (overshoot, settling time) specify pole locations guiding systematic controller synthesis..
ME 460: Internal model principle: Control design principle stating that perfect asymptotic tracking and disturbance rejection requires incorporating a model of reference/disturbance signals into the controller structure. For example, tracking constant references requires integral action (1/s), sinusoidal tracking requires resonant controller at disturbance frequency, and ramp tracking requires double integration. Principle explains why PID controllers work (I-action models step disturbances), why resonant controllers reject periodic disturbances (model sinusoids), and guides design of specialized controllers for specific signal classes. Applies to any output regulation problem. Applications include rejecting periodic disturbances in rotating machinery and tracking ramp references in motion control..
ME 445: Tracking and disturbance rejecti: Tracking and disturbance rejection are key control objectives ensuring robots accurately follow desired trajectories despite external forces and model uncertainties. Tracking control minimizes the error between desired and actual states over time, requiring proper controller design with sufficient gain and appropriate dynamics. Disturbance rejection addresses unexpected forces like payload variations, friction, or external contacts by using feedback to detect and compensate for these perturbations.
