Effective controllers balance tracking performance with disturbance rejection while maintaining stability, often using techniques like integral control, state estimation, and feedforward compensation..
ME 460: Robustness and performance trade: The fundamental tension in control design where improving performance (fast tracking, tight regulation, disturbance rejection) typically reduces robustness (sensitivity to model uncertainty, parameter variations, noise), and vice versa. High-gain controllers provide good performance but are sensitive to unmodeled dynamics and noise. Conservative low-gain designs are robust but sluggish. Optimal design balances these competing objectives through sensitivity functions quantifying closed-loop properties. Waterbed effect illustrates that reducing sensitivity at some frequencies increases it at others. Understanding tradeoffs guides specification of achievable requirements and selection of appropriate design compromises. Applications include all control systems requiring reliable performance despite uncertainties..
ME 462: Ct and dt servo design: Designing servo controllers achieving asymptotic tracking of references and disturbance rejection in both continuous-time (CT) and discrete-time (DT) domains through state augmentation incorporating internal models of reference and disturbance signals. CT design uses s-domain techniques, DT uses z-domain. Augmented system includes plant states plus integrators or signal generators ensuring zero steady-state error by internal model principle. Design methods include LQR/LQG on augmented system, two-degree-of-freedom structures separating tracking and regulation, and frequency-domain loop-shaping. Applications include motion control, process regulation, and any system requiring precise command following and disturbance rejection implemented on digital or analog platforms..
ME 460: Lead and lag compensations: Transfer function modifications improving closed-loop performance through phase lead or gain boost. Lead compensators (s+z)/(s+p) with p>z add positive phase around geometric mean frequency improving phase margin and transient response, useful for stabilizing systems with inadequate phase. Lag compensators with z>p increase low-frequency gain reducing steady-state errors without affecting stability margins, positioned below crossover frequency. Lead-lag combines both for improved transient response and steady-state accuracy. Design involves selecting corner frequencies balancing phase contribution, gain effects, and bandwidth. Applications include stabilizing servomechanisms and improving disturbance rejection in process control..
ME 445: Pid control: PID (Proportional-Integral-Derivative) control is a widely-used feedback controller combining three terms to minimize tracking errors. The proportional term provides control action proportional to current error, the integral term eliminates steady-state errors by accumulating past errors, and the derivative term provides damping by responding to error rate of change. Each term has a tunable gain (Kp, Ki, Kd) that must be adjusted for desired performance. Despite its simplicity, PID control effectively handles many robotic systems, though it may struggle with highly nonlinear dynamics or significant coupling between joints..
ME 460: Pid control with trajectory foll: Extending PID control to track time-varying reference trajectories rather than constant setpoints by incorporating feedforward compensation based on desired trajectory derivatives. Position control benefits from velocity feedforward, velocity control from acceleration feedforward, reducing feedback burden and tracking errors. Trajectory planning generates smooth reference paths satisfying velocity and acceleration limits, with common profiles including trapezoidal velocity and S-curve acceleration. Combined feedforward-feedback structure (two-degree-of-freedom) provides fast tracking from feedforward while feedback compensates for disturbances and model errors. Applications include robotic motion control, CNC machining, and automated guided vehicles requiring precise path following..
ME 460: Basic control actions: Fundamental controller building blocks including on-off control (bang-bang, thermostat-like switching), proportional control (output proportional to error), integral control (accumulating error over time), and derivative control (responding to error rate). Each action has characteristic effects: on-off causes oscillation around setpoint, proportional reduces but doesn't eliminate steady-state error, integral eliminates offset but may cause overshoot, derivative improves transient response but amplifies noise. Understanding individual actions and their combinations enables systematic controller synthesis. Real implementations require practical considerations like integral windup prevention, derivative filtering, and output saturation. Applications span all feedback control from simple thermostats to sophisticated industrial regulation..
ME 460: Pid controllers: Three-term controllers combining proportional action (P: responding to current error), integral action (I: eliminating steady-state error by accumulating past errors), and derivative action (D: anticipating future error from rate of change). Control law u(t) = Kp·e(t) + Ki·∫e(τ)dτ + Kd·de/dt balances tracking performance, disturbance rejection, and noise sensitivity. P-gain affects speed and stability, I-action eliminates offsets but may cause overshoot, D-action improves transient response but amplifies noise. Tuning methods include Ziegler-Nichols, Cohen-Coon, and optimization approaches. PID dominates industrial control due to simplicity, effectiveness, and operator familiarity across temperature, flow, pressure, and level control applications..
ME 460: Control system design by root lo: Graphical technique plotting closed-loop pole locations as controller gain varies from zero to infinity, enabling visualization of gain's effect on transient response and stability. Root locus rules (angles, asymptotes, breakaway points) generate plots from open-loop poles and zeros. Desired closed-loop pole locations corresponding to performance specifications (settling time, overshoot) determine required gain. Additional poles/zeros (compensators) reshape locus achieving desired pole placement. Method provides intuitive understanding of how feedback gain affects stability and performance, enabling systematic design meeting time-domain specifications. Applications include servo controller tuning and compensator design for mechanical positioning systems..
ME 462: Output feedback: Control design using measured outputs rather than full state, either through static output feedback u = -Ky or dynamic compensators combining observer and state feedback. Static output feedback is simpler but more restrictive, solved through optimization or search methods. Dynamic output feedback (observer-based controller) provides greater flexibility, designed via separation principle where controller and observer are designed independently. Closed-loop stability and performance depend on combined controller-observer dynamics. Trade-offs include implementation complexity versus achievable performance. Applications include controlling systems with limited sensors where full state measurement is economically or physically impractical, common in industrial processes and aerospace systems..
ME 445: Feed forward control: Feed forward control anticipates required control actions based on the desired trajectory and system model, rather than reacting to errors. By computing expected actuator commands from known dynamics and the desired motion, feedforward reduces the burden on feedback control and improves tracking accuracy. In robotics, feedforward compensation accounts for predictable effects like gravity, inertia, and Coriolis forces. While feedforward doesn't compensate for disturbances or model errors (requiring feedback for that), it significantly improves performance when combined with feedback control, enabling faster, more accurate trajectory tracking..
ME 462: Dt control fundamentals: Core concepts of discrete-time control including digital controller structures (difference equations), discrete-time transfer functions relating z-transformed inputs to outputs, frequency response using discrete-time Fourier transform, stability analysis through unit circle pole locations, and performance specifications translated to discrete-time domain. Design approaches include direct digital design (working in z-domain from start) and digital redesign (discretizing continuous controllers using Tustin, ZOH, or matched pole-zero methods). Understanding enables leveraging discrete-time domain advantages including exact modeling of sampling effects and nonlinear control implementations infeasible in analog. Applications span all digitally implemented control systems..
ME 462: Control objectives: Formal specifications defining desired closed-loop system behavior including stability (bounded responses), tracking performance (following reference commands with specified accuracy and speed), disturbance rejection (minimizing external influence), robustness (maintaining properties despite uncertainty), constraint satisfaction (input/output limits), and optimization criteria (minimum fuel, time, energy). Objectives translated to mathematical requirements like bandwidth, margins, settling time, overshoot, and cost function weights. Trade-offs require prioritization and multi-objective optimization. Clear objectives guide systematic design and enable validation. Applications include all control systems requiring precisely stated goals for systematic synthesis and verification rather than ad-hoc tuning..
ME 460: Phase and gain margins: Quantitative stability measures for feedback systems indicating robustness to gain changes and time delays. Gain margin is additional gain (in dB) before instability, measured at frequency where phase crosses -180°. Phase margin is additional phase lag (in degrees) before instability, measured at gain crossover frequency where magnitude is 0dB. Typical specifications require gain margin >6dB and phase margin >30° for adequate robustness. Larger margins indicate greater tolerance to modeling uncertainty, component variations, and disturbances. Understanding margins enables specification of robust designs and assessment of relative stability. Applications include ensuring reliable control despite parameter variations in industrial processes..
ME 460: Lead compensator design: Systematic procedure for designing lead compensators to improve phase margin and transient response by adding positive phase near gain crossover frequency. Design process includes: 1) determining required additional phase from desired phase margin plus safety margin, 2) calculating compensator parameter α from required phase using sin(φmax) = (1-α)/(1+α), 3) placing geometric mean frequency √(αωc) at gain crossover for maximum phase contribution, 4) adjusting gain to achieve desired crossover. Lead compensation increases bandwidth accelerating response but amplifies high-frequency noise. Applications include stabilizing systems with insufficient phase margin like servo drives and improving transient response in positioning systems..
ME 445: Transfer functions: Transfer functions mathematically describe the input-output relationship of linear time-invariant systems in the frequency domain using Laplace transforms. For robotic systems, transfer functions characterize how actuator commands relate to joint positions or velocities, capturing system dynamics including inertia, damping, and stiffness. Represented as ratios of polynomials in the complex variable s, transfer functions facilitate analysis of stability, frequency response, and control design. They enable engineers to design controllers using classical techniques like root locus and Bode plots, providing intuitive understanding of system behavior across different frequencies..
ME 461: System identification and model-: Experimental determination of dynamic system models through input-output data collection and parameter estimation, followed by controller design based on identified model rather than first-principles modeling. Identification methods include step response fitting, frequency response estimation, and parametric time-series modeling. Validation compares model predictions to measured data. Model-based control design uses identified model for pole placement, LQR, or model predictive control synthesis. Approach enables control of complex systems where analytical modeling is impractical. Applications include controlling flexible structures, thermal processes, and custom mechanical systems requiring experimental model identification followed by systematic control synthesis..
ME 462: Diophantine equations: Polynomial equations in control theory relating controller numerator and denominator polynomials to achieve desired closed-loop characteristic equation. For plant A(z)/B(z) and controller R(z)/S(z), equation AR + BS = T specifies closed-loop poles through T, solved for controller coefficients. Named after ancient Greek mathematician, these equations have integer solutions (Bezout identity). In control, solutions provide controller parameters placing closed-loop poles at desired locations or achieving model-matching. Essential for pole placement, self-tuning regulators, and polynomial control design. Applications include discrete-time controller synthesis for any system admitting polynomial representation..
ME 460: Frequency domain analysis of con: Studying system behavior based on steady-state sinusoidal responses across frequency range, using frequency response functions showing magnitude and phase versus frequency. Bode plots display gain and phase on logarithmic scales revealing bandwidth, resonant peaks, and phase margins. Nyquist plots show complex frequency response on polar coordinates enabling stability assessment. Frequency domain reveals robustness to model uncertainty, disturbance rejection characteristics, and measurement noise sensitivity. Design specifications translate to frequency domain requirements like bandwidth for tracking, low-frequency gain for disturbance rejection, and high-frequency rolloff for noise attenuation. Method complements time-domain design providing alternative perspectives on control system performance..
ME 460: Time domain analysis of linear t: Studying system response to various inputs (step, impulse, ramp) in time domain through differential equation solutions, revealing transient and steady-state behavior. Analysis includes calculating time constants, rise time, settling time, overshoot, and steady-state error characterizing performance. System order determines response complexity - first-order exhibits exponential approach to steady-state, second-order exhibits oscillatory behavior when underdamped. Initial conditions, forcing functions, and system parameters (damping ratio, natural frequency) govern trajectories. Understanding time-domain behavior enables specification of performance requirements, prediction of transient responses, and assessment of design adequacy before implementation in control systems..
ME 462: Matlab control systems toolbox f: Software functions in MATLAB for analyzing and designing control systems including model representation (tf, ss, zpk), time response (step, impulse), frequency response (bode, nyquist), stability analysis (pole, margin), controller design (place, lqr, kalman), and simulation (lsim). Functions enable rapid prototyping of controllers, visualization of system behavior, and validation of designs without custom coding. Handles continuous and discrete-time, SISO and MIMO systems. Integration with Simulink enables graphical simulation. Essential tool for modern control engineering education and practice enabling focus on concepts rather than numerical implementation details..
ME 461: Introduction to sampled time con: Extending continuous-time control theory to discrete-time domain where controller operates on sampled measurements at periodic intervals, computing control outputs applied through zero-order hold. Key concepts include z-transform (discrete equivalent of Laplace), discrete transfer functions, sampling theorem requirements, zero-order hold effects adding phase lag, and discrete-time stability (poles inside unit circle). Design methods include digital redesign (discretizing continuous controllers) and direct digital design. Understanding enables implementing control algorithms on digital computers and microcontrollers. Applications include all computer-controlled systems from automotive engine control to industrial robotics requiring discrete-time implementation..
ME 460: Time domain system identificatio: Experimental procedure determining mathematical models from measured input-output time-series data through parameter estimation techniques. Methods include least-squares fitting of difference equations to step response data, autoregressive models (ARX, ARMAX) from general excitation, and subspace methods from state-space realizations. Model validation compares predicted outputs to measured data using correlation tests and residual analysis. Model structures balance complexity (higher-order for accuracy) versus parsimony (lower-order for robustness and interpretability). Applications include obtaining models for controller design when analytical modeling is impractical, validating simulation models against experimental data, and adaptive control where models are identified online..
ME 460: Laplace transform: Integral transform converting time-domain differential equations to algebraic equations in complex s-domain, enabling simplified analysis of linear time-invariant systems. Transform converts differentiation to multiplication by s, integration to division by s, and convolution to multiplication. Transfer functions H(s) = Y(s)/U(s) characterize input-output relationships with poles determining system behavior and zeros affecting frequency response. Partial fraction expansion and inverse transform tables return solutions to time domain. Understanding Laplace methods enables root locus design, frequency response analysis, and systematic control synthesis. Applications include analyzing stability, designing compensators, and predicting system performance across industrial control applications..
ME 460: Transfer functions: Ratio of Laplace-transformed output to input (H(s) = Y(s)/U(s)) characterizing linear time-invariant system dynamics, expressed as rational polynomial with numerator zeros and denominator poles determining frequency response and transient behavior. Transfer function completely describes system assuming zero initial conditions, enabling series/parallel/feedback connection analysis through algebraic manipulation. Poles in left half-plane indicate stability, pole locations determine transient response speed and oscillation, zeros affect amplitude and phase but not stability. First-order has one pole (time constant), second-order has two poles (natural frequency, damping). Understanding transfer functions enables frequency domain design and systematic analysis of industrial control systems..
ME 462: Lqg and lqg/ltr servo design: Applying LQG (optimal stochastic control) and LQG/LTR (robustness recovery) methodologies to servo problems requiring tracking and disturbance rejection through augmentation with reference/disturbance models. Augmented plant includes original dynamics plus integrators or command generators. LQG design minimizes quadratic tracking cost and control effort. LQG/LTR recovers robustness by tuning Kalman filter. Design provides optimal tracking under uncertainty with guaranteed robustness margins. Trade-offs include tuning complexity versus systematic optimal approach. Applications include aerospace, robotics, and industrial motion control requiring optimal tracking performance with robustness despite sensor noise and model uncertainty..
ME 360: Introduction to feedback control: Control system architectures using sensor measurements to adjust actuator inputs maintaining desired system outputs despite disturbances. Feedback compares measured output to reference setpoint, generating error signal driving controller computing actuator command. Stability requires careful loop gain design preventing oscillations, analyzed using Nyquist criterion, Bode plots, or root locus metho
ME 360: Introduction to control system i: Practical aspects of realizing control algorithms in hardware including discrete-time implementation through difference equations on microcontrollers or DSPs, sampling rate selection balancing performance and computational load, anti-aliasing and reconstruction filtering, actuator saturation and rate limiting, integral windup prevention, and real-time computational constraints. Implementation cons
ME 340: System identification: System identification involves determining mathematical models of dynamic systems from experimental input-output data. Rather than deriving models from first principles, engineers apply known inputs, measure outputs, and use parameter estimation techniques to identify system parameters like time constants, natural frequencies, and damping ratios. Common approaches include time-domain fitting of st
ME 446: Pd and pid joint control: PD (Proportional-Derivative) and PID (Proportional-Integral-Derivative) joint control are classical feedback strategies for robot position regulation. PD control uses position error and velocity error to compute control torques, providing effective setpoint regulation with damping. Adding integral action (PID) eliminates steady-state errors caused by constant disturbances like gravity or friction. In robotics, gravity compensation is often added to PD control as feedforward, creating PD plus gravity control. Tuning involves selecting gains (Kp, Kd, Ki) for desired transient response and stability margins while handling robot dynamics and disturbances..
ME 446: Inverse dynamics joint control: Inverse dynamics control (also called computed torque control) uses the robot's dynamic model to linearize the nonlinear system and achieve precise trajectory tracking. The controller computes feedforward torques that exactly cancel nonlinear dynamics (inertia, Coriolis, gravity), then adds feedback control for robustness. This transforms the complex multi-link robot into decoupled linear systems for each joint. Performance depends on model accuracy - perfect models yield perfect tracking theoretically. In practice, model errors and unmodeled effects necessitate feedback. This approach enables aggressive trajectory tracking impossible with simple PID control..
ME 445: Polynomial splines: Polynomial splines are piecewise polynomial functions that create smooth trajectories by connecting multiple polynomial segments at waypoints with continuous derivatives. In robotics, cubic splines are particularly popular as they provide continuous velocity and acceleration while remaining computationally simple. Splines allow specification of boundary conditions like initial and final velocities, and can pass through multiple waypoints while maintaining smoothness. The local nature of splines means changing one segment doesn't affect distant portions of the trajectory, unlike global polynomial fits which can exhibit oscillations..
ME 445: Joint space interpolation: Joint space interpolation generates robot motion by smoothly varying joint angles between specified configurations over time. Rather than planning in Cartesian workspace, this approach directly interpolates joint positions using functions like linear, polynomial, or spline interpolation. Each joint follows an independent interpolated trajectory synchronized in time. While simple to compute and guaranteed to be kinematically feasible, joint space interpolation may produce non-intuitive end-effector paths in workspace. It's commonly used when joint configurations are specified at waypoints and the exact workspace path between them is not critical..
ME 447: Space discretization: numerical: Space discretization numerical analysis studies how dividing continuous structures into discrete elements affects solution quality. Students examine convergence behavior as element count increases, investigate accuracy of different element types (linear vs. higher-order), and analyze computational scaling. For Cosserat rods, this includes studying how many discrete segments adequately capture bending modes and how element size affects simulation accuracy. Trade-offs between resolution, accuracy, and computational cost guide practical mesh selection. Understanding spatial discretization error is crucial for reliable soft robotics simulation..
ME 445: Trajectory generation: Trajectory generation creates smooth, time-parameterized paths that specify how a robot should move from an initial configuration to a goal configuration. Unlike path planning which only determines geometric routes, trajectory generation incorporates temporal aspects including velocities, accelerations, and timing constraints. The process must respect kinematic and dynamic limits of the robot such as maximum joint velocities, accelerations, and jerks while ensuring smooth motion that minimizes mechanical wear and vibration. Good trajectories balance speed, smoothness, and adherence to physical constraints..
ME 360: Signal conversion techniques (a/: Methods for converting between analog continuous-amplitude signals and digital discrete-value representations, including analog-to-digital conversion (ADC) through sampling, quantization to finite precision levels, and encoding to binary values, and digital-to-analog conversion (DAC) reconstructing continuous signals from discrete values through zero-order hold or interpolation. ADC specifications
ME 370: Graphical linkage synthesis: Graphical linkage synthesis uses geometric constructions to design linkages achieving specified motion requirements, such as guiding a point through prescribed positions or coordinating input-output relationships. Methods include two-position synthesis (finding linkage passing through two poses), three-position synthesis, and function generation (coordinating angular relationships). Graphical tech
ME 360: Signal reconstruction: The process of recovering continuous-time signals from discrete sample sequences through interpolation, ideally using sinc function interpolation (Shannon-Whittaker) for bandlimited signals sampled above Nyquist rate, practically using zero-order hold (staircase), first-order hold (linear interpolation), or higher-order polynomial interpolation. Reconstruction filters smooth the staircase output f
MATH 231: Curves defined by parametric e: Parametric equations define curves using a parameter t: x = f(t), y = g(t), where t varies over an interval. Instead of expressing y directly as a function of x, both coordinates depend on the parameter, allowing representation of curves that fail the vertical line test and enabling description of motion with time as parameter. Parametric curves can loop, self-intersect, and trace paths multiple t
MATH 231: Calculus with parametric curve: Calculus with parametric curves adapts derivative and integral techniques to parametric forms. The slope is dy/dx = (dy/dt)/(dx/dt) where dx/dt ≠ 0, found by differentiating both parametric equations and dividing. Arc length is L = ∫√[(dx/dt)² + (dy/dt)²]dt. Surface area of revolution uses similar parametric formulas. Second derivatives d²y/dx² require the chain rule applied to dy/dx. These extens
MATH 241: Tangent planes: The tangent plane to a surface z = f(x,y) at point (x₀, y₀, f(x₀,y₀)) is the best linear approximation to the surface near that point. Its equation is z - z₀ = fₓ(x₀,y₀)(x - x₀) + f_y(x₀,y₀)(y - y₀), where fₓ and f_y are partial derivatives at (x₀,y₀). Geometrically, the tangent plane contains all tangent lines to curves on the surface through that point. The tangent plane is to surfaces what the
CS 101: Numerical algebra & calculus: Numerical methods approximate mathematical operations that lack analytical solutions or are computationally expensive symbolically. Techniques include numerical integration (trapezoidal, Simpson's), differentiation (finite differences), root finding (Newton's method), and solving differential equations (Euler, Runge-Kutta). Understanding numerical methods enables solving real-world problems where
MATH 285: Numerical methods: Numerical methods for differential equations provide approximate solutions when analytical methods fail or are impractical. Techniques like Euler's method, Runge-Kutta methods, and multistep methods generate discrete approximations by stepping through the domain using the differential equation to predict function values. These methods are essential in practice since most real-world differential eq
ME 340: Matlab computations and graphing: MATLAB provides computational tools for numerical analysis and visualization of dynamic systems. Students learn to implement numerical integration schemes, eigenvalue solvers, and Fourier transforms while creating informative plots of time histories, frequency responses, and complex-plane representations. Graphing complex-valued functions requires understanding how to display four-dimensional info
MATH 221: Summary of curve sketching: Curve sketching synthesizes derivative information to draw accurate function graphs. The systematic process includes: find domain, identify intercepts, test for symmetry, find asymptotes, determine intervals of increase/decrease using f', locate local extrema, determine concavity using f'', find inflection points, and sketch combining all information. This comprehensive analysis reveals function b
MATH 221: Implicit differentiation: Implicit differentiation finds derivatives when the relationship between variables is given implicitly (e.g., x² + y² = 25) rather than explicitly as y = f(x). The technique applies d/dx to both sides of the equation, treating y as a function of x and using the chain rule to obtain dy/dx terms. Terms with dy/dx are collected and solved for dy/dx algebraically. This method handles equations that ar
ME 170: Curved surfaces and blends: Curved surfaces in CAD represent complex free-form geometry using mathematical descriptions (NURBS, Bezier splines, etc.) enabling smooth, continuous shapes. Blends create smooth transitions between surfaces or edges, including fillets (constant or variable radius), chamfers (angled transitions), and complex surface blends. Blend quality depends on continuity: G0 (touching), G1 (tangent continuous
MATH 221: Derivative as a function: The derivative f'(x) is itself a function giving the instantaneous rate of change or slope of the tangent line at each point x in the domain. Defined as f'(x) = lim(h→0) [f(x+h)-f(x)]/h, it transforms one function into another that describes rates of change. The derivative can be interpreted geometrically (slope), physically (velocity, acceleration), or algebraically (rate of change). Students lea
ME 460: B&r automation studio programmin: Industrial automation software environment for programming programmable logic controllers (PLCs) and motion controllers using IEC 61131-3 languages (ladder logic, structured text, function block diagram) for factory automation applications. Platform provides integrated development environment for control logic programming, HMI design, motion control setup, safety system configuration, and diagnostics. Features include online debugging, simulation, version control, and library management. Understanding enables implementation of industrial control systems including packaging lines, material handling, robotics cells, and process control requiring deterministic real-time execution, robust hardware, and standardized programming following industrial automation best practices..
ME 445: Computer/robot interfacing: Computer/robot interfacing establishes communication and control connections between computational systems and robotic hardware. This involves understanding communication protocols (Ethernet, serial, fieldbus), real-time constraints, signal types (digital I/O, analog, encoder feedback), and software architectures. Interfaces must handle sensor data acquisition, actuator command transmission, and synchronization between high-level planning and low-level control. Modern systems often use middleware like ROS (Robot Operating System) to abstract hardware details and enable modular software development. Proper interfacing ensures reliable, deterministic control while supporting integration with vision systems, PLCs, and other automation components..
ME 460: Software and hardware of control: The computational infrastructure implementing control algorithms including microcontrollers or PLCs executing real-time code, I/O interfaces connecting to sensors and actuators, communication buses (Ethernet, CAN, Modbus) enabling distributed control, and development tools for programming and debugging. Software architectures include interrupt-driven task scheduling ensuring deterministic timing, state machines organizing control logic, and data structures managing sensor readings and control outputs. Hardware considerations include processor speed, memory capacity, I/O specifications, and environmental hardening. Understanding enables appropriate platform selection, reliable implementation, and troubleshooting of industrial control systems in manufacturing automation, building HVAC, and process control applications..
ME 461: Microcontroller programming and: Developing embedded software controlling microcontroller operation and configuring hardware peripherals for sensor reading, actuator control, and communication. Programming involves C/C++ or assembly language, register manipulation for peripheral configuration, interrupt service routines for real-time event handling, and timing management for control loop execution. Interfacing requires understanding electrical specifications (voltage levels, current limits), pin multiplexing, pull-up/pull-down resistors, and protection circuits. Debugging uses serial monitors, LED indicators, and hardware debuggers. Applications include implementing control algorithms, managing sensor acquisition, generating PWM for motor control, and communicating with external devices in mechatronic systems..
ME 461: Soldering and introduction to th: Hands-on laboratory introduction to essential mechatronics skills including soldering electronic components to circuit boards, connecting sensors and actuators to microcontrollers, installing and configuring development software (IDE, compiler, programmer), loading firmware onto hardware, and using basic test equipment (multimeter, oscilloscope). Soldering techniques include proper temperature, tinning, component placement, and inspection for cold joints. Hardware familiarization covers board layout, power connections, I/O pins, and protection circuits. Software setup includes toolchain installation, project creation, compilation, and programming. Foundation for subsequent laboratory exercises implementing control systems requiring competent hardware construction and software development..
ME 452: Introduction to iec61499 functio: IEC 61499 is an international standard for distributed industrial control systems using function blocks as modular software components. Function blocks encapsulate algorithms with defined inputs, outputs, and internal state, connected graphically to create control logic. This architecture supports distributed control across networked devices, enabling flexible, reconfigurable automation. In NC context, function blocks can implement custom control strategies, coordinate multi-machine operations, or integrate with factory systems. Understanding IEC 61499 enables developing portable, reusable control software for advanced manufacturing automation..
ME 447: Introduction to the unix termina: The Unix terminal provides a command-line interface for interacting with operating systems, essential for computational research workflows. Basic utilities include navigation (cd, ls, pwd), file manipulation (cp, mv, rm), text processing (grep, sed, awk), and process management. Understanding the shell environment enables automation through scripts, efficient file organization, remote server access via SSH, and version control with Git. For computational soft robotics, terminal proficiency facilitates running simulations, processing data, managing code, and utilizing high-performance computing resources..
ME 462: Matlab simulink testbed: Graphical simulation environment integrating control algorithms, plant models, sensors, actuators, and disturbances for testing control designs before hardware implementation. Simulink provides block diagram programming, numerical integration, visualization, and code generation capabilities. Testbed includes plant models, controller blocks, noise sources, and analysis tools enabling virtual testing under various scenarios. Hardware-in-the-loop capabilities connect physical components. Auto-code generation deploys tested controllers to embedded targets. Essential for validation reducing risk and cost of premature hardware testing. Applications span all control engineering domains enabling rapid prototyping, teaching, and verification before committing to hardware builds..
ME 460: Dynamic simulations with simulin: Graphical programming environment for modeling, simulating, and analyzing dynamic systems using block diagrams representing mathematical relationships between signals. Simulink enables rapid prototyping of control systems through drag-and-drop components (transfer functions, state-space blocks, nonlinearities, scopes), numerical integration of differential equations, and visualization of time responses. Capabilities include linear and nonlinear system simulation, discrete-time system modeling, multi-domain system integration, and hardware-in-the-loop testing. Code generation enables deploying designs to real-time controllers. Applications include validating control designs before implementation, testing under various scenarios, and education demonstrating control concepts through interactive simulation of industrial systems..
ECE 206: Arduino (or similar microcontro: Arduino is an open-source microcontroller platform for embedded systems programming and physical computing. Features digital I/O pins, analog inputs, PWM outputs, and serial communication. Programming in C/C++-based Arduino language using IDE. Basic structure: setup() (initialization) and loop() (continuous execution). Learn to read sensors, control actuators, implement timing, and communicate wit
ME 461: Embedded linux and i2c communica: Using Linux operating system on embedded single-board computers (Raspberry Pi, BeagleBone) for mechatronic applications requiring advanced computation, networking, and multitasking, while interfacing with sensors and actuators through I2C serial bus protocol. Embedded Linux provides full OS features (file systems, networking, multithreading) with hardware access through device drivers and GPIO libraries. I2C (Inter-Integrated Circuit) is two-wire serial protocol (SDA, SCL) enabling multi-master, multi-slave communication with addressing. Applications include vision-guided robotics, IoT devices, and advanced control systems requiring computational power, connectivity, and peripheral interfacing beyond microcontroller capabilities..
ME 461: Microcontroller architecture: Internal structure of single-chip computers integrating CPU, memory (RAM, ROM, Flash), peripherals (timers, ADC, UART, PWM), and I/O on one integrated circuit optimized for embedded control applications. Architecture includes Harvard or von Neumann organization, RISC or CISC instruction sets, interrupt handling for real-time responsiveness, clock systems, and memory-mapped I/O. Understanding enables selecting appropriate microcontroller, optimizing code for hardware constraints, and utilizing peripherals efficiently. Popular families include AVR, PIC, ARM Cortex-M, and MSP430. Applications span consumer electronics, automotive systems, industrial automation, and robotics requiring low-cost, low-power dedicated control implementation..
ME 461: Data conversion and transmission: Techniques for converting between analog and digital representations (ADC/DAC) and communicating information between devices through serial interfaces (UART, SPI, I2C), parallel buses, and network protocols (Ethernet, CAN). ADC sampling and quantization considerations include aliasing prevention, resolution requirements, and conversion timing. DAC reconstruction involves settling time and glitch management. Serial communication provides efficient wiring using protocols defining timing, framing, and error detection. Parallel communication offers speed at cost of wiring complexity. Understanding enables interfacing sensors, actuators, and processors in distributed mechatronic systems requiring reliable data exchange..
ME 461: Wall following and rc driving: Implementing mobile robot behaviors including maintaining fixed distance from walls using distance sensors (infrared, ultrasonic) through proportional control of steering, and remote manual control via radio control (RC) receiver interfacing for teleoperation. Wall-following controller measures lateral distance error, computes steering correction proportional to error, and regulates forward speed. RC interfacing decodes PWM signals from receiver channels mapping to throttle and steering commands. Sensor fusion combines multiple distance measurements for robust wall detection. Applications include autonomous navigation in corridors, warehouse logistics, and human-supervised exploration requiring shared autonomy combining automatic behaviors with manual override capability..
ME 445: Basic terminology and structure: Robot terminology defines fundamental concepts: manipulator (mechanical arm), end-effector (tool mounted on arm), links (rigid segments), joints (connections allowing motion - revolute/rotary or prismatic/linear), degrees of freedom (DOF - independent motions), workspace (reachable positions), payload (carrying capacity), and repeatability (precision returning to positions). Robot structure includes kinematic chain (link-joint arrangement), actuation system (motors, pneumatics, hydraulics), sensors (position, force, vision), and control system. Understanding terminology enables communicating about robots, reading specifications, and analyzing capabilities. Standard terminology facilitates collaboration and technology transfer across organizations..
ME 445: Historical development of robots: Robotics history traces from ancient automata through industrial revolution mechanical devices to modern programmable robots. Key milestones include programmable machines (Jacquard loom 1801), industrial robots (Unimate 1961 by Engelberger and Devol), mobile robots (Shakey 1969), and contemporary developments in AI-enabled, collaborative, and autonomous robots. Evolution reflects advances in mechanical design, actuators, sensors, control systems, and computing. Understanding history provides context for current capabilities, limitations, and future directions. Historical perspective reveals recurring themes: automation replacing dangerous/repetitive human tasks, human-robot collaboration, and the ongoing challenge of robot intelligence and adaptability..
ME 462: Matlab and python fpga and gpu p: Software tools enabling high-level programming of FPGAs and GPUs for control applications without low-level hardware description languages. MATLAB HDL Coder generates VHDL/Verilog from MATLAB code for FPGA synthesis. GPU Coder generates CUDA from MATLAB for GPU execution. Python alternatives include PyOpenCL, Numba, and hardware libraries. Tools handle memory management, parallelization, and optimization automatically. Enable control engineers to leverage specialized hardware without deep computer engineering expertise. Applications include real-time MPC on FPGAs, GPU-accelerated optimization, and high-bandwidth control requiring specialized hardware but developed by control specialists..
ECE 206: H-bridge design and constructio: An H-bridge is a circuit using four switches (typically MOSFETs or BJTs) arranged in H-shape to control DC motor direction and speed. Four switches labeled: Q1, Q2 (high side), Q3, Q4 (low side). Forward: Q1, Q4 on → current flows left-to-right through motor. Reverse: Q2, Q3 on → current flows right-to-left. Brake: Q3, Q4 on (or Q1, Q2) → motor terminals shorted. Combining with PWM enables speed c
ME 455: Laser sensing and monitoring: Laser sensing uses laser-based measurement techniques for non-contact, high-resolution monitoring of micro-manufacturing processes. Methods include laser triangulation for displacement, laser Doppler vibrometry for vibration, laser interferometry for nanometer-scale positioning, and structured light for 3D geometry. Advantages include fast response, no mechanical contact, and high precision. Applications span tool positioning, surface measurement, and in-process quality monitoring. Understanding laser sensing principles enables implementing advanced metrology and process control in micro-manufacturing systems..
ME 453: High-performance computing: High-performance computing (HPC) applies parallel processing, GPU acceleration, and distributed computing to handle large-scale manufacturing quality data analysis. Big data from modern sensors, vision systems, and IoT devices exceeds single-computer processing capabilities. HPC techniques include parallel algorithms, MapReduce frameworks, cloud computing, and specialized hardware accelerators. Applications include real-time image inspection, complex simulation, deep learning training, and factory-wide analytics. Understanding HPC enables scaling quality monitoring and predictive analytics to meet Industry 4.0 data volumes and real-time requirements..
ME 462: Introduction to highly paralleli: Modern computing platforms exploiting parallelism for real-time control including multi-core CPUs, GPUs (Graphics Processing Units), FPGAs (Field Programmable Gate Arrays), and specialized processors. GPUs provide massive parallel processing for matrix operations in MPC and optimization. FPGAs offer reconfigurable hardware for ultra-low latency control loops. Multi-core enables distributed control architectures. Understanding architecture, programming models (CUDA, OpenCL, HDL), and deployment considerations enables leveraging computational power for advanced control algorithms requiring intensive calculations. Applications include high-bandwidth control, large-scale MPC, real-time optimization, and high-dimensional systems impossible with traditional processors..
ME 461: Digital-to-analog conversion and: Reconstructing continuous analog signals from discrete digital values through DAC (converting binary numbers to voltages) and filtering (smoothing staircase output). DAC specifications include resolution (bits), settling time (stabilization after code change), output range, and linearity. Common architectures include resistor ladder (R-2R), weighted resistor, and sigma-delta. Output typically exhibits zero-order hold (staircase), requiring lowpass filtering to smooth high-frequency sampling images. Sampling considerations ensure Nyquist criterion satisfaction. Applications include generating analog control signals for valves, analog servo drives, waveform synthesis, and test signal generation requiring microcontroller to produce analog outputs..
ME 461: Analog-to-digital conversion: Converting continuous-amplitude analog sensor signals to discrete digital values through sampling (measuring at time intervals) and quantization (mapping to finite precision levels). ADC specifications include resolution (bits determining quantization levels, 10-bit gives 1024 levels), sampling rate (measurements per second), conversion time, and reference voltage (full-scale range). Successive approximation, sigma-delta, and flash architectures offer different speed-accuracy tradeoffs. Software configuration selects input channels, reference, and timing. Anti-aliasing filtering prevents frequency folding. Applications include reading temperature, pressure, position sensors and any analog instrumentation requiring digital processing in microcontroller-based control systems..
ME 460: Frequency identification: Experimental determination of system frequency response function by applying sinusoidal or broadband excitation (chirp, noise, multisine) at known frequencies and measuring steady-state amplitude ratio and phase shift between output and input. Frequency response data reveals resonances, bandwidth, and system order. Parametric fitting to transfer function models (least squares in frequency domain) extracts poles and zeros. Non-parametric methods (FFT-based) estimate frequency response without assuming model structure. Advantages include direct frequency-domain data for loop-shaping design and robustness against transient disturbances. Applications include characterizing mechanical resonances, servo bandwidth, and process dynamics for frequency-domain controller design..
ME 445: Resolved motion rate control: Resolved motion rate control enables specifying end-effector velocities in Cartesian space while computing corresponding joint velocities to achieve that motion. Using the robot's Jacobian matrix, this approach resolves desired workspace velocities into joint space velocities through matrix inversion or pseudo-inverse operations. This method allows intuitive control where operators can command the robot to move in straight lines or specific directions in workspace, while the controller handles the complex transformation to joint velocities. It's particularly useful for teleoperation and task-space control applications..
ME 445: Symbolic math packages for robot: Symbolic math packages like Mathematica, Maple, and Python's SymPy enable analytical derivation of kinematic equations using symbolic computation rather than numerical values. These tools manipulate algebraic expressions, compute Jacobians symbolically, simplify complex trigonometric equations, and generate optimized code for real-time implementation. For robotics, symbolic computation facilitates deriving closed-form inverse kinematics solutions, computing geometric Jacobians, and analyzing singularities. The analytical expressions provide insights into robot behavior and enable efficient numerical computation after symbolic simplification and code generation..
ME 360: Laplace transforms: Mathematical transformation converting time-domain differential equations to algebraic equations in complex frequency domain (s-domain), enabling simplified analysis of linear time-invariant systems. Laplace transform converts differentiation to multiplication by s and integration to division by s, transforming convolution to multiplication. Transfer functions H(s) = Y(s)/X(s) characterize system
ME 360: Sampling theory and introduction: The process of converting continuous-time signals to discrete-time sequences by measuring values at regular intervals, governed by Nyquist-Shannon sampling theorem requiring sampling rate exceeding twice the highest signal frequency to enable perfect reconstruction. Aliasing occurs when sampling rate is insufficient, causing high-frequency components to masquerade as lower frequencies in sampled d
ME 360: Signal conditioning and a/d conv: Comprehensive process preparing analog sensor outputs for digitization including amplification to match ADC input range, filtering to remove noise and prevent aliasing, offset adjustment for proper ranging, isolation for safety and ground loop prevention, and linearization for nonlinear sensors. Conditioning circuits interface low-level sensor signals (millivolts from thermocouples, microvolt chan
ME 360: Impulse response and convolution: The impulse response h(t) characterizes a linear time-invariant system's output to an ideal impulse (infinitely narrow, unit area), completely determining system behavior for any input through convolution. Convolution integral y(t) = ∫x(τ)h(t-τ)dτ computes output as weighted superposition of time-shifted impulse responses, with weights given by input signal values. Graphical interpretation involve
ME 340: Time- and frequency-domain block: Block diagrams can represent systems in both time domain (using integrators ∫dt, differentiators d/dt, and gains) and frequency domain (using transfer functions and complex gains). Integrators are fundamental building blocks converting acceleration to velocity to displacement, while amplifiers scale signals. Understanding the equivalence between time-domain differential equations and frequency-dom
ECE 205: Op-amp- integrator: An op-amp integrator performs mathematical integration of input voltage: Vout = -(1/RC)∫Vin dt. Circuit uses capacitor in feedback path of inverting configuration. Output voltage accumulates (integrates) input over time. At DC (ω = 0), gain is infinite (open loop); at high frequencies, gain decreases as 1/ω. Integrators are used in analog computers, waveform generation (converting square waves to
ECE 206: H-bridge control of dc motor (o: Open-loop motor control uses H-bridge without feedback - command input directly determines PWM duty cycle and direction, but system doesn't measure actual motor response. Microcontroller sends PWM signals to H-bridge switches to set desired speed (duty cycle) and direction (which transistor pair active). Advantages: simple implementation, fast response. Disadvantages: no compensation for load chan
ME 371: Gear selection: Gear selection matches gear types, materials, sizes, and quality grades to application requirements considering power, speed, ratio, duty cycle, accuracy, noise, cost, and space. Selection process includes determining torque and speed, selecting type (spur, helical, bevel, worm) based on shaft arrangement and performance needs, sizing for bending and contact stress using AGMA standards, choosing m
ME 360: Electric motors (dc, ac, stepper: Actuators converting electrical energy to rotational mechanical energy through electromagnetic interactions between stator fields and rotor conductors. DC motors use commutators for continuous rotation with speed proportional to voltage and torque proportional to current, offering simple control. AC induction motors use rotating stator fields inducing rotor currents, robust but requiring variable
ME 360: System identification with frequ: Experimental method for determining system transfer functions by applying swept-sine or random excitation signals while measuring input and output with dynamic signal analyzers computing frequency response functions (FRFs) through FFT-based cross-spectral analysis. The technique measures magnitude and phase versus frequency, revealing resonances, damping characteristics, and system order. Coherenc
ME 340: Mechanical/electromechanical sys: Mechanical systems: springs (F = kx), dampers (F = cẋ), masses (F = mẍ). Electromechanical: motors (torque from current), generators (voltage from motion), sensors (voltage from displacement/velocity). Coupling: electromagnetic force/torque relates electrical and mechanical domains. DC motor model: Vₐ = Raᵢₐ + La dᵢₐ/dt + Kₑω (electrical), T = Kₜᵢₐ = Jα + Bω + Tₗ (mechanical). Transducers convert
ME 446: Force/torque control: Force/torque control regulates the forces and torques applied by the robot rather than just position or velocity. This capability is essential for tasks requiring controlled contact like assembly, polishing, deburring, and human-robot interaction. Approaches include impedance control (specifying mechanical impedance behavior), admittance control (computing motion from measured forces), and hybrid position/force control (controlling position in some directions, force in others). Force/torque sensors or observer-based methods provide feedback. Proper force control prevents damage to parts and fixtures while ensuring adequate contact for task completion..
ME 462: Dt self-tuning control with cons: Adaptive control strategy identifying plant polynomial model online using recursive parameter estimation (RLS), then redesigning controller coefficients to maintain desired closed-loop characteristic polynomial while enforcing input/output constraints. Polynomial design uses Diophantine equations relating controller parameters to desired closed-loop poles. Self-tuning adapts to changing plant dynamics by continuously updating model and controller. Constraints handled through anti-windup, saturation handling, or constrained optimization. Applications include adaptive cruise control, chemical processes with varying properties, and aerospace systems experiencing changing dynamics requiring automatic controller adjustment..
TAM 335: Turbosimilitude: Turbosimilitude applies dimensional analysis and similarity principles to turbomachinery, enabling prediction of performance at different speeds, sizes, and fluids. Affinity laws relate performance parameters: flow rate Q ∝ ND³, head H ∝ N²D², power P ∝ N³D⁵ where N is speed, D is diameter. Homologous points have same flow coefficient φ = Q/ND³, head coefficient ψ = gH/N²D², and efficiency. Testing model turbomachines predicts prototype performance when dimensionless parameters match. Specific speed N_s = NQ^(1/2)/H^(3/4) characterizes turbomachine type independent of size. Understanding similitude enables turbomachine selection, performance prediction, and experimental testing with scale models..
ME 200: Steady state and unsteady analys: Steady-state analysis assumes that all properties at any point within a control volume remain constant with time, though properties may vary from point to point. Mass, energy, and other quantities entering equal those leaving. Unsteady (or transient) analysis applies when properties change with time, such as during startup, shutdown, or charging processes. Engineers must choose the appropriate ana
ME 403: Ideal models of engine processes: Ideal engine models use simplified assumptions to enable analytical analysis and establish performance benchmarks. The air-standard Otto cycle models spark-ignition engines with isentropic compression/expansion and constant-volume heat addition/rejection. The Diesel cycle assumes constant-pressure heat addition. Dual cycles combine both constant-volume and constant-pressure combustion. These models use ideal gas with constant specific heats and neglect heat losses, friction, and incomplete combustion. Analysis yields thermal efficiency depending on compression ratio and specific heat ratio. While real engines differ significantly, ideal models provide physical insight into how compression ratio, fuel properties, and operating parameters affect efficiency, guiding design improvements..
ME 403: Thermodynamic properties of engi: Engine working fluids undergo significant composition and property changes during combustion. Fresh charge is a mixture of air and fuel vapor. After combustion, products include nitrogen, carbon dioxide, water vapor, and species from incomplete combustion (CO, unburned hydrocarbons). Properties (specific heats, gas constants) vary with temperature and composition. Real cycles account for variable specific heats, dissociation at high temperatures, and heat losses. Fuel properties (heating value, octane/cetane rating, stoichiometry) affect performance. Understanding property variations enables accurate cycle analysis predicting power, efficiency, peak pressures, and temperatures. This provides the foundation for detailed engine simulation, optimization, and emissions prediction..
ME 403: Combustion thermodynamics: Combustion thermodynamics analyzes chemical reactions releasing thermal energy in engines, focusing on fuel oxidation producing high-temperature combustion products. Key concepts include enthalpy of formation, heating values (lower and higher), adiabatic flame temperature (maximum theoretical temperature), and equilibrium composition at high temperatures. First law analysis determines energy release and temperature rise. Second law considerations reveal entropy generation and availability destruction. Chemical equilibrium governs product species concentrations including dissociation effects. Understanding combustion thermodynamics enables prediction of peak cylinder temperatures and pressures, available work potential, and theoretical efficiency limits, providing foundations for engine performance analysis and emissions formation mechanisms..
ME 403: Fuel/air cycle analysis: Fuel/air cycle analysis models engine processes more realistically than air-standard cycles by accounting for actual fuel-air mixture properties, combustion product composition, and variable specific heats with temperature. The cycle considers actual working fluid rather than pure air, tracking composition changes through combustion. Temperature-dependent properties significantly affect compression and expansion work. Dissociation at high temperatures reduces peak temperatures and available energy. This refined analysis provides more accurate predictions of indicated efficiency, peak pressure, and thermal behavior compared to ideal cycles. Fuel/air cycles bridge the gap between simple air-standard models and detailed simulation, offering improved accuracy while remaining analytically tractable..
ME 403: Spark-ignition engine combustion: Spark-ignition (SI) engine combustion involves flame propagation through premixed fuel-air mixture following spark discharge. The combustion process includes ignition delay, flame development, rapid burning, and flame termination. Flame speed depends on mixture composition, turbulence, temperature, and pressure. Abnormal combustion includes knock (autoignition of end-gas) and pre-ignition. Emissions formation mechanisms produce nitrogen oxides (NOx from high-temperature reactions), carbon monoxide (incomplete combustion), unburned hydrocarbons (wall quenching, crevices), and particulates. Three-way catalysts control emissions in stoichiometric engines. Understanding combustion physics and emissions chemistry enables engine design optimizing performance, efficiency, and environmental compliance through mixture control, ignition timing, and combustion chamber geometry..
ME 400: Chemical reactions: Chemical reactions in energy systems involve fuel combustion and other reactive processes that release or consume thermal energy. Topics include stoichiometry (reactant-product mass/mole balances), heating values (energy released per unit fuel), adiabatic flame temperature (maximum temperature achieved in complete combustion), and chemical equilibrium at high temperatures. Combustion analysis determines air-fuel ratios, exhaust gas composition, and efficiency. Understanding reaction thermodynamics, combustion chemistry, and emissions formation is essential for analyzing internal combustion engines, gas turbines, furnaces, and emerging technologies like fuel cells and chemical looping. Environmental considerations including NOx, CO, and particulate formation increasingly drive combustion system design..
ME 403: Diesel engine combustion and emi: Diesel engine combustion differs fundamentally from SI engines: fuel injected into hot compressed air autoignites and burns as a diffusion flame. The combustion process includes ignition delay (physical and chemical), premixed burn of fuel prepared during delay, mixing-controlled diffusion burning, and late combustion/burnout. Injection timing, rate shaping, and spray characteristics control combustion phasing and rate. Diesel combustion produces high NOx from high-temperature diffusion flames and particulate matter (soot) from fuel-rich zones. Trade-offs exist between NOx and particulates. Advanced strategies include exhaust gas recirculation (EGR), high-pressure injection, multiple injections, and aftertreatment (diesel particulate filters, selective catalytic reduction). Understanding diesel combustion enables optimization of efficiency and emissions compliance..
ME 400: Review of thermodynamics: A review of thermodynamics establishes the foundation for energy conversion analysis, covering fundamental concepts including systems and properties, first and second laws, entropy, and thermodynamic cycles. Key topics include energy conservation (first law), entropy generation and irreversibility (second law), properties of pure substances and ideal gases, processes (isothermal, adiabatic, isentropic), and efficiency definitions. Understanding state functions, path functions, and property relationships (equations of state) enables analysis of energy conversion devices. This review bridges undergraduate thermodynamics and advanced energy systems topics, ensuring students have the prerequisite knowledge for analyzing power generation, refrigeration, and novel energy conversion technologies..
ME 400: Direct energy conversion: Direct energy conversion transforms energy between forms without intermediate mechanical processes, typically achieving higher efficiency than conventional thermal-mechanical-electrical conversion. Technologies include fuel cells (chemical-to-electrical via electrochemical reactions), thermoelectric generators (heat-to-electricity via Seebeck effect), photovoltaics (light-to-electricity), magnetohydrodynamics (MHD), and thermionic emission. Fuel cells offer high efficiency and low emissions for stationary and transportation applications. Thermoelectrics enable waste heat recovery and cooling. Understanding governing physics, efficiency limits, materials challenges, and system integration enables evaluation and design of emerging energy conversion technologies increasingly important for distributed generation, vehicle electrification, and waste energy harvesting..
ME 400: Refrigeration cycles: Refrigeration cycles transfer heat from low to high temperature regions using work input, enabling cooling and air conditioning. The vapor-compression cycle dominates commercial applications: a compressor raises refrigerant pressure, a condenser rejects heat, an expansion valve drops pressure, and an evaporator absorbs heat from the cold space. Performance is quantified by coefficient of performance (COP = cooling effect / work input). Alternative cycles include absorption (using heat input instead of mechanical work), thermoelectric (solid-state Peltier effect), and magnetic refrigeration. Applications span food preservation, air conditioning, cryogenics, and industrial processes. Refrigerant selection balances thermodynamic performance, environmental impact (GWP, ODP), safety, and cost..
ME 401: Air cycles: Air cycle refrigeration uses air as the working fluid in a reverse Brayton cycle: compression, cooling, expansion with work extraction, and heating (refrigeration load). Unlike vapor compression, air remains gaseous throughout, simplifying design and eliminating refrigerant environmental concerns. However, COP is significantly lower than vapor compression. Applications include aircraft air conditioning (utilizing engine bleed air), gas liquefaction precooling, and specialized industrial processes. The bootstrap and simple air cycle configurations differ in heat exchanger arrangements. Understanding air cycle thermodynamics, including the effects of component efficiencies and pressure ratios, enables design of weight-critical or environmentally-sensitive cooling systems where vapor compression is impractical..
ME 404: Chemical equilibrium: Chemical equilibrium describes the composition of reactive mixtures when forward and reverse reaction rates balance, yielding no net composition change. At equilibrium, the Gibbs free energy is minimized, and the equilibrium constant K relates to standard Gibbs energy change: ΔG° = -RT ln K. Equilibrium composition depends on temperature, pressure, and initial composition, calculated using equilibrium constants and stoichiometry. Le Chatelier's principle predicts shifts with changing conditions. Applications include combustion product composition (affecting temperature and emissions), chemical reactor design, and fuel cell operation. Understanding chemical equilibrium enables prediction of species concentrations in reactive systems, essential for combustion analysis, emissions modeling, and chemical process design..
ME 401: Compressors: Compressors increase refrigerant pressure and temperature, providing circulation and pressure lift in vapor compression systems. Types include reciprocating (piston-cylinder), rotary (rolling piston, screw), scroll (orbiting spiral), and centrifugal (dynamic) compressors. Selection depends on capacity, refrigerant, efficiency, cost, reliability, and noise. Key parameters include displacement, volumetric efficiency, isentropic efficiency, and compression ratio. Variable-speed compressors improve part-load efficiency. Oil management, motor cooling, capacity modulation, and reliability affect system performance. Understanding compressor thermodynamics, performance maps, and matching to system requirements is essential for refrigeration and air-conditioning design. Compressor technology advances drive system efficiency improvements..
ME 410: Flow with friction (fanno flow): Fanno flow describes adiabatic flow through constant-area ducts with wall friction. The Fanno line on h-s diagram represents possible states connecting inlet to various outlet conditions for given mass flux and area. Entropy increases due to friction, limiting flow length before choking. In subsonic flow, friction accelerates flow toward M=1; in supersonic flow, friction decelerates toward M=1. Maximum duct length for given inlet Mach number is L_max = (1-M²)/(γM²) × (D/f). Applications include pipe flow analysis, constant-area combustors, and any situation where friction dominates. Understanding Fanno flow enables prediction of pressure drop, Mach number evolution, and choking limits in high-speed internal flows..
ME 402: Fundamental concepts of thermal: Fundamental concepts unify thermodynamics, heat transfer, and fluid mechanics for thermal system analysis. These include energy conservation (first law), entropy and irreversibility (second law), heat transfer modes (conduction, convection, radiation), fluid flow principles, property relationships, and coupling between energy, mass, and momentum transport. Understanding how these concepts integrate enables holistic system analysis rather than isolated component analysis. Real systems involve simultaneous heat transfer, fluid flow, and energy conversion. Recognizing governing equations, characteristic dimensionless numbers, and dominant physical phenomena guides modeling simplifications. This foundation enables engineers to develop appropriate mathematical models balancing accuracy and complexity for effective system design..
ME 402: Introduction to thermal systems: Thermal system design integrates thermodynamics, heat transfer, fluid mechanics, and economics to create complete systems meeting specified performance objectives. The design process encompasses problem definition, conceptual design, component selection, system simulation, optimization, and economic analysis. Systems include HVAC, power generation, refrigeration, manufacturing processes, and energy conversion. Design differs from analysis: designers make choices, manage constraints, and balance competing objectives (performance, cost, reliability). Successful design requires understanding individual components, their interactions, control strategies, and real-world constraints. This introductory topic establishes the framework, methodology, and multidisciplinary nature of thermal system engineering, preparing students for practical design challenges..
ME 401: Thermodynamic cooling processes: Thermodynamic cooling processes achieve low temperatures through various physical mechanisms including vapor compression (exploiting phase change), Joule-Thomson expansion (throttling of gases), adiabatic expansion with work extraction, magnetic cooling (exploiting magnetocaloric effect), and absorption cycles (using thermal energy as input). Each process has characteristic temperature ranges, efficiencies, and applications. Vapor compression dominates commercial refrigeration and air-conditioning. Joule-Thomson is used in gas liquefaction. Adiabatic expansion enables ultra-low temperatures. Absorption systems utilize waste heat. Understanding multiple cooling mechanisms and their thermodynamic foundations enables selection of appropriate technology for specific temperature ranges, capacity requirements, energy sources, and cost constraints..
ME 320: Modes of heat transfer: The three fundamental modes of heat transfer are conduction (heat diffusion through matter via molecular interactions), convection (heat transport by bulk fluid motion), and radiation (electromagnetic energy emission from surfaces). Conduction follows Fourier's law (q = -k∇T), convection follows Newton's law of cooling (q = hA(T_s - T_∞)), and radiation follows Stefan-Boltzmann law (q = εσAT⁴). Re
ME 401: Expansion devices: Expansion devices reduce refrigerant pressure from condenser to evaporator level while controlling refrigerant flow rate to match cooling load. Types include capillary tubes (fixed restriction), thermostatic expansion valves (TXVs with superheat control), electronic expansion valves (precise modulation), and float valves (level control). The expansion process is typically throttling (isenthalpic), producing a two-phase mixture at evaporator inlet. Proper selection and adjustment ensure optimal evaporator superheat, preventing liquid floodback to compressor while maximizing capacity. Electronic valves enable sophisticated control strategies for efficiency and comfort. Understanding expansion device operation, selection criteria, and control strategies is essential for refrigeration system design and troubleshooting..
ME 401: Single-and multistage vapor comp: Single-stage vapor compression systems use one compressor operating between evaporator and condenser pressures, suitable for moderate temperature lifts. Multistage systems employ two or more compressors with intercooling, improving efficiency and enabling larger temperature differences. Cascade systems use separate refrigerant circuits at different temperature levels, each optimized for its operating range, enabling very low temperatures. Flash intercooling and economizers further enhance performance. Multistage systems reduce compressor discharge temperature, decrease compression ratio per stage, and improve volumetric efficiency. Applications include industrial refrigeration, food freezing, cryogenic cooling, and any situation requiring large temperature lifts or very low temperatures beyond single-stage system capabilities..
ME 320: Steady-state: Steady-state heat transfer occurs when temperatures at all locations remain constant with time, though spatial temperature variations exist. Heat flow rates are constant, and energy storage terms in governing equations vanish. This simplifies analysis significantly - ordinary differential equations replace partial differential equations. Examples include heat conduction through building walls, ste
ME 401: Cryogenic refrigeration and liqu: Cryogenic systems achieve very low temperatures (below ~120 K) and liquefy gases (nitrogen, oxygen, hydrogen, helium) using advanced thermodynamic cycles. Technologies include Linde-Hampson (simple Joule-Thomson), Claude (expansion engine cycle), cascade systems, Brayton refrigerators, and Stirling coolers. Achieving cryogenic temperatures requires multiple stages, precooling, regenerative heat exchange, and work extraction during expansion. Efficiency improves through minimizing heat leaks, optimizing regenerator effectiveness, and approaching reversible processes. Applications include industrial gas production, superconducting magnets, space propulsion, medical imaging (MRI), and scientific research. Understanding cryogenic thermodynamics, property behavior at low temperatures, and specialized equipment design enables development of liquefaction and refrigeration systems..
ME 401: Absorption refrigeration: Absorption refrigeration uses thermal energy (heat) instead of mechanical work to drive the cycle, making it attractive for waste heat recovery or solar applications. A working pair (ammonia-water or lithium bromide-water) replaces the compressor: refrigerant vapor is absorbed into solution (absorber), pumped to high pressure (requiring minimal work), then driven off by heating (generator). The cycle includes condenser, expansion valve, and evaporator like vapor compression. COP is lower than vapor compression but uses low-grade heat. Applications include industrial waste heat recovery, solar cooling, combined heat and power systems, and situations where electricity is expensive or unavailable but thermal energy is abundant..
ME 401: Low temperature gas separation: Low-temperature gas separation exploits differences in condensation temperatures to separate air into components (nitrogen, oxygen, argon) or purify gases. Cryogenic distillation involves cooling air to liquefaction, then fractional distillation in columns based on boiling point differences. The process requires compression, cooling, expansion, and rectification columns. Alternatively, membrane separation or pressure swing adsorption can be used. Cryogenic air separation produces high-purity products for steel, chemical, medical, and electronics industries. Understanding phase equilibria, distillation principles, refrigeration requirements, and process integration enables design of efficient separation systems. Energy consumption is a major operating cost, driving integration with power cycles and waste heat recovery..
ME 200: Power and refrigeration cycles: Power cycles (Rankine, Brayton, Otto, Diesel) convert thermal energy from fuel combustion or heat sources into mechanical work through cyclic processes. Refrigeration cycles (vapor-compression, absorption) transfer heat from low to high temperature regions using work input, enabling cooling and air conditioning. Both involve working fluids undergoing phase changes or temperature/pressure variation
TAM 335: Characteristics of a francis re: Francis turbine experiments measure performance by varying load (generator resistance) while maintaining constant head. Measurements include discharge Q, shaft torque T, rotational speed N, and power output P. Plotting efficiency η, power P, and flow Q versus speed N at constant head generates characteristic curves showing optimal operating range. Students determine maximum efficiency point, observe speed runaway with no load, and calculate specific speed. Understanding turbine testing demonstrates how reaction turbines extract energy from flowing water, validates turbomachinery theory, and illustrates hydroelectric power generation principles. Testing reveals trade-offs between efficiency, power output, and operating flexibility..
TAM 335: Characteristics of a centrifuga: Pump characteristic experiments measure performance curves relating head H, power P, efficiency η, and NPSH (Net Positive Suction Head) to discharge Q at constant speed. Throttling valve varies flow rate while measuring differential head (pressure rise), shaft power (motor input), and suction pressure. Plotting reveals characteristic curves: head decreases with flow, power increases, efficiency peaks at design point. Students determine best efficiency point (BEP), compare with manufacturer data, and observe cavitation onset at low NPSH.
