CHEM 104: Weak acids: Weak acids are acids that only partially dissociate in aqueous solution, establishing an equilibrium between molecular and ionic forms. The equilibrium is described by the acid dissociation constant (Ka), where smaller Ka values indicate weaker acids. Weak acid calculations involve ICE tables (Initial, Change, Equilibrium) and often require the quadratic formula or simplifying assumptions when Ka
CHEM 104: Redox reactions: Redox (reduction-oxidation) reactions involve electron transfer between species, where one species is oxidized (loses electrons) and another is reduced (gains electrons). Oxidation states track electron distribution in molecules. Oxidizing agents gain electrons and are reduced; reducing agents lose electrons and are oxidized. Redox reactions can be split into half-reactions (oxidation and reductio
CHEM 104: Galvanic cells: Galvanic (voltaic) cells are electrochemical devices that spontaneously generate electrical energy from redox reactions. They consist of two half-cells with different electrodes in electrolyte solutions connected by a salt bridge. Oxidation occurs at the anode (negative terminal), releasing electrons that flow through an external circuit to the cathode (positive terminal) where reduction occurs. C
CHEM 104: Polyprotic acids: Polyprotic acids contain multiple ionizable protons that dissociate sequentially, each with its own dissociation constant (Ka1, Ka2, Ka3...). Common examples include H₂SO₄ (diprotic), H₃PO₄ (triprotic), and H₂CO₃ (diprotic). Each successive dissociation is weaker (Ka1 > Ka2 > Ka3) because removing a proton from an increasingly negative species becomes progressively more difficult. Polyprotic acid
CHEM 102: Atomic structure and isotopes: Atomic structure consists of a dense nucleus containing protons and neutrons, surrounded by electrons in specific energy levels. The number of protons (atomic number) defines the element's identity. Isotopes are atoms of the same element with identical proton numbers but different neutron counts, resulting in different mass numbers. Isotopes have nearly identical chemical properties but differ in
MATH 221: Calculating limits using the l: Limit laws are algebraic rules for evaluating limits of combinations of functions, including sums, differences, products, quotients, and compositions. Key laws state that limits distribute over addition, subtraction, multiplication, division (when denominator limit ≠ 0), and powers. The direct substitution property allows evaluation by simply plugging in the value for continuous functions. Student
CHEM 104: Entropy: Entropy (S) is a thermodynamic property measuring the disorder, randomness, or number of microstates available to a system. Higher entropy means greater disorder. Entropy increases with temperature, volume, and number of particles. Phase transitions show large entropy changes: gases have much higher entropy than liquids, which have higher entropy than solids. Standard molar entropies (S°) are alwa
CHEM 103: Using the calorimeter to measu: This laboratory application uses calorimetry to experimentally determine enthalpies of phase transitions (fusion, vaporization, solidification, condensation) by measuring heat transfer during state changes. When a substance undergoes a phase change at constant temperature, the heat absorbed or released can be calculated from temperature changes in surrounding water. For example, adding ice to warm
CHEM 102: Phase transitions: Phase transitions are changes between different states of matter: solid, liquid, and gas. Major transitions include melting/fusion (solid→liquid), freezing/solidification (liquid→solid), vaporization (liquid→gas), condensation (gas→liquid), sublimation (solid→gas), and deposition (gas→solid). Transitions involve energy changes and occur at characteristic temperatures (melting point, boiling point)
PHYS 213: Gibbs free energy: equilibrium: Gibbs free energy G = H - TS (where H is enthalpy) determines spontaneity for processes at constant temperature and pressure. A system minimizes G at equilibrium when in contact with a thermal bath (reservoir). Processes with ΔG
ME 404: Phase equilibrium: Phase equilibrium governs the coexistence of phases (solid, liquid, vapor) when phase transformations cease, achieving mechanical, thermal, and chemical equilibrium. For pure substances, the Clapeyron equation relates saturation pressure and temperature using latent heat and volume change. For mixtures, vapor-liquid equilibrium involves fugacity equality of each component across phases, enabling bubble point, dew point, and flash calculations. Raoult's law models ideal solutions; non-ideal systems require activity coefficients. Phase diagrams visualize equilibrium states. Applications include distillation design, refrigerant cycle analysis, air-conditioning psychrometrics, and any multi-phase system. Understanding phase equilibrium enables prediction of phase distributions and compositions critical for separation processes and thermal systems..
CHEM 104: Standard free energy: Standard Gibbs free energy (ΔG°) is the free energy change for a process occurring under standard conditions (1 atm pressure, 1 M concentrations, 25°C). It determines spontaneity: ΔG°
ME 320: Temperature and measurement devi: Temperature quantifies thermal energy level and determines heat flow direction. Measurement devices include thermocouples (thermoelectric voltage from junction of dissimilar metals), resistance temperature detectors (RTDs: resistance varies with temperature), thermistors (semiconductor resistance devices), infrared sensors (measure thermal radiation), and liquid-in-glass thermometers. Each has spe
ME 401: Evaporative cooling processes: Evaporative cooling reduces air temperature by evaporating water into the air stream, trading sensible heat for latent heat at constant enthalpy (adiabatic saturation). As water evaporates, air temperature decreases while humidity increases, following constant wet-bulb temperature lines on psychrometric charts. Effectiveness depends on inlet air humidity—dry climates achieve greater cooling. Direct evaporative cooling humidifies the cooled air, while indirect systems separate process and working air streams. Applications include cooling towers, evaporative coolers (swamp coolers), gas turbine inlet cooling, and agricultural applications. This energy-efficient cooling method requires minimal electricity compared to vapor-compression systems but is climate-dependent and water-consuming..
ME 320: Temperature measurement: Temperature measurement techniques quantify thermal state using various physical principles. Thermocouples generate thermoelectric voltage at junctions of dissimilar metals. RTDs (Resistance Temperature Detectors) exploit temperature-dependent electrical resistance of metals like platinum. Thermistors use semiconductor resistance changes. Infrared thermometers measure thermal radiation from surfac
ME 483: Osmotic pressure: The pressure difference required to prevent net water flow across a semipermeable membrane separating solutions with different solute concentrations, arising from entropy-driven water movement toward higher solute concentration. Osmotic pressure follows van't Hoff equation (Π = iCRT) where i is the van't Hoff factor, C is concentration, R is gas constant, and T is temperature. In biology, osmotic pressure maintains cell volume, drives water transport in kidney tubules, causes tissue swelling from trapped proteoglycans in cartilage, and influences mechanotransduction through volume-regulated channels. Cells actively regulate osmotic balance through ion pumps and organic osmolytes to prevent harmful swelling or shrinkage..
PHYS 213: Phase diagrams: Phase diagrams plot regions of thermodynamic stability for different phases (solid, liquid, gas) as functions of temperature and pressure. Boundary lines represent coexistence conditions where two phases are in equilibrium. The triple point is where all three phases coexist. The critical point marks the end of the liquid-gas boundary, beyond which the distinction disappears. Phase diagrams summari
PHYS 213: Phase boundaries: Phase boundaries are curves on phase diagrams separating regions of different phase stability. Along a boundary, two phases coexist in equilibrium with equal chemical potentials and temperatures. The Clausius-Clapeyron equation describes boundary slopes: dP/dT = ΔS/ΔV = L/(TΔV), where L is latent heat. Crossing a boundary requires adding/removing latent heat. Boundary slopes differ: solid-liquid i
CHEM 104: Non-standard free energy: Non-standard free energy (ΔG) describes spontaneity under conditions different from standard state, calculated using ΔG = ΔG° + RT ln Q, where Q is the reaction quotient. This equation shows how free energy depends on actual concentrations/pressures. When Q
CHEM 104: Spontaneity: Spontaneity describes whether a process occurs naturally without external intervention, determined by the sign of free energy change (ΔG). Spontaneous processes have ΔG
MATH 221: Rates of change in the natural: Derivatives quantify rates of change across disciplines: velocity and acceleration in physics, population growth rates in biology, marginal cost and revenue in economics, and reaction rates in chemistry. The instantaneous rate of change f'(a) measures how quickly the dependent variable changes with respect to the independent variable at a specific point. Students apply derivatives to real-world pr
ME 340: Physical experiments with a leak: These laboratory experiments provide hands-on experience with first-order system behavior using fluid systems. A leaking tank demonstrates how liquid level decreases exponentially as flow rate depends on current level (height), exemplifying a first-order system with observable time constant. The hydraulic motor experiment shows how fluid flow drives rotation, with speed dynamics governed by first-
ME 458: Patents and intellectual propert: The legal frameworks and strategic practices for protecting novel inventions, designs, and proprietary information from unauthorized use. Patents grant exclusive rights to make, use, or sell inventions for limited periods in exchange for public disclosure. In additive manufacturing, IP considerations include patentability of new geometries enabled by 3D printing, design files as copyrightable works, trade secrets around process parameters, and freedom-to-operate analyses. Understanding prior art searches, patent claims, infringement risks, licensing strategies, and international IP regimes is essential for protecting innovations and avoiding costly litigation..
ME 370: Design thinking: empathy: Empathy in design thinking means deeply understanding users' experiences, needs, frustrations, and contexts through observation, interviews, and immersion. It involves suspending assumptions, actively listening, observing body language and environment, and asking probing questions. Empathy tools include empathy maps (thinking, feeling, seeing, saying), user journey maps, and personas. The goal is
ME 447: Implementing stochastic optimiza: Implementing CMA-ES (Covariance Matrix Adaptation Evolution Strategy) involves coding an advanced evolutionary algorithm that adapts its search distribution. Key components include sampling from multivariate Gaussian distributions, updating mean and covariance matrix based on successful samples, and adapting step size. Implementation requires careful handling of matrix operations, eigenvalue decomposition, and numerical stability considerations. Students apply CMA-ES to soft robotics design optimization where the algorithm learns problem structure and efficiently navigates high-dimensional design spaces. Practical implementation reveals the algorithm's power and computational requirements..
ME 447: Introduction to modeling and sim: Inverse design uses computational optimization to discover system parameters or configurations that achieve desired behavior, reversing the typical forward design process. Rather than specifying a design and simulating its performance, inverse design specifies desired performance and searches for designs that achieve it. In soft robotics and materials, this involves simulating candidate designs' mechanical response, comparing with targets, and iteratively updating designs. Applications include designing soft actuators with specific motion patterns, materials with target properties, and structures optimized for performance metrics. Modeling and simulation provide the forward evaluations enabling automated design space exploration..
ME 447: Numerical analysis of optimizati: Numerical analysis of optimization schemes involves systematically evaluating algorithm performance through convergence studies, parameter sensitivity analysis, and benchmark comparisons. Students analyze how GA and CMA-ES perform on test functions with known optima, measuring convergence rate, computational cost, and solution quality. Analysis includes studying effect of population size, mutation rates, and other hyperparameters. Visualization of fitness landscapes, population evolution, and convergence trajectories provides insights. Understanding algorithm behavior guides appropriate selection and tuning for specific soft robotics design problems..
ME 447: Implementing stochastic optimiza: Implementing genetic algorithms (GA) involves coding the evolutionary optimization process including population initialization, fitness evaluation, selection, crossover, and mutation operators. Students create GA frameworks applicable to soft robotics design problems like optimizing actuator placement, material distribution, or control parameters. Implementation requires designing appropriate genome representations, selection strategies (tournament, roulette wheel), and genetic operators specific to the problem. Practical considerations include population sizing, convergence criteria, and computational efficiency. Hands-on implementation deepens understanding of how evolutionary principles translate to optimization algorithms..
ME 370: Ideation methods: Ideation methods generate numerous diverse solution concepts before converging on promising directions. Techniques include brainstorming (quantity over quality, defer judgment, build on others' ideas), SCAMPER (Substitute, Combine, Adapt, Modify, Put to other uses, Eliminate, Reverse), mind mapping (visual idea association), sketching, analogy thinking, and worst possible idea (then inverting). Ef
ME 475: Empathy and design embodiment: The practice of deeply understanding user experiences, environmental contexts, and stakeholder perspectives while physically manifesting design concepts through sketches, models, and prototypes. Empathy involves observing users in context, interviewing stakeholders, experiencing challenges firsthand, and considering diverse abilities and needs to ensure designs serve real-world requirements. Design embodiment translates abstract concepts into tangible representations - from rough sketches to CAD models to physical prototypes - enabling evaluation, communication, and iterative refinement. In bioinspired design, embodiment may include physical models demonstrating biological principles, proof-of-concept prototypes testing key functions, or scaled implementations evaluating performance..
ME 475: Discover state-of-the-art litera: The research methodology for identifying, accessing, and synthesizing relevant biological and engineering knowledge from academic databases, journals, patents, and biomimicry resources. Effective literature discovery requires searching biological literature (ecology, physiology, evolution journals), engineering literature (materials science, robotics, design journals), and interdisciplinary biomimetics publications. Techniques include keyword development with biological and technical terminology, database searching (PubMed, Web of Science, Google Scholar, AskNature), citation chaining to find related work, and critical evaluation of source quality and relevance. Understanding the state-of-the-art prevents reinventing solutions, identifies knowledge gaps, validates biological models, and reveals previous bio-inspired applications..
ME 475: Project-based critical design re: A comprehensive assessment of final bioinspired design projects evaluating functional performance, biological fidelity, innovation, technical execution, and presentation quality. Critical reviews examine how well the design solves the identified problem, whether biological principles were appropriately abstracted and implemented, what performance testing reveals about effectiveness, and how the solution compares to existing approaches. Evaluations include quantitative metrics (performance specifications, efficiency measurements, cost analysis) and qualitative assessments (sustainability benefits, user experience, aesthetic considerations). This culminating review demonstrates learning outcomes, validates design decisions, identifies future development opportunities, and celebrates successful translation of biological insights into engineering innovations..
MATH 221: Limits at infinity: Limits at infinity describe function behavior as x approaches ±∞, written as lim(x→∞) f(x) or lim(x→-∞) f(x). These limits determine end behavior of functions and horizontal asymptotes. For rational functions, the limit depends on degree comparison of numerator and denominator. Students learn techniques including dividing by highest power, recognizing dominant terms, and applying rules for exponen
ME 481: Prosthetics and orthotics: Assistive devices replacing missing limbs (prosthetics) or supporting and correcting existing limb function (orthotics) through mechanical design optimizing biomechanical function, comfort, and durability. Prosthetic design addresses socket interface distributing loads comfortably, mechanical components mimicking lost joint and limb functions, and control systems enabling user intent translation to device motion. Orthotic design focuses on alignment correction, motion restriction or assistance, load redistribution, and pain reduction. Biomechanical principles guide energy return in running prostheses, microprocessor control in powered knees, carbon fiber spring characteristics in ankle-foot orthoses, and socket pressure distribution. Understanding user needs, gait deviations, and device mechanics enables optimal prescription and training..
ME 475: Bio-inspiration background and m: The philosophical and practical foundation for learning from nature's 3.8 billion years of evolutionary problem-solving to create innovative engineering solutions. Bio-inspiration recognizes that organisms have evolved efficient, sustainable, and resilient strategies for sensing, moving, manufacturing, self-healing, and adapting to environmental challenges. This approach goes beyond simple biomimicry to understand underlying principles transferable to technology, offering solutions for energy efficiency, material optimization, adaptive systems, and sustainable manufacturing. Motivation stems from nature's proven track record of success, resource efficiency, closed-loop processes, and solutions that enhance rather than degrade ecosystems..
ME 475: Problem identification and produ: The process of clearly defining engineering challenges and developing initial solution concepts inspired by biological strategies. Problem identification involves recognizing unmet needs, framing challenges at appropriate abstraction levels to enable biological analogies, identifying functional requirements and constraints, and scoping boundaries. Solution concept development translates biological principles into preliminary engineering implementations, considering material feasibility, manufacturing methods, scalability, and performance targets. This phase bridges biological inspiration and practical implementation, requiring creative synthesis of biological insights with engineering knowledge while remaining flexible to iterate as understanding deepens through prototyping and testing..
ME 170: Dimension driven 3d sketching: Dimension-driven 3D sketching creates parametric 2D profiles that define feature geometry through dimensions and constraints rather than fixed coordinates. Sketches are under-constrained (flexible), fully-constrained (defined), or over-constrained (conflicting) based on dimension sufficiency. Dimensions can be driving (controlling geometry) or driven (measuring existing geometry). This approach en
ME 170: Shaded view: Shaded view renders 3D models with surfaces colored/shaded based on lighting, material properties, and viewing angle, providing realistic appearance that clarifies form and depth. Simple shading uses flat or smooth surface coloring, while advanced rendering includes reflections, shadows, and textures. Shaded views enable intuitive understanding of complex geometry, support design review without ma
ME 170: Line and text forms: Line and text forms in technical drawings follow standards for clarity and universal interpretation. Line types include visible edges (continuous thick), hidden edges (dashed), centerlines (chain thin), dimension lines (continuous thin), and extension lines. Text formatting covers dimensioning numbers, notes, specifications, and labels using standard fonts, sizes, and placement conventions. Proper
ME 170: 3d wireframe: 3D wireframe modeling represents objects as collections of points, lines, and curves in three-dimensional space, showing only edges without surfaces or volumes. This approach provides spatial visualization while being computationally simple and allowing views from any angle. However, wireframe models are ambiguous (unclear which side is 'inside'), lack mass properties, and cannot show hidden line
ME 402: Basic mathematical methods: Mathematical methods for thermal system design include solving systems of equations (linear and nonlinear), numerical differentiation and integration, root-finding algorithms, regression analysis, and optimization techniques. Iterative methods solve coupled energy balances when explicit solutions don't exist. Newton-Raphson and secant methods find roots of equations. Lagrange multipliers and gradient methods optimize with constraints. Finite difference approximations enable numerical solutions of differential equations. Understanding numerical accuracy, convergence, stability, and computational efficiency is essential. These mathematical tools enable analysis and design of complex thermal systems where analytical solutions are impossible, forming the foundation for computer-aided design and simulation..
ME 401: Heat exchangers: Heat exchangers in refrigeration and cryogenic systems transfer thermal energy between refrigerant and external fluids (air, water, brine) or between refrigerant streams. Key types include evaporators (absorbing heat from refrigerated space), condensers (rejecting heat to environment), regenerators/recuperators (internal heat exchange), and subcoolers. Design considerations include heat transfer area, flow arrangement, pressure drop, frosting (in evaporators), fouling, and refrigerant distribution. Performance depends on overall heat transfer coefficient incorporating convection, conduction, and possibly phase change. Compact heat exchangers with enhanced surfaces maximize performance in space-constrained applications. Understanding heat exchanger design and selection is critical for optimizing system capacity, efficiency, and cost..
ME 320: Transient one-dimensional heat c: Transient (unsteady) conduction occurs when temperatures change with time, described by the heat diffusion equation ∂T/∂t = α∇²T, where α is thermal diffusivity. Solutions depend on initial conditions, boundary conditions, and geometry. The lumped capacitance method applies when internal resistance is negligible (Bi
ME 420: Steady heat conduction: Steady heat conduction analyzes temperature distributions and heat flow when thermal conditions don't change with time. Governed by Laplace equation (no sources) or Poisson equation (with sources), steady conduction in 1D yields linear or nonlinear temperature profiles depending on thermal conductivity variation and geometry (plane, cylindrical, spherical). Multi-layer composite systems use thermal resistance networks analogous to electrical circuits. Applications include building insulation design, pipe insulation, furnace walls, and electronics packaging. Understanding steady conduction provides baseline performance for thermal systems and enables analytical solutions forming the foundation for more complex transient and multi-dimensional analyses..
ME 420: Lumped parameter analysis with v: Lumped parameter analysis assumes uniform temperature within the body (valid when Bi
ME 420: Unsteady heat conduction: Unsteady (transient) heat conduction describes time-dependent temperature distributions: ∂T/∂t = α∇²T. The lumped capacitance method applies when Biot number Bi
ME 340: Heat transfer: Heat transfer modeling: conduction (Fourier's law: q = -k∇T), convection (q = hA(Ts - T∞)), radiation (q = εσA(T⁴ - T∞⁴)). Energy balance: mcp dT/dt = Q̇ᵢₙ - Q̇ₒᵤₜ for lumped capacitance. Thermal resistance Rth = ΔT/Q̇ (analogous to electrical resistance). Thermal capacitance C = mcp. Time constant τ = RthC. Applications: temperature control, thermal management, coupled electromechanical systems.
ME 320: Numerical methods in conduction: Numerical methods solve complex conduction problems that lack analytical solutions due to irregular geometries, temperature-dependent properties, or complex boundary conditions. Finite difference methods discretize the heat equation into algebraic equations on a grid or mesh. Explicit methods (forward-time) are simple but stability-limited, while implicit methods (backward-time, Crank-Nicolson) ar
TAM 470: Problems, history, methodology: Computational mechanics emerged from need to solve complex engineering problems. History: hand calculations → analog computers → digital computers → modern supercomputing. Key milestones: finite difference methods (1940s), finite element method (1960s), computational fluid dynamics development. Methodology follows scientific computing paradigm: problem formulation → mathematical model → discretiza
TAM 335: Hydraulic and energy gradelines: Hydraulic Grade Line (HGL) plots total head minus velocity head: HGL = P/γ + z, representing liquid surface elevation in piezometer tubes. Energy Grade Line (EGL) plots total head including velocity: EGL = P/γ + z + V²/2g. EGL always slopes downward in flow direction (losses); HGL can rise or fall depending on velocity changes. Vertical distance from EGL to HGL equals velocity head V²/2g; from HGL to pipe centerline equals pressure head P/γ. These graphical representations visualize energy distribution in pipe systems, identify cavitation risk (HGL below pipe), and diagnose system problems. Understanding gradelines enables visual analysis of complex piping systems..
ME 420: Multidimensional steady problems: Multidimensional steady heat conduction involves temperature varying in two or three spatial dimensions: ∇²T = 0 (Laplace) or ∇²T + q'''/k = 0 (Poisson). Analytical solutions use separation of variables yielding infinite series of eigenfunctions satisfying boundary conditions. Shape factors for simple geometries enable quick calculations without detailed solutions. Numerical methods (finite difference, finite element) handle complex geometries where analytical solutions don't exist. Applications include corner effects in buildings, heat flow in composite structures, temperature distribution in electronic substrates, and thermal bridges. Understanding multidimensional conduction reveals how heat flows three-dimensionally, finding paths of least resistance unlike 1D idealization..
ME 420: Numerical methods: Numerical methods solve heat transfer problems where analytical solutions don't exist or are impractical due to complex geometry, variable properties, nonlinear boundary conditions, or coupled phenomena. Finite difference discretizes differential equations at grid points; finite element uses variational formulation with basis functions; finite volume ensures conservation over control volumes. Time integration uses explicit (stability-limited) or implicit (unconditionally stable) schemes. Iteration handles nonlinearity and coupled problems. Convergence, accuracy, and stability must be verified. Modern heat transfer analysis relies heavily on numerical simulation using commercial software (ANSYS, COMSOL) or custom codes. Understanding numerical methods enables engineers to solve realistic problems and validate results..
ME 412: 2d unsteady heat conduction: Two-dimensional unsteady heat conduction extends transient analysis to spatial problems: ∂T/∂t = α(∂²T/∂x² + ∂²T/∂y²). This combines challenges of time integration and multi-dimensional spatial discretization. Explicit schemes (computationally simple but stability-limited), implicit schemes (stable but requiring large matrix solutions), and alternating direction implicit (ADI) methods balance efficiency and stability. The heat equation is parabolic, allowing marching solutions in time. Applications include transient thermal analysis of plates, electronic devices during startup/shutdown, and time-dependent heat treatment processes. This topic develops skills in handling multi-dimensional parabolic PDEs essential for practical thermal simulation..
ME 340: First-order systems: First-order systems are governed by first-order ordinary differential equations of the form τ(dy/dt) + y = u(t), where the highest derivative is first order. These systems contain a single energy storage element (like thermal capacitance or fluid volume) and exhibit exponential approach to steady state without oscillation. Common examples include RC circuits, temperature diffusion, and fluid level
MATH 285: Inhomogeneous heat equation: The inhomogeneous heat equation includes a source term representing internal heat generation or external heat input, not just conduction from boundaries. This makes the equation more realistic for situations with heaters, chemical reactions, or radiation absorption. Solutions combine the homogeneous heat equation solution with a particular solution accounting for the source term, often using eigen
ME 460: Modeling of dynamical systems by: Systematic approach to deriving state-space models from physical systems by assigning causality (cause-effect relationships) ensuring integrators (energy storage elements) have proper input-output relationships. Integral causality assigns states to energy storage elements (capacitor voltages, inductor currents, spring compressions, inertia velocities) with derivatives determined by system inputs and other states. Bond graph notation facilitates causal analysis ensuring physically realizable models. This approach guarantees minimal state realization, reveals algebraic constraints from derivative causality, and provides structured methodology for multi-domain system modeling. Applications include modeling electro-mechanical systems, hydraulic circuits, and thermal systems for control design..
TAM 335: Head losses in a pipe with elbo: This experiment measures total head loss in piping system including both major (friction in straight pipe) and minor (fittings like elbows, valves) losses. Pressure taps upstream and downstream of components enable separation of loss contributions. Loss coefficients K for each fitting are determined experimentally. Students measure pressure drop across various elbow types (90°, 45°, long radius, short radius), valves, and sudden expansions/contractions. Total system loss is sum of individual contributions. Understanding this experiment enables accurate system design accounting for all losses, proper component selection minimizing energy costs, and validation of handbook loss coefficient values..
TAM 335: Pumps: Pumps add energy to liquids, increasing pressure or head to overcome elevation change, friction losses, and downstream pressure. Types include centrifugal (rotating impeller imparts kinetic energy converted to pressure in volute/diffuser) and positive displacement (reciprocating or rotary). Performance curves plot head, power, and efficiency vs. flow rate. Operating point occurs where pump curve intersects system curve (required head vs. flow). Cavitation (NPSH) limits suction conditions. Affinity laws relate performance at different speeds and sizes. Pumps in series add heads; in parallel add flow rates. Applications include water supply, chemical processing, petroleum, and HVAC. Understanding pump operation enables proper selection, system design, and troubleshooting..
ME 310: Centrifugal pump characterizatio: Centrifugal pump characterization involves experimentally determining performance curves relating head (pressure increase), power consumption, and efficiency to flow rate. A rotating impeller imparts kinetic energy to fluid, which converts to pressure in the volute or diffuser. Characteristic curves (head-capacity, power-capacity, efficiency-capacity) define operating range, best efficiency point,
ME 340: Equilibrium configurations: Equilibrium configurations are system states where all velocities and accelerations are zero, representing balance between forces and moments. For conservative systems, equilibria occur at critical points of potential energy (where ∂V/∂qᵢ = 0 for all coordinates). Systems may have multiple equilibria, some stable (small perturbations decay) and others unstable (small perturbations grow). Finding e
ME 462: Simulated annealing: Probabilistic optimization technique inspired by metallurgical annealing, accepting worse solutions probabilistically (controlled by temperature parameter) to escape local minima, with acceptance probability decreasing over time (cooling schedule) converging to optimal solution. High initial temperature enables exploration; gradual cooling focuses search. Metropolis criterion P = exp(-ΔE/T) accepts worse solutions with probability depending on degradation ΔE and temperature T. Theoretically guaranteed to find global optimum with appropriate cooling. Applications include combinatorial optimization (scheduling, routing), controller synthesis with non-convex objectives, and parameter identification where multiple local minima exist requiring occasional uphill moves to find global optimum..
ME 462: Matlab global optimization toolb: MATLAB functions for solving optimization problems with multiple local optima requiring global search algorithms including genetic algorithms (ga), particle swarm (particleswarm), simulated annealing (simulannealbnd), pattern search, and surrogate optimization. Unlike gradient-based methods converging to local optima, global methods explore entire search space. Handles nonlinear, non-convex, and non-smooth objectives. Parallel computing accelerates population-based methods. Applications include controller tuning where cost landscape has multiple local minima, parameter identification with non-convex objectives, and any optimization problem where local methods fail finding globally optimal solutions..
ME 462: Controller tuning by means of gl: Applying global optimization methods (genetic algorithms, PSO, simulated annealing) to automatically tune controller parameters (PID gains, observer gains, MPC weights) by minimizing performance objectives (ISE, IAE, overshoot, settling time) evaluated through simulation or experiments. Handles non-convex cost landscapes where gradient methods fail. Multi-objective formulation balances competing requirements. Constraints enforce stability and physical limits. Computationally intensive but finds better solutions than manual tuning. Applications include complex systems where analytical tuning rules unavailable, multi-objective requirements requiring trade-off exploration, and systems where simulation/experiment cheaper than analytical analysis enabling data-driven optimization approaches..
MATH 241: Optimization subject to a cons: Constrained optimization finds extrema of f(x,y,z) subject to constraint g(x,y,z) = k. This restricts search to points on a surface or curve rather than the entire domain. Methods include: (1) substitution - solve constraint for one variable and substitute into f, reducing to unconstrained problem; (2) parameterization - parameterize the constraint surface and optimize over parameters; (3) Lagrang
MATH 241: Extreme value theorem and abso: The Extreme Value Theorem for multivariable functions states that a continuous function on a closed, bounded region D attains absolute maximum and minimum values. These extrema occur either at critical points in the interior of D or on the boundary of D. Finding absolute extrema requires: (1) finding all critical points inside D, (2) finding extreme values on the boundary (often using parameteriza
ME 340: Multi-degree-of-freedom systems: Multi-degree-of-freedom (MDOF) systems require multiple independent coordinates to describe their configuration completely. Unlike single-DOF systems described by scalar differential equations, MDOF systems involve coupled matrix differential equations where motion in one coordinate affects others. These systems exhibit multiple natural frequencies and mode shapes, with each mode representing a ch
ME 445: T-matrices: T-matrices represent overall transformations from robot base to intermediate or end-effector frames, obtained by composing A-matrices. T_i^0 = A_1·A_2·...·A_i represents transformation from base (frame 0) to frame i. The end-effector transformation T_n^0 gives complete forward kinematics solution. T-matrices enable computing positions and orientations of any link relative to base, useful for collision checking, visualization, and inverse kinematics. Understanding distinction between A-matrices (single-link transformations) and T-matrices (cumulative transformations) clarifies kinematic analysis structure and computational implementation..
ME 445: Denavit-hartenberg convention: Denavit-Hartenberg (DH) convention systematically assigns coordinate frames to robot links and parameterizes transformations between adjacent frames using four parameters: link length a, link twist α, link offset d, and joint angle θ. DH parameters completely describe robot geometry, enabling deriving forward kinematics systematically. The convention places Z-axes along joint axes, X-axes along common normals between Z-axes, reducing six transformation parameters to four through geometric constraints. Modified DH and other variations exist. Understanding DH convention enables systematic kinematic modeling, comparing different robots through their DH tables, and implementing forward kinematics computationally..
ME 340: Base excitation: Base excitation refers to dynamic loading where the foundation or support structure of a system moves, inducing motion in the mounted components. Common in seismic analysis, vehicle dynamics, and vibration testing, base excitation problems analyze how vibrations transmit from a moving base to the system. The analysis differs from force excitation because the input is a displacement, velocity, or a
CHEM 104: Molecular orbitals: Molecular Orbital (MO) theory describes bonding through delocalized orbitals formed by combining atomic orbitals from multiple atoms. When atomic orbitals combine, they form equal numbers of bonding (lower energy, increase electron density between nuclei) and antibonding (higher energy, decrease electron density between nuclei) molecular orbitals. Electrons fill MOs following aufbau principle, Pau
CHEM 104: Addition reactions: Addition reactions involve atoms or groups adding across a multiple bond (C=C, C≡C, C=O), converting it to a single bond with increased saturation. Common types include hydrogenation (adding H₂), halogenation (adding X₂), hydrohalogenation (adding HX), and hydration (adding H₂O). Markovnikov's rule predicts regioselectivity - in HX additions to asymmetric alkenes, hydrogen adds to the carbon with
CHEM 102: Aufbau principle: The Aufbau principle states that electrons fill atomic orbitals starting from the lowest available energy level and progressing to higher levels. Electrons occupy orbitals in order of increasing energy: 1s, 2s, 2p, 3s, 3p, 4s, 3d, and so on. This principle, combined with Pauli's exclusion principle and Hund's rule, determines the electron configuration of atoms and explains the periodic table's st
CHEM 102: Resonance structures: Resonance structures are multiple valid Lewis structures for a molecule where electron positions differ but atomic positions remain fixed. The actual molecule is a hybrid of these structures, with electron density distributed across multiple bonds. Resonance occurs when electrons can be arranged in different ways without changing the molecular skeleton, commonly seen in molecules with double bonds
PHYS 213: Microscopic probabilities: bol: The Boltzmann distribution describes probability that a system in thermal equilibrium occupies a state with energy E: P(E) ∝ e^(-E/kT), where k is Boltzmann's constant. This exponential decay means higher energy states are exponentially less likely. The distribution connects microscopic quantum states to macroscopic thermodynamic properties. The partition function Z = Σe^(-Eᵢ/kT) normalizes probab
PHYS 213: Application to semiconductors: In semiconductors, the Boltzmann distribution governs electron excitation from the valence band to conduction band across the band gap Eg. Carrier concentration depends exponentially on temperature: n ∝ e^(-Eg/2kT). At room temperature, only a small fraction of electrons are thermally excited, giving intrinsic semiconductors their properties. Doping introduces impurity levels that modify carrier c
CHEM 102: Energy of ionic bonds formatio: Ionic bond formation involves electron transfer followed by electrostatic attraction between oppositely charged ions, with energy changes at each step. The process includes ionization energy (removing electrons from metal, endothermic), electron affinity (adding electrons to nonmetal, usually exothermic), and lattice energy (bringing gaseous ions together to form solid, highly exothermic). The Bor
CHEM 103: Molecule maker lab: The molecule maker lab involves building three-dimensional molecular models using physical model kits to visualize molecular geometry, bond angles, and spatial arrangements. Students construct molecules to understand VSEPR theory, identify electron and molecular geometries, recognize different hybridizations, and see how molecular shape affects properties. This hands-on activity reinforces abstrac
ME 330: Bonding: Atomic bonding determines how atoms connect in materials, fundamentally controlling properties. Primary bonds include ionic (electron transfer, ceramics), covalent (electron sharing, polymers/semiconductors), and metallic (delocalized electron sea, metals). Secondary bonds (van der Waals, hydrogen) are weaker intermolecular forces. Bond type dictates material characteristics: metals conduct due to
TAM 324: Bonding and molecular structure: The study of atomic-level interactions that hold materials together, including ionic, covalent, metallic, and van der Waals bonding, and how these bonds determine material properties and behavior. Bonding type influences melting temperature, mechanical strength, electrical conductivity, thermal expansion, and chemical reactivity. Molecular structure encompasses crystal lattices in metals and ceramics, polymer chain configurations, and microstructural features like grain boundaries and phase distributions. Understanding bonding explains why ceramics are brittle, metals are ductile, polymers are flexible, and composites can be engineered to combine favorable properties from different material classes..
PHYS 214: Multiple electrons: Systems with multiple electrons require considering indistinguishability, Pauli exclusion principle (no two electrons in identical quantum states), and antisymmetric wave functions. Total wave function must change sign under electron exchange: ψ(r₁,r₂) = -ψ(r₂,r₁). Electron spin (intrinsic angular momentum with s = ½) means each orbital holds two electrons (spin up/down). Electron-electron repulsi
PHYS 214: Band structure: In crystalline solids, periodic atomic potential creates allowed energy bands separated by forbidden gaps. Valence band (filled) and conduction band (empty/partially filled) determine electrical properties. Metals have overlapping bands, insulators have large gaps (Eg > 3 eV), semiconductors have moderate gaps (~1 eV). Band structure arises from constructive/destructive interference of electron wa
ME 404: Introduction to statistical ther: Statistical thermodynamics derives macroscopic thermodynamic properties from microscopic molecular behavior using statistical mechanics and quantum mechanics. Unlike classical thermodynamics (phenomenological laws), statistical thermodynamics explains WHY substances have specific properties based on molecular structure and energy states. The partition function connects microscopic energy levels to macroscopic properties (u, h, s, Cp, Cv). The Boltzmann distribution describes population of energy states. This approach predicts properties ab initio from molecular parameters rather than empirical measurement. Understanding statistical foundations deepens thermodynamic insight, enables property estimation for hypothetical substances, and bridges molecular physics with engineering thermodynamics, particularly valuable for high-temperature gases and complex molecules..
ME 404: Statistical concepts: Statistical concepts underpin statistical thermodynamics, including microstates (specific molecular configurations), macrostates (thermodynamic states characterized by macroscopic properties), ensemble theory (collections of systems), and probability distributions. The Boltzmann distribution P_i ∝ exp(-E_i/kT) gives probability of occupying energy state E_i. The partition function Z = Σ exp(-E_i/kT) serves as a generating function for thermodynamic properties. Entropy relates to the number of accessible microstates: S = k ln Ω. The equipartition theorem assigns kT/2 energy per degree of freedom classically. Understanding these statistical foundations enables rigorous derivation of thermodynamic properties from molecular information, explaining temperature dependence and providing insight into entropy's statistical interpretation..
ME 483: Henderson-hassebalch equation: The mathematical relationship (pH = pKa + log([A⁻]/[HA])) describing the pH of buffer solutions in terms of the acid dissociation constant (pKa) and the ratio of deprotonated (A⁻) to protonated (HA) forms. This equation enables calculation of protonation states for ionizable groups in proteins, nucleic acids, and small molecules at physiological pH, determining charge distributions affecting protein folding, binding interactions, and electrostatic forces. In mechanobiology, pH changes from metabolic activity or inflammation alter protonation states, modulating protein charge, conformation, and mechanical properties. Understanding pH buffering is essential for maintaining conditions in tissue engineering bioreactors and cell culture systems..
CHEM 104: Electrolytic cells: Electrolytic cells use electrical energy to drive nonspontaneous redox reactions (ΔG > 0), performing electrolysis. Unlike galvanic cells, electrolytic cells require external power supply. The positive terminal connects to the anode where oxidation occurs, and the negative terminal connects to the cathode where reduction occurs. Applied voltage must exceed the cell's opposing potential to force th
PHYS 212: Coulomb's law: Coulomb's law describes the electrostatic force between two point charges, stating force magnitude is proportional to the product of charges and inversely proportional to the square of distance between them. Force direction is along the line connecting charges—repulsive for like charges, attractive for opposite charges. This fundamental law, analogous to Newton's gravitational law, underlies all e
ME 483: Gibbs free energy: The thermodynamic potential (G = H - TS) quantifying the maximum reversible work obtainable from a system at constant temperature and pressure, determining spontaneity of processes including chemical reactions, binding events, and conformational changes. Processes with negative ΔG are thermodynamically favorable (spontaneous), while positive ΔG processes require energy input. Free energy balances enthalpy changes (bond breaking/forming, ΔH) and entropy changes (disorder, ΔS), with temperature determining their relative importance. In mechanobiology, mechanical work can provide free energy to drive otherwise unfavorable processes like protein unfolding, or can be stored as chemical energy in ATP synthesis, linking mechanical and chemical energy landscapes..
PHYS 212: Electric potential: Electric potential (voltage) is the electric potential energy per unit charge at a point, representing the work needed to bring a unit positive charge from infinity to that point. Potential is a scalar field, simpler than the vector electric field, with electric field pointing in the direction of maximum potential decrease (E = -∇V). Potential differences drive current flow and determine energy ch
ECE 205: Current: Electric current (I) is the rate of charge flow through a conductor: I = dQ/dt, measured in amperes (A), where 1 A = 1 C/s. Conventional current direction is positive charge flow (opposite to electron flow). Current can be direct (DC - constant direction) or alternating (AC - periodically reversing). In conductors, current results from drift velocity of charge carriers. Current density J = I/A des
ECE 205: Voltage: Voltage (V) or electric potential difference is the energy per unit charge required to move charge between two points: V = W/Q, measured in volts (V), where 1 V = 1 J/C. Voltage represents electrical 'pressure' driving current flow. It's the work done by electric field per charge moved. Voltage is measured between two points (potential difference), with higher potential being positive terminal. Vo
PHYS 212: Kirchoff's laws: Kirchhoff's laws are fundamental rules for circuit analysis. The junction rule (current law) states that total current entering a junction equals total current leaving, reflecting charge conservation. The loop rule (voltage law) states that the sum of potential differences around any closed loop is zero, reflecting energy conservation. These laws enable systematic analysis of complex circuits with
ECE 205: Charge: Electric charge (Q) is a fundamental property of matter measured in coulombs (C), existing in discrete units of elementary charge e = 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C. Charges come in two types: positive (protons) and negative (electrons). Like charges repel, opposite charges attract via Coulomb's law. Charge is conserved - total charge in isolated system remains constant. Current is the flow rate of charge: I = d
ECE 205: Kirchhoff's current and voltage: Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL): algebraic sum of currents entering any node equals zero (ΣI = 0), reflecting charge conservation. Current in equals current out. Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL): algebraic sum of voltages around any closed loop equals zero (ΣV = 0), reflecting energy conservation. Voltage rises equal voltage drops. These laws, combined with element equations, enable systematic circuit
PHYS 212: Biot-savart law: The Biot-Savart law calculates the magnetic field produced by a current element, stating that field contribution is proportional to current, element length, and inversely proportional to distance squared, with direction given by the right-hand rule. Integrating over current distributions (wires, loops, solenoids) yields total field. This law is fundamental to understanding how currents create magn
ECE 205: P-n junction diodes: A p-n junction diode is a semiconductor device formed by joining p-type (hole-rich) and n-type (electron-rich) materials. It conducts current easily in forward bias (p-side positive) but blocks in reverse bias (p-side negative). Current-voltage relationship follows Shockley equation: I = Is(e^(V/nVT) - 1), where VT ≈ 26 mV at room temperature. Threshold voltage (≈0.7V for silicon) must be exceeded
PHYS 212: Simple circuits: Simple circuits consist of power sources (batteries), resistors, and connecting wires arranged in series or parallel configurations. Current flows from high to low potential, with resistance limiting flow rate. Ohm's law (V = IR) relates voltage, current, and resistance. Understanding series circuits (same current everywhere) versus parallel circuits (same voltage across branches) and calculating
PHYS 212: Electric fields: An electric field is a vector field representing the force per unit charge at each point in space, created by source charges. It enables analyzing electrical forces without explicitly considering test charges. Field lines visualize field direction (tangent to line) and strength (density of lines). Understanding field calculations from point charges, continuous distributions, and using superpositio
PHYS 212: Gauss's law: Gauss's law states that the net electric flux through any closed surface equals the enclosed charge divided by the permittivity constant (ε₀). This fundamental law, one of Maxwell's equations, relates electric field behavior at boundaries to interior charge distributions. For highly symmetric charge distributions (spherical, cylindrical, planar), Gauss's law provides an elegant method for calculat
PHYS 212: Capacitors: Capacitors are devices that store electrical energy in electric fields, consisting of conductors separated by insulators. Parallel plate capacitors have uniform fields, with capacitance depending on area, separation, and dielectric material. Capacitors in series and parallel combine differently than resistors. Capacitors store energy (U = ½CV²), block DC current while passing AC, and are fundament
ME 360: Thermocouples: Temperature sensors based on Seebeck effect generating voltage at junction of dissimilar metals proportional to temperature difference between measurement junction and reference junction. Common types (J, K, T, E, R, S, B) use standardized metal combinations spanning -200°C to +1800°C with different sensitivities (Seebeck coefficients around 40 μV/°C for type K). Cold junction compensation account
ME 432: Iv curves: Current-voltage (I-V) curves graphically represent solar cell electrical characteristics showing current output at different voltages. The curve shape reveals device quality: ideal cells have sharp knee transitioning from flat current region to flat voltage region. Key features include Isc (y-intercept), Voc (x-intercept), maximum power rectangle inscribed under curve, and curve slope indicating series resistance (flattening near Voc) and shunt resistance (steepening near Isc). I-V curves shift with irradiance (vertical translation - current) and temperature (horizontal translation - voltage). I-V tracing diagnoses problems: shunt resistance causes drooping at Isc, series resistance rounds the knee, poor junction quality reduces Voc and FF..
ME 483: Nernst equation: The thermodynamic relationship (E = E° - (RT/nF)ln([reduced]/[oxidized])) determining the electrochemical potential difference across membranes based on ion concentration gradients, where E° is standard potential, n is electron number, and F is Faraday's constant. For biological membranes, the simplified form E = (RT/zF)ln([ion]out/[ion]in) calculates equilibrium potential for specific ions, determining resting membrane potentials and driving forces for ion flux. In mechanobiology, mechanical stimulation can alter ion channel activity, changing intracellular ion concentrations and membrane potentials, linking mechanical and electrical signaling. Understanding Nernst potentials is essential for interpreting mechanically-gated ion channel function and excitable cell responses..
ME 432: Charge carrier separation: Charge carrier separation drives photo-generated electrons and holes in opposite directions, preventing recombination and enabling current extraction. In p-n junction solar cells, the built-in electric field at the depletion region separates carriers: electrons swept to n-side, holes to p-side. Field strength and depletion width depend on doping concentrations. Alternative separation mechanisms include heterojunctions (band alignment differences), Schottky barriers (metal-semiconductor junctions), and excitonic solar cells (organic PV using donor-acceptor interfaces). Effective separation requires field strength exceeding thermal energy (kT), sufficient depletion width, and minimal recombination in the junction region..
ME 483: Membrane potential: The electrical voltage difference across cell membranes arising from unequal ion distributions maintained by ion pumps and selective permeability through ion channels. At rest, typical cells maintain -70mV inside relative to outside through sodium-potassium pumps and differential permeability. Membrane potential influences mechanotransduction by affecting voltage-gated ion channels, electrostatic protein interactions, and electrochemical driving forces for ion flux. Mechanical stimulation can alter membrane tension affecting mechanosensitive channels, changing ion permeability and membrane potential. Understanding membrane potential dynamics is essential for interpreting how mechanical forces trigger electrical signals in excitable cells and modulate signaling in non-excitable cells..
ECE 205: Power: Electric power (P) is the rate of energy transfer or work done: P = dW/dt = VI, measured in watts (W), where 1 W = 1 J/s. For resistive elements, P = I²R = V²/R (Joule heating). Power can be delivered (sources like batteries) or dissipated (loads like resistors). Conservation of energy requires total power delivered equals total power dissipated. In AC circuits, distinguish between real power (ave
ME 432: The solar cell as a thermodynami: Solar cells function as heat engines operating between the sun's temperature (~6000K) and Earth's ambient temperature (~300K), converting thermal radiation to electrical work. Unlike mechanical heat engines using working fluids, solar cells use photon absorption exciting electrons to higher energy states (hot reservoir), electricity extraction, and radiative/thermal emission (cold reservoir rejection). The Carnot efficiency limit for this temperature ratio is ~95%, but practical limits are much lower due to semiconductor physics constraints. Understanding thermodynamic framework explains fundamental efficiency limits, relates PV to other energy conversion devices, and guides theoretical efficiency optimization..
ME 330: Corrosion: Corrosion is electrochemical degradation of materials (primarily metals) through environmental interaction. Types include uniform attack, galvanic (dissimilar metals), pitting (localized), crevice, intergranular, and stress corrosion cracking. Corrosion requires anode (metal dissolving), cathode (reduction reaction), electrolyte (conducting medium), and electrical connection. Prevention methods in
ME 330: Properties and classification of: Materials are classified into metals, ceramics, polymers, and composites based on bonding and structure. Metals have metallic bonding, crystalline structure, high conductivity, ductility, and moderate strength. Ceramics have ionic/covalent bonding, crystalline/amorphous structure, high hardness/brittleness, low conductivity, high melting points. Polymers have covalent backbone chains with secondar
ME 330: Strengthening mechanisms: Strengthening mechanisms increase material strength by impeding dislocation motion. Grain boundary strengthening (Hall-Petch) uses fine grains as barriers. Solid solution strengthening adds alloying atoms creating lattice distortions. Precipitation hardening forms fine precipitate particles obstructing dislocations. Work hardening increases dislocation density causing entanglement. Each mechanism
TAM 324: Compression and hardness tests: Mechanical tests where specimens are subjected to compressive forces (compression test) or indentation by hard indenters (hardness test) to evaluate resistance to crushing, bearing capacity, and surface resistance to penetration. Compression testing of brittle materials like concrete determines compressive strength, while ductile materials exhibit barreling. Hardness tests include Brinell (spherical indenter), Rockwell (cone or ball with pre-load), Vickers (diamond pyramid), and Shore (spring-loaded indenter for polymers). Hardness correlates with tensile strength in metals and indicates wear resistance. These tests are often non-destructive or minimally destructive, enabling quality control without sacrificing components..
ME 330: Hardness, strength and compressi: Hardness measures resistance to localized plastic deformation (indentation), correlating with strength and wear resistance. Common tests include Rockwell, Brinell, Vickers, and Knoop using different indenters and loads. Compression testing applies compressive loads measuring deformation behavior. Most engineering materials are stronger in compression than tension since compression closes rather th
ME 330: Ceramics: ceramic microstructure: Ceramic microstructures consist of crystalline grains, glass phases, and porosity. Grain size, shape, and distribution affect mechanical properties: fine grains improve strength (Hall-Petch), elongated grains can provide toughness. Glass phases at grain boundaries affect high-temperature properties and processing. Porosity (unavoidable in most ceramics) significantly reduces strength and stiffness
TAM 324: Heat treatment of steels: Controlled heating and cooling processes that manipulate steel microstructure to achieve desired mechanical properties by controlling phase transformations between austenite, ferrite, pearlite, bainite, and martensite. Common treatments include annealing (softening through slow cooling), normalizing (refining grain structure), quenching (rapid cooling to form hard martensite), and tempering (reheating martensite to increase toughness while reducing brittleness). Time-temperature-transformation (TTT) diagrams predict which phases form during isothermal holds or continuous cooling. Understanding hardenability, critical cooling rates, and tempering temperature effects enables tailoring steel properties from soft and ductile to hard and strong for specific applications..
TAM 324: Brittle fracture: Sudden, catastrophic material separation with minimal plastic deformation, occurring when crack propagation is energetically favorable, typically in materials with low fracture toughness, at low temperatures, high loading rates, or with pre-existing cracks. Brittle fracture initiates at stress concentrations and propagates rapidly when the stress intensity factor exceeds the critical value (fracture toughness KIc). Cleavage fracture follows specific crystallographic planes creating flat, shiny surfaces, while intergranular fracture follows grain boundaries. Prevention strategies include controlling flaw sizes, avoiding stress concentrations, limiting service temperatures above ductile-brittle transition, and selecting high-toughness materials..
ME 431: High temperature service: High temperature service (typically >0.4 Tm where Tm is absolute melting temperature) causes time-dependent deformation (creep), strength degradation, microstructural changes, and oxidation/corrosion. Creep involves primary (transient), secondary (steady-state), and tertiary (accelerating to rupture) stages. Design uses stress-rupture data, creep-rupture curves, and Larson-Miller parameters. High temperature also reduces yield strength, fatigue resistance, and fracture toughness. Materials selection includes creep-resistant alloys (nickel superalloys, ceramics, refractory metals), protective coatings, and thermal barriers. Understanding high-temperature behavior enables designing turbines, furnaces, petrochemical equipment, and power generation systems..
TAM 456: Stress: In experimental stress analysis, stress is internal force intensity measured or inferred from experiments rather than purely calculated. Measurement techniques: strain gages (measure strain, calculate stress using Hooke's law), photoelasticity (visualize stress patterns using birefringence), brittle coatings (crack patterns indicate stress), hole-drilling (residual stress measurement). Stress concentration factors from experiments validate analytical/numerical predictions. Full-field methods capture stress distributions. Understanding stress measurement limitations, uncertainties, and calibration is essential. Experimental stress analysis bridges theory and reality, validating designs and revealing unexpected stress states..
MATH 221: Exponential growth and decay: Exponential growth and decay models describe quantities changing at rates proportional to their current value: dy/dt = ky, with solution y(t) = y₀e^(kt). When k > 0, the model describes growth (population, compound interest); when k
ME 446: Multivariable control: Multivariable control addresses robot systems as coupled multi-input multi-output (MIMO) systems where joint interactions are explicitly considered. Rather than treating joints independently, multivariable controllers account for dynamic coupling, using the full dynamic model to compute coordinated control actions. Techniques include computed torque control, model predictive control, and optimal control methods. These approaches compensate for inertial coupling, Coriolis, and centrifugal effects, achieving superior tracking performance especially during fast motions and with heavy payloads. The complexity and computational requirements are higher than independent joint control but justified for high-performance applications..
ME 446: Independent joint control: Independent joint control treats each robot joint as a separate single-input single-output (SISO) system, ignoring dynamic coupling between joints. Each joint has its own controller (typically PID) that regulates position, velocity, or torque based on local feedback. This simplified approach is computationally efficient and robust, working well when coupling effects are small (slow motion, lightweight links). However, it may exhibit poor performance during high-speed operation or with heavy payloads where inertial coupling, Coriolis, and centrifugal forces become significant. Despite limitations, independent joint control remains widely used in industrial robotics for its simplicity and reliability..
ME 445: Inverse kinematics: Inverse kinematics solves for joint angles required to achieve a desired end-effector position and orientation. Unlike forward kinematics which has a unique solution, inverse kinematics may have multiple solutions, no solution (target unreachable), or infinitely many solutions (redundant robots). Solution approaches include analytical methods using geometric or algebraic techniques for specific robot architectures, and numerical methods like Jacobian-based iterative algorithms for general cases. Handling singularities, selecting among multiple solutions, and ensuring continuity of motion are key challenges. Inverse kinematics is fundamental for Cartesian path planning and task-space control..
ME 445: Velocity kinematics: Velocity kinematics relates joint velocities to end-effector linear and angular velocities through the Jacobian matrix: v = J(q)·q̇, where v is 6×1 end-effector velocity (3 linear + 3 angular), q̇ is n×1 joint velocity, and J is 6×n Jacobian. Velocity kinematics enables Cartesian velocity control (specifying end-effector velocities, computing required joint velocities), force analysis (relating joint torques to end-effector forces via J^T), and singularity analysis (J loses rank). Understanding velocity kinematics enables implementing resolved-rate control, analyzing manipulability, and identifying configurations where motion capability degenerates..
ME 445: Singular configurations: Singular configurations occur where Jacobian loses rank, causing loss of mobility in some directions or requiring infinite joint velocities for finite end-effector velocities. At singularities, robot cannot instantaneously move in certain directions regardless of joint velocities. Types include boundary singularities (workspace edge), wrist singularities (wrist axes align), and elbow singularities (arm fully extended/retracted). Singularities cause control problems: solution non-uniqueness in inverse kinematics, large joint velocities near singularities, and potential mechanical lock-up. Understanding singularities enables avoiding problematic configurations, implementing singularity-robust control, and recognizing inherent limitations of specific robot designs..
ME 445: A-matrices: A-matrices (link transformation matrices) represent transformations between adjacent coordinate frames in robot kinematic chains, derived from DH parameters. Each A-matrix is a 4×4 homogeneous transformation: A_i = Rot_z(θ_i)·Trans_z(d_i)·Trans_x(a_i)·Rot_x(α_i), combining rotation and translation determined by the four DH parameters. A-matrices are building blocks for forward kinematics: composing A-matrices from base to end-effector yields overall transformation. Understanding A-matrices enables implementing forward kinematics computationally, debugging kinematic models by examining individual link transformations, and validating DH parameter extraction..
ME 445: Lagrangian dynamics: Lagrangian dynamics provides a systematic, energy-based approach to deriving the equations of motion for robotic systems. By formulating the Lagrangian as the difference between kinetic and potential energy, then applying the Euler-Lagrange equations, one obtains the complete dynamic model including inertia, Coriolis, centrifugal, and gravitational terms. This method is particularly powerful for complex multi-link robots because it automatically accounts for constraints and uses generalized coordinates. The resulting equations relate joint torques to joint accelerations, enabling model-based control, simulation, and analysis of robotic manipulators..
ME 445: Minimum time trajectories: Minimum time trajectories determine the fastest possible motion between configurations while satisfying all kinematic and dynamic constraints including maximum velocities, accelerations, torques, and forces. This optimization problem often involves bang-bang control where actuators operate at their limits, switching between maximum acceleration and deceleration. Computing true minimum time trajectories is computationally intensive, requiring consideration of the robot's dynamics and coupling between joints. Numerical optimization methods and dynamic programming are commonly employed to find these time-optimal solutions for high-performance applications..
ME 445: Singular values: Singular values of Jacobian (from Singular Value Decomposition J = UΣV^T) quantify motion capabilities along principal directions. Largest singular value σ_max indicates direction of greatest velocity amplification; smallest σ_min indicates poorest. Condition number κ = σ_max/σ_min measures proximity to singularity (κ→∞ at singularities). Singular values enable analyzing manipulability throughout workspace, identifying near-singular configurations, and implementing singularity-robust control using damped least squares. Understanding singular values provides quantitative measures of robot dexterity varying with configuration, guiding trajectory planning to maintain good manipulability..
ME 481: Forward dynamics: The computational approach predicting resulting motion from specified muscle forces, external forces, and initial conditions by solving equations of motion forward in time. Unlike inverse dynamics calculating forces from known motion, forward dynamics simulates motion from known or estimated forces, enabling 'what-if' analyses and optimization of control strategies. Applications include predicting surgical outcomes, optimizing athletic technique through simulation, designing assistive device control algorithms, understanding neuromuscular control strategies, and developing humanoid robots. Challenges include the redundancy problem (infinite force combinations producing the same motion) requiring optimization criteria, computational intensity, and sensitivity to initial conditions and parameter uncertainties..
ME 445: Trapezoidal velocity profiles: Trapezoidal velocity profiles generate time-optimal trajectories under velocity and acceleration constraints by using constant acceleration, constant velocity, and constant deceleration phases. The velocity profile resembles a trapezoid: the robot accelerates at maximum acceleration until reaching maximum velocity, maintains that velocity, then decelerates at maximum deceleration to stop at the goal. For short moves, the profile becomes triangular as maximum velocity isn't reached. This approach is widely used in industrial robotics because it minimizes motion time while respecting actuator limitations and produces predictable, efficient motion..
ME 445: Pseudoinverse: The pseudoinverse J† of Jacobian J enables computing joint velocities for desired end-effector velocity when J is not square or singular: q̇ = J†·v. For overdetermined systems (more constraints than DOFs), pseudoinverse gives least-squares solution minimizing error. For underdetermined systems (more DOFs than constraints), it gives minimum-norm solution. Damped least squares pseudoinverse (J^T·J + λI)^(-1)·J^T adds regularization parameter λ, preventing large joint velocities near singularities. Understanding pseudoinverse enables implementing redundancy resolution, velocity control for non-square Jacobians, and singularity-robust control algorithms..
ME 445: Manipulability: Manipulability measures robot dexterity at a given configuration, quantifying ability to move/apply forces in all directions. Yoshikawa's manipulability ellipsoid uses Jacobian singular values defining ellipsoid in velocity space - volume indicates isotropic capability. Manipulability index w = √(det(J·J^T)) provides scalar measure (w=0 at singularities). High manipulability configurations enable motion in all directions with reasonable joint velocities; low manipulability indicates near-singular configurations. Understanding manipulability enables planning trajectories maintaining dexterity, choosing optimal configurations for tasks, and designing workspace layouts maximizing manipulability..
ME 445: Geometric approaches: Geometric approaches solve inverse kinematics by exploiting robot structure geometry, visualizing mechanism as triangles, circles, and projections. For articulated arms, decompose position into planar projection (solving 2D problem) and out-of-plane angle. Geometric solutions provide closed-form equations, physical intuition, and clear identification of multiple solutions. Applicable to specific robot designs (6-DOF arms with spherical wrist), geometric methods are fast and reliable but don't generalize to arbitrary configurations. Understanding geometric approaches enables deriving analytical IK solutions for structured robots and appreciating structure-exploiting algorithms versus general numerical methods..
ME 446: Task space pd control, impedance: Task space PD control regulates end-effector position in Cartesian coordinates rather than joint space, using PD feedback on task-space errors. Impedance control extends this by specifying the robot's mechanical impedance (relationship between force and motion), making the robot behave like a mass-spring-damper system. This enables compliant behavior useful for contact tasks, human-robot interaction, and uncertain environments. The controller computes joint torques that achieve desired task-space impedance characteristics. Applications include assembly with clearance uncertainties, force-controlled polishing, and safe physical human-robot collaboration..
ME 445: Parallel robots: Parallel robots feature multiple kinematic chains connecting the base to the end-effector, contrasting with serial manipulators' single chain architecture. Examples include Stewart platforms (hexapods) and Delta robots used for high-speed pick-and-place operations. Parallel mechanisms offer advantages including higher stiffness, better accuracy, higher payload-to-weight ratios, and faster dynamics since actuators can be mounted on the base. However, they typically have smaller workspaces, more complex kinematics, and singularities within the workspace. Applications span precision machining, flight simulators, medical robotics, and high-speed assembly..
ME 445: Common robot configurations: Common robot configurations include Cartesian/Gantry (three prismatic joints for XYZ motion), Cylindrical (prismatic + revolute + prismatic), Spherical/Polar (two revolute + prismatic), SCARA (Selective Compliance Assembly Robot Arm - vertical compliance, horizontal stiffness), and Articulated/Anthropomorphic (all revolute joints like human arm). Each configuration offers different workspace shape, stiffness characteristics, and complexity. Configuration selection depends on application requirements: Cartesian for simple straight-line motion, SCARA for high-speed assembly, articulated for versatility and reach. Understanding configurations enables selecting appropriate robot types and appreciating workspace and performance trade-offs..
ME 440: Stewart platforms: Stewart platforms (hexapods) are parallel manipulators consisting of a moving platform connected to a fixed base by six variable-length actuators, typically arranged in pairs at three locations. These mechanisms provide six degrees of freedom (three translations, three rotations) with high stiffness, precision, and load capacity. Forward kinematics (determining platform pose from actuator lengths) is computationally challenging, while inverse kinematics (finding actuator lengths for desired pose) is straightforward. Applications include flight simulators, precision positioning stages, telescopes, and machine tools..
ME 462: Ct and dt robust adaptive contro: Adaptive control ensuring stability and performance despite parametric uncertainty, unmodeled dynamics, and disturbances in both continuous-time (CT) and discrete-time (DT) formulations. Robust modifications include σ-modification, ε-modification, and projection preventing parameter drift from unmodeled dynamics and disturbances. Lyapunov-based designs prove stability despite imperfect adaptation. Combines adaptation benefits (handling unknown parameters) with robustness guarantees (handling model mismatches). Persistent excitation conditions ensure parameter convergence. Applications include aircraft with uncertain aerodynamics, robotic manipulators with unknown payloads, and any system requiring adaptation while guaranteeing robustness..
ME 462: Linear h\u221e design: Systematic controller synthesis for linear systems minimizing H∞ norm of weighted closed-loop transfer functions, formulated as solving two Riccati equations (state feedback and filter) with coupling through γ parameter. Frequency-dependent weights W1, W2, W3 shape sensitivity, complementary sensitivity, and control effort. γ-iteration finds minimum achievable H∞ norm. Controller order equals plant plus weights order. Provides explicit robustness guarantees and systematic handling of multivariable systems. Applications include active suspension, disk drives, aircraft flutter suppression, and any system requiring guaranteed frequency-domain performance bounds..
