ME 460: State equations: First-order differential equations in matrix form (ẋ = Ax + Bu, y = Cx + Du) representing system dynamics through state vector x, input vector u, and output vector y. State-space representation provides complete system description enabling analysis of multi-input multi-output systems, time-varying systems, and nonlinear systems. Matrices A (system), B (input), C (output), and D (feedthrough) encode system structure. State equations enable modern control design methods including pole placement, optimal control, and observer design. Solution involves matrix exponentials computing state trajectories. Applications include aircraft dynamics modeling, robot manipulator control, and chemical process control requiring advanced control synthesis methods..
ME 462: Nonlinear h\u221e control for sy: H∞ control design extended to nonlinear systems with hard nonlinearities (saturation, backlash, friction, deadzone) that cannot be smoothly approximated. Design involves solving Hamilton-Jacobi-Isaacs equations or using Lyapunov-based methods guaranteeing L2-gain bound from disturbances to outputs. Nonlinearities handled through exact models, sector bounds, or describing functions. Control Lyapunov functions provide constructive synthesis. Trade-offs include conservatism of linearization versus complexity of nonlinear design. Applications include mechanical systems with friction and saturation, hydraulic systems with valve nonlinearities, and aerospace systems with actuator limits requiring robust performance guarantees..
ME 462: H\u221e control: Robust control design minimizing H∞ norm of transfer function from disturbances to outputs, representing worst-case gain across all frequencies. Design formulated as optimization problem minimizing γ such that ||G||∞
ME 340: First and second order systems: First and second order systems represent differential equations that govern the dynamic behavior of mechanical systems. First-order systems involve one energy storage element and are described by a single derivative, while second-order systems involve two energy storage elements and are described by second derivatives. These mathematical models predict how systems respond to inputs over time, with
ME 445: Actuators and sensors: Actuators and sensors are the physical components enabling robots to interact with their environment. Actuators convert electrical, hydraulic, or pneumatic energy into mechanical motion, including electric motors (DC, stepper, servo), hydraulic cylinders, and pneumatic pistons. Sensors measure physical quantities like position (encoders), velocity (tachometers), force (strain gauges), proximity (ultrasonic, infrared), and vision (cameras). The selection and integration of appropriate actuators and sensors determine a robot's capabilities, precision, speed, and adaptability. Understanding their characteristics, limitations, and interfacing requirements is essential for effective robot design..
ME 445: Roll-pitch-yaw: Roll-pitch-yaw (RPY) is a specific Euler angle convention using fixed coordinate axes: yaw (Z-axis rotation/heading), pitch (Y-axis rotation/elevation), roll (X-axis rotation/bank). RPY corresponds to ZYX Euler angles and is widely used in aerospace and mobile robotics because angles relate intuitively to aircraft/vehicle motion. RPY suffers gimbal lock at pitch = ±90°. Conversion between RPY and rotation matrices uses trigonometric functions. Understanding RPY enables working with navigation systems, aircraft dynamics, and mobile robot orientation. The intuitive nature makes RPY preferred for human operators despite mathematical complications..
ME 462: Projection: Mathematical technique in adaptive control preventing parameter estimates from drifting outside physically meaningful bounds by projecting adaptation law onto feasible parameter region. Projection ensures parameters remain in known convex set (e.g., positive stiffness, bounded gains) while maintaining adaptation when away from boundaries. Modified adaptation law zeros parameter update component perpendicular to boundary when at constraint. Preserves stability proofs while enforcing prior knowledge constraints. Applications include adaptive control where physical constraints exist (positive masses, bounded gains, known parameter ranges), preventing parameter wind-up and ensuring estimates remain meaningful..
ME 360: Strain gage measurements: Complete measurement system capturing strain-induced resistance changes from gages bonded to structures, including Wheatstone bridge excitation providing sensitivity and temperature compensation, bridge balancing for nulling offsets, amplification of millivolt-level bridge outputs, completion resistors for quarter- and half-bridge configurations, and shunt calibration verifying system gain. Bridge
ME 360: Strain gages: Resistive transducers bonded to structural surfaces measuring strain through resistance change proportional to deformation, based on piezoresistive effect where stretching increases resistance and compression decreases it. Typical metallic foil gages have gage factors around 2, meaning 0.2% resistance change per 1000 microstrain. Gages configured in Wheatbridge circuits enable temperature compensa
TAM 445: The geometry of general deforma: Strain measures deformation intensity, quantifying stretching and angular distortion. Right Cauchy-Green tensor C = FᵀF and Green-Lagrange strain E = (C-I)/2 measure strain in material description. Left Cauchy-Green B = FFᵀ and Euler-Almansi strain e = (I-B⁻¹)/2 for spatial description. For small deformations, linearize: ε = (∇u + ∇uᵀ)/2 (infinitesimal strain). Principal strains are eigenvalues of
TAM 456: Strain: Strain measurement in experimental analysis uses electrical resistance strain gages as primary technique. Gage resistance changes with deformation: ΔR/R = GFε, where GF ≈ 2 is gage factor. Wheatstone bridge circuit measures resistance change. Rosette gages (three gages at different angles) determine principal strains. Other methods: extensometers (mechanical/optical displacement measurement), digital image correlation (DIC) tracking surface patterns, fiber optic sensors. Measured strain relates to stress via Hooke's law. Thermal compensation, installation technique, and data acquisition affect accuracy. Strain measurement is most common experimental stress analysis technique..
TAM 456: Load cells: Load cells are force transducers converting applied load to electrical signal using strain gages configured as full Wheatstone bridge. Common designs include beam-type (cantilever, bending), column-type (compression/tension), and shear-beam configurations. Internal strain gages positioned strategically to maximize sensitivity while rejecting bending, torsion, or side loads. Calibration relates output voltage to applied force. Specifications include capacity, accuracy, linearity, hysteresis, and temperature effects. Applications span industrial weighing, testing machines, structural monitoring. Understanding load cell design principles enables proper selection, installation, and interpretation of force measurements..
TAM 324: Fatigue: Progressive, localized structural damage accumulating under cyclic loading at stress levels below the material's static strength, eventually leading to crack initiation and propagation until catastrophic failure. Fatigue life depends on stress amplitude, mean stress, loading frequency, environmental conditions, and material microstructure. The process involves cyclic slip creating persistent slip bands, microcrack nucleation typically at surface stress concentrations, stable crack growth (Stage II) following Paris law relating crack growth rate to stress intensity range, and unstable final fracture. S-N curves characterize fatigue resistance, while damage accumulation follows rules like Miner's law for variable amplitude loading..
TAM 324: Mechanical behavior of concrete: The response of concrete to applied loads including compressive strength (primary design parameter), tensile strength (typically 10% of compressive), modulus of elasticity, creep under sustained loads, shrinkage from moisture loss and hydration, and fracture behavior as a quasi-brittle material. Concrete exhibits nonlinear stress-strain behavior with increasing inelasticity approaching peak strength, followed by strain-softening post-peak. Time-dependent effects include creep deformation under constant stress and stress relaxation under constant strain. Microcracking initiates at aggregate-paste interfaces and propagates through the cement matrix. Understanding these behaviors guides structural design, reinforcement requirements, and serviceability predictions..
ME 431: Fatigue analysis: Fatigue analysis predicts crack initiation and growth under cyclic loading, determining component life or inspection intervals. High-cycle fatigue (HCF) uses S-N curves and modified Goodman diagrams for stress-based life prediction. Low-cycle fatigue (LCF) uses strain-based approaches (Coffin-Manson) when plasticity occurs. Crack growth analysis uses Paris law (da/dN = C(ΔK)^m) predicting cycles to grow crack from detectable to critical size. Factors affecting fatigue include stress range, mean stress, surface finish, stress concentrations, size, environment, and temperature. Fatigue analysis guides material selection, design modifications, and maintenance strategies..
ME 360: Hydraulic actuators: High-force actuators using incompressible fluid (oil) pressure to drive pistons in cylinders or rotate hydraulic motors, providing excellent power-to-weight ratios and precise force control. Hydraulic systems generate high forces (hundreds of kilonewtons) through Pascal's principle where pressure acts equally throughout confined fluid. Components include pumps generating pressure, directional cont
ME 371: Rolling element bearings: Rolling element bearings support rotating shafts using balls or rollers between races, providing low friction, high load capacity, and long life. Types include deep groove ball (radial loads, moderate thrust), angular contact ball (combined loads), cylindrical roller (high radial loads), tapered roller (high radial and thrust), and needle (compact, high radial). Selection considers load magnitude/
ME 462: Modeling and control of timoshen: Mathematical modeling and control design for flexible beam structures with shear deformation and rotary inertia (Timoshenko theory) actuated by hydraulic cylinders. Model combines distributed-parameter beam dynamics (partial differential equations) with hydraulic actuator dynamics (nonlinear valve flow, pressure dynamics). Control challenges include infinite-dimensional plant, modal coupling, hydraulic nonlinearities, and actuation constraints. Approaches include modal truncation, spillover prevention, and nonlinear compensation. Applications include flexible robot arms, large space structures, construction equipment, and any system combining structural flexibility with hydraulic actuation requiring vibration suppression while achieving positioning objectives..
TAM 324: Composites: Engineered materials combining two or more constituent materials with distinct properties to achieve superior performance unattainable by individual components, typically consisting of reinforcing fibers or particles embedded in a continuous matrix phase. Common types include fiber-reinforced polymers (carbon, glass, aramid in epoxy), metal matrix composites, and concrete (aggregate in cement matrix). Composite behavior follows rule of mixtures for longitudinal properties, with performance depending on volume fraction, fiber orientation, interfacial bonding, and load transfer efficiency. Design considerations include anisotropy, manufacturability, joining methods, environmental degradation, and failure modes like fiber pullout and delamination..
TAM 324: Fracture of concrete, wood and: Failure mechanisms in quasi-brittle heterogeneous materials where crack propagation involves complex interactions with microstructural features. Concrete fractures through crack bridging by aggregates, crack deflection at interfaces, and formation of fracture process zones with distributed microcracking. Wood exhibits orthotropic fracture with different toughness values for crack propagation parallel versus perpendicular to grain, and along grain boundaries. Asphaltic concrete shows temperature and rate-dependent fracture transitioning from ductile at high temperatures to brittle at low temperatures. Understanding these mechanisms guides mix design, reinforcement strategies, and service temperature limitations..
ME 371: Behavior of composite materials: Composite material behavior differs from isotropic metals due to anisotropy (direction-dependent properties), heterogeneity (fiber-matrix structure), and unique failure modes (fiber breakage, matrix cracking, delamination, fiber-matrix debonding). Analysis requires considering fiber orientation, laminate stacking sequence, and hygrothermal effects (moisture, temperature). Composite advantages incl
MATH 441: Higher order linear des: Higher-order linear DEs have form aₙy^(n) + ... + a₁y' + a₀y = g(x), where n ≥ 3. For constant coefficients, characteristic equation aₙr^n + ... + a₁r + a₀ = 0 has n roots. Each real root r contributes e^(rx); each complex pair α ± βi contributes e^(αx)(c₁cos(βx) + c₂sin(βx)); repeated root of multiplicity k contributes e^(rx)(c₁ + c₂x + ... + cₖx^(k-1)). Solution space has dimension n (requires n initial/boundary conditions). Applications include beam vibrations, electrical networks, and control systems. Extension of second-order methods to arbitrary order..
ECE 205: Mesh-current methods: The mesh-current method (loop analysis) solves circuits by assigning currents to meshes (fundamental loops) and writing KVL equations. Each mesh current flows around its loop; actual branch currents are algebraic sums of mesh currents. For each mesh, write KVL: sum of voltage drops equals sum of voltage rises. Express voltages using Ohm's law: V = I_mesh R. Produces M equations for M meshes. This
MATH 241: Tests for conservativity: To test if vector field F =
MATH 442: Rectangles and cubes: Laplace's equation on rectangular domain [0,a]×[0,b] or cubic domain [0,a]×[0,b]×[0,c] solved by separation of variables. Assume u(x,y) = X(x)Y(y) or u(x,y,z) = X(x)Y(y)Z(z). Each coordinate yields Sturm-Liouville problem with eigenvalues/eigenfunctions depending on boundary conditions. Solution: u = ΣΣ cₘₙ Xₘ(x)Yₙ(y) or triple sum for cube. Boundary data determines coefficients via 2D or 3D Fourier series. Products of trigonometric functions form basis. Method extends to higher dimensions. Reveals multi-dimensional mode structure..
MATH 442: Poisson's formula: Poisson's formula solves Laplace's equation in disk or ball with Dirichlet boundary condition. For disk of radius R with u = g(θ) on boundary: u(r,θ) = (1/2π)∫₀²π g(φ) (R²-r²)/(R²-2Rr cos(θ-φ)+r²) dφ. The kernel (R²-r²)/(R²-2Rr cos(θ-φ)+r²) is Poisson kernel, weighting boundary data. In ball: 3D version. Formula shows harmonic functions are weighted averages of boundary values. Smooth interior solution from boundary data. Maximum principle evident. Used in potential theory, complex analysis, and probability..
MATH 221: The substitution rule: The substitution rule (u-substitution) evaluates integrals by changing variables: if u = g(x), then ∫f(g(x))g'(x)dx = ∫f(u)du. This reverses the chain rule for differentiation. The technique involves identifying an inner function u whose derivative (or multiple thereof) appears in the integrand, substituting to simplify, integrating with respect to u, then substituting back. For definite integrals
ME 360: Load cells: Force and weight measurement transducers using strain gages bonded to elastic elements (beams, columns, rings) that deform predictably under load. Common configurations include bending beam (shear/bending sensitive), column (compression), S-beam (tension and compression), and pancake (low profile). Strain gages in full Wheatbridge configuration provide temperature compensation and maximize signal
ME 481: Cursory review of musculoskeleta: An overview of the primary biological tissues comprising the musculoskeletal system including muscle (contractile tissue generating force), bone (rigid load-bearing structure), cartilage (low-friction bearing surface and shock absorber), tendons (connecting muscle to bone), and ligaments (connecting bone to bone). This review establishes foundational understanding of tissue composition, hierarchical organization from molecular to macroscopic scales, mechanical properties and functions, and integration into functional units. Understanding tissue characteristics provides context for analyzing whole-body movements, injury mechanisms, performance optimization, and clinical interventions. The review sets the stage for detailed study of individual tissue mechanics and system-level biomechanical analysis..
MATH 285: Slope fields: Slope fields (or direction fields) are visual tools for understanding first-order differential equations without solving them explicitly. At each point in the plane, a small line segment is drawn with slope equal to the differential equation's value at that point. These segments show the direction solution curves must follow, allowing qualitative analysis of solution behavior, stability, and long-
MATH 285: Interactive graph: laplace equ: The Laplace equation is a partial differential equation describing steady-state phenomena where there are no sources or sinks, such as electrostatic potential or steady-state heat distribution. Interactive visualizations show how solutions satisfy the equation with specified boundary values, demonstrating the maximum principle and harmonic functions. Students can modify boundary conditions to see
MATH 241: Level sets in 3d: Level sets (level surfaces) of a function f(x,y,z) are surfaces where the function takes a constant value: f(x,y,z) = k for various constants k. These surfaces show how the function value changes through space - moving between level surfaces means function value changes. Level sets provide 3D visualization of four-dimensional graphs (can't directly plot w = f(x,y,z) in 3D). Examples include equipo
TAM 456: Photoelasticity: Photoelasticity uses birefringent materials (stress-induced optical anisotropy) to visualize stress patterns. When stressed transparent model is viewed in polarized light, interference fringes appear. Isochromatic fringes indicate regions of constant maximum shear stress (σ₁-σ₂); isoclinic fringes show principal stress directions. Stress-optic law: optical retardation δ = Cσt where C is material fringe constant, σ is stress difference, t is thickness. Enables qualitative stress visualization and quantitative analysis through fringe counting. Applications include stress concentration visualization, validation of analytical solutions, and educational demonstrations. Largely superseded by numerical methods but valuable for insight..
TAM 445: Tensor square roots: For positive-definite symmetric tensor A, unique positive-definite square root √A exists such that (√A)² = A. Computed using spectral decomposition: if A = Σλᵢnᵢ⊗nᵢ, then √A = Σ√λᵢnᵢ⊗nᵢ. In component form: √A operates by taking square root of eigenvalues while preserving eigenvectors. Applications: polar decomposition F = RU relates deformation gradient F to rotation R and right stretch U = √(FᵀF)
ME 482: Finite element simulation and an: A computational technique discretizing complex geometries into meshes of simple elements (tetrahedra, hexahedra) to numerically solve mechanical problems involving irregular shapes, heterogeneous material properties, and complex boundary conditions. For musculoskeletal tissues, FE analysis predicts stress distributions in bones under physiological loading, optimizes implant designs, simulates cartilage contact mechanics, models tissue growth and remodeling, and investigates injury mechanisms. Process involves geometry creation from medical images, mesh generation balancing resolution and computational cost, material property assignment including anisotropy and nonlinearity, boundary condition application, solver execution, and post-processing visualization. Validation against experimental data ensures model accuracy and builds confidence in predictions..
TAM 324: Creep and thermal properties: Time-dependent plastic deformation occurring under constant stress at elevated temperatures as atoms diffuse and dislocations climb, causing materials to progressively strain and eventually fail through creep rupture. Creep behavior follows three stages: primary (decreasing strain rate), secondary (steady-state with constant rate), and tertiary (accelerating strain rate leading to failure). Thermal properties including thermal expansion, conductivity, and specific heat affect dimensional stability, thermal stresses, and heat dissipation. Understanding creep mechanisms (diffusional flow, dislocation creep, grain boundary sliding) and thermal management enables design of components for high-temperature service like turbine blades and pressure vessels..
ME 487: Device testing: Systematic characterization and validation of MEMS/NEMS device performance, reliability, and conformance to specifications through electrical, mechanical, optical, and environmental measurements. Testing encompasses parameter extraction (resistance, capacitance, resonant frequency), functional verification (actuation range, sensor response), reliability assessment (fatigue, creep, drift), and failure analysis. Methods include probe stations for electrical testing, interferometry for displacement measurement, environmental chambers for temperature cycling, and package-level testing. Design for testability principles ensure devices include test structures, accessibility for probing, and measurable parameters. Statistical process control monitors fabrication consistency and yield..
ME 487: Packaging: The critical process of enclosing, protecting, and interfacing MEMS/NEMS devices with the external world while maintaining functionality and reliability. Packaging addresses mechanical protection from handling and environment, electrical connections through wire bonding or flip-chip, thermal management, hermetic sealing against moisture and contaminants, and optical windows for sensors. Challenges include coefficient of thermal expansion mismatch, stress-induced performance drift, outgassing from package materials, and cost considerations since packaging often exceeds fabrication costs. Wafer-level packaging integrates encapsulation directly in fabrication, while hybrid approaches combine multiple die in custom packages..
ME 487: Subtractive processes: Fabrication techniques that selectively remove material from substrates or deposited layers to create patterns, cavities, and three-dimensional structures, primarily through etching processes. Subtractive methods include wet chemical etching using liquid etchants, dry plasma etching using reactive ions and radicals, and mechanical processes like polishing or dicing. Key characteristics include etch rate, selectivity (preferential etching of target materials over masks), anisotropy (directional versus isotropic profiles), and surface roughness. Etching processes are critical for releasing structures, creating cavities, defining device dimensions, and removing sacrificial layers in MEMS/NEMS fabrication..
ME 487: Bulk and surface micromachining: Two fundamental MEMS fabrication paradigms distinguished by whether structures are formed by etching into the substrate (bulk) or building layers on the surface (surface). Bulk micromachining uses anisotropic wet etching or deep reactive ion etching to create cavities, membranes, and cantilevers from the silicon wafer itself, producing robust structures with excellent material properties. Surface micromachining deposits and patterns multiple thin film layers, using sacrificial layer release to create suspended structures like beams and membranes. Each approach offers distinct advantages in structure geometry, material selection, integration density, and process complexity..
ME 487: Lithography: The fundamental patterning process for transferring geometric designs from masks onto substrate surfaces using light-sensitive photoresist materials and controlled exposure to ultraviolet radiation or other energy sources. Lithography defines critical features in MEMS/NEMS devices through photoresist coating, soft baking, mask alignment, exposure, development, and hard baking steps. Resolution depends on wavelength, numerical aperture, and photoresist chemistry. Advanced techniques include contact, proximity, and projection lithography as well as electron beam and X-ray lithography for nanoscale features. Pattern fidelity depends on controlling exposure dose, focus, diffraction effects, and process conditions..
ME 487: Scaling: The analysis of how physical phenomena, forces, and device performance change as characteristic dimensions shrink from macro to micro to nanoscale. Scaling laws reveal that surface forces (adhesion, surface tension) dominate over volume forces (gravity, inertia) at small scales, fundamentally altering design priorities and operational principles. Surface-to-volume ratio increases inversely with size, dramatically affecting heat transfer, electrical resistance, and mechanical resonance. Understanding scaling enables prediction of device behavior, identification of dominant physical effects, and exploitation of micro/nanoscale advantages like high resonant frequencies, low power consumption, and rapid thermal response..
ME 487: Drie and liga: Advanced MEMS fabrication technologies enabling high aspect ratio structures. Deep Reactive Ion Etching (DRIE) uses alternating cycles of etching and sidewall passivation (Bosch process) to create nearly vertical sidewalls hundreds of microns deep in silicon with aspect ratios exceeding 30:1. LIGA (Lithographie, Galvanoformung, Abformung - Lithography, Electroplating, Molding) uses X-ray lithography through thick photoresist to create ultra-high aspect ratio molds (>100:1), followed by electroplating to fill patterns and molding for replication. Both processes enable three-dimensional microstructures with precise vertical control for applications requiring large out-of-plane dimensions..
ME 487: Actuators: Microdevices that convert various forms of energy (electrical, thermal, magnetic, chemical) into controlled mechanical motion including displacement, force, or torque at microscales. Common actuation mechanisms include electrostatic forces between capacitor plates, thermal expansion in bimorph structures, piezoelectric strain in crystals, electromagnetic Lorentz forces, and shape memory alloy phase transformations. Performance metrics include stroke (displacement range), force generation, power consumption, response speed, and efficiency. MEMS actuators enable functions like mirror steering in optical switches, valve control in microfluidics, and resonator excitation in sensors, with design choices depending on application requirements..
ME 487: Photolithography: The specific lithographic process using ultraviolet light to transfer patterns from photomasks onto photoresist-coated substrates, serving as the primary patterning method in microelectronics and MEMS fabrication. The process involves photoresist application by spin coating, soft baking to remove solvents, mask alignment using precision stages and microscopes, UV exposure through the mask creating latent images in photoresist, post-exposure baking to complete chemical reactions, development to remove soluble regions, and hard baking for stability. Resolution depends on UV wavelength (typically 365-436nm), diffraction limits, photoresist contrast, and proximity between mask and wafer..
ME 487: Pressure sensors: Microfabricated devices that convert pressure changes into measurable electrical signals, typically using piezoresistive, capacitive, or resonant transduction mechanisms. Common designs include diaphragms that deflect under differential pressure, with piezoresistors detecting strain-induced resistance changes, capacitive gaps changing with deflection, or resonant frequencies shifting with stress. MEMS pressure sensors exploit bulk micromachining to create thin silicon diaphragms with controlled thickness and area, optimizing sensitivity, linearity, and frequency response. Applications span automotive manifold pressure, medical blood pressure monitoring, barometric altitude sensing, and industrial process control, with performance specifications including range, accuracy, temperature stability, and packaging compatibility..
ME 487: Microfluidics: The science and engineering of precisely controlling and manipulating fluids at volumes from nanoliters to microliters in channels with cross-sections of micrometers, enabling miniaturized chemical and biological analysis systems. Microfluidic devices leverage laminar flow regimes where fluids flow in parallel streams without turbulent mixing, relying on diffusion for controlled mixing. Surface effects dominate including capillary action, surface tension, and electrokinetic phenomena. Applications include point-of-care diagnostics, DNA amplification and sequencing, cell culture and analysis, drug screening, and chemical synthesis. Design challenges involve bubble management, dead volume minimization, sample introduction, waste handling, and integration with detection systems..
ME 360: Grounding and shielding techniqu: Methods for minimizing electromagnetic interference, ground loops, and noise coupling in measurement systems through proper ground connection topology, cable shielding, and signal routing. Single-point grounding prevents ground loops (current flow through ground creating voltage differences), while multipoint grounding reduces high-frequency impedance. Shielding uses conductive enclosures grounded
ME 401: Low-temperature properties of ma: Material properties change dramatically at cryogenic temperatures (below ~120 K), affecting structural, thermal, electrical, and magnetic behavior. Metals become brittle, requiring careful material selection for low-temperature equipment. Specific heat decreases significantly (following Debye T³ law), affecting cooling power requirements. Thermal conductivity of pure metals increases while that of alloys decreases. Some materials become superconducting, losing electrical resistance. Thermal contraction mismatches cause stress. Understanding these property variations is essential for designing cryogenic equipment, selecting structural materials, calculating heat leaks, and ensuring safety. Applications include LNG storage, space systems, superconducting magnets, and scientific instruments operating at cryogenic temperatures..
ME 360: Noise reduction techniques: Methods for minimizing unwanted signal corruption from various noise sources including electromagnetic interference, ground loops, thermal noise, shot noise, and quantization noise. Techniques include shielding and grounding to prevent pickup, twisted pair wiring for balanced transmission rejecting common-mode interference, filtering to remove out-of-band noise, averaging multiple measurements to
ME 360: Transducers: Devices converting physical quantities (force, pressure, temperature, displacement, acceleration, flow) into electrical signals (voltage, current, resistance, capacitance) or vice versa for measurement and actuation. Sensor transducers exploit physical principles including piezoresistivity (resistance change with strain), piezoelectricity (charge generation from stress), thermoelectricity (voltage
ME 455: Micro-sensors and actuators: Micro-sensors measure physical quantities (force, displacement, temperature, flow) while micro-actuators produce motion or forces at micro-scale, enabling automated micro-manufacturing. Sensors include capacitive displacement sensors (nanometer resolution), micro-force sensors (millinewton range), and vision systems (micrometer resolution). Actuators encompass piezoelectric positioners, micro-grippers, and precision stages. Integration provides feedback for closed-loop control essential for reliable micro-assembly and process monitoring. Understanding sensor-actuator characteristics enables designing effective micro-manufacturing automation systems..
CHEM 103: Laboratory orientation & safet: Laboratory orientation and safety encompasses essential protocols, procedures, and safety measures required for working in a chemistry laboratory. This includes understanding Material Safety Data Sheets (MSDS), proper use of personal protective equipment (PPE), emergency procedures, chemical handling and disposal, location of safety equipment like eyewash stations and fire extinguishers, and gener
ME 432: Fabrication and manufacturing of: Silicon solar cell manufacturing transforms raw silicon through multiple processing steps: ingot growth (Czochralski or float-zone), wafer sawing, surface texturing (pyramids for light trapping), junction formation (phosphorus diffusion creating n-type layer on p-type base), anti-reflection coating (silicon nitride), metallization (screen-printed silver contacts), and testing/binning. Manufacturing considerations include throughput, yield, cost, and environmental impact. Advances reducing cost include thinner wafers (diamond wire sawing), selective emitter structures, passivated contacts (PERC, TOPCon), and module-level improvements. Understanding manufacturing enables identifying cost-reduction opportunities and appreciating trade-offs between efficiency, throughput, and capital investment..
ME 432: Silicon based photovoltaics and: Silicon PV dominates the market (~95% share) using wafer-based cells with 15-22% module efficiency. Advantages include material abundance, mature manufacturing, and high efficiency. Disadvantages include energy-intensive purification and rigid, heavy modules. Thin-film technologies (CdTe, CIGS, a-Si) deposit micron-thick semiconductor layers on substrates, using less material and enabling flexible modules. Advantages include lower manufacturing energy, flexible/lightweight form factors, and better temperature coefficients. Disadvantages include lower efficiency (10-18%), stability concerns (some technologies), and material scarcity (some elements). Technology selection depends on application: efficiency-critical (silicon), lightweight/flexible (thin-film), or specific performance requirements..
ME 432: Module assembly: Module assembly packages individual solar cells into weatherproof, mechanically robust units for field deployment. Process steps include cell interconnection (soldering or conductive adhesive), lamination (encapsulating cells between glass and backsheet using EVA polymer), frame attachment (aluminum for mechanical support), and junction box installation (electrical termination and bypass diodes). Module design balances optical transmission, mechanical protection, thermal management, electrical isolation, and cost. Quality issues include delamination, corrosion, cell cracking, and hotspots. Understanding module assembly enables appreciating balance between component cost and reliability, and troubleshooting field failures..
ME 432: Light absorption: Light absorption in semiconductors occurs when photon energy exceeds the bandgap, promoting electrons from valence to conduction band. Absorption coefficient α(λ) determines penetration depth (1/α) varying with wavelength: strong absorption near bandgap, weak absorption for sub-bandgap photons. Direct bandgap semiconductors (GaAs, CdTe) have strong absorption (~10⁴ cm⁻¹), requiring only μm thickness. Indirect bandgap semiconductors (Si) have weaker absorption (~10² cm⁻¹), requiring hundreds of μm. Strategies enhancing absorption include surface texturing (reducing reflection), anti-reflection coatings, light trapping (internal reflection increasing effective path length), and tandem cells (capturing broader spectrum)..
ME 432: Pv materials including cdte, cig: Alternative PV materials offer different property combinations than silicon. CdTe (Cadmium Telluride) has optimal bandgap (1.5 eV), high absorption, and simple manufacturing but faces cadmium toxicity concerns and tellurium scarcity. CIGS (Copper Indium Gallium Selenide) offers tunable bandgap, high efficiency potential, and good stability but complex composition control and indium scarcity. a-Si (amorphous silicon) enables low-temperature deposition and flexibility but lower efficiency and light degradation. Organic PV uses carbon-based semiconductors offering ultra-low cost, flexibility, and solution processing but currently low efficiency and stability. Each technology occupies different market niches based on performance, cost, and application requirements..
ME 330: Crystallography: Crystallography studies the arrangement of atoms in crystalline materials forming periodic, repeating three-dimensional patterns called crystal lattices. Common structures include face-centered cubic (FCC), body-centered cubic (BCC), and hexagonal close-packed (HCP). Unit cells are the smallest repeating units defining the entire crystal. Crystal structure determines packing density, slip systems
ME 455: Microrobotics for micromanipulat: Microrobotics develops miniature robotic systems and specialized end-effectors for manipulating micro-components with precision and dexterity. Systems include parallel micro-grippers, compliant mechanisms, and multi-degree-of-freedom micromanipulators. Design challenges encompass actuator selection (piezoelectric, electrostatic, thermal), sensor integration, and control architecture. Approaches include minimizing adhesion through surface treatment, employing release strategies, and using compliance for error tolerance. Understanding microrobotics enables creating effective automation solutions for micro-assembly and manipulation tasks..
PHYS 212: Mirrors: Mirrors reflect light to form images through specular reflection. Plane mirrors create virtual, upright, same-size images. Curved mirrors (concave and convex) focus or diverge light with focal length f = R/2 (R is radius of curvature). The mirror equation (1/f = 1/do + 1/di) determines image location and properties. Understanding mirrors is essential for telescopes, solar concentrators, vehicle mi
PHYS 212: Reflection and refraction: Reflection occurs when light bounces off surfaces, obeying the law that incident and reflected angles are equal. Refraction is light bending when entering different media due to wave speed changes, governed by Snell's law (n₁sinθ₁ = n₂sinθ₂). The refractive index measures light speed reduction in materials. Understanding these phenomena explains mirrors, lenses, rainbows, mirages, fiber optics, an
PHYS 212: Lenses: Lenses are transparent curved optical elements that refract light to converge or diverge rays, forming images. Converging lenses focus parallel rays to a focal point; diverging lenses spread rays apart. The thin lens equation (1/f = 1/do + 1/di) relates object distance, image distance, and focal length. Magnification, real versus virtual images, and lens combinations are crucial for cameras, eyegl
ME 482: Nanoindentation: A technique measuring local mechanical properties at micro and nanoscale by pressing a sharp indenter (Berkovich pyramid or spherical tip) into material surfaces while continuously monitoring load and displacement with nano-Newton and nanometer resolution. Analysis of loading and unloading curves using Oliver-Pharr method extracts elastic modulus and hardness from contact stiffness and projected contact area. For biological tissues, nanoindentation enables property mapping across tissue zones (cartilage layers, bone osteons), characterization of individual microstructural features, hydration effects on properties, and evaluation of disease or treatment effects at cellular scales. Challenges include viscoelastic creep during holds, tip shape calibration, and substrate effects for thin samples..
ME 310: Pressure transducers: Pressure transducers convert fluid pressure into electrical signals (voltage, current, or digital output) using various sensing principles including strain gauges, piezoelectric crystals, capacitive elements, or optical sensors. They offer advantages over mechanical gauges: fast response time, remote monitoring capability, easy data logging, and compatibility with automated control systems. Common
ECE 206: Mosfet device operation and cha: MOSFETs (Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor Field-Effect Transistors) are voltage-controlled switches/amplifiers with three terminals: gate, drain, source. N-channel (NMOS) conducts when VGS > VTH; P-channel (PMOS) conducts when VGS
ME 401: Thermoelectrics: Thermoelectric cooling exploits the Peltier effect: passing electrical current through junctions of dissimilar semiconductors transfers heat from one junction (cold side) to the other (hot side). These solid-state devices have no moving parts, enabling precise temperature control, compact size, and high reliability. COP is low (~0.3-1.0) compared to vapor compression, limiting applications to small cooling loads, spot cooling, or where size/vibration are critical. Figure of merit ZT characterizes material performance. Applications include electronics cooling, portable coolers, temperature-stabilized laser diodes, and scientific instruments. Advanced materials research aims to improve ZT for broader applications. Understanding thermoelectric physics and device design enables selection for appropriate niche applications..
ECE 205: Circuit elements: Circuit elements are idealized components with specific voltage-current relationships. Resistors (V = IR) dissipate energy as heat. Capacitors (Q = CV, I = C dV/dt) store energy in electric fields. Inductors (V = L dI/dt) store energy in magnetic fields. Voltage sources maintain constant voltage; current sources maintain constant current. Elements connect at nodes through conductors. Passive eleme
ME 432: Charge carrier transport: Charge carrier transport describes electron and hole motion through semiconductors via drift (field-driven) and diffusion (concentration gradient-driven). Drift current density Jdrift = qμnE (mobility μ, carrier density n, field E) dominates in depletion regions. Diffusion current Jdiff = qD(dn/dx) (diffusivity D, gradient dn/dx) dominates in quasi-neutral regions. The continuity equation governs carrier concentration evolution including generation, recombination, and transport. Transport quality affects collection efficiency: higher mobility enables faster transport before recombination, while defects and impurities scatter carriers reducing mobility. Understanding transport mechanisms enables optimizing doping profiles, thicknesses, and material quality..
ME 432: Charge carrier collection: Charge carrier collection is the extraction of separated carriers at electrical contacts, generating photocurrent. Collection efficiency depends on carriers reaching contacts before recombining, requiring transport time
ME 432: Charge carrier thermalization: Thermalization is the rapid energy loss (~picoseconds) of hot carriers generated by photons with energy exceeding the bandgap, relaxing to band edges through phonon emission (heat). For example, a 3 eV photon absorbed in a 1 eV bandgap semiconductor creates a 2 eV hot electron that quickly thermalizes to 1 eV, wasting 2 eV as heat. This represents a fundamental efficiency loss in single-junction cells. Thermalization limits efficiency because excess photon energy cannot be extracted as electrical work. Strategies to reduce losses include hot carrier solar cells (extracting energy before thermalization), multi-junction cells (matching photon energies to multiple bandgaps), and quantum dots (discrete energy levels slowing thermalization)..
ME 320: Extended surface heat transfer: Extended surfaces (fins) are appendages attached to primary surfaces to enhance heat transfer by increasing surface area exposed to the surrounding fluid. Fin analysis determines temperature distribution and heat transfer rate using an energy balance that accounts for conduction along the fin and convection from the surface. Fin efficiency (ηf) and fin effectiveness (εf) quantify performance. Comm
ECE 205: Ohm's law: Ohm's law states that voltage across a resistor is proportional to current through it: V = IR, where R is resistance in ohms (Ω). This linear relationship holds for ohmic materials at constant temperature. Resistance depends on material (resistivity ρ), length (l), and cross-sectional area (A): R = ρl/A. Conductance G = 1/R is the inverse, measured in siemens (S). Ohm's law is fundamental for circ
ME 483: Definitions of life: The philosophical and scientific frameworks characterizing living systems through properties including organization (complex hierarchical structure), metabolism (energy and matter transformation), growth and development, reproduction, response to stimuli, adaptation through evolution, and homeostasis (maintaining internal conditions). In mechanobiology, understanding life's characteristics frames investigation of how mechanical forces influence these defining properties - how forces affect cellular organization through cytoskeleton, modulate metabolism through mechanotransduction pathways, guide development through tissue morphogenesis, and drive adaptation through mechanical selection pressures. These definitions distinguish living mechanically responsive systems from passive materials, emphasizing active sensing, signal processing, and adaptive responses to mechanical environments..
ME 371: Springs: Springs store and release elastic energy, providing controlled forces, absorbing shocks, maintaining contact, and isolating vibrations. Common types include helical compression/extension springs, torsion springs, Belleville washers, and leaf springs. Design parameters include wire diameter, coil diameter, number of coils, and material, determining spring rate (force per deflection), maximum stress
ME 360: Pneumatic actuators: Devices generating mechanical motion and force using compressed air expanding in cylinders driving pistons, rotating vanes, or inflating bladders. Pneumatic cylinders convert air pressure to linear force following F=P×A, with double-acting cylinders powered in both directions and single-acting using springs for return. Advantages include high power-to-weight ratio, inherent compliance, spark-free
ME 360: Pressure sensors: Transducers measuring fluid pressure through diaphragm deflection detected using piezoresistive, capacitive, piezoelectric, or optical sensing mechanisms. Piezoresistive sensors use diffused or bonded strain gages on diaphragms, capacitive sensors measure gap changes, piezoelectric sensors detect dynamic pressure through crystal charge generation. Sensor types include absolute (referenced to vacuu
ME 360: Systems frequency response: The steady-state output of a system to sinusoidal inputs across a range of frequencies, characterized by magnitude (gain) and phase shift as functions of frequency. Frequency response H(jω) is obtained by evaluating transfer function H(s) at s=jω, revealing resonant peaks, filtering characteristics, and bandwidth limitations. Bode plots display magnitude in decibels and phase in degrees versus log
TAM 335: Open-channel flow: Open-channel experiments demonstrate free-surface flow phenomena in flumes with adjustable bed slope. Measurements include flow depth, velocity profiles, discharge, and surface profiles. Experiments verify Manning's equation for uniform flow, demonstrate critical flow over weirs, create hydraulic jumps showing sequent depths, and generate gradually varied flow profiles upstream of gates or weirs. Students measure specific energy and Froude number at various locations. Flow visualization reveals surface patterns and turbulence. Understanding these experiments reinforces open-channel theory, develops measurement techniques for shallow flows, and demonstrates subcritical/supercritical transitions essential for hydraulic structure design..
ME 420: Radiation properties of solids: Radiation properties characterize how solid surfaces interact with thermal radiation: emissivity (ε) quantifies emission relative to blackbody, absorptivity (α) measures fraction absorbed, reflectivity (ρ) indicates reflection, and transmissivity (τ) for transparent media. Energy balance requires α + ρ + τ = 1. Kirchhoff's law relates emissivity to absorptivity. Properties depend on surface condition (polished, oxidized, painted), temperature, wavelength (spectral properties), and direction (directional properties). Understanding radiation properties enables selection of surface treatments for desired thermal behavior: high emissivity for heat rejection, low emissivity for insulation, selective surfaces for solar collectors. Property measurements use calorimetry, spectrophotometry, and integrating spheres..
TAM 456: Strain gages: Strain gages are resistive sensors bonded to surfaces to measure local strain through resistance change. Metallic foil gages operate on principle ΔR/R = GF·ε where GF ≈ 2 is gage factor. Wheatstone bridge circuit detects small resistance changes with high sensitivity. Rosette configurations (three gages at angles) determine complete strain state and principal strains. Applications include stress analysis, load monitoring, structural health monitoring. Installation requires proper surface preparation, adhesive selection, and lead wire management. Temperature compensation, gage calibration, and data acquisition system understanding are essential. Most widely used experimental stress measurement technique..
ME 360: Instrumentation amplifiers: Precision differential amplifiers specifically designed for sensor signal conditioning with very high input impedance (preventing sensor loading), extremely high common-mode rejection ratio (rejecting interference), low input offset voltage and drift, and precise gain control. Three-op-amp architecture provides high impedance buffering on both inputs with gain set by single external resistor while
ME 360: Instrumentation amplifiers and t: Specialized amplifier circuits with high input impedance, high common-mode rejection ratio (CMRR), low noise, and precise gain for amplifying small differential signals from sensors in presence of large common-mode voltages and noise. Instrumentation amplifiers typically use three-op-amp architecture enabling gain setting with single resistor while maintaining high input impedance on both inputs.
ME 360: Rtd's: Resistance Temperature Detectors measuring temperature through predictable resistance increase of pure metals (typically platinum, nickel, copper) with temperature. Platinum RTDs (Pt100, Pt1000) offer excellent accuracy, stability, and linearity following Callendar-Van Dusen equation, with positive temperature coefficient around 0.385%/°C. Four-wire measurement technique eliminates lead wire resis
ME 360: Piezoelectric and piezoresistive: Accelerometers measuring vibration and shock using piezoelectric crystals generating charge proportional to applied stress from seismic mass acceleration, or piezoresistive elements changing resistance under stress. Piezoelectric accelerometers offer high sensitivity, wide frequency range (DC-limited), and rugged construction for general vibration measurement. Piezoresistive accelerometers measure
TAM 335: Pressure and flow rate measurem: Pressure measurement techniques include manometers (U-tube, inclined), mechanical gauges (Bourdon, diaphragm), and transducers (strain gauge, piezoelectric, capacitive). Flow rate measurement uses differential pressure devices (pitot tube, venturi, orifice, nozzle), velocity-area methods, positive displacement meters, turbine meters, and electromagnetic or ultrasonic meters. Selection depends on accuracy requirements, fluid properties, flow range, and cost. Calibration ensures accuracy; uncertainty analysis quantifies measurement quality. Understanding measurement principles enables proper instrument selection, installation, and interpretation for hydraulic systems, HVAC, process control, and research applications. Accurate measurement is essential for system monitoring, control, and performance validation..
ECE 205: Voltage and current divisions: Voltage divider: for series resistors, voltage divides proportionally: Vₓ = (Rₓ/(R₁+R₂+...))Vtotal. Higher resistance gets more voltage. Current divider: for parallel resistors, current divides inversely proportional to resistance: Iₓ = (Gtotal/Gₓ)Itotal, where G = 1/R. Lower resistance gets more current. These rules provide shortcuts for calculating voltages and currents without full circuit anal
ME 461: Dc circuits: Fundamental electrical networks with constant voltages and currents governed by Ohm's law (V=IR), Kirchhoff's voltage law (sum of voltages around loop equals zero), and Kirchhoff's current law (sum of currents at node equals zero). Circuit elements include resistors (energy dissipation), voltage sources (constant potential), current sources (constant flow), and switches (circuit interruption). Analysis techniques include series/parallel reduction, voltage/current dividers, Thevenin/Norton equivalents, and node/mesh analysis. Understanding DC circuits enables design of power supplies, sensor interfacing, and actuator drive circuits. Applications in mechatronics include motor power distribution, sensor biasing, LED indicators, and relay control circuits requiring DC power and control..
ME 360: Laboratory instruments: Electronic test equipment for signal generation, measurement, and analysis including oscilloscopes (displaying voltage waveforms versus time), function generators (creating test signals), multimeters (measuring voltage, current, resistance), spectrum analyzers (frequency domain display), and data acquisition systems (computerized multi-channel recording). Oscilloscopes reveal signal shape, timing,
ECE 205: Time domain analysis: Time-domain analysis examines circuit behavior as functions of time t. Involves solving differential equations arising from capacitor (I = C dV/dt) and inductor (V = L dI/dt) relations. Response components: natural response (transient, source-free, decays), forced response (steady-state due to sources), and complete response (sum). Initial conditions from energy storage determine natural response
ME 340: Measurement systems: Measurement systems in dynamics utilize the principles of vibrating systems to sense and record physical quantities like displacement, velocity, and acceleration. Accelerometers, for example, are essentially spring-mass-damper systems where the relative motion between the mass and housing indicates the base acceleration. Understanding the frequency response of these instruments is crucial for accu
ME 170: Hidden lines: Hidden line removal displays 3D objects with obscured edges shown as dashed or removed entirely, clarifying which surfaces are visible from the current viewpoint. This technique eliminates ambiguity inherent in wireframe views by indicating depth relationships. Hidden line views are essential for technical drawings, allowing readers to understand 3D form from 2D projections. The algorithm determin
ME 170: Section and part-section views: Section views cut through objects along a defined plane, removing material on one side to reveal interior features otherwise hidden in standard orthographic views. Full sections cut completely through, half sections cut halfway (useful for symmetric parts), and partial sections remove only necessary portions. Hatching (cross-sectional lines) indicates cut surfaces, following material-specific patt
ME 170: Perspective view: Perspective view projects 3D models with size decreasing with distance from viewpoint, mimicking human vision and photography. Unlike orthographic projection maintaining constant scale, perspective creates converging parallel lines (like railroad tracks appearing to meet at horizon). Perspective views provide intuitive depth perception, useful for design review and presentations but inappropriate
MATH 241: Surfaces in 3-space: Surfaces in three-dimensional space are two-dimensional objects embedded in 3D, defined by equations relating x, y, and z coordinates, or parametrically using two parameters. They can be represented as level sets of functions (implicit form), explicit functions z=f(x,y), or parametric equations. Understanding surfaces is fundamental to visualizing and analyzing three-dimensional geometric objects
MATH 241: Quadric surfaces: Quadric surfaces are 3D surfaces defined by second-degree equations in x, y, and z, generalizing conic sections to three dimensions. Types include ellipsoids (egg-shaped), hyperboloids (saddle-like, one or two sheets), paraboloids (bowl-shaped, elliptic or hyperbolic), cones, and cylinders. Standard forms like x²/a² + y²/b² + z²/c² = 1 (ellipsoid) help identify and graph these surfaces. Understand
ME 451: Surface modeling: Surface modeling represents object boundaries using mathematical surface patches, enabling complex freeform shapes beyond simple primitives. Techniques include Bezier surfaces, B-spline surfaces, and NURBS (Non-Uniform Rational B-Splines) which provide local control, smooth continuity, and exact representation of both freeform and analytic surfaces. Surface modeling is essential for automotive styling, aerospace design, consumer products, and mold design where aesthetic and aerodynamic considerations require smooth, complex geometries. Surfaces are defined by control points, weights, and knot vectors, allowing precise shape manipulation..
ME 170: 3d solids and surfaces: 3D solid modeling creates complete volumetric representations with defined interior and exterior, enabling mass property calculations, interference detection, and unambiguous visualization. Surface modeling defines only the bounding skin using patches (NURBS, Bezier, etc.), suitable for complex curved shapes. Solid modelers use Constructive Solid Geometry (CSG) combining primitives or Boundary Rep
MATH 241: Curves in space: Curves in 3D space are defined by vector-valued functions r(t) =
TAM 335: Flow nets: Flow nets are graphical representations of two-dimensional potential flow showing streamlines (lines tangent to velocity) and equipotential lines (constant velocity potential). Streamlines and equipotential lines intersect orthogonally forming curvilinear squares. Flow nets enable visualization and approximate quantitative analysis of complex 2D flows including seepage through porous media, groundwater flow, and ideal fluid flow around objects. Sketching flow nets requires satisfying boundary conditions and orthogonality. Though computational methods largely supersede manual flow nets, understanding them provides intuition about flow patterns and verifies computational results. Flow nets beautifully connect mathematical description to physical flow visualization..
TAM 335: Flow visualization: Flow visualization makes invisible fluid motion visible through various techniques revealing flow patterns, separation, vortices, and turbulence. Methods include dye/smoke injection (marking fluid pathlines/streaklines), surface flow visualization (oil films, tufts), shadowgraph/schlieren (density gradients in gases), particle image velocimetry (PIV) capturing particle fields, and computational visualization (CFD post-processing). Streamlines show instantaneous velocity direction; pathlines track particle trajectories; streaklines connect particles from common origin. Visualization aids understanding of complex flows, validates computational models, and identifies flow features guiding design improvements. Modern engineering relies on both experimental and computational visualization for flow analysis..
ME 447: Introduction to povray: POV-Ray (Persistence of Vision Raytracer) is a ray-tracing software for creating photorealistic 3D visualizations through scene description language. In soft robotics research, POV-Ray generates high-quality renderings of simulated robots, illustrating deformation, motion sequences, and complex geometries. Users define objects, materials, lighting, and camera perspectives using a scripting language. POV-Ray complements scientific visualization by producing publication-quality images and animations. While modern alternatives exist, POV-Ray remains valuable for understanding ray-tracing principles and creating compelling visual representations of computational results..
MATH 241: Surface area: Surface area in multivariable calculus measures the total area of a curved surface in three-dimensional space. For parametric surfaces, it is computed using a double integral involving the magnitude of the cross product of partial derivative vectors. For surfaces given as z=f(x,y), the formula incorporates the partial derivatives to account for how the surface stretches and tilts relative to the x
MATH 241: Tangent planes to parametric s: A tangent plane to a parametric surface at a point is the flat plane that best approximates the surface near that point. It is constructed using two tangent vectors obtained by taking partial derivatives of the parametric equations with respect to each parameter. The normal vector to this plane is found via the cross product of these tangent vectors, providing crucial information for surface orien
MATH 241: Surface integrals: Surface integrals extend the concept of integration to functions defined over curved surfaces in three-dimensional space. They compute the accumulated value of a scalar field over a surface, accounting for the surface's geometry. Surface integrals are used to calculate quantities like mass, charge distribution, or flux, and require integrating the product of a function with the surface area elemen
TAM 470: Finite volume methods: Finite Volume Method (FVM) enforces conservation laws on control volumes. Integrates governing equations over cells: d/dt∫u dV = -∫F·n dS + ∫S dV, where F is flux. Fluxes computed at cell faces via interpolation and Riemann solvers. Inherently conservative - discretization exactly preserves conservation. Advantages: natural for conservation laws, handles shocks well, flexible geometries. Applicati
TAM 470: Finite element methods: Finite Element Method (FEM) discretizes domain into elements with piecewise polynomial approximations. Weak formulation via weighted residuals or variational principle. Shape functions interpolate within elements; assembly creates global system. Advantages: handles complex geometries, systematic error control, mature theory and software. Process: mesh generation → element matrices → assembly → bou
TAM 445: Theorems from advanced calculus: Key theorems relate volume and surface integrals, essential for continuum mechanics. Divergence theorem (Gauss): ∫v(∇·T)dV = ∫s T·n dS converts volume integral of divergence to surface integral. Stokes theorem: ∫s(∇×v)·n dS = ∫c v·dx relates surface integral of curl to line integral. Reynolds transport theorem: d/dt ∫v φ dV = ∫v (∂φ/∂t + ∇·(φv)) dV for moving volumes. These enable converting betwe
ME 412: Gpu accelerated heat conduction: GPU acceleration dramatically speeds computation for heat conduction and fluid flow in complex geometries using parallel processing. Challenges include geometric complexity (requiring unstructured or adaptive meshes), boundary condition implementation on irregular domains, and memory management for large 3D problems. GPU kernels perform stencil operations on millions of grid points simultaneously. Techniques include immersed boundary methods, level-set methods for interface tracking, and body-fitted coordinates. Applications span realistic engineering geometries: turbine blades, biomedical devices, porous media, and multi-component assemblies. Combining GPU computing with complex geometry handling enables practical high-fidelity simulations previously requiring supercomputers..
ME 170: Orthographic projections: Orthographic projection creates 2D views by projecting 3D objects onto planes perpendicular to viewing directions, maintaining true dimensions without perspective distortion. Standard views include front, top, right side, following either first-angle (European) or third-angle (American) projection conventions. Unlike perspective views, parallel lines remain parallel and dimensions are measurable,
ME 320: View factors: View factors (configuration factors or shape factors) quantify the geometric relationship between surfaces exchanging radiation, representing the fraction of radiation leaving surface i that directly strikes surface j. Denoted F_ij, they depend only on geometry and orientation, not surface properties. Key relations include reciprocity (A_i F_ij = A_j F_ji), summation (ΣF_ij = 1 for surface i), and
ME 420: Diffuse gray analysis: Diffuse gray analysis assumes surfaces emit and reflect diffusely (direction-independent) and properties are wavelength-independent (gray). These simplifying assumptions enable practical radiation exchange calculations using the radiosity method. For N-surface enclosures, N equations relate radiosities to surface temperatures and incident radiation. The solution determines net heat transfer at each surface. Resistance networks provide physical insight: surface resistance (1-ε)/(εA) represents emission difficulty, space resistance 1/(A_i F_ij) represents geometric obstruction. Though real surfaces aren't perfectly gray or diffuse, this analysis provides reasonable accuracy for many engineering applications. Understanding diffuse-gray analysis forms the foundation for practical radiation calculations..
ME 420: Specular analysis: Specular analysis handles surfaces with mirror-like reflection where incident and reflected angles are equal (like geometric optics). Specular surfaces redirect radiation deterministically unlike diffuse surfaces that scatter in all directions. Multiple specular reflections require ray tracing to track radiation paths. The radiosity method extends to specular surfaces by carefully accounting for reflection directions. Applications include concentrating solar collectors using mirrors, radiation shields with polished surfaces, and optical systems. Specular surfaces enable beam steering and concentration impossible with diffuse surfaces. Most real surfaces exhibit mixed behavior (directional-diffuse), but limiting cases (purely diffuse vs. purely specular) bound actual behavior and guide design..
ME 445: The geometry of image formation: The geometry of image formation describes the mathematical relationship between 3D world coordinates and 2D image coordinates in a camera. Using perspective projection models, points in 3D space are mapped onto the image plane through the camera's optical center. The pinhole camera model captures this geometry with parameters including focal length, principal point, and lens distortion coefficients. Understanding image formation geometry is essential for relating image measurements to world coordinates, enabling depth estimation, pose computation, and visual servoing. Homogeneous coordinates and projection matrices provide elegant mathematical frameworks for these transformations..
MATH 241: Lines from gradient: Normal lines to surfaces are lines perpendicular to the surface at a point, with direction given by the gradient vector at that point. For surface f(x,y,z) = k at point (x₀,y₀,z₀), the normal line is r(t) =
CS 101: Variables: Variables are named storage locations holding data values that can change during program execution. Variable types include integers, floats, strings, and booleans. Understanding variable declaration, assignment, naming conventions, and type conversions enables storing and manipulating data in programs. Variables are fundamental to all programming, allowing calculations, storing results, and repres
MATH 257: Orthogonal projection onto lin: Orthogonal projection of vector b onto subspace V is the closest point in V to b, denoted proj_V(b). For projection onto a line through vector a, the formula is proj_a(b) = ((a·b)/(a·a))a. For projection onto column space of matrix A, it's proj(b) = A(A^TA)^(-1)A^Tb = Pb, where P is the projection matrix. The projection satisfies b = proj_V(b) + perp_V(b), where the perpendicular component is orthogonal to V. Projections are fundamental to least squares, optimization, and decomposing vectors..
ME 432: Current electricity production s: Current electricity production is dominated by fossil fuels (coal, natural gas, oil ~65%), with nuclear (~10%), hydroelectric (~16%), and renewables (~10% and growing) comprising the remainder. Challenges include greenhouse gas emissions driving climate change, finite fossil fuel reserves, grid infrastructure limitations, energy storage needs for intermittent renewables, geopolitical dependencies, and balancing cost, reliability, and environmental impact. The transition to renewable electricity faces technical hurdles (storage, transmission), economic barriers (upfront costs), and institutional inertia. Understanding these challenges contextualizes photovoltaics' role in future energy systems..
ME 432: Estimating solar array outputs: Solar array output estimation predicts electrical generation from system specifications and environmental conditions. Calculations consider solar irradiance (varies by location, time, season, weather), panel specifications (rated power, temperature coefficients, degradation), system losses (wiring, inverters, soiling, shading, mismatch), and orientation/tilt. Methods include simple capacity factor approaches, hour-by-hour simulation using TMY (Typical Meteorological Year) data, and software tools (PVWatts, SAM). Accurate predictions require understanding that nameplate rating assumes Standard Test Conditions (1000 W/m², 25°C, AM1.5) rarely achieved in practice..
ME 432: The sun and incoming solar radia: The sun emits approximately 3.8×10²⁶ watts total power, with Earth intercepting ~1.7×10¹⁷ watts. The solar constant (1361 W/m² at Earth's orbit) represents extraterrestrial solar irradiance. Atmospheric effects (absorption, scattering, reflection) reduce and modify the spectrum reaching Earth's surface, quantified by Air Mass (AM) - the path length ratio relative to vertical. AM0 is extraterrestrial, AM1.5 standard for terrestrial PV testing represents sun at 48° zenith angle. Understanding solar resource variability (latitude, season, time, weather, atmospheric conditions) is essential for PV system design and performance prediction..
ME 432: Solar photovoltaic array outputs: PV array output is the electrical power generated under specific operating conditions, characterized by current-voltage (I-V) and power-voltage (P-V) curves. Key parameters include short-circuit current Isc (maximum current at zero voltage), open-circuit voltage Voc (maximum voltage at zero current), maximum power point (Pmax at Vmp, Imp), and fill factor FF = Pmax/(Voc·Isc) indicating curve 'squareness'. Output varies with irradiance (current increases linearly), temperature (voltage decreases ~0.5%/°C), and spectral content. Maximum power point tracking (MPPT) algorithms optimize inverter operating point. Understanding array output characteristics enables system design, performance monitoring, and troubleshooting..
ME 432: Design of arrays: Array design optimizes PV system configuration for site conditions, energy requirements, and constraints. Design considerations include panel selection (technology, efficiency, cost), orientation and tilt (typically facing equator at latitude angle, but varies with application), string sizing (series panels for voltage, parallel strings for current), inverter matching, balance-of-system components, structural support, electrical code compliance, and grid interconnection. Design tools perform shading analysis, layout optimization, and performance/economic modeling. Good design balances energy production, system cost, reliability, aesthetics, and installation constraints..
ME 432: Fundamental limits to conversion: Solar cell efficiency is fundamentally limited by thermodynamics, spectral mismatch between solar spectrum and semiconductor bandgap, and unavoidable loss mechanisms. The Shockley-Queisser limit (~33% for single junction) arises from sub-bandgap photons not absorbed, excess photon energy lost as heat (thermalization), radiative recombination, and voltage loss required for current extraction. Strategies exceeding single-junction limits include multi-junction cells (stacking bandgaps), hot carrier extraction (preventing thermalization), and photon management (concentration, spectrum splitting). Understanding fundamental limits guides material selection, device architectures, and realistic efficiency expectations..
ME 420: Property definitions: Precise property definitions distinguish between total/spectral, directional/hemispherical, and emissive/absorptive/reflective characteristics. Spectral properties vary with wavelength λ; total properties integrate over entire spectrum. Directional properties depend on angle θ from surface normal; hemispherical properties integrate over hemisphere. Emissive power E is energy emitted per unit area per unit time; irradiation G is incident radiation; radiosity J includes emitted and reflected radiation. Understanding these definitions prevents confusion in radiation analysis and enables proper application of property data from handbooks or measurements. Correct property selection is critical for accurate thermal design in high-temperature applications, spacecraft, and solar energy systems..
ME 432: Fundamental processes underlying: Solar cell operation involves sequential processes: (1) photon absorption generating electron-hole pairs, (2) carrier thermalization dissipating excess energy, (3) charge separation by built-in electric field at p-n junction, (4) carrier transport through semiconductor driven by diffusion and drift, (5) carrier collection at contacts extracting electrical current. Each process has associated losses: incomplete absorption, thermalization waste, recombination preventing collection, and resistive losses. Optimizing solar cell efficiency requires maximizing absorption, minimizing thermalization, enhancing separation, improving transport, and efficiently extracting carriers. Understanding these processes guides material selection, device structure design, and performance improvement strategies..
ME 320: Overview of radiation heat trans: Radiation heat transfer involves electromagnetic wave emission from surfaces due to thermal motion of charged particles, enabling energy transport without a medium. All surfaces above absolute zero emit radiation following the Stefan-Boltzmann law (E = σT⁴). Radiation spans wavelengths from ultraviolet through visible to infrared. Key concepts include blackbody radiation (perfect emitter/absorber)
ME 432: Energy and electricity productio: Global energy production examines worldwide energy consumption patterns, sources (fossil fuels, nuclear, renewables), generation capacity, and distribution infrastructure. Electricity represents approximately 20% of total energy use, with the remainder in transportation, heating, and industrial processes. Understanding global energy scales provides context for photovoltaics' potential impact: annual global electricity consumption is approximately 25,000 TWh, while solar insolation on Earth's surface exceeds this by orders of magnitude. Analysis includes energy units (Joules, Watt-hours, Quads), regional variations, growth projections, and the challenge of transitioning from fossil fuels to sustainable sources..
ME 462: Introduction to thermal and wind: Dynamic models and control strategies for power generation plants including thermal (coal, gas, nuclear) with boiler-turbine-generator dynamics, and wind farms with turbine aerodynamics, electrical generation, and grid integration. Thermal plant control addresses load following, pressure regulation, and efficiency optimization with slow thermal dynamics and constraints. Wind control handles variable speed operation, pitch angle regulation, power quality, and grid stability with stochastic wind inputs. Both require coordinated multi-variable control balancing performance, efficiency, equipment stress, and grid requirements. Applications in power systems requiring reliable generation meeting demand while optimizing economics and environmental impact..
ME 432: Semiconductors: optical and elec: Semiconductors have electrical conductivity between metals and insulators, with electronic properties arising from band structure. The valence band (filled electron states) and conduction band (empty states enabling conduction) are separated by bandgap Eg. Optical absorption occurs when photon energy exceeds Eg, promoting electrons from valence to conduction band, creating electron-hole pairs. Key properties include bandgap (determining absorption spectrum), carrier mobility (affecting conductivity), recombination lifetime (affecting carrier collection), and doping (controlling conductivity type and magnitude). Understanding semiconductor physics is fundamental to solar cell operation and optimization..
ECE 205: Ac steady-state analysis and ac: AC steady-state analysis uses phasors and impedances to find circuit response to sinusoidal sources after transients decay. All voltages and currents are sinusoidal at source frequency. AC power has three forms: real power P = Vrms Irms cos(θ) (average, does work, watts), reactive power Q = Vrms Irms sin(θ) (stored/returned, VARs), and apparent power S = Vrms Irms (total, VA). Power factor cos(θ)
ME 432: Identification of loss mechanism: Loss mechanisms reduce solar cell efficiency below theoretical limits, including optical losses (reflection, transmission, shading), recombination losses (bulk, surface, Auger), resistance losses (series resistance from contacts/metallization, shunt resistance from defects), and mismatch losses (cell-to-cell variations in modules). Diagnostic techniques identifying losses include I-V curve analysis (series/shunt resistance), quantum efficiency measurements (wavelength-dependent losses), electroluminescence imaging (shunts, cracks), and lock-in thermography (hot spots, resistance). Understanding and quantifying losses guides improvement strategies: anti-reflection coatings reduce optical losses, passivation reduces surface recombination, improved metallization reduces series resistance..
ME 320: Wavelength-dependent surface pro: Real surfaces exhibit wavelength-selective radiation properties where emissivity, absorptivity, and reflectivity vary with wavelength. Spectral emissivity ελ describes emission at specific wavelengths, while total emissivity averages over the entire spectrum weighted by blackbody distribution. This wavelength dependence enables selective surfaces for solar collectors (high absorptivity in solar sp
ME 320: Radiation: Radiation heat transfer involves electromagnetic wave emission, absorption, and transmission, enabling energy transport across vacuum or transparent media. All matter above absolute zero radiates according to temperature (Stefan-Boltzmann law: E = εσT⁴) with emission spectrum described by Planck's law for blackbodies. Real surfaces are characterized by emissivity (emission), absorptivity (absorpti
ECE 206: Bjt review and logic gates: Laboratory exploration of BJT characteristics and logic gate implementation. Measure BJT parameters: IC-VBE curves, β determination, and switching times. Build and test logic gates using discrete BJTs and resistors. Observe voltage levels for logic 0 and 1, verify truth tables, and measure propagation delays. Compare theoretical predictions with measurements. Use oscilloscope to observe switching
