ECE 205: Introduction to bjts: Bipolar Junction Transistors (BJTs) are three-terminal semiconductor devices (emitter, base, collector) that amplify current or act as switches. Two types: NPN (common) and PNP. Small base current IB controls large collector current IC: IC = βIB, where β (gain) typically 50-300. BJT operates in three regions: cutoff (off, no IC), active (amplifier, linear IC-VBE relation), and saturation (on, IC l
ECE 205: Logic gates using bjts: BJTs implement logic gates by operating in cutoff (logic 0) and saturation (logic 1) regions. For NPN BJT: high base voltage drives saturation (output low if common emitter), low base keeps cutoff (output high). RTL (Resistor-Transistor Logic) uses resistors for pull-up/down. DTL (Diode-Transistor Logic) adds diodes for logic functions. TTL (Transistor-Transistor Logic) is standard BJT logic famil
ME 420: Directional and spectral effects: Directional effects describe how radiation properties vary with angle from surface normal. Most surfaces exhibit diffuse behavior (Lambertian) at large wavelengths but specular reflection at small wavelengths when surface roughness is less than wavelength. Emissivity typically decreases at grazing angles. Spectral effects arise because properties vary with wavelength: polished metals have low emissivity in infrared but higher in visible; selective surfaces exploit spectral variation for solar applications (high α_solar, low ε_infrared). Understanding these effects enables advanced thermal control: spacecraft surface design, thermophotovoltaic devices, and temperature-dependent emissivity affecting radiation thermometry. Proper accounting for directional and spectral effects improves accuracy of radiation exchange calculations..
ME 420: Finite and semi-finite bodies: Finite bodies have boundaries in all directions (slab, cylinder, sphere), requiring complete spatial solution with boundary conditions on all surfaces. Semi-infinite bodies extend infinitely in one or more directions, with temperature disturbance not reaching the far boundary during the time of interest. Semi-infinite solutions use error functions: T(x,t) = T_i + (T_s - T_i)erf(x/2√αt) for constant surface temperature. The penetration depth δ ~ √αt characterizes how far thermal disturbance travels. Finite body solutions use separation of variables or superposition. Applications include surface heat treatment (semi-infinite), planetary diurnal temperature variation, and food freezing/thawing. Choice between finite and semi-infinite models depends on time scale relative to geometry..
ME 420: Radiant surface exchange: Radiant surface exchange analyzes heat transfer between surfaces via thermal radiation in enclosures. Energy leaving a surface (radiosity) includes emission and reflection; energy incident on a surface (irradiation) originates from all other surfaces. The net radiation heat transfer depends on surface temperatures, emissivities, and geometric view factors quantifying what fraction of radiation leaving one surface strikes another. For gray, diffuse surfaces, the radiosity method solves simultaneous equations for each surface. Two-surface problems reduce to simple resistance networks. Applications include furnace design, radiation shields, spacecraft thermal control, and radiant heating. Understanding surface exchange enables thermal design of systems where radiation dominates..
ME 420: Shape factors: Shape factors (view factors, configuration factors) F_ij quantify the fraction of diffuse radiation leaving surface i that directly strikes surface j. They depend only on geometry and orientation, not surface properties or temperatures. Key relations include reciprocity (A_i F_ij = A_j F_ji), summation (Σ F_ij = 1 for surface i), and view factor algebra for complex geometries. Analytical formulas exist for simple configurations (parallel plates, perpendicular plates, concentric cylinders/spheres). Numerical methods (contour integration, Monte Carlo ray tracing) calculate factors for complex geometries. Understanding shape factors is essential for radiation exchange analysis; they appear in radiosity equations and determine radiation resistance networks..
ME 320: Directional characteristics of t: Thermal radiation emission and reflection vary with direction relative to the surface normal. Directional (hemispherical) emissivity ε(θ) describes emission into a specific direction at angle θ. Diffuse surfaces emit/reflect equally in all directions following Lambert's cosine law (intensity proportional to cos θ). Specular surfaces reflect like mirrors with incident and reflected angles equal. Re
ME 320: Graybody exchange: Graybody radiation exchange analyzes heat transfer between surfaces in an enclosure where surfaces are gray (wavelength-independent properties) and diffuse. The radiosity method tracks leaving radiation (emitted plus reflected) and incident radiation on each surface. Net radiation heat transfer depends on emissivities, surface temperatures, view factors, and enclosure geometry. Two-surface enclosu
ME 340: Dynamic system classification: Systems classified by properties: Linear vs. nonlinear (superposition applies or not). Time-invariant vs. time-varying (parameters constant or changing). Continuous vs. discrete time. Lumped vs. distributed parameters. Deterministic vs. stochastic. SISO (single input/output) vs. MIMO. Order: number of energy storage elements (determines ODE order). Stable vs. unstable. Understanding classification
PHYS 212: Ac circuits: AC (alternating current) circuits use sinusoidally varying voltages and currents, requiring analysis with phasors, complex impedance, and RMS values. Resistors, capacitors, and inductors respond differently to AC: resistors dissipate power, capacitors block DC but pass AC (capacitive reactance), and inductors pass DC but oppose AC (inductive reactance). Understanding AC is essential for power dist
ECE 205: Equivalence theorems: Equivalence theorems replace complex circuit portions with simpler equivalents. Thévenin's theorem: any linear two-terminal network can be replaced by voltage source Vth in series with resistance Rth. Norton's theorem: equivalent current source IN in parallel with RN. These are dual: Vth = IN·RN, Rth = RN. Source transformation converts between voltage and current source models. Maximum power tran
ECE 205: Sinusoidal excitation and phaso: Phasor representation transforms sinusoidal time-domain signals v(t) = Vm cos(ωt + φ) to complex frequency-domain: V = Vm∠φ = Vm e^(jφ). Euler's formula: e^(jωt) = cos(ωt) + j sin(ωt) enables this transformation. Phasors convert differential equations to algebraic equations using complex impedances. Time derivatives become jω multiplication: d/dt → jω. Phasor diagrams visualize amplitude and phase
ECE 205: Node-voltage: The node-voltage method (nodal analysis) solves circuits by writing KCL equations at nodes, expressing currents in terms of node voltages. Choose a reference node (ground, 0V), then unknown node voltages become variables. For each non-reference node, write KCL: sum of currents leaving equals zero. Use Ohm's law to express currents as (Vi - Vj)/R. Produces N-1 equations for N nodes. This systematic
PHYS 212: Capacitance: Capacitance measures a conductor's ability to store electric charge at a given potential, defined as C = Q/V. Capacitance depends on geometry and materials, not on charge or voltage. Capacitors consist of two conductors separated by an insulator (dielectric), storing energy in the electric field between plates. Understanding capacitance is essential for electronics, filtering, energy storage, and
MATH 241: Electrostatics and gauss's law: Gauss's law applies the divergence theorem to electrostatics, stating that the electric flux through a closed surface equals the enclosed charge divided by the permittivity constant. This fundamental law relates the electric field's behavior at boundaries to charge distributions in the interior. It provides an elegant method for calculating electric fields with high symmetry and demonstrates a dee
PHYS 212: Electric flux: Electric flux quantifies the amount of electric field penetrating through a surface, calculated as the surface integral of the electric field's normal component. It represents the 'flow' of field lines through an area, though nothing physically flows. Flux is positive when field lines emerge from a surface, negative when entering. Understanding flux, including its relationship to charge and surfac
PHYS 214: Superconductors: Superconductors exhibit zero electrical resistance and perfect diamagnetism (Meissner effect - expelling magnetic fields) below critical temperature Tc. Cooper pairs - bound electron pairs mediated by lattice vibrations (phonons) - form a macroscopic quantum state described by a single wave function. BCS theory explains conventional superconductivity. Type I superconductors completely expel fields
PHYS 212: Magnetic force: Magnetic force acts on moving charges in magnetic fields, perpendicular to both velocity and field direction, given by F = qv × B. This force does no work since it's perpendicular to velocity, causing charges to move in circular or helical paths. Magnetic force on current-carrying wires depends on current, length, and field strength. Understanding the right-hand rule and motion in magnetic fields
ME 461: Timers and pulse-width modulatio: Microcontroller peripherals for precise timing control and generating pulse-width modulated signals for motor speed control, LED dimming, and analog signal synthesis. Timers count clock cycles enabling periodic interrupts, event timing, and waveform generation. PWM varies duty cycle (percentage of period signal is HIGH) at fixed frequency, with average voltage proportional to duty cycle. Hardware PWM peripherals generate precise waveforms while CPU executes other tasks. Applications include DC motor speed control (average voltage sets speed), servo positioning (pulse width sets angle), LED brightness control, and switched-mode power supplies requiring efficient variable power delivery through duty cycle modulation..
ECE 206: Pwm operation: Pulse Width Modulation (PWM) encodes analog information in digital signal by varying duty cycle (ratio of on-time to period). PWM signal has fixed frequency but adjustable pulse width. Duty cycle D = Ton/T, where 0 ≤ D ≤ 1. Average voltage Vavg = D × Vsupply. Applications include motor speed control, LED brightness, DC-DC conversion, and audio synthesis. Low-pass filtering recovers analog signal.
ECE 206: Mosfet based logic and switchin: CMOS (Complementary MOS) logic uses NMOS and PMOS transistors to create power-efficient gates. NMOS pulls output low (strong 0), PMOS pulls output high (strong 1). CMOS inverter: NMOS and PMOS in series; input high → NMOS on, PMOS off → output low. NAND, NOR, and complex gates follow similar complementary design. Key advantages: near-zero static power (one transistor always off), full voltage swin
ECE 205: Rc and rl circuits: RC circuits contain resistors and capacitors; RL circuits contain resistors and inductors. First-order circuits (one energy storage element) have exponential responses with time constant τ. For RC: τ = RC; capacitor voltage: v(t) = Vf + (Vi - Vf)e^(-t/τ). For RL: τ = L/R; inductor current: i(t) = If + (Ii - If)e^(-t/τ). Time constant represents time to reach 63% of final value. Applications includ
ECE 205: Response to sinusoidal inputs: Steady-state response to sinusoidal input v(t) = Vm cos(ωt) is sinusoidal at same frequency but different amplitude and phase: vout(t) = Vo cos(ωt + φ). Phasors represent sinusoids as complex numbers: V = Vm∠0°, enabling algebraic instead of differential equation analysis. Impedances generalize resistance: ZR = R, ZL = jωL, ZC = 1/(jωC). All circuit analysis techniques (Ohm's law, KVL, KCL, nodal,
ECE 205: Frequency response: Frequency response describes how a circuit's output amplitude and phase vary with input signal frequency. Characterized by transfer function H(ω) = Vout/Vin as a function of frequency ω. Magnitude |H(ω)| shows gain at each frequency; phase ∠H(ω) shows time delay. Bode plots (log-log magnitude, semi-log phase) visualize frequency response. Key features include cutoff frequencies, passband, stopband
TAM 324: Colloidal materials; microstruc: The study of colloidal suspensions where microscopic particles remain dispersed in fluid media without settling, and the application to concrete as a composite material formed through hydration reactions creating calcium silicate hydrate gel. Concrete microstructure comprises unhydrated cement particles, hydration products forming a gel matrix, aggregate particles, interfacial transition zones, and capillary porosity. Understanding colloid stability, particle packing, hydration kinetics, and pore structure development explains concrete's time-dependent strength gain, shrinkage, creep behavior, and durability. Water-cement ratio critically affects porosity and strength through percolation of the gel phase..
ME 330: Other composites: Beyond fiber-reinforced composites, other composite types include particle-reinforced (concrete, cermets, filled polymers), laminar (plywood, bimetals), and hybrid composites. Particle composites use particles improving stiffness, wear resistance, or reducing cost. Laminar composites stack layers exploiting individual layer properties or creating tailored properties through stacking sequence. Nano
MATH 241: Fundamental theorem of line in: The Fundamental Theorem for Line Integrals states that if F = ∇f (F is conservative with potential function f), then ∫_C F·dr = f(B) - f(A) where A and B are the endpoints of curve C. This means work in a conservative field depends only on endpoints, not the path taken. The theorem generalizes the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus to line integrals, providing powerful simplification for conservative
PHYS 212: Ampere's law: Ampère's law states that the line integral of magnetic field around a closed path equals the current passing through the enclosed area (times μ₀). For symmetric current distributions (infinite wires, solenoids, toroids), Ampère's law provides an elegant method for calculating magnetic fields, analogous to how Gauss's law simplifies electric field calculations. This law, part of Maxwell's equations
PHYS 212: Motional emf: Motional EMF is the voltage generated when a conductor moves through a magnetic field, with charges inside experiencing magnetic force causing charge separation and potential difference. The EMF depends on conductor velocity, field strength, and length (ε = Bℓv). This phenomenon, explained by the Lorentz force on moving charges, is fundamental to electric generators, where mechanical energy conver
MATH 241: Integrating vector fields: Integrating vector fields encompasses line integrals ∫_C F·dr along curves and surface integrals ∫∫_S F·dS over surfaces. Line integrals measure circulation or work along paths. Surface integrals measure flux through surfaces - how much field 'flows through' the surface. For F =
PHYS 212: Forces and magnetic dipoles: Magnetic dipoles are objects with north and south magnetic poles, like bar magnets or current loops, characterized by magnetic dipole moment. They experience torque in magnetic fields tending to align the dipole with the field, and forces in non-uniform fields pulling toward stronger field regions. Understanding dipole behavior explains compass operation, magnetic materials, atomic magnetism, and
MATH 442: Laplace's equation: Laplace's equation ∇²u = ∂²u/∂x² + ∂²u/∂y² + ∂²u/∂z² = 0 is the fundamental elliptic PDE describing steady-state phenomena with no sources. Solutions (harmonic functions) represent equilibrium: temperature in steady heat conduction, electrostatic potential, steady fluid flow potential. Boundary value problems require u or ∂u/∂n specified on domain boundary. Maximum principle: extrema occur only on boundary. Mean value property: u at point equals average over surrounding sphere. Uniqueness of solutions. Applications span physics and engineering: electrostatics, potential flow, minimal surfaces..
PHYS 212: Faraday's law: Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction states that changing magnetic flux through a loop induces an EMF proportional to the rate of flux change (ε = -dΦ_B/dt). The negative sign (Lenz's law) indicates induced current opposes the flux change. This fundamental law explains generators, transformers, inductors, and electromagnetic wave production. Faraday's law connects changing magnetic fields to
MATH 285: Electric circuits: Electric circuit analysis using differential equations models the behavior of RLC circuits containing resistors, inductors, and capacitors. Kirchhoff's laws lead to differential equations where current and charge are the dependent variables. The mathematical structure parallels mechanical vibration problems, with inductance analogous to mass, resistance to damping, and capacitance to spring compli
MATH 241: Divergence theorem in 3d: The divergence theorem in three dimensions relates the flux of a vector field through a closed surface to the triple integral of the field's divergence over the enclosed volume. It states that the total outward flow through a boundary surface equals the sum of all sources and sinks within the volume. This powerful theorem converts surface integrals into volume integrals and is fundamental to physi
MATH 241: 2d divergence theorem: The 2D divergence theorem (also called Green's theorem in flux form) relates the flux of a vector field across a closed curve to the double integral of the field's divergence over the enclosed region. It states that the total outward flow through a boundary equals the sum of all sources and sinks within. This provides a powerful tool for converting boundary integrals to area integrals in planar re
MATH 241: More examples of double integr: Additional double integral examples reinforce techniques including: choosing appropriate integration order, handling regions requiring splitting, applying symmetry to simplify calculations, computing volumes between surfaces, finding areas using ∫∫ 1 dA, calculating averages f_avg = (1/A) ∫∫ f dA, and modeling physical quantities like mass (density integrated), center of mass, and probability (joi
MATH 221: The definite integral: The definite integral ∫[a,b] f(x)dx represents the signed area between f(x) and the x-axis from x=a to x=b, defined as the limit of Riemann sums as partition width approaches zero. Positive areas (f(x) > 0) count positively; negative areas (f(x)
MATH 442: The neumann condition: Neumann boundary conditions specify derivative (flux) at boundaries: ∂u/∂x(0,t) = h₁(t), ∂u/∂x(L,t) = h₂(t). Physical interpretation: heat flux (insulated if ∂u/∂x = 0), force on boundary, or specified gradient. For homogeneous Neumann on [0,L]: eigenfunctions Xₙ(x) = cos(nπx/L), eigenvalues λₙ = (nπ/L)², with n=0 giving constant mode. Zero Neumann means no flux through boundary (isolated system). Conservation laws arise from Neumann conditions. Important for diffusion and heat transfer problems..
PHYS 212: Oscillations: lc circuits: LC circuits contain inductors and capacitors with no resistance, exhibiting electrical oscillations where energy continuously exchanges between electric field (capacitor) and magnetic field (inductor). The oscillation frequency ω = 1/√(LC) depends only on circuit components. LC circuits demonstrate electrical resonance, analogous to mechanical oscillators, and are fundamental to radio tuning, sign
PHYS 212: Rc circuits: RC circuits contain resistors and capacitors, exhibiting time-dependent behavior as capacitors charge or discharge through resistors. The time constant τ = RC characterizes how quickly the circuit responds, with voltage and current decaying or growing exponentially. RC circuits act as filters, timing devices, and smoothing circuits. Understanding exponential charging/discharging, time constants, a
PHYS 212: Self-inductance rl circuits: Self-inductance (L) measures a coil's opposition to current changes, with changing current inducing an EMF opposing that change (ε = -L dI/dt). RL circuits containing resistors and inductors exhibit time-dependent current growth or decay with time constant τ = L/R. Inductors store energy in magnetic fields (U = ½LI²) and oppose sudden current changes, acting as electrical inertia. They're essentia
ECE 205: Rlc circuits: RLC circuits contain resistors, inductors, and capacitors - second-order systems described by second-order differential equations. Series RLC: V = RI + L(dI/dt) + Q/C. Natural frequency ω₀ = 1/√(LC), damping ratio ζ = R/(2)√(C/L). Response types: overdamped (ζ > 1, exponential decay), critically damped (ζ = 1, fastest non-oscillatory), underdamped (ζ
ECE 205: Passive filters: Passive filters use only resistors, capacitors, and inductors (no active components) to selectively pass or block frequency ranges. Types include low-pass (passes low frequencies), high-pass (passes high frequencies), band-pass (passes middle range), and band-stop (blocks middle range). First-order RC/RL filters have -20 dB/decade roll-off; higher orders provide sharper transitions. Cutoff frequen
PHYS 212: Ac power & resonant circuits: AC power differs from DC power because voltage and current may be out of phase, with real power depending on the power factor (cos φ). Resonance occurs in RLC circuits when inductive and capacitive reactances cancel, minimizing impedance and maximizing current at resonant frequency ω₀ = 1/√(LC). Understanding power factor, resonance, and frequency response is crucial for power transmission, radio
ME 360: Analog filters: Continuous-time circuits selectively attenuating or passing signal frequency components using resistors, capacitors, inductors, and operational amplifiers. Filter types include lowpass (passing low frequencies, blocking high), highpass (opposite), bandpass (passing range around center frequency), and bandstop (rejecting specific band). Design specifications include cutoff frequency, passband rippl
ECE 205: Step response: Step response is circuit behavior when input suddenly changes (typically from 0 to constant value - step function). Represents switching events like closing switch or abrupt voltage change. For first-order circuits, response is exponential approach to new steady state: v(t) = Vfinal(1 - e^(-t/τ)) for initially relaxed RC. Rise time, settling time, and time constant characterize response speed. Ste
ECE 205: Binary logic and logic gates: Binary logic uses two states: 0 (low voltage, false) and 1 (high voltage, true). Logic gates implement Boolean operations: NOT (inverter), AND (output 1 only if all inputs 1), OR (output 1 if any input 1), NAND (inverted AND), NOR (inverted OR), XOR (output 1 if inputs differ). Gates combine to create complex digital systems. Truth tables define gate behavior for all input combinations. Logic fami
ECE 205: Current source comparator: A comparator is an op-amp circuit (typically without feedback) that compares two voltages and outputs high or low based on which is larger. Output switches between saturation levels (typically ±Vsat or rail voltages). When V+ > V-, output is high; when V+
MATH 231: Representation of functions by: Functions can be represented as power series by manipulating known series (like geometric series 1/(1-x) = Σx^n for |x|
ME 340: Exponentially decaying signals: Exponentially decaying signals are functions that decrease at a rate proportional to their current value, mathematically expressed as y(t) = y₀e^(-t/τ). These signals characterize first-order system responses and damped oscillations in second-order systems. The decay rate is determined by the time constant or damping coefficient, with larger values producing slower decay. Understanding exponential
ME 340: Impulse response and convolution: The impulse response is the output of a system when subjected to an idealized instantaneous input (Dirac delta function). It completely characterizes a linear time-invariant system's behavior. The convolution integral uses the impulse response as a kernel to calculate the system's output for any arbitrary input by summing the weighted, time-shifted impulse responses. This powerful mathematical too
ME 340: Free, step, and unit impulse res: These three canonical responses characterize second-order system behavior completely. Free response (initial condition response) shows natural oscillation at the damped natural frequency. Step response reveals how systems approach new equilibria, with characteristics like rise time, settling time, overshoot, and steady-state error depending on damping. Impulse response represents the system's fund
ME 340: Frequency response: Frequency response describes how a system's output amplitude and phase change relative to a sinusoidal input as the input frequency varies. Typically visualized using Bode plots (magnitude and phase versus frequency), frequency response reveals critical system characteristics including resonant frequencies, bandwidth, and stability margins. For linear time-invariant systems, the frequency response
ME 360: Continuous time systems: Systems processing signals that exist at all points in time, characterized by differential equations relating input and output signals, with properties including linearity (superposition), time-invariance (consistent behavior over time), causality (output depends only on present and past inputs), and stability (bounded outputs for bounded inputs). Analysis involves impulse response characterizing
ME 461: Analog and digital electronics: Electronic circuits processing continuous-amplitude signals (analog: amplifiers, filters, oscillators) versus discrete binary logic (digital: gates, flip-flops, counters, microprocessors). Analog circuits use operational amplifiers for signal conditioning, comparators for thresholding, and transistors for switching/amplification. Digital circuits implement Boolean logic through gates (AND, OR, NOT), store states in flip-flops, and sequence operations with counters and state machines. Interface circuits (ADC, DAC, level shifters) bridge analog and digital domains. Understanding enables mechatronic system design combining analog sensors and digital controllers. Applications include sensor signal conditioning, microcontroller interfacing, and motor drive power electronics..
MATH 241: Green's theorem and conservati: This topic explores the connection between Green's theorem and conservative vector fields, which have zero circulation around closed curves. A vector field is conservative if its line integral around any closed path is zero, which by Green's theorem means the field has zero curl. Conservative fields can be expressed as gradients of scalar potential functions, making path integrals path-independent
MATH 442: Diffusion with a source: Inhomogeneous diffusion equation ∂u/∂t = α∂²u/∂x² + f(x,t) includes source term f representing heat generation, chemical reaction, or external input. Solution combines homogeneous solution (diffusion from initial conditions) and particular solution (response to source). Duhamel's principle: treat source as sequence of instantaneous pulses, each generating heat kernel response, integrate over time. Physical examples: heated rod with internal heat source, population with birth/death, reactor with neutron production. Source terms break pure diffusive behavior, adding forcing effects..
ME 360: Introduction to analog filter de: Systematic methods for designing continuous-time filters meeting specifications including passband/stopband frequencies, maximum passband ripple, minimum stopband attenuation, and phase linearity. Design process involves specifying requirements, selecting approximation function (Butterworth, Chebyshev, Bessel, Elliptic), determining filter order meeting specifications, calculating transfer functio
ME 340: Physical experiments with a sing: This laboratory experiment provides hands-on experience with canonical second-order systems using a physical apparatus with adjustable mass, spring stiffness, and damping. Students excite the system with various inputs (initial displacement, step force, impulse, harmonic forcing), measure responses, and identify parameters by comparing theory to measurements. Experiments demonstrate phenomena like
ME 340: Frequency domain analysis: Frequency domain analysis examines system behavior by studying how systems respond to sinusoidal inputs at different frequencies, using Fourier transforms to convert time-domain signals into frequency-domain representations. This approach reveals how systems amplify or attenuate signals at various frequencies, making it invaluable for understanding filtering characteristics, resonance phenomena, a
MATH 257: Linear differential equations: Linear differential equations of the form dx/dt = Ax describe systems where the rate of change is a linear function of the current state. The solution is x(t) = e^(At)x(0), where e^(At) is the matrix exponential. When A is diagonalizable, solutions decompose into modes corresponding to eigenvectors, each evolving as e^(λt). Stability depends on eigenvalues: negative real parts mean decay (stable), positive mean growth (unstable). Applications include coupled oscillators, population dynamics, and electrical circuits..
MATH 285: Laplace transform: The Laplace transform converts differential equations in the time domain into algebraic equations in the frequency domain, making them easier to solve. After solving algebraically, the inverse Laplace transform returns the solution to the time domain. This method excels at handling initial value problems, discontinuous forcing functions, and systems with multiple interconnected equations. It's fun
ECE 205: Op-amp - inverting and non-inve: Active filters use operational amplifiers (op-amps) with resistors and capacitors to create filters with gain and sharper roll-offs than passive filters. Inverting configuration: input to inverting terminal, gain = -Zf/Zi. Non-inverting configuration: input to non-inverting terminal, gain = 1 + Zf/Zi. By making impedances frequency-dependent (using capacitors), active filters achieve low-pass, hig
ME 340: Application to vibration isolati: Vibration isolation involves designing systems to minimize the transmission of unwanted vibrations from a source to a sensitive component or from a component to its surroundings. By understanding frequency response, engineers can design isolation systems (typically using springs and dampers) that attenuate vibrations at problematic frequencies. Effective isolation requires operating above the syst
ME 340: Laplace transformation: properti: Laplace transform converts time-domain function f(t) to complex frequency domain: F(s) = ∫₀^∞ f(t)e^(-st)dt. Properties: linearity, differentiation (ℒ{f'} = sF(s) - f(0)), integration, shifting, convolution. Converts differential equations to algebraic equations. Inverse Laplace: f(t) = ℒ^(-1){F(s)} found via partial fractions, tables, or residue theorem. Advantages: handles initial conditions nat
MATH 442: Properties of laplace transfor: Laplace transform converts time-domain function to complex frequency: L[f](s) = ∫₀^∞ f(t)e^(-st)dt. Properties: linearity, s-shifting (e^(at)f(t) ↔ F(s-a)), t-shifting (f(t-a)u(t-a) ↔ e^(-as)F(s)), derivative (f' ↔ sF - f(0)), integral (∫f ↔ F/s), convolution (f*g ↔ FG). Converts initial value problems to algebraic equations. Inverse via residue theory or tables. Particularly useful for ODEs and PDEs with time derivatives on semi-infinite domains. Common in engineering: control theory, circuit analysis..
ME 460: Bode plots: Logarithmic graphs displaying system frequency response with magnitude (in dB) and phase (in degrees) versus frequency (logarithmic scale), enabling quick assessment of system characteristics and simplified construction from transfer function factors. Straight-line asymptotes approximate actual response with slopes of ±20n dB/decade for poles/zeros of order n, with corners at pole/zero frequencies. Bode plots reveal bandwidth (frequency where gain drops 3dB), resonant peaks (indicating low damping), phase margin (stability measure), and crossover frequencies. Graphical addition of compensator Bode plots enables intuitive design achieving specifications. Applications include loop-shaping control design and frequency response analysis across industrial automation systems..
TAM 456: Particle tracking: Particle tracking (Particle Image Velocimetry - PIV for fluids, or surface particle tracking for solids) measures displacement and strain fields by tracking position of markers over time. Seed particles or surface patterns photographed at successive time intervals. Cross-correlation algorithms identify particle displacements between images. Displacement fields differentiated to obtain strain and velocity. Advantages: full-field measurement, non-contact, suitable for large deformations. Challenges: marker application, imaging quality, resolution vs. field of view trade-off, displacement calculation accuracy. Applications include fluid mechanics (PIV), soft tissue biomechanics, and large deformation solid mechanics..
TAM 456: Digital image correlation: Digital Image Correlation (DIC) is non-contact optical method measuring full-field displacement and strain by tracking surface speckle patterns. Random speckle pattern (paint, natural texture) applied to specimen surface. Stereoscopic cameras capture images before and after deformation. Correlation algorithms match small subsets (facets) between images to calculate displacement field. Strain obtained by spatial differentiation. Advantages: full-field 3D measurement, large displacement capability, no sensor attachment. Applications span material characterization, fracture mechanics, biomechanics. Modern DIC systems provide strain accuracy ~100 microstrain. Increasingly standard technique in experimental mechanics laboratories..
ME 340: Solutions of differential equati: Laplace transform method: (1) transform differential equation, (2) solve algebraic equation for F(s), (3) inverse transform to get f(t). Initial conditions automatically incorporated. Works for linear ODEs with constant coefficients. Example: mẍ + cẋ + kx = f(t) becomes ms²X(s) + csX(s) + kX(s) = F(s) + initial condition terms. Solve for X(s), then x(t) = ℒ^(-1){X(s)}. Particularly useful for piec
ME 340: Transfer functions - poles and z: Transfer function G(s) = Y(s)/U(s) relates output Y to input U in Laplace domain (zero initial conditions). Rational function: G(s) = N(s)/D(s). Poles are roots of denominator D(s) = 0, govern stability and transient response. Zeros are roots of numerator N(s) = 0. System stable if all poles in left half-plane (Re(s)
ME 340: Partial fractions: Partial fraction decomposition is a technique for breaking complex rational functions into sums of simpler fractions, essential for solving differential equations using Laplace transforms. In dynamics, this method allows engineers to invert transformed solutions back to the time domain by decomposing transfer functions into recognizable forms with known inverse transforms. Each partial fraction co
MATH 231: Taylor and maclaurin series: Taylor series represents a function as a power series centered at x = a: f(x) = Σ[f^(n)(a)/n!]·(x-a)^n, where f^(n)(a) is the nth derivative at a. Maclaurin series is the special case when a = 0. If the series converges to f(x) near a, the function is analytic at a. Important Maclaurin series include e^x = Σx^n/n!, sin(x) = Σ(-1)^n·x^(2n+1)/(2n+1)!, and cos(x) = Σ(-1)^n·x^(2n)/(2n)!. Taylor series
MATH 285: Even and odd extensions: Even and odd extensions artificially extend a function defined on a half-interval to the full interval by creating either even (symmetric) or odd (antisymmetric) versions. This technique allows use of Fourier cosine series (even extension) or sine series (odd extension) to solve problems on half-intervals. The choice of extension determines which type of Fourier series applies and must be consiste
MATH 231: Applications of taylor polynom: Taylor polynomials T_n(x) are partial sums of Taylor series, providing polynomial approximations to functions near a point. The nth Taylor polynomial includes terms up to (x-a)^n. Taylor's Remainder Theorem bounds the error: |R_n(x)| ≤ M·|x-a|^(n+1)/(n+1)! where M bounds the (n+1)st derivative. Applications include approximating function values, evaluating limits using series expansions, estimatin
MATH 221: Antiderivatives: An antiderivative (indefinite integral) of f(x) is a function F(x) whose derivative is f(x): F'(x) = f(x). Antiderivatives differ by constants, so the general antiderivative is F(x) + C where C is an arbitrary constant. Finding antiderivatives reverses differentiation, requiring recognition of derivative patterns. Basic antiderivative formulas include: ∫x^n dx = x^(n+1)/(n+1) + C, ∫e^x dx = e^x +
ME 360: Discrete time systems and z-tran: Mathematical framework for analyzing digital systems processing discrete-time signals through difference equations, with z-transform providing frequency-domain representation analogous to Laplace transform for continuous systems. Z-transform converts difference equations to algebraic equations in z-domain, where z=e^(jωT) relates to continuous frequency. Transfer functions H(z) characterize system
ME 360: Fourier transform: Extension of Fourier series to non-periodic signals, representing arbitrary time-domain signals as continuous superpositions of sinusoids with infinitesimal frequency spacing. The Fourier transform X(jω) decomposes signal x(t) into frequency spectrum showing amplitude and phase versus frequency, while inverse transform reconstructs time signal from frequency components. Properties include linearit
ME 360: Discrete time fourier transform: The frequency domain representation of discrete-time signals x[n] as continuous periodic functions X(e^jω) of normalized frequency, relating discrete samples to continuous frequency spectrum with periodicity of 2π. DTFT enables analysis of digital signal frequency content, filter design in frequency domain, and understanding effects of sampling on signal spectra. The transform is periodic because
ME 360: Introduction to digital filter d: Methods for creating discrete-time filters processing sampled signals through difference equations or convolution with finite impulse response. FIR filters have finite-length impulse responses, guaranteed stability, and linear phase, designed using windowing or optimization methods. IIR filters have infinite impulse responses, higher efficiency requiring fewer coefficients but potential instabilit
MATH 442: Properties of fourier transfor: Fourier transform converts function to frequency domain: F[f](k) = ∫₋∞^∞ f(x)e^(-ikx)dx. Inverse: f(x) = (1/2π)∫F(k)e^(ikx)dk. Key properties: linearity, shift (f(x-a) ↔ e^(-ika)F(k)), scaling (f(ax) ↔ (1/|a|)F(k/a)), derivative (f' ↔ ikF), convolution (f*g ↔ FG). Transform converts derivatives to multiplication, enabling algebraic solution of PDEs. Parseval/Plancherel: ∫|f|²dx = (1/2π)∫|F|²dk (energy preservation). Applications: solving PDEs on infinite domains, signal processing, quantum mechanics..
MATH 442: The coefficients: In Fourier series u(x,t) = Σcₙ Xₙ(x)Tₙ(t), coefficients cₙ match initial condition u(x,0) = f(x). Using orthogonality: ∫Xₙ(x)Xₘ(x)dx = 0 for n≠m, coefficients determined by cₙ = ⟨f,Xₙ⟩/⟨Xₙ,Xₙ⟩ = (∫f(x)Xₙ(x)dx)/(∫Xₙ²(x)dx). For sine series: cₙ = (2/L)∫f(x)sin(nπx/L)dx. Coefficients weight each mode's contribution. Convergence of series (uniform, L², pointwise) depends on smoothness of f. Computing coefficients projects initial data onto eigenfunction basis..
MATH 441: Euler's method: Euler's method is a numerical technique for approximating solutions to initial value problems dy/dx = f(x,y), y(x₀) = y₀. Starting from (x₀,y₀), take small steps of size h: xₙ₊₁ = xₙ + h, yₙ₊₁ = yₙ + h·f(xₙ,yₙ). Each step follows tangent line for distance h. Error accumulates: local truncation error O(h²), global error O(h). Smaller h gives more accuracy but requires more steps. More sophisticated methods (Runge-Kutta) improve accuracy. Euler's method illustrates fundamental numerical DE concept: approximate continuous process with discrete steps..
MATH 285: Separable equations: Separable differential equations are first-order equations that can be rewritten so all terms involving the dependent variable are on one side and all terms involving the independent variable are on the other side. The solution is found by integrating both sides separately. This is one of the simplest and most fundamental techniques for solving differential equations analytically and applies to ma
MATH 285: Existence and uniqueness: Existence and uniqueness theorems provide conditions guaranteeing that a differential equation with an initial condition has a solution (existence) and that this solution is unique (uniqueness). Typically, if the function and its partial derivatives are continuous in a region containing the initial condition, then a unique solution exists locally. These theorems are fundamental to ensuring differe
MATH 441: Series solutions near an ordin: When elementary solutions fail, power series provide general solution method. Near ordinary point x₀ (coefficients analytic), assume y = Σaₙ(x-x₀)^n, substitute into DE, match coefficients to find recurrence relation for aₙ. Regular singular point: coefficient has simple pole but specific behavior allows Frobenius method: y = (x-x₀)^r Σaₙ(x-x₀)^n. Indicial equation determines r. Solutions converge in radius of convergence. Examples: Bessel, Legendre, Hermite equations. Series solutions handle equations with variable coefficients where other methods fail..
MATH 285: Variation of vector parameters: Variation of vector parameters extends the variation of parameters method to systems of differential equations. Instead of varying scalar parameters, this method treats the coefficient vector in the complementary solution as an unknown vector function. By determining this vector through a system derived from substitution, particular solutions to inhomogeneous systems can be found systematically, e
MATH 442: Initial and boundary condition: PDEs require additional conditions to determine unique solutions. Initial conditions specify function and derivatives at t = 0 (time-dependent problems). Boundary conditions specify function behavior at domain edges. Types: Dirichlet (specify u on boundary), Neumann (specify ∂u/∂n on boundary), Robin (combination). Wave equation needs two initial conditions (position and velocity); heat equation needs one (initial temperature). Boundary conditions model physical constraints: fixed temperature (Dirichlet), insulated boundary (Neumann). Well-posed problems have unique solutions depending continuously on data. Matching conditions to physics is crucial..
TAM 470: Finite-difference methods: Finite-difference methods approximate derivatives using function values at discrete grid points. Forward difference: f'(x) ≈ (f(x+h)-f(x))/h (O(h) error). Central difference: f'(x) ≈ (f(x+h)-f(x-h))/(2h) (O(h²) error). Higher derivatives: f''(x) ≈ (f(x+h)-2f(x)+f(x-h))/h². Applied to PDEs by discretizing spatial and temporal derivatives, creating algebraic equations. Structured grids simplify impl
MATH 442: Separation of variables: Separation of variables assumes solution u(x,t) = X(x)T(t) as product of single-variable functions. Substituting into PDE and separating variables yields ODEs: X''/X = -λ (eigenvalue problem) and T'/T = -λα (time evolution). Boundary conditions determine eigenvalues λₙ and eigenfunctions Xₙ(x). General solution: u(x,t) = Σcₙ Xₙ(x)Tₙ(t). Initial condition determines coefficients cₙ via orthogonality. Method works for linear PDEs with separable boundary conditions on rectangular domains. Fundamental technique yielding explicit solutions and revealing mode structure..
MATH 442: The dirichlet condition: Dirichlet boundary conditions specify function values at domain boundaries: u(0,t) = g₁(t), u(L,t) = g₂(t). For steady problems: u(0) = a, u(L) = b. Physical interpretation: fixed temperature, prescribed displacement, or known potential at boundaries. For separation of variables on [0,L] with homogeneous Dirichlet (u=0 at boundaries): eigenfunctions Xₙ(x) = sin(nπx/L), eigenvalues λₙ = (nπ/L)². Dirichlet conditions are most common, representing controlled boundary values. Well-posed for elliptic and parabolic PDEs..
TAM 470: Functional expansions: Functional expansions represent solutions as series of basis functions: u(x) = Σcₙφₙ(x). Basis functions: polynomials (Chebyshev, Legendre), trigonometric (Fourier), wavelets. Galerkin method: project residual onto basis functions to determine coefficients cₙ. Spectral convergence: exponential error decrease for smooth functions (vs. polynomial for finite difference/element). Advantages: high accu
TAM 470: Partial differential equations: Mechanics PDEs arise from conservation laws and constitutive relations. Elliptic: steady elasticity ∇²u = f, Laplace equation (steady heat, potential flow). Parabolic: diffusion ∂u/∂t = α∇²u, heat equation. Hyperbolic: wave equation ∂²u/∂t² = c²∇²u, advection equation ∂u/∂t + c·∇u = 0. Navier-Stokes (fluid): ρ(∂v/∂t + v·∇v) = -∇p + μ∇²v. Elastodynamics: ρ∂²u/∂t² = ∇·σ + f. Classification determine
MATH 442: Inhomogeneous boundary conditi: Inhomogeneous boundary conditions have non-zero values: u(0,t) = α(t), u(L,t) = β(t). Cannot directly apply separation of variables (requires homogeneous BC). Solution strategy: decompose u = v + w, where w satisfies inhomogeneous BC (steady or time-dependent), and v satisfies homogeneous BC (solvable by separation). Common choice for steady BC: w(x) = α + (β-α)x/L (linear interpolation). Then solve for v with modified initial condition and source term. Method transforms inhomogeneous problem to homogeneous one, enabling standard techniques..
TAM 470: Basic theory and numerical meth: ODE theory covers existence and uniqueness (Lipschitz condition), stability (solution behavior), and stiffness. Numerical methods: Euler (explicit, first-order), implicit methods (backward Euler, trapezoidal), Runge-Kutta (explicit multi-stage, higher accuracy), and multistep methods (Adams, BDF). Key concepts: local truncation error (single step error), global error (accumulated), stability (boun
TAM 470: Spectral methods: Spectral methods use global basis functions (Fourier, Chebyshev) for spatial discretization. Derivatives computed exactly in spectral space or via differentiation matrices. Spectral accuracy: exponential convergence for smooth solutions. Collocation method evaluates at specific points (Gauss-Lobatto). Applications: turbulence simulation (DNS), geophysical flows, stability analysis. Advantages: ver
MATH 442: Applications to waves/diffusio: PDEs on semi-infinite domain [0,∞) with boundary condition at x=0 solved using Laplace transforms in x (or sine/cosine transforms). Diffusion on half-line: ∂u/∂t = α∂²u/∂x² with u(0,t) = g(t). Transform in x eliminates spatial derivatives, yielding ODE in transform variable. Boundary condition enters as initial condition in transform domain. Method of images alternative: extend problem to full line using symmetry. Applications: heat flow in semi-infinite rod, diffusion from surface, ground temperature variation..
ME 412: Strong vs weak form of equations: Strong form (classical formulation) of differential equations requires solutions to satisfy equations exactly at every point with continuous derivatives to the equation's order. Weak form (variational formulation) relaxes smoothness requirements by integrating equations with test functions, allowing solutions with less regularity. Weak formulations enable inclusion of discontinuities and are the foundation of finite element methods. The Galerkin method uses basis functions as test functions. Variational principles (minimum energy, weighted residuals) lead to weak forms. Boundary conditions are incorporated naturally (essential) or through variational terms (natural). Understanding strong vs. weak forms provides theoretical foundation for finite element analysis and explains why numerical solutions work even when functions aren't perfectly smooth..
TAM 470: Stability and accuracy: Stability: perturbations remain bounded, solution doesn't blow up. Analyzed via: von Neumann (Fourier modes), matrix eigenvalues, energy methods. Accuracy: discrete solution approaches exact solution. Measured by order: O(Δxᵖ) spatial, O(Δtᵍ) temporal. Trade-offs: explicit methods simple but conditionally stable (small time step required); implicit methods unconditionally stable but require solvin
TAM 470: Multistep methods: Multistep methods use solution history for higher accuracy without multiple function evaluations per step. Adams-Bashforth (explicit): yₙ₊₁ = yₙ + h(b₀fₙ + b₁fₙ₋₁ + ... + bₖfₙ₋ₖ). Adams-Moulton (implicit): includes fₙ₊₁, more stable. Backward Differentiation Formulas (BDF): implicit, excellent for stiff problems. Predictor-corrector combines explicit prediction with implicit correction. Advantages
ME 412: Preliminary topics (error, stabi: Error analysis, stability, and convergence are fundamental to numerical methods. Errors include truncation error (discretization approximation), round-off error (finite precision arithmetic), and iteration error (incomplete convergence). Consistency ensures discrete equations approach differential equations as grid spacing approaches zero. Stability ensures errors don't grow unboundedly during computation. Convergence ensures numerical solution approaches exact solution with refinement. Lax equivalence theorem states consistency + stability = convergence for linear problems. CFL condition relates time step to spatial resolution for stability. Understanding these concepts enables selection of appropriate schemes, time steps, and grid resolutions ensuring reliable, accurate numerical results without instabilities or divergence..
ME 420: Separation of variables and orth: Separation of variables solves linear PDEs by assuming product solutions T(x,y,t) = X(x)Y(y)T(t), transforming PDEs into ordinary ODEs. Each ODE solution forms an eigenfunction satisfying boundary conditions with corresponding eigenvalue. The complete solution is an infinite series of eigenfunctions weighted to satisfy initial conditions using orthogonality. Fourier series represent solutions in rectangular coordinates; Bessel functions in cylindrical; Legendre polynomials in spherical. This powerful technique provides exact analytical solutions for standard geometries, revealing modal structure of heat distribution. Understanding separation of variables connects mathematical physics to engineering applications and provides benchmark solutions for validation..
TAM 470: Physical interpretation of cons: Consistency means numerical scheme approximates differential equation as grid refined: truncation error → 0 as Δx, Δt → 0. Physically: discrete equations approach continuous equations. Stability means numerical solution remains bounded and errors don't grow uncontrollably. Physically: numerical method doesn't amplify perturbations. Lax Equivalence Theorem: for linear PDEs, consistency + stability
TAM 470: Diffusion equations: Diffusion equation ∂u/∂t = α∇²u models heat conduction, mass diffusion, viscous effects. Parabolic PDE: infinite propagation speed, smoothing effect. Finite difference discretization: explicit (forward Euler in time) has stability restriction Δt ≤ Δx²/(2α) for 1D; implicit (backward Euler, Crank-Nicolson) unconditionally stable. Crank-Nicolson second-order accurate in time and space. Applications:
ME 412: Finite difference discretization: Finite difference discretization approximates derivatives using function values at discrete grid points. Forward, backward, and central differences approximate first derivatives with different truncation errors. Second derivatives use three-point stencils. Taylor series analysis determines accuracy order (first-order, second-order, etc.). Finite difference converts PDEs into algebraic equations solvable by linear algebra. Grid spacing Δx and Δy affect accuracy and computational cost. Structured (uniform, non-uniform) and curvilinear grids adapt to geometries. Finite difference methods are intuitive, easy to implement, and effective for rectangular domains. Understanding finite differences provides foundation for discretization concepts applicable to all numerical methods used in thermo-fluid simulation..
ME 340: Time domain analysis of linear t: LTI system analysis in time domain: impulse response h(t) = ℒ^(-1){G(s)} characterizes system completely. Output y(t) = h(t)*u(t) (convolution). Step response s(t) = ∫h(τ)dτ. Transient response: natural modes from poles (exponentials, damped sinusoids). Steady-state response to sinusoidal input: magnitude and phase from frequency response. Performance metrics: rise time, settling time, overshoot,
ME 340: State(phase) space representatio: State space representation uses first-order differential equations: ẋ = Ax + Bu (state equation), y = Cx + Du (output equation), where x is state vector (position, velocity, etc.), u is input, y is output. State dimension n equals system order. Matrices A, B, C, D characterize system. Advantages: handles MIMO (multiple input/multiple output), nonlinear extension, modern control theory foundation,
ME 340: Block diagrams and simulation: Block diagrams provide graphical representations of dynamic systems using blocks for mathematical operations (integration, gain, summation) and arrows showing signal flow. This visual language simplifies understanding of complex system interconnections, feedback loops, and signal processing. Simulation involves numerically solving the equations represented by block diagrams to predict time-domain
ME 460: Nyquist stability criterion: Graphical stability test for closed-loop systems based on plotting open-loop frequency response on complex plane and examining encirclements of critical point (-1, 0). Criterion states that for stability, Nyquist contour must encircle -1 point Z = P - N times counterclockwise, where P is open-loop unstable poles and N is encirclements. Method handles time delays exactly, assesses relative stability through gain and phase margins, and provides robustness insights. Simplified for stable open-loop systems (P=0), stability requires no encirclements of -1. More powerful than Bode for systems with time delays or RHP poles. Applications include analyzing feedback systems with transport delays in chemical processes..
MATH 441: Introduction to systems: Systems of DEs involve multiple dependent variables with coupled equations: dx/dt = f(x,y), dy/dt = g(x,y). Can represent single higher-order DE (y'' = f(t,y,y') becomes x=y, y'=f(t,x,y), creating first-order system) or genuinely coupled phenomena (predator-prey, chemical reactions). Vector notation: d𝐱/dt = 𝐅(𝐱), where 𝐱 = [x,y]ᵀ. Solutions are trajectories in phase space. Systems reveal behavior invisible in single equations: limit cycles, chaos, multiple equilibria. Understanding systems is essential for multivariable dynamics..
MATH 441: Existence and uniqueness: For systems d𝐱/dt = 𝐅(𝐱) with initial condition 𝐱(t₀) = 𝐱₀, existence and uniqueness extends from single equations. If 𝐅 and its Jacobian ∂𝐅/∂𝐱 are continuous in region containing 𝐱₀, then unique solution exists locally. Lipschitz condition (|𝐅(𝐱) - 𝐅(𝐲)| ≤ L|𝐱 - 𝐲|) ensures uniqueness. Guarantees deterministic dynamics - same initial condition always produces same trajectory. Essential for validating numerical simulations and modeling physical systems where determinism is assumed. Provides theoretical foundation for computational methods..
MATH 285: Constant coefficient linear sy: Constant coefficient linear systems are systems of differential equations where the coefficient matrix has constant entries. Solutions are found using eigenvalue-eigenvector analysis: eigenvalues determine exponential growth rates while eigenvectors determine solution directions. The general solution combines these exponential modes. For complex or repeated eigenvalues, solutions involve complex e
MATH 441: Long-time behavior: Long-time behavior describes system state as t → ∞. Possibilities include: approach to stable equilibrium, divergence to infinity, limit cycle (periodic orbit), quasi-periodic motion (multiple incommensurate frequencies), or chaos (sensitive dependence on initial conditions). Attractors are sets that capture trajectories: point attractors (stable equilibria), periodic attractors (limit cycles), strange attractors (chaotic). Basin of attraction: set of initial conditions leading to specific attractor. Understanding long-time behavior predicts eventual system state regardless of transients, crucial for engineering design and ecological predictions..
ME 340: Time constant: The time constant is a characteristic parameter of first-order systems that quantifies how quickly the system responds to changes. Mathematically denoted as τ (tau), it represents the time required for the system's response to reach approximately 63.2% of its final steady-state value. After about five time constants, the system is considered to have reached its steady state. The time constant is i
MATH 285: System of ordinary differentia: Systems of ordinary differential equations involve multiple interrelated differential equations with multiple dependent variables that evolve simultaneously. These systems model coupled processes where variables influence each other, such as predator-prey populations, chemical reactions, or interconnected mechanical systems. Solution methods include matrix techniques, eigenvalue analysis, and phas
MATH 441: Models, basic examples, classi: Differential equations (DEs) relate functions to their derivatives, modeling rates of change in natural phenomena. Classification by order (highest derivative), linearity (linear vs. nonlinear), and type (ordinary DE - one independent variable; partial DE - multiple). Examples: population growth dy/dt = ky, Newton's cooling dT/dt = -k(T - Tamb), spring-mass system mx'' + cx' + kx = 0. Initial value problems (IVPs) specify function value at one point; boundary value problems (BVPs) specify at multiple points. Understanding DE classification guides solution techniques..
ME 412: One-dimensional unsteady heat co: One-dimensional unsteady (transient) heat conduction introduces time dependence: ∂T/∂t = α∂²T/∂x². Time discretization uses explicit (forward Euler), implicit (backward Euler), or Crank-Nicolson schemes. Explicit methods are simple but stability-limited (Δt ≤ Δx²/2α); implicit methods are stable but require solving linear systems each time step. The method of lines separates spatial and temporal discretization. Von Neumann stability analysis determines stability limits. This problem teaches time integration, stability constraints, implicit vs. explicit trade-offs, and marching solutions. Applications include thermal transients in walls, cooling processes, and startup behaviors. Understanding unsteady conduction extends to fluid dynamics where time dependence and stability are crucial..
ME 412: One-dimensional steady heat cond: One-dimensional steady heat conduction provides an ideal introduction to numerical methods. The governing equation d²T/dx² = 0 (constant conductivity, no source) or d²T/dx² + S(x) = 0 (with source) is discretized using finite differences. The resulting tridiagonal system is solved efficiently by Thomas algorithm. Temperature-dependent conductivity and nonlinear boundary conditions (radiation, convection) introduce nonlinearity requiring iterative solution. This seemingly simple problem illustrates discretization, boundary condition implementation, solution algorithms, and convergence behavior. Extending to variable properties and sources demonstrates handling of more complex physics while maintaining analytical solvability for verification, making 1D steady conduction the perfect pedagogical starting point..
ME 412: Steady 2d heat conduction: Two-dimensional steady heat conduction extends to spatial problems: ∂²T/∂x² + ∂²T/∂y² = 0 (Laplace equation) or with source terms (Poisson equation). Five-point stencils discretize the Laplacian on rectangular grids. The resulting large sparse linear system requires iterative solution methods: Jacobi, Gauss-Seidel, successive over-relaxation (SOR), or conjugate gradient. Convergence acceleration techniques and multigrid methods improve efficiency. Complex geometries use boundary-fitted grids or immersed boundary methods. This topic teaches handling multiple dimensions, sparse matrix solvers, iteration convergence, and computational efficiency—skills directly transferable to fluid flow problems. Applications include fin analysis, electronic cooling, and building heat transfer..
ME 360: Response to periodic signals: Analysis of system steady-state output when driven by periodic inputs, including sinusoidal responses characterized by frequency response function and multi-harmonic periodic signals analyzed using Fourier series. For linear time-invariant systems, each harmonic component is scaled and phase-shifted independently according to system frequency response at that frequency. Total response is superposi
TAM 470: Vibrations: Vibrations involve oscillatory motion about equilibrium. Single DOF: mẍ + cẋ + kx = F(t) with natural frequency ωₙ = √(k/m), damping ratio ζ = c/(2√(km)). Free vibration: undamped (sinusoidal), underdamped (exponential decay × sinusoid), critically damped (fastest return), overdamped (slow return). Forced vibration: resonance when driving frequency ≈ ωₙ, frequency response function H(ω). Multi-DOF
ME 340: Linearization and stability: Linearization approximates nonlinear systems by Taylor expansion about equilibrium points, retaining only first-order terms to obtain linear equations governing small perturbations. The resulting linear system's eigenvalues determine equilibrium stability: negative real parts indicate stable (perturbations decay), positive real parts indicate unstable (perturbations grow), and imaginary parts indi
ME 462: Observer design: Designing dynamic systems (observers or estimators) that reconstruct unmeasured state variables from available output measurements and known inputs, enabling state feedback control when full state measurement is impractical. Luenberger observer uses error correction structure ẋ̂ = Aẋ̂ + Bu + L(y - Cx̂) where gain L places observer poles for desired estimation convergence speed, typically faster than controller dynamics. Separation principle allows independent design of controller and observer. Requires system observability. Kalman filter provides optimal observer for stochastic systems. Applications include estimating velocities from position sensors, internal states of chemical processes, and battery state-of-charge in electric vehicles..
TAM 412: Stability: Stability characterizes whether small perturbations from equilibrium or steady motion decay (stable), persist (neutrally stable), or grow (unstable). Linear stability analysis examines eigenvalues of the linearized system: negative real parts indicate stability, positive indicate instability. For equilibria, stability relates to potential energy: local minima are stable, maxima unstable, saddles c
ME 462: System-theoretic properties of d: Fundamental properties of discrete-time (DT) and sampled-data systems including controllability (ability to reach all states), observability (ability to determine states from outputs), stability (bounded responses), and realizability. Sampled-data systems combine continuous-time plants with discrete-time controllers through sample-and-hold, exhibiting intersample behavior between control updates. Stability analysis uses z-domain (poles inside unit circle) and modified Nyquist criterion. Controllability/observability require rank conditions on discrete-time state-space matrices. Understanding these properties ensures designed controllers achieve fundamental control objectives despite sampling effects. Applications include all computer-controlled systems requiring verification of theoretical control properties..
ME 462: H\u221e adaptive control: Adaptive control design minimizing H∞ norm of transfer function from disturbances to tracking errors, providing worst-case performance guarantees under adaptation. Combines adaptive parameter estimation with H∞ control synthesis, updating controller as parameters are identified. Ensures bounded disturbance-to-error gain even during transients when parameters uncertain. More conservative than L1 but provides explicit robustness bounds. Requires solving modified Riccati equations with adaptation terms. Applications include systems with significant disturbances and uncertainty requiring guaranteed disturbance attenuation during learning, such as aerospace vehicles and industrial processes with unknown parameters and external disturbances..
ME 462: Linear dt optimal filtering and: Designing discrete-time filters and controllers optimizing performance criteria expressed as quadratic cost functions balancing tracking accuracy, control effort, and estimation errors. Optimal filtering (Kalman filter) minimizes mean-squared estimation error given process and measurement noise statistics. Optimal control (LQR) minimizes quadratic cost J = Σ(x'Qx + u'Ru) weighting state deviations and control effort. Solutions involve solving discrete-time algebraic Riccati equations. Combining Kalman filter with LQR yields LQG controller. Understanding enables systematic design balancing competing objectives with mathematical optimality guarantees. Applications include navigation systems, economic optimal control, and any system requiring principled trade-offs between performance and control cost..
ME 462: System uncertainties: Modeling and quantifying sources of uncertainty in control systems including parametric variations (mass, aerodynamic coefficients), unmodeled dynamics (high-frequency modes, actuator dynamics), nonlinearities (friction, saturation), sensor noise, and external disturbances. Representations include additive (ΔG added to nominal), multiplicative (ΔG multiplies nominal), parametric (varying parameter sets), and stochastic (probability distributions). Uncertainty bounds derived from physical constraints, identification experiments, or worst-case assumptions. Essential for robust control design ensuring stability and performance despite model imperfections. Applications include all real systems where perfect models impossible, requiring explicit uncertainty consideration in controller synthesis..
ME 340: Steady-state response: Steady-state response is the system behavior that persists after initial transients decay, representing the long-term response pattern. For linear systems under harmonic excitation, the steady-state response is sinusoidal at the forcing frequency with amplitude and phase determined by the system's frequency response function. The steady state is independent of initial conditions, depending only on
MATH 441: Linear homogeneous systems: Linear homogeneous systems have form d𝐱/dt = A𝐱, where A is constant matrix. Solution: 𝐱(t) = e^(At)𝐱₀, where matrix exponential e^(At) = I + At + (At)²/2! + ... General solution: 𝐱 = c₁𝐯₁e^(λ₁t) + c₂𝐯₂e^(λ₂t), where λᵢ are eigenvalues and 𝐯ᵢ are eigenvectors of A. Eigenvalues determine stability and behavior type; eigenvectors give invariant directions. Principle of superposition applies - solutions form vector space. Understanding linear systems provides foundation for linearization near equilibria..
MATH 441: Stability: Stability characterizes whether solutions near equilibrium 𝐱* remain nearby (stable) or diverge (unstable). Asymptotically stable: trajectories approach 𝐱* as t → ∞. Lyapunov stable: trajectories starting near 𝐱* stay near (but may not converge). Unstable: some trajectories starting arbitrarily close diverge. For linear systems, stability determined by eigenvalues: all Re(λ)
ME 340: Linearization of nonlinear syste: Linearization approximates nonlinear system near operating point (equilibrium) using Taylor series. For ẋ = f(x,u), expand about (x̄,ū): Δẋ ≈ A·Δx + B·Δu where A = ∂f/∂x|ₓ̄, B = ∂f/∂u|ₓ̄. Perturbation variables: Δx = x - x̄, Δu = u - ū. Linearized system analyzed using linear tools (eigenvalues, transfer functions, control design). Valid for small deviations. Local stability from linearization pre
MATH 441: Linearization of autonomous sy: Linearization approximates nonlinear system d𝐱/dt = 𝐅(𝐱) near equilibrium 𝐱* by linear system d𝐮/dt = J(𝐱*)𝐮, where 𝐮 = 𝐱 - 𝐱* is perturbation and J = ∂𝐅/∂𝐱 is Jacobian at 𝐱*. Taylor expansion: 𝐅(𝐱* + 𝐮) ≈ 𝐅(𝐱*) + J𝐮 + O(|𝐮|²). Since 𝐅(𝐱*) = 𝟎, d𝐮/dt ≈ J𝐮. Eigenvalues of J determine local stability and behavior type. Hartman-Grobman theorem: if all eigenvalues have Re(λ) ≠ 0 (hyperbolic equilibrium), nonlinear and linear systems are topologically equivalent near 𝐱*. Linearization enables local analysis of complex nonlinear systems..
ME 462: L1 adaptive control: Novel adaptive control architecture separating adaptation (fast parameter estimation) from control (bandwidth-limited implementation) through lowpass filter, enabling fast adaptation without high-frequency control activity. Architecture includes predictor updating parameters rapidly, adaptation law minimizing prediction error, and control filter limiting bandwidth preventing excitation of unmodeled dynamics. Provides guaranteed transient performance and robustness compared to classical adaptive control. Time-delay margin analysis guides filter design. Applications include aircraft with rapid aerodynamic changes, spacecraft, and UAVs requiring fast adaptation to failures or damage while maintaining robustness to unmodeled dynamics and sensor noise..
ME 462: Lateral and longitudinal dynamic: Approximation for aircraft dynamics separating lateral (roll, yaw, side-slip) and longitudinal (pitch, airspeed, altitude) motions exploiting weak coupling at trim conditions, enabling independent controller design for each axis. Longitudinal controlled by elevator and throttle affects pitch and speed. Lateral controlled by aileron and rudder affects roll and yaw. Decoupling simplifies MIMO problem into two SISO or reduced MIMO problems. Valid for small perturbations from trim; coupling increases during aggressive maneuvers. Applications include autopilot design with separate pitch, roll, and yaw controllers, and flight control system architecture exploiting natural dynamics separation..
ME 340: Under-, critically-, and over-da: These three damping regimes categorize second-order system behavior based on damping ratio ζ. Underdamped systems (ζ
ME 462: Lqg/ltr: LQG Loop Transfer Recovery design methodology systematically recovering LQR robustness properties in LQG implementations by appropriately tuning Kalman filter design parameters. LQR guarantees minimum 6dB gain margin and 60° phase margin, but adding observer degrades these margins. LTR adjusts process noise covariance Q in filter design making closed-loop loop transfer function approach target LQR transfer function, recovering robustness at cost of higher observer bandwidth. Provides principled approach balancing LQG optimality with robustness requirements. Applications include aerospace systems, chemical processes, and industrial automation requiring both optimal nominal performance and guaranteed robustness margins..
ME 462: Lqg loop transfer recovery (lqg/: Design technique recovering robustness properties of LQR control in LQG output feedback designs by adjusting Kalman filter tuning parameters. Pure LQR has guaranteed gain and phase margins, but adding observer (LQG) degrades robustness. LTR procedure adjusts filter noise covariance matrices making loop transfer function approach target LQR transfer function, recovering robustness margins. Trade-off requires high observer bandwidth potentially amplifying sensor noise. Provides systematic method combining LQG optimality with robustness guarantees. Applications include aerospace systems, industrial processes, and any application requiring both optimal performance and robustness where LQG alone has insufficient margins..
MATH 285: Eigenvalues: Eigenvalues are special scalars associated with a matrix that indicate the factor by which eigenvectors are scaled during matrix transformation. Finding eigenvalues involves solving the characteristic equation derived from det(A - λI) = 0. In differential equations, eigenvalues determine solution behavior in systems of equations: real negative eigenvalues indicate stability, positive indicate inst
ME 340: Eigenvalues and eigenvectors: Eigenvalues and eigenvectors are fundamental concepts from linear algebra crucial for analyzing MDOF systems. For a matrix A, eigenvalue λ and eigenvector v satisfy Av = λv. In dynamics, eigenvalues of the system matrix determine natural frequencies and decay rates, while eigenvectors define mode shapes. The eigenvalue problem transforms coupled differential equations into decoupled modal equation
MATH 231: The comparison tests: Comparison tests determine series convergence by comparing with series of known behavior. The Direct Comparison Test states: if 0 ≤ a_n ≤ b_n and Σb_n converges, then Σa_n converges; if 0 ≤ b_n ≤ a_n and Σb_n diverges, then Σa_n diverges. The Limit Comparison Test compares lim(n→∞) (a_n/b_n): if the limit is positive and finite, both series behave the same. These tests are particularly useful when
MATH 231: Power series: A power series is an infinite series of the form Σc_n(x-a)^n where c_n are coefficients and a is the center. Power series behave like polynomials of infinite degree and converge for x values within the radius of convergence R, diverge outside, and require individual testing at endpoints. The interval of convergence is (a-R, a+R) plus possibly the endpoints. Power series can be differentiated and i
ME 360: Anti-aliasing filters: Analog lowpass filters placed before analog-to-digital converters to remove signal frequency components above the Nyquist frequency, preventing aliasing distortion in sampled data. Filter requirements include cutoff frequency slightly below Nyquist limit, sharp transition band to maximize usable signal bandwidth while ensuring adequate attenuation in stopband, and minimal phase distortion for pres
ME 360: Discrete fourier transform, wind: Practical computation of frequency spectra for finite-length discrete signals through the Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT), efficiently calculated using Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) algorithms. DFT produces N frequency samples for N time samples, assuming periodic extension of finite-length data. Windowing applies tapered weighting functions (Hamming, Hann, Blackman) to finite-length signals reduc
ME 481: Accelerometers: Sensors measuring acceleration of body segments using micro-electromechanical systems (MEMS) that detect inertial forces from changing velocity. Accelerometers quantify linear accelerations in one, two, or three orthogonal axes, enabling calculation of orientation (using gravity as reference), impact magnitudes, vibration exposure, activity classification, and when integrated, velocity and displacement. Wearable accelerometer applications include gait analysis, fall detection in elderly populations, activity monitoring for health assessment, sports performance evaluation of jump height and impact forces, and workplace ergonomics quantifying whole-body vibration exposure. Understanding sensor principles, mounting considerations, noise characteristics, and integration drift enables accurate measurement in mobile settings..
ME 340: Modal decompositions: Modal decomposition expresses arbitrary motion of continuous systems as infinite series of modal contributions: x(y,t) = Σ qᵢ(t)φᵢ(y), where φᵢ(y) are mode shapes and qᵢ(t) are time-varying modal coordinates. This transformation converts the PDE into infinite uncoupled ordinary differential equations, one per mode, vastly simplifying analysis. Truncating to dominant modes provides accurate approxi
TAM 412: Normal coordinates and modal an: Normal coordinates are linear combinations of generalized coordinates that decouple equations of motion, transforming coupled oscillators into independent single-DOF systems. The transformation q = Φη uses modal matrix Φ whose columns are normalized mode shapes, converting Mq̈ + Kq = 0 to η̈ + Ω²η = 0, where Ω is a diagonal matrix of natural frequencies. Modal analysis uses this decoupling to solv
MATH 231: Integration of rational functi: Partial fraction decomposition breaks rational functions (ratios of polynomials) into simpler fractions that can be integrated individually. The method applies when the numerator degree is less than denominator degree; otherwise, polynomial division is performed first. The denominator is factored into linear and irreducible quadratic factors, each contributing terms to the decomposition. Coefficie
MATH 241: Partial derivatives: Partial derivatives measure how a multivariable function changes with respect to one variable while holding others constant. For f(x,y), the partial derivative ∂f/∂x = lim(h→0) [f(x+h,y) - f(x,y)]/h measures the rate of change in the x-direction. Geometrically, ∂f/∂x gives the slope of the curve formed by intersecting the surface z = f(x,y) with a plane of constant y. Partial derivatives are compu
MATH 285: Exact equations: An exact differential equation is one where the left side is the exact differential of some function, meaning it comes from taking the total derivative of a potential function. If an equation is exact, the solution can be found by integrating to recover the potential function and setting it equal to a constant. Testing for exactness involves checking if mixed partial derivatives are equal, reflect
MATH 442: Completeness: Completeness means eigenfunction set {φₙ} can represent any reasonable function: every f in function space can be approximated arbitrarily well by finite sum Σₙᴺcₙφₙ as N→∞. Formally: ||f - Σₙᴺcₙφₙ|| → 0. For L² space: ∫|f - Σcₙφₙ|² dx → 0 (mean-square convergence). Parseval's identity: ||f||² = Σ|cₙ|², relating function norm to coefficient norm. Completeness justifies using eigenfunction expansions to solve PDEs. Proved using functional analysis. Essential for rigorous PDE theory..
MATH 257: Properties of eigenvectors: Eigenvectors have several important properties: eigenvectors corresponding to distinct eigenvalues are linearly independent; for symmetric matrices, eigenvectors corresponding to distinct eigenvalues are orthogonal; the sum of eigenvalues equals the trace (sum of diagonal entries); and the product of eigenvalues equals the determinant. An n×n matrix with n linearly independent eigenvectors is diagonalizable. These properties simplify analysis of matrix powers, differential equations, and understanding transformation geometry..
MATH 257: Diagonalization: A matrix A is diagonalizable if it can be written as A = PDP⁻¹, where D is diagonal (containing eigenvalues) and P has eigenvectors as columns. Diagonalization simplifies matrix powers: A^k = PD^kP⁻¹, where D^k is easy to compute. Not all matrices are diagonalizable; a necessary condition is having n linearly independent eigenvectors for an n×n matrix. Symmetric matrices are always diagonalizable with orthogonal eigenvector matrices. Diagonalization is central to solving differential equations and analyzing dynamical systems..
MATH 257: Spectral theorem: The spectral theorem states that every symmetric matrix A can be orthogonally diagonalized: A = QΛQ^T, where Q has orthonormal eigenvectors as columns and Λ is diagonal with eigenvalues.
