TAM 335: Free-surface profiles: Free-surface profiles describe water surface elevation variation along channels in gradually varied flow. Profile types depend on channel slope (mild M, critical C, steep S, horizontal H, adverse A) and depth relative to normal and critical depths. M1 (backwater curve): depth decreases downstream approaching normal depth, occurring upstream of dams. M2 (drawdown curve): depth increases downstream, occurring upstream of free overfalls. S2 curves approach critical depth from below. Each profile type has characteristic shape governed by GVF equation. Understanding profiles enables prediction of water surface elevation for flood studies, bridge design clearance, and impacts of hydraulic structures like weirs and gates on upstream water levels..
TAM 335: Similitude and modeling of over: Spillway model testing uses Froude similitude (gravity-dominated flow) where Fr_model = Fr_prototype ensures dynamic similarity. Scale ratios relate geometric, kinematic, and dynamic quantities: length ratio λ_L, velocity λ_V = √λ_L, discharge λ_Q = λ_L^(5/2). Models (typically 1:10 to 1:100 scale) predict prototype discharge capacity, pressure distribution, cavitation potential, and downstream scour. Reynolds and Weber number mismatch (scale effects) introduces uncertainty. Experiments measure discharge coefficients, pressure distributions, and flow patterns using pressure taps and visualization. Understanding spillway modeling enables safe, economical dam design preventing overtopping failures, optimizing discharge capacity, and protecting downstream structures from erosion..
TAM 335: Pipe roughness and friction: Pipe roughness describes surface irregularities affecting friction in turbulent flow. Roughness height ε is characterized relative to diameter as relative roughness ε/D. Pipes are hydraulically smooth (friction independent of roughness), transitional, or fully rough (friction independent of Re) depending on roughness Reynolds number. The friction factor f in Darcy-Weisbach equation depends on both Re and ε/D in turbulent flow; Colebrook equation relates these: 1/√f = -2log(ε/3.7D + 2.51/Re√f). Surface condition (new, corroded, scaled) affects roughness. Understanding roughness effects enables accurate pressure drop prediction and economic pipe selection balancing material cost against pumping costs..
TAM 335: Uniform flow: Uniform flow in open channels has constant depth, cross-sectional area, and velocity along the channel, requiring balance between gravity force driving flow and friction resisting it. This occurs in prismatic channels (constant cross-section) with constant slope and roughness. Manning's equation calculates velocity: V = (k/n)R_h^(2/3)S_0^(1/2) where R_h is hydraulic radius, S_0 is channel slope, n is roughness coefficient. Normal depth y_n corresponds to uniform flow for given discharge and channel geometry. Uniform flow is an idealization rarely achieved in practice but provides baseline for analyzing gradually varied flow. Understanding uniform flow enables channel design determining required slope and cross-section for desired capacity..
TAM 470: Numerical and physical diffusio: Physical diffusion: actual smoothing in physics (heat conduction, viscosity). Governed by diffusion equation ∂u/∂t = α∇²u. Numerical diffusion: artificial smoothing introduced by discretization errors. First-order upwind scheme for advection adds numerical diffusion O(Δx), acting like physical diffusion with coefficient ~cΔx/2. Dispersive errors cause oscillations. High-order schemes reduce numeri
TAM 335: Pipe systems and branching flow: Pipe systems combine multiple pipes, junctions, valves, and pumps in series, parallel, or branching networks. Analysis uses continuity (flow conservation at junctions) and energy equation (identical head between common points). Series pipes: same flow rate, additive head losses. Parallel pipes: same head loss, additive flow rates. Branching systems require iterative solution satisfying all constraints simultaneously. The Hardy Cross method solves pipe networks. Three-reservoir problems illustrate branching complexity. Applications include water distribution, industrial piping, and HVAC systems. Understanding pipe network analysis enables design of municipal water systems, fire protection networks, and complex industrial fluid distribution optimizing pipe sizes for economic operation..
ME 412: Bottlenecks and acceleration tec: Different PDE types exhibit characteristic computational bottlenecks requiring specialized acceleration techniques. Parabolic PDEs (diffusion) are limited by stability constraints on time steps; implicit methods and operator splitting overcome this. Elliptic PDEs (steady-state) require iterative solvers; multigrid methods and preconditioners accelerate convergence dramatically. Hyperbolic PDEs (wave propagation) need special schemes for shock capturing and characteristic-based methods prevent dispersion. Understanding PDE-specific challenges enables selecting appropriate numerical strategies. Techniques include ADI for parabolic systems, algebraic multigrid for elliptic problems, and flux-splitting for hyperbolic equations. This knowledge optimizes simulation performance by matching methods to physics..
TAM 445: Tensor fields: Tensor fields assign tensor value to each point in space: scalar field φ(x), vector field v(x), tensor field T(x). Spatial variation described by gradients, divergences, curls. Gradient ∇φ points toward maximum increase; divergence ∇·v measures outflow; curl ∇×v measures rotation. For tensor fields: gradient ∇T gives higher-order tensor, divergence ∇·T gives lower-order. Field equations (PDEs) gov
ME 462: Linear quadratic gaussian (lqg): Optimal control design for stochastic linear systems combining Kalman filter (optimal state estimation) with LQR controller (optimal state feedback), exploiting separation principle allowing independent design of estimator and controller. LQG minimizes expected quadratic cost for systems with process and measurement noise. Controller u = -Kx̂ uses estimated states from Kalman filter, with gains computed from Riccati equations. Provides systematic design but may lack robustness to model uncertainty. LQG/LTR techniques recover robustness. Applications include aircraft autopilots, economic regulators, and spacecraft attitude control where optimal performance under uncertainty is required despite imperfect measurements..
ME 462: Multiresolution nonlinear self-t: Advanced adaptive control combining multiresolution analysis (wavelets) decomposing signals into frequency bands, nonlinear control handling system nonlinearities, self-tuning adapting to parameter changes, and constraint handling ensuring limits satisfaction. Multiresolution enables separate control of fast and slow dynamics. Nonlinear control uses techniques like feedback linearization. Self-tuning continuously identifies models updating controller. Constraints handled through optimization or saturation. Combines multiple advanced techniques for complex, uncertain, nonlinear systems with safety limits. Applications include flexible structures with vibration control, chemical processes with nonlinear kinetics, and aerospace systems requiring multi-scale control..
ME 462: \u039c-synthesis: Advanced robust control design method (structured singular value synthesis) handling structured uncertainty including parametric variations, unmodeled dynamics, and cross-coupling, providing less conservative designs than H∞ by exploiting uncertainty structure. Design involves iterative D-K iteration alternating between H∞ synthesis and μ analysis. Structured singular value μ quantifies robustness to structured uncertainty. Method handles mixed uncertainty (parametric and dynamic), multiple performance objectives, and MIMO systems. Computationally intensive but provides tightest robustness guarantees. Applications include flight control with uncertain aerodynamics, flexible structures with modal uncertainty, and complex industrial processes requiring guaranteed stability and performance margins..
ME 462: Identification for robust contro: System identification methods specifically tailored for robust control design, providing not just nominal model but also uncertainty bounds quantifying model error. Techniques include worst-case identification minimizing H∞ identification error, set-membership identification computing feasible parameter sets consistent with data, and stochastic embedding providing probabilistic uncertainty descriptions. Frequency-domain methods identify multiplicative or additive uncertainty weights. Validation uses model invalidation tests. Identified uncertainty models directly used in robust controller synthesis (H∞, μ-synthesis). Closes loop between identification and control design. Applications include all robust control applications requiring experimentally-derived uncertainty models for reliable controller design..
ME 462: Polynomial h2 predictors: Optimal predictors for stochastic systems designed in polynomial framework minimizing H2 norm (expected value of squared prediction error), providing k-step-ahead output predictions. Predictor structure derived using spectral factorization of noise spectrum and Diophantine equation solutions. H2 optimization ensures minimum variance prediction. Used in generalized predictive control (GPC) computing control actions based on predicted future outputs. Handles ARMAX models with colored noise. Applications include economic forecasting, demand prediction for inventory control, and any model predictive control requiring optimal multi-step predictions accounting for noise characteristics..
ME 462: Matlab robust control toolbox: MATLAB software package providing functions for robust control analysis and synthesis including uncertainty modeling (ultidyn, ureal), μ-analysis (mussv), H∞ synthesis (hinfsyn), LMI-based design (hinflmi), model reduction (balred), and robustness analysis (robstab, robgain). Enables systematic robust controller design through numerical algorithms without manual calculation. Functions handle uncertainty descriptions, compute worst-case stability and performance, and synthesize controllers guaranteeing robustness. Integration with Control Systems Toolbox provides complete workflow from modeling through robust design to validation. Essential tool for practical robust control engineering enabling focus on problem formulation rather than numerical solution details..
ME 462: Particle swarm optimization (pso: Population-based optimization inspired by social behavior of bird flocking or fish schooling, where particles (candidate solutions) explore search space updating positions based on personal best experience and neighborhood best, balancing exploration (global search) and exploitation (local refinement). Each particle has position (solution) and velocity (search direction) updated using cognitive and social components with inertia weight controlling exploration-exploitation trade-off. Simple implementation, few parameters, effective for continuous optimization. Applications include PID tuning, neural network training, sensor placement, and any high-dimensional continuous optimization where gradient information unavailable or local minima problematic..
ME 447: Basics of genetic algorithms and: Genetic algorithms (GAs) and evolutionary strategies (ES) are population-based optimization methods inspired by biological evolution. They maintain a population of candidate solutions, evaluate their fitness, select high-performing individuals for reproduction, and create new candidates through mutation and recombination operators. Over generations, the population evolves toward better solutions. These methods handle complex, non-convex, high-dimensional design spaces without requiring gradient information, making them suitable for soft system design where analytical gradients are unavailable. Applications include morphology optimization, controller evolution, and material property design for soft robots..
ME 482: Components, building blocks of t: The fundamental molecular and cellular constituents comprising musculoskeletal tissues, including collagen proteins providing tensile strength, elastin enabling recoil, proteoglycans creating swelling pressure, water as the primary space filler and load distributor, cells (chondrocytes, osteoblasts, fibroblasts, myocytes) synthesizing and maintaining matrix, and mineral crystals (hydroxyapatite in bone) providing compressive rigidity. These components assemble hierarchically from molecular to macroscopic scales, with composition and organization determining tissue-specific mechanical properties. Understanding building blocks explains how structural proteins form fibers, how fibers organize into sheets or bundles, how cells sense and respond to mechanical loads, and how composition changes affect tissue mechanics in development, aging, and disease..
ME 481: Muscle: Contractile tissue composed of sarcomeres containing actin and myosin proteins that slide past each other to generate force and produce movement. Muscle mechanics include active force generation dependent on calcium-triggered cross-bridge cycling, passive elastic properties from connective tissue and titin proteins, force-length relationships reflecting optimal sarcomere overlap, force-velocity relationships showing decreased force at higher contraction speeds, and activation dynamics involving motor unit recruitment. Understanding muscle as both force generator and spring enables analysis of movement efficiency, power output, fatigue mechanisms, injury susceptibility, and rehabilitation strategies. Muscle models range from simple Hill-type representations to detailed finite element simulations..
ME 482: Structure, microstructure, compo: The multi-zone organization of articular cartilage from the superficial zone with tangentially oriented collagen fibers, through transitional and deep zones with increasingly perpendicular fiber orientation, to the calcified cartilage and subchondral bone interface. Composition includes 60-80% water, 10-20% collagen (primarily type II), 5-10% proteoglycans (aggrecan) trapping water through negative charge repulsion, and sparse chondrocyte cells. Mechanical properties include depth-dependent modulus increasing from surface to deep tissue, poroelastic behavior with time-dependent stiffening during loading as fluid exudes, aggregate modulus in the MPa range, and ultra-low friction coefficient from fluid pressurization and boundary lubrication. Understanding this structure-function relationship explains joint tribology and osteoarthritis progression..

The hierarchical organization of dense regular connective tissue from tropocollagen molecules forming fibrils with characteristic D-banding, fibrils assembling into fibers showing crimp waviness, and fibers bundled into fascicles surrounded by endotenon containing vessels and nerves. Composition is approximately 70% water, 25% collagen (primarily type I), 2% elastin, and sparse fibroblasts. Mechanical behavior includes nonlinear stress-strain curve with toe region (crimp straightening), linear region (fiber stretching), and yield region (fiber damage), viscoelastic properties causing strain-rate dependence and hysteresis, and ultimate tensile strength of 50-100 MPa. Understanding structure-mechanics relationships explains injury mechanisms, healing challenges due to poor vascularity, and tissue engineering approaches for reconstruction..
ME 482: Unconfined compression of porcin: An experimental technique measuring cartilage mechanical properties by compressing cylindrical samples between platens without lateral constraint, allowing radial expansion as the tissue is compressed axially. This test reveals stress-relaxation behavior as interstitial fluid exudes from the porous solid matrix, time-dependent equilibrium modulus representing drained matrix stiffness, and nonlinear stress-strain relationships. Analysis using poroelastic theory extracts material parameters including aggregate modulus, permeability, and Poisson's ratio. Porcine cartilage serves as a model system with properties similar to human tissue, accessibility for testing, and standardized protocols. Understanding this test enables characterization of cartilage degradation in osteoarthritis and evaluation of tissue engineering constructs..
ME 482: 3-point bend testing of mouse fe: A mechanical test placing a bone on two supports and applying load at the midpoint to induce bending, measuring force-displacement behavior to extract structural properties (stiffness, maximum force, energy to failure) and, combined with geometric measurements, material properties (elastic modulus, bending strength). Mouse femurs serve as models for genetic studies of bone formation and remodeling, disease models, and treatment effects. The test reveals contributions of bone geometry (cross-sectional moment of inertia) and material properties (tissue-level modulus and strength) to whole-bone mechanical performance. Standardized protocols enable comparison across studies, though small specimen size requires careful alignment and grip design to ensure valid bending-dominated failure..
ME 481: Orthopedic biomechanics and impl: The application of mechanical principles to understand musculoskeletal injuries, surgical interventions, and performance of medical devices including joint replacements, fracture fixation hardware, and spinal implants. Analysis evaluates implant-bone interfaces, load transfer mechanisms, wear and fatigue properties, biological integration through osseointegration, and failure modes. Design considerations include biocompatibility, matching mechanical properties to avoid stress shielding, surface treatments promoting bone ingrowth, and long-term durability under cyclic loading. Understanding orthopedic biomechanics guides implant selection, surgical technique optimization, rehabilitation protocol development, and prediction of clinical outcomes through computational modeling and mechanical testing..
ME 481: Bone: The mineralized connective tissue providing structural support, protection of organs, muscle attachment sites, and mineral homeostasis. Bone's hierarchical structure spans from collagen-mineral composites at nanoscale to osteons (Haversian systems) at microscale to cortical and trabecular architectures at macroscale. Mechanical properties include high compressive and tensile strength from mineral content, toughness from collagen providing crack resistance, anisotropy from directional organization, and adaptive remodeling responding to mechanical loading through Wolff's Law. Understanding bone mechanics explains fracture patterns, osteoporosis susceptibility, implant integration, healing processes, and optimization of bone geometry for weight minimization while maintaining strength..
ME 481: Articular cartilage: The specialized avascular connective tissue covering bone surfaces in synovial joints, providing nearly frictionless articulation and distributing contact loads. Cartilage consists of chondrocytes embedded in an extracellular matrix of collagen fibers, proteoglycans, and interstitial fluid, creating a poroelastic material with time-dependent behavior. Mechanical properties include high compressive stiffness from trapped fluid and matrix swelling pressure, low friction coefficient from fluid pressurization and boundary lubrication, and viscoelastic response with depth-dependent properties. Understanding cartilage mechanics explains joint function, osteoarthritis progression involving matrix degradation, implant design requirements, and tissue engineering challenges including limited healing capacity..
ME 483: Extracellular matrix mechanics: The mechanical properties and force-responsive behaviors of the network of proteins and polysaccharides outside cells, including collagens (tensile strength), elastin (elastic recoil), fibronectin (cell adhesion), proteoglycans (swelling pressure), and glycosaminoglycans. ECM provides structural support, resists mechanical loads, stores elastic energy, and presents mechanical cues affecting cell behavior through substrate stiffness, topography, and alignment. Matrix mechanics influence cell differentiation, migration, proliferation, and gene expression through mechanotransduction pathways. Matrix remodeling by cells through secretion, degradation, and cross-linking creates reciprocal mechanical feedback. Understanding ECM mechanics explains tissue biomechanics, wound healing, fibrosis, and mechanically-regulated development..
ME 482: Structure, microstructure, compo: The hierarchical organization from actin and myosin filaments forming sarcomeres, sarcomeres in series creating myofibrils, myofibrils within muscle fibers (cells) up to 30cm long, fibers grouped into fascicles surrounded by perimysium, and fascicles comprising whole muscles enclosed by epimysium. Composition includes contractile proteins (actin, myosin), regulatory proteins (troponin, tropomyosin), structural proteins (titin, desmin), and connective tissue sheaths providing passive elasticity. Mechanical properties include active force generation through cross-bridge cycling, passive elastic response from titin and collagen, force-length relationship with optimal sarcomere overlap at 2-2.5μm, force-velocity relationship showing decreased force at higher shortening speeds, and pennation angle affecting force-length characteristics. Understanding these properties enables muscle modeling for movement analysis..
ME 483: Stem cell mechanics and mechanob: The mechanical properties of stem cells and how mechanical forces regulate their self-renewal, differentiation, and function. Stem cells are mechanically softer than differentiated cells, with stiffness increasing during differentiation. Matrix stiffness directs lineage specification - soft matrices promote neurogenic fates, intermediate stiffness myogenic fates, and stiff matrices osteogenic fates. Mechanical factors including substrate topology, applied strains, shear stress, and confinement regulate stem cell fate through mechanotransduction pathways involving YAP/TAZ, β-catenin, and epigenetic modifications. Understanding stem cell mechanobiology enables optimization of tissue engineering scaffolds, bioreactor culture conditions, and regenerative medicine strategies leveraging mechanical cues for directed differentiation..
ME 482: Structure, microstructure, compo: The multi-scale organization of bone from collagen-mineral nanocomposite to lamellar structures to osteons in cortical bone and trabecular struts in cancellous bone, with composition of approximately 65% mineral (hydroxyapatite), 25% organic matrix (primarily type I collagen), and 10% water by weight. Microstructural features include Haversian systems oriented along principal loading directions, cement lines marking remodeling boundaries, and lacunae housing osteocytes. Mechanical properties include anisotropic elastic modulus (10-20 GPa cortical, 0.1-1 GPa trabecular), high compressive and tensile strength, toughness from crack deflection mechanisms, and adaptive remodeling responding to mechanical stimuli. Understanding these relationships explains fracture patterns, age-related changes, disease effects, and implant design..
ME 481: Whole-body motions and locomotio: The study of coordinated movements involving multiple body segments to achieve purposeful displacement, including walking, running, jumping, climbing, and sport-specific activities. Locomotion analysis examines gait cycles, ground reaction forces, joint kinematics and kinetics, muscle activation patterns, metabolic energy expenditure, and mechanical efficiency. Key concepts include determinants of gait minimizing energy cost, spring-mass models representing running mechanics, inverted pendulum models for walking, and optimization principles governing movement strategies. Understanding locomotion biomechanics informs clinical gait analysis for pathology diagnosis, prosthetic and orthotic design, sports performance enhancement, exoskeleton development, and robotic bipedal locomotion..
ME 482: Second harmonic generation imagi: An advanced microscopy technique exploiting nonlinear optical properties of collagen to visualize fibrillar organization and orientation without staining. When intense laser light illuminates ordered non-centrosymmetric structures like collagen, two photons combine to generate a single photon at exactly half the wavelength (double the frequency). SHG signal intensity and directionality reveal collagen fiber alignment, crimp patterns, damage accumulation, and regional variations in organization. Image analysis quantifies fiber orientation distributions, alignment indices, crimp wavelength and amplitude, and structural changes with mechanical loading or degeneration. This non-destructive imaging enables correlation of microstructural features with mechanical behavior in tendons and ligaments..
ME 483: Aging mechanics: The changes in mechanical properties of cells, tissues, and organisms with age, including matrix stiffening through advanced glycation end-products and collagen cross-linking, reduced cellular contractility and migration, altered mechanosensitivity, decreased tissue elasticity and resilience, and accumulation of senescent cells with altered mechanical phenotypes. Age-related mechanical changes affect tissue function including arterial stiffening increasing cardiac workload, cartilage stiffening reducing shock absorption, tendon stiffening increasing injury risk, and stem cell niche stiffening impairing regenerative capacity. Understanding aging mechanics reveals mechanical contributions to age-related diseases and potential mechanical interventions to promote healthy aging through exercise, mechanical loading, and matrix modification..
ME 483: Cytoskeletal structures: The dynamic network of protein filaments providing mechanical structure, force generation, and intracellular organization, consisting of actin microfilaments (7nm, tension and motility), intermediate filaments (10nm, mechanical stability), and microtubules (25nm, compression resistance and transport tracks). The cytoskeleton resists external mechanical forces, generates contractile forces through myosin motors, reorganizes in response to mechanical stimulation, and transmits forces from adhesion sites to the nucleus affecting gene expression. Cross-linking proteins like filamin, spectrin, and plectin connect different filament systems creating composite mechanical networks. Understanding cytoskeletal mechanics explains cell shape, migration, division, and mechanosensitive responses to substrate stiffness and applied forces..
ME 483: Molecular motors: Protein machines that convert chemical energy (typically ATP hydrolysis) into mechanical work, including myosins (moving along actin, generating contractile forces), kinesins and dyneins (transporting cargo along microtubules), and RNA/DNA polymerases. Motors work through conformational changes coupled to nucleotide binding and hydrolysis, producing forces of piconewtons and stepping nanometers per cycle. In mechanobiology, motors generate cellular contractile forces affecting tissue mechanics, transport mechanosensitive proteins to appropriate locations, and experience mechanical loads affecting their processivity and directionality. Understanding motor mechanics explains muscle contraction, cell migration, vesicle transport, and force generation in cellular processes..
ME 481: Electromyography: The measurement of electrical activity produced by skeletal muscles during contraction using surface electrodes placed on skin or needle electrodes inserted into muscle tissue. EMG signals reflect motor unit action potentials generated when motor neurons activate muscle fibers, providing information about muscle activation timing, relative intensity, fatigue development, and coordination patterns. Signal processing includes amplification, filtering to remove noise and motion artifacts, rectification, and envelope calculation to extract amplitude information. Applications include clinical diagnosis of neuromuscular disorders, assessment of rehabilitation progress, ergonomic evaluation of muscle loading, sports performance analysis, and control signals for prosthetic devices and exoskeletons..
ME 481: Analysis & measurement technique: The methodological approaches and instrumentation systems for quantifying human movement including kinematics (position, velocity, acceleration), kinetics (forces, moments, power), and neuromuscular activation. Techniques encompass motion capture for tracking body segment positions, force plates measuring ground reaction forces, electromyography detecting muscle electrical activity, accelerometers and inertial sensors for portable monitoring, and pressure sensors quantifying contact forces. Integration of these measurements enables comprehensive analysis of gait, athletic performance, workplace ergonomics, rehabilitation progress, and assistive device effectiveness. Understanding measurement principles, error sources, data processing methods, and interpretation frameworks is essential for rigorous biomechanical research and clinical assessment..
ME 483: Proliferation mechanics: The mechanical aspects and regulation of cell division including mitotic rounding where cells stiffen and detach to achieve spherical shape, cytokinesis driven by actomyosin contractile ring generating forces exceeding nanonewtons, mechanical checkpoints ensuring proper spindle formation, and force transmission through adherens junctions coordinating division in epithelia. Mechanical factors regulate proliferation rate through contact inhibition, substrate stiffness effects on growth factor signaling, confinement restricting division, and mechanical damage triggering compensatory proliferation. Post-mitotic cell spreading and re-adhesion involve force generation and mechanosensing. Understanding proliferation mechanics explains morphogenesis, tissue homeostasis, cancer growth, and wound healing where mechanical signals coordinate proliferative responses..
ME 481: Balance and postural control: The neuromuscular mechanisms maintaining center of mass position within stability limits during static standing and dynamic activities through sensory integration, motor planning, and coordinated muscle activation. Balance relies on visual, vestibular, and somatosensory inputs processed by the central nervous system to detect body sway and generate corrective responses. Control strategies include ankle strategy (using ankle torques like an inverted pendulum), hip strategy (flexing at the hip for larger perturbations), and stepping strategy (moving the base of support). Understanding balance mechanisms explains fall risk in aging and neurological disorders, guides rehabilitation interventions, informs assistive device design, and elucidates human movement control principles..
ME 483: Mechanical signaling and mechano: The processes by which cells convert mechanical stimuli (force, deformation, stiffness, flow) into biochemical signals affecting gene expression, protein activity, and cell behavior. Mechanisms include mechanosensitive ion channels opening under membrane tension, integrin clustering triggering focal adhesion kinase activation, force-induced protein unfolding exposing cryptic binding sites, nuclear deformation affecting chromatin organization and transcription factor access, and primary cilia bending detecting fluid flow. Signaling pathways activated by mechanical forces include MAPK cascades, Rho GTPases regulating cytoskeleton, YAP/TAZ shuttling to nucleus on stiff substrates, and calcium signaling through mechanosensitive channels. Understanding mechanotransduction explains how physical forces regulate development, homeostasis, disease, and therapeutic responses..
ME 483: Cancer cell mechanics and mechan: The altered mechanical properties of cancer cells compared to normal cells and how mechanical factors contribute to cancer progression, invasion, and metastasis. Cancer cells typically exhibit reduced stiffness enabling deformation through tissue barriers, altered adhesion affecting migration and detachment, increased contractility driving invasion, and modified mechanosensing affecting response to tissue mechanics. Tumor microenvironment mechanics including increased matrix stiffness from fibrosis, elevated interstitial pressure, and altered topography promote malignant behaviors. Mechanical forces influence cancer cell proliferation, epithelial-mesenchymal transition, drug resistance, and metastatic potential. Understanding cancer mechanobiology reveals mechanical biomarkers for diagnosis and mechanical vulnerabilities as therapeutic targets..
ME 483: Mechanics of cell adhesion: The mechanical aspects of how cells attach to substrates and each other, including bond formation kinetics, force-dependent bond lifetimes, adhesion strength, focal adhesion assembly under force, and catch-bond behavior where some bonds strengthen under tension. Cell adhesion involves clustering of adhesion receptors, recruitment of adaptor proteins, connection to cytoskeleton, and generation of traction forces. Adhesion mechanics regulate cell spreading, migration speed and directionality, matrix remodeling, and mechanosensitive signaling. Quantitative approaches include measuring adhesion forces with atomic force microscopy, traction force microscopy revealing cellular force patterns, and micropipette aspiration testing membrane-cytoskeleton adhesion..
ME 330: Polyphase materials: Polyphase (multiphase) materials contain two or more distinct phases with different compositions and/or crystal structures, including steels (ferrite, cementite), aluminum alloys (α-aluminum, precipitates), and composites. Phase mixtures provide property combinations unattainable from single phases: steel combines soft ductile ferrite with hard cementite for balanced strength and ductility. Phase
ME 447: Application to snake slithering: Modeling snake slithering applies soft robotic simulation to understand and replicate serpentine locomotion. Snakes generate thrust through body undulation combined with anisotropic friction between scales and substrate. Computational models using Cosserat rods or similar frameworks simulate muscle actuation patterns, body-ground contact forces, and resulting motion. This application demonstrates how soft body mechanics, friction models, and control strategies combine to produce efficient limbless locomotion. Insights inform design of snake-like robots for confined space navigation, search and rescue, and inspection applications..
ME 483: Conformational changes: Alterations in three-dimensional protein structure arising from bond rotations, domain movements, or subunit rearrangements in response to ligand binding, chemical modifications, or mechanical forces, without breaking covalent bonds. Conformational changes enable protein function including enzyme catalysis (substrate-induced fit), signal transduction (receptor activation), molecular motors (myosin power stroke), and mechanotransduction (force-induced exposure of binding sites). Changes range from local side-chain rotations to large-scale domain movements, occurring on timescales from picoseconds to seconds. In mechanobiology, mechanical forces can drive conformational changes in mechanosensitive proteins like talin, vinculin, and ion channels, converting mechanical stimuli into biochemical signals..
ME 483: Apoptosis: Programmed cell death involving systematic cellular dismantling through caspase activation, chromatin condensation, membrane blebbing, cell shrinkage, and fragmentation into apoptotic bodies for phagocytosis. Mechanical factors both trigger and are affected by apoptosis - loss of matrix adhesion (anoikis), excessive mechanical stress, and confinement can induce apoptosis, while apoptotic cells lose mechanical integrity, detach from substrates, and round up as cytoskeleton disassembles. Apoptotic mechanical changes include reduced cell stiffness, membrane blebbing driven by actomyosin contractility, and production of apoptotic bodies. Understanding apoptosis mechanics explains tissue remodeling during development, wound healing, and clearance of damaged cells without inflammation..
ME 483: Adhesion proteins: Specialized transmembrane and extracellular proteins mediating cell-cell and cell-matrix attachments, including integrins (connecting extracellular matrix to cytoskeleton), cadherins (calcium-dependent cell-cell adhesion), selectins (rolling adhesion in inflammation), and immunoglobulin superfamily members. These proteins not only provide mechanical linkages but also transmit forces bidirectionally and trigger signaling cascades in response to mechanical loading. Adhesion protein clustering, conformational changes under force, and assembly/disassembly dynamics regulate cell migration, tissue organization, mechanotransduction, and force transmission. Understanding adhesion proteins explains how cells sense substrate stiffness, respond to shear stress, and organize into tissues with mechanical integrity..
MATH 441: Proof of existence and uniquen: Existence and uniqueness theorems guarantee when IVPs have solutions and when those solutions are unique. For dy/dx = f(x,y) with y(x₀) = y₀: if f and ∂f/∂y are continuous in a region containing (x₀,y₀), then a unique solution exists in some interval around x₀. Existence ensures the problem is well-posed (has solution); uniqueness ensures deterministic behavior (one initial condition → one solution path). Without uniqueness, multiple solutions can pass through same point (e.g., dy/dx = √y). These theorems provide theoretical foundation, though they don't construct solutions explicitly..
CS 101: File i/o: File input/output (I/O) reads data from and writes data to files, enabling programs to process large datasets, save results, and exchange data with other programs. Operations include opening files, reading/writing (text, CSV, binary), closing files, and error handling. Understanding file I/O enables analyzing experimental data, saving simulation results, and automating data processing pipelines es
TAM 324: Viscosity of asphalt cements: The temperature and rate-dependent resistance to flow in asphalt binders, quantifying the relationship between applied shear stress and resulting strain rate in these viscoelastic materials. Viscosity decreases dramatically with increasing temperature, following Arrhenius-type relationships, enabling pumping and mixing at elevated temperatures while providing stiffness at service temperatures. Asphalt exhibits both viscous (time-dependent flow) and elastic (recoverable deformation) components, characterized through dynamic shear rheometer testing measuring complex modulus and phase angle. Understanding viscosity-temperature relationships guides selection of asphalt grades for climate conditions, construction temperatures, and pavement performance including rutting resistance and thermal cracking susceptibility..
MATH 231: Strategy for testing series: Series testing strategy involves systematically determining convergence or divergence by selecting appropriate tests. General approach: check if lim(n→∞) a_n = 0 (if not, series diverges by divergence test); identify series type (geometric, p-series, alternating); for positive terms, try comparison, limit comparison, ratio, or root tests; use integral test if terms match a nice function; for alter
CS 521: Basic Cryptographic Primitives: Introduces fundamental cryptographic building blocks such as hashing, digital signatures, and symmetric/asymmetric encryption essential for secure communication and data integrity.
CS 521: Beyond Blockchains: Weak Consens: Weak Consensus and Scalable Settlement: Explores advanced distributed ledger technologies and consensus models that deviate from traditional blockchain structures, focusing on scalability and settlement.
CS 521: Bitcoin: Covers the foundational principles, architecture, transaction mechanisms, and proof-of-work consensus of the Bitcoin blockchain.
CS 521: Consensus Mechanisms: Analyzes different algorithms for achieving agreement in a distributed network, including Proof-of-Work, Proof-of-Stake, and others.
CS 521: DeFi, NFTs, and Applications: Covers the practical applications of blockchain technology in decentralized finance (DeFi), non-fungible tokens (NFTs), and other emerging use cases.
CS 521: Ethereum and Smart Contracts: Explores the Ethereum platform, its virtual machine, and the concept and implementation of smart contracts for decentralized applications.
CS 521: History of Money: Examines the historical evolution of monetary systems and their underlying principles, providing context for digital currencies.
CS 521: Scaling and Layer 2 Solutions: Investigates techniques and architectures designed to improve the transaction throughput and scalability of blockchain networks, such as lightning networks and sidechains.
CS 521: Security, Attacks, and Formal Ve: Details common security threats, attack vectors against blockchain systems, and methods for formally verifying the correctness and security of smart contracts and protocols.
CS 521: Zero-Knowledge Proofs (zk-SNARKs: Studies advanced cryptographic techniques that enable verifiable computation without revealing the underlying data, crucial for privacy and scalability.
CS 568: AI vs. IA (Artificial Intelligen: Differentiates between fully automated artificial intelligence and intelligent assistance systems that augment human capabilities.
CS 568: Algorithmic Ethics: Analyzes potential ethical harms, biases, and fairness issues that can arise from the design and deployment of algorithms.
CS 568: Artificial General Intelligence: Discusses concepts and progress related to the development of Artificial General Intelligence, which possesses human-like cognitive abilities.
CS 568: Creativity Support with AI: Explores the use of AI in augmenting human creativity, including applications in art generation and intellectual property considerations.
CS 568: Crowdsourcing Datasets: Investigates techniques for collecting and labeling large-scale datasets using crowdsourcing platforms and gamification.
CS 568: Data Ethics: Considers the ethical implications related to the collection, use, and documentation of datasets for machine learning.
CS 568: Decision-Making Support with AI: Investigates the design and impact of AI systems intended to assist humans in complex decision-making processes.
CS 568: Design Guidelines for Human-AI I: Examines established and emerging guidelines for creating intuitive and effective interactions between humans and AI systems.
CS 568: Evaluation Metrics for ML System: Addresses the challenges and appropriate selection of performance metrics for evaluating machine learning models in real-world applications.
CS 568: Feedback Loops in Interactive ML: Examines the role and design of feedback mechanisms in systems where humans and machine learning models collaborate and learn from each other.
CS 568: Generating Datasets with LLMs: Covers the use of Large Language Models (LLMs) for generating synthetic data to train and evaluate machine learning models.
CS 568: LLM-powered Interactions: Explores how Large Language Models can be integrated to create novel and controllable human-AI interaction experiences.
CS 568: Model Interpretability: Investigates methods and challenges in understanding and explaining the decision-making processes of machine learning models.
CS 568: Needfinding with Prototypes: Explores methods for identifying user needs and validating design concepts through iterative prototyping.
CS 568: Prompt Engineering: Focuses on the techniques and best practices for crafting effective prompts to guide the behavior of generative AI models, particularly LLMs and text-to-image models.
CS 568: Retrieval Augmented Generation (: Studies architectures that combine retrieval mechanisms with generative models to produce more informed and contextually relevant outputs.
CS 568: User-Centered AI Design: Introduces guidelines, processes, and systems for designing effective user experiences powered by machine learning models.
